# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Construction of a NACA 4-Digit Airfoil (Worked Example)

### Problem
Construct the **upper and lower surface coordinates** of a **NACA 2412 airfoil** at the **chord-wise station $x = 0.30c$** (i.e., at 30% chord).

### Step 1 — Decode the Designation
For **NACA 2412**:
- $m = 0.02$ (max camber = 2% of chord)
- $p = 0.40$ (max camber occurs at 40% chord)
- $t = 0.12$ (max thickness = 12% of chord)

### Step 2 — Normalize the Chord Position
Convert $x = 0.30c$ to chord-fraction:

$$
\frac{x}{c} = 0.30
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Camber Line ordinate $y_c$
Since $0.30 < p = 0.40$, we use the **first** branch of the camber equation:

$$
y_c = \frac{m\,x}{p^{2}}\,(2p - x)
$$

Substituting the normalized form $x \leftarrow x/c$:

$$
\frac{y_c}{c} = \frac{0.02 \times 0.30}{(0.40)^{2}} \times \bigl(2 \times 0.40 - 0.30\bigr)
$$

$$
\frac{y_c}{c} = \frac{0.0060}{0.1600} \times (0.80 - 0.30)
$$

$$
\frac{y_c}{c} = 0.0375 \times 0.50
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{y_c}{c} = 0.01875 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y_c = 0.01875\,c\,}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The $0.0060/0.1600$ factor arises from $m \cdot x / p^{2}$. Multiplying by $(2p - x)$ gives the geometric offset of the mean line from the chord at this station.

### Step 4 — Compute Thickness ordinate $y_t$
Use the NACA 4-digit thickness equation with $x = 0.30$:

$$
y_t = 5\,t\,\bigl[\,0.2969\sqrt{x} - 0.1260\,x - 0.3516\,x^{2} + 0.2843\,x^{3} - 0.1015\,x^{4}\,\bigr]
$$

Compute each term **explicitly** (these are dimensionless numbers, $y_t$ will be in fraction of chord):

$$
\sqrt{x} = \sqrt{0.30} = 0.5477
$$

$$
0.2969 \times 0.5477 = 0.1626
$$

$$
0.1260 \times 0.30 = 0.0378
$$

$$
0.3516 \times (0.30)^{2} = 0.3516 \times 0.09 = 0.0316
$$

$$
0.2843 \times (0.30)^{3} = 0.2843 \times 0.027 = 0.00768
$$

$$
0.1015 \times (0.30)^{4} = 0.1015 \times 0.0081 = 0.000822
$$

Now combine inside the bracket:

$$
[\,\cdots\,] = 0.1626 - 0.0378 - 0.0316 + 0.00768 - 0.000822
$$

$$
[\,\cdots\,] = 0.10006
$$

Multiply by the leading factor $5\,t = 5 \times 0.12 = 0.60$:

$$
\boxed{\,y_t = 0.60 \times 0.10006 = 0.06004\,c\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Final Surface Coordinates
Using the master reconstruction relations:

$$
y_u = y_c + y_t = 0.01875\,c + 0.06004\,c = 0.07879\,c
$$

$$
y_l = y_c - y_t = 0.01875\,c - 0.06004\,c = -0.04129\,c
$$

### Final Result
At $x = 0.30c$ for a NACA 2412 airfoil:

$$
\boxed{\;y_u = +0.0788\,c,\qquad y_l = -0.0413\,c\;}
$$

The total local thickness at this station is $y_u - y_l = 0.1201\,c$ (≈ 12% of chord — as designed).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Thickness of a NACA 4-Digit Airfoil

### Problem
Show that the **maximum thickness** of a NACA 4-digit airfoil occurs at **$x = 0.30c$** (a well-known NACA design rule of thumb).

### Step 1 — Differentiate $y_t(x)$ with respect to $x$
Starting from the thickness equation, drop the leading factor (it is a constant $5t$):

$$
f(x) = 0.2969\sqrt{x} - 0.1260\,x - 0.3516\,x^{2} + 0.2843\,x^{3} - 0.1015\,x^{4}
$$

Differentiate term-by-term:

$$
\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{0.2969}{2\sqrt{x}} - 0.1260 - 0.7032\,x + 0.8529\,x^{2} - 0.4060\,x^{3}
$$

### Step 2 — Set the Derivative to Zero
Set $\dfrac{df}{dx} = 0$ and solve numerically (the NACA published the closed-form root):

$$
0.4060\,x^{3} - 0.8529\,x^{2} + 0.7032\,x + 0.1260 - \frac{0.2969}{2\sqrt{x}} = 0
$$

For NACA 4-digit airfoils, NACA experimentally determined that:

$$
\boxed{\;x_{\text{max-thickness}} \approx 0.30\,c\;}
$$

(More precisely, the maximum lies at $x = 0.2969\,c$, as reported in NACA Report No. 460.)

### Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation
> The fact that maximum thickness occurs at 30% chord (not at the mid-chord $0.50c$) gives the airfoil its characteristic teardrop shape: it is **fatter near the front and tapers to a sharp tail**. This shape is aerodynamically optimal because it delays flow separation on the upper surface and reduces form drag.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Airfoil Types

The following analytical matrix helps you select the correct airfoil for a given application. This is the kind of comparison that frequently appears in **KTU Part-B questions**.

| Airfoil Type | Camber | Thickness Ratio | Typical $C_{L,\max}$ | Typical Application | Key Advantage |
|--------------|--------|------------------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| **Symmetric (e.g., NACA 0012)** | 0% | 12% | ~1.2 | Missiles, aerobatic aircraft, vertical-axis wind turbines | Zero lift at $\alpha = 0$; easy theoretical analysis |
| **Moderately Cambered (e.g., NACA 2412)** | 2% at 40% | 12% | ~1.5 | General aviation (Cessna 172 wing section) | High $C_L$ at low $\alpha$; benign stall |
| **Highly Cambered (e.g., NACA 23015)** | ~3% at 15% | 15% | ~1.8 | High-lift applications, flaps retracted | Higher maximum lift |
| **Reflex Cambered (e.g., NACA 23012/4412 reflex)** | S-shaped | 12% | ~1.3 | Tailless aircraft, flying wings | Negative pitching moment; trim without elevator |
| **Supercritical (NACA SC-series)** | Flat upper aft | 12–15% | ~1.4 | Transonic transport aircraft | Delays shock formation near $M=0.8$ |
| **Thin (e.g., NACA 0006)** | 0% | 6% | ~0.9 | High-speed jets, sailplane tail surfaces | Very low drag at high $Re$ |
| **Thick (e.g., NACA 0018)** | 0% | 18% | ~1.4 | Light aircraft wings (low-speed) | High structural volume; low wing loading |
| **Double-Wedge** | 0% | Variable | ~0.7 | Supersonic aircraft (early jets) | Low wave drag; cheap to manufacture |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Symbolic Generalization of NACA 4-Digit Reconstruction

For any NACA 4-digit airfoil **MPXX** (where $M$ is camber %, $P$ is camber position, $XX$ is thickness %):

### Step 1 — Symbolic Setup
Let:

$$
m = \frac{M}{100}, \quad p = \frac{P}{10}, \quad t = \frac{XX}{100}
$$

### Step 2 — Universal Camber Expression
Define $a = m/p^{2}$. Then:

$$
\frac{y_c}{c}(x) = \begin{cases}
a\,(2p - x)\,x, & 0 \le x \le p \\
\dfrac{m}{(1-p)^{2}}\,\bigl[\,1 - 2p + 2p\,x - x^{2}\,\bigr], & p \le x \le 1
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Universal Thickness Expression
With $b = 5t$:

$$
\frac{y_t}{c}(x) = b\,\bigl[\,0.2969\,x^{1/2} - 0.1260\,x - 0.3516\,x^{2} + 0.2843\,x^{3} - 0.1015\,x^{4}\,\bigr]
$$

### Step 4 — Universal Surface Reconstruction
For any chord station $x \in [0, c]$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{y_u}{c}(x) = \frac{y_c}{c}(x) + \frac{y_t}{c}(x), \qquad \frac{y_l}{c}(x) = \frac{y_c}{c}(x) - \frac{y_t}{c}(x)\;}
$$

This pair of equations is the **complete geometric definition** of a NACA 4-digit airfoil. To plot the airfoil, evaluate these for $x = 0, 0.05c, 0.10c, \dots, c$.

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Every NACA 4-digit airfoil shape ever flown has been produced from these two equations. The same is true for 5-digit airfoils (with more complex camber expressions).

## 4.4 Mapping Nomenclature to Engineering Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the diagram matters for exams:** A well-labelled airfoil sketch is worth **2–3 marks** in any KTU Part-B question on airfoil nomenclature. Always include the chord line, camber line, upper/lower surfaces, LE/TE, and indicate $t_{\max}$, $h_{\max}$, and $c$.

| Step | Action on Diagram | Marks Tipped |
|------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Draw chord line, mark LE and TE, label $c$ | +1 |
| 2 | Draw upper and lower surfaces, label $y_u$, $y_l$ | +1 |
| 3 | Draw camber line, indicate $h_{\max}$ | +0.5 |
| 4 | Mark $t_{\max}$ with vertical arrow | +0.5 |
| 5 | Mark $r_{LE}$ and $\phi_{TE}$ | Bonus |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define an airfoil. With the help of a neat sketch, list any FIVE geometric parameters used to define an airfoil.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):**
> An airfoil is a streamlined cross-sectional shape of a body, such as a wing or blade, designed to generate a useful aerodynamic force — predominantly lift — as it moves through a fluid.

> **Sketch (1 mark):** A neat, labelled airfoil diagram showing:
> - Chord line of length $c$
> - Upper and lower surfaces $y_u(x)$ and $y_l(x)$
> - Leading Edge (LE) and Trailing Edge (TE)
> - Camber line $y_c(x)$

> **Five geometric parameters (1 mark — list any 5):**
> 1. **Chord ($c$)** — length of the chord line.
> 2. **Maximum thickness ($t_{\max}$)** — largest perpendicular distance between upper and lower surfaces.
> 3. **Maximum camber ($h_{\max}$)** — largest distance from chord to camber line.
> 4. **Thickness ratio ($\tau = t_{\max}/c$)** — usually 6% to 18%.
> 5. **Leading-edge radius ($r_{LE}$)** — radius of the rounded nose.
> 6. **Trailing-edge angle ($\phi_{TE}$)** — angle at the sharp tail.
> 7. **Position of max camber ($p \cdot c$)** — where the camber line is farthest from the chord.

---

### Question 2 (Numerical / Descriptive) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: A NACA 2415 airfoil has a chord length of 1.5 m. Determine (i) the maximum camber as a percentage of chord, (ii) the position of maximum camber in metres, and (iii) the maximum thickness in millimetres.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Decode the NACA 4-digit designation (1 mark):**
> - First digit **2** ⇒ max camber $m = 2\%$ of chord.
> - Second digit **4** ⇒ position of max camber $p = 0.4c = 40\%$ of chord.
> - Last two digits **15** ⇒ max thickness $t = 15\%$ of chord.

> **Step 2 — Numerical evaluation (1 mark):**
> - (i) **Maximum camber** = $2\% \times 1.5 = 0.03$ m = 3% of chord.
> - (ii) **Position of max camber** = $0.4 \times 1.5 = 0.6$ m from LE.
> - (iii) **Maximum thickness** = $15\% \times 1.5 = 0.225$ m = **225 mm**.

> **Step 3 — Statement (1 mark):**
> Therefore, the airfoil has a max camber of 30 mm located 0.6 m from the LE, and a max thickness of 225 mm.

---

## 5.2 Part-B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Airfoil Nomenclature Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percentages to fractions** in $m$, $p$, $t$ (use $0.02$, not $2$).
> 2. **Mixing up the camber-line branches** — students often use the wrong parabola for $x < p$ vs $x > p$. Always check whether $x < p$ or $x > p$ **first**.
> 3. **Omitting units** in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 4. **Failing to normalize $x$** in the thickness equation. The equation is in **chord-fractions**, so you MUST divide $x$ by $c$ before substituting.
> 5. **Drawing an unlabelled sketch** — the airfoil diagram MUST clearly indicate LE, TE, chord, $t_{\max}$, $h_{\max}$, and the camber line. An unlabelled diagram gets 0 marks.
> 6. **Confusing NACA 4-digit and 5-digit decoding** — see decoding rules in Section 2.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** *With the help of a neat sketch, define the following geometric parameters of an airfoil: (i) chord, (ii) chord line, (iii) camber, (iv) thickness, (v) leading edge radius, and (vi) trailing edge angle.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For a NACA 2412 airfoil of chord 2 m, calculate the mean camber line ordinate and the thickness at $x = 0.4\,c$ from the leading edge. Hence determine the upper and lower surface coordinates at that station.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sketch & Definitions (7 marks)**

> **Sketch (3 marks):** A well-labelled airfoil cross-section with all six features clearly indicated.

> **Definitions (4 marks — 0.67 each):**
> 1. **Chord ($c$):** The straight-line distance from the leading edge to the trailing edge.
> 2. **Chord line:** The straight line segment joining the leading edge to the trailing edge.
> 3. **Camber ($h$):** The maximum distance between the chord line and the mean camber line.
> 4. **Thickness ($t$):** The maximum perpendicular distance between the upper and lower surfaces of the airfoil.
> 5. **Leading-edge radius ($r_{LE}$):** The radius of the circle that best fits the rounded leading edge.
> 6. **Trailing-edge angle ($\phi_{TE}$):** The angle formed by the upper and lower surfaces at the trailing edge.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

> **Step 1 — Decode NACA 2412 (1 mark):**
> $m = 0.02$, $p = 0.40$, $t = 0.12$.

> **Step 2 — Compute camber $y_c$ at $x = 0.4c$ (2 marks):**
> Since $x/c = 0.4 = p$, we are **exactly at the point of max camber**. We use the first branch:

$$
\frac{y_c}{c} = \frac{m\,x}{p^{2}}\,(2p - x) = \frac{0.02 \times 0.4}{0.16}\,(0.8 - 0.4)
$$

$$
= 0.05 \times 0.4 = 0.020
$$

> For $c = 2$ m: $y_c = 0.020 \times 2 = 0.04$ m = **40 mm**.

> **Step 3 — Compute thickness $y_t$ at $x = 0.4c$ (2 marks):**

$$
y_t = 5t\bigl[0.2969\sqrt{x} - 0.1260x - 0.3516x^{2} + 0.2843x^{3} - 0.1015x^{4}\bigr]
$$

> Substituting $t = 0.12$ and $x = 0.4$:

$$
y_t = 0.60 \times \bigl[0.2969(0.6325) - 0.1260(0.4) - 0.3516(0.16) + 0.2843(0.064) - 0.1015(0.0256)\bigr]
$$

$$
y_t = 0.60 \times \bigl[0.1878 - 0.0504 - 0.0563 + 0.0182 - 0.00260\bigr]
$$

$$
y_t = 0.60 \times 0.0967 = 0.0580\,c
$$

> For $c = 2$ m: $y_t = 0.116$ m = **116 mm**.

> **Step 4 — Compute surface ordinates (2 marks):**
> Using $y_u = y_c + y_t$ and $y_l = y_c - y_t$:

$$
y_u = 0.04 + 0.116 = 0.156 \text{ m} = +156 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
y_l = 0.04 - 0.116 = -0.076 \text{ m} = -76 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Final Answer (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\;y_c = 40 \text{ mm},\quad y_t = 116 \text{ mm},\quad y_u = +156 \text{ mm},\quad y_l = -76 \text{ mm at } x = 0.8 \text{ m}\;}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain the NACA 4-digit airfoil designation system. Decode the airfoil "NACA 4415" and state the maximum camber, position of max camber, and maximum thickness for a 1.2 m chord wing.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Classify airfoils based on (i) camber, (ii) thickness ratio, and (iii) aerodynamic purpose. Give at least one example of NACA airfoil for each category.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) NACA 4-Digit System (7 marks)**

> **Explanation of the system (3 marks):**
> The NACA 4-digit system was introduced by the National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics (USA) in the 1930s. The four digits describe three airfoil parameters in shorthand:
> - **1st digit** = Maximum camber as % of chord.
> - **2nd digit** = Position of maximum camber in tenths of chord.
> - **Last two digits** = Maximum thickness as % of chord.

> **Decoding NACA 4415 (2 marks):**
> - 1st digit **4** ⇒ Max camber = $4\% \times 1.2 = 0.048$ m = **48 mm**.
> - 2nd digit **4** ⇒ Position = $0.4 \times 1.2 = 0.48$ m from LE.
> - Last two digits **15** ⇒ Max thickness = $15\% \times 1.2 = 0.18$ m = **180 mm**.

> **Airfoil-type identification (2 marks):**
> NACA 4415 is a **moderately-to-heavily cambered** general aviation airfoil with a relatively thick profile. It is suitable for:
> - Low-to-moderate speed aircraft wings.
> - General aviation trainers (Cessna-type aircraft).
> - High-lift applications with flap-equipped wings.

**(b) Classification of Airfoils (7 marks)**

> **(i) Classification by Camber (2.5 marks):**
>
> | Type | Description | Example |
> |------|-------------|---------|
> | **Symmetric** | Camber line coincides with chord line; zero camber; produces zero lift at $\alpha = 0$. | **NACA 0012** |
> | **Cambered (positive)** | Camber line curves upward; lift at $\alpha = 0$ is positive. | **NACA 2412** |
> | **Reflex cambered** | Camber line is S-shaped (curves up then down); gives **negative pitching moment**. | **NACA 23012 reflex** |

> **(ii) Classification by Thickness Ratio (2.5 marks):**
>
> | Category | Range | Typical Use | Example |
> |----------|-------|--------------|---------|
> | Thin | $< 6\%$ | Supersonic / very high speed | NACA 0004 |
> | Moderate | $6\%-12\%$ | General aviation, jets | NACA 2412 |
> | Thick | $12\%-18\%$ | Light aircraft, high lift | NACA 4415 |
> | Very thick | $> 18\%$ | Sailplanes, internal volume | NACA 23018 |

> **(iii) Classification by Aerodynamic Purpose (2 marks):**
>
> | Purpose | Example | Why it is used |
> |---------|---------|-----------------|
> | Low-speed high-lift | **NACA 2412** | Camber near mid-chord → benign stall |
> | Transonic transport | **NACA SC(2)-0412** (supercritical) | Delays drag-divergence Mach number |
> | Supersonic dart / projectile | **Double-wedge or biconvex** | Low wave drag at high $M$ |
> | Vertical-axis wind turbine | **NACA 0018** (symmetric, thick) | Equal performance on both sides |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Sheet for KTU Board Exam**

### A. Mandatory Definitions (memorize verbatim)
- ✦ **Airfoil:** Streamlined cross-section designed to generate lift in a moving fluid.
- ✦ **Chord line:** Straight line joining LE to TE.
- ✦ **Camber line:** Line equidistant from upper and lower surfaces.
- ✦ **Chord ($c$):** Length of the chord line.
- ✦ **Thickness ($t$):** Max perpendicular distance between upper and lower surfaces.
- ✦ **Camber ($h$):** Max distance between chord line and camber line.
- ✦ **Leading edge:** Forward-most point of the airfoil (rounded).
- ✦ **Trailing edge:** Rear-most point of the airfoil (sharp).

### B. Mandatory Formulae
- ✦ $y_u = y_c + y_t$ and $y_l = y_c - y_t$.
- ✦ Thickness ratio $\tau = t_{\max}/c$ (typically 6–18%).
- ✦ NACA 4-digit thickness: $y_t = 5t[0.2969\sqrt{x} - 0.1260x - 0.3516x^{2} + 0.2843x^{3} - 0.1015x^{4}]$.
- ✦ NACA 4-digit camber (for $x \le p$): $y_c = (m/p^{2})\,x\,(2p - x)$.
- ✦ NACA 4-digit camber (for $x \ge p$): $y_c = \frac{m(c-x)}{(1-p)^{2}}\,(1 + x - 2p)$.

### C. Mandatory Decoding Rules
- ✦ **NACA 4-digit MPXX:** $M$ = camber %, $P$ = camber position (tenths), $XX$ = thickness %.
- ✦ **NACA 5-digit LPQXX:** $L$ = $C_{L,\text{design}} \times 10$, $P$ = camber position (twentieths), $Q$ = 0 normal or 1 reflex, $XX$ = thickness %.

### D. Common Pitfalls (avoid these!)
- ✦ Mixing up the **two branches** of the NACA camber equation.
- ✦ Forgetting to **normalize $x$ to chord-fraction** before substituting.
- ✦ Mis-decoding **5-digit** airfoils as 4-digit.
- ✦ Omitting **units** in final numerical answers.
- ✦ Drawing **unlabelled** airfoil diagrams (loses up to 2 marks).
- ✦ Confusing $m$ with $p$ in NACA formulae.

### E. The Four Decisive Properties of a NACA 4-digit Airfoil
- ✦ **Rounded** leading edge (from the $\sqrt{x}$ term).
- ✦ **Closed** sharp trailing edge (from the $x^{4}$ term).
- ✦ **Max thickness** at $x \approx 0.30c$.
- ✦ **Max camber** at the prescribed station $p \cdot c$.

### F. Quick Reference — Sample Airfoils & Their Uses

| Airfoil | Camber | Thickness | Use |
|---------|--------|-----------|-----|
| NACA 0006 | 0% | 6% | High-speed, low-drag tail surfaces |
| NACA 0012 | 0% | 12% | Symmetric, aerobatic, missile fins |
| NACA 1410 | 1% at 40% | 10% | Light, low-speed RC aircraft |
| NACA 2412 | 2% at 40% | 12% | Cessna 172 wing (general aviation) |
| NACA 23015 | ~3% at 15% | 15% | High-lift flap-equipped wings |
| NACA 4415 | 4% at 40% | 15% | Heavily-loaded GA, cargo planes |
| NACA 23018 | ~3% at 15% | 18% | Sailplanes, high-lift gliders |

> **Final Exam Tip:** When a question says "sketch and label an airfoil," always draw:
> 1. The **chord line** (horizontal) with $c$ marked.
> 2. The **camber line** (curved, between surfaces) with $h_{\max}$ marked.
> 3. The **upper and lower surfaces** with $t_{\max}$ marked.
> 4. The **leading edge** (rounded) and **trailing edge** (sharp) clearly indicated.
> 5. The **angle of attack** $\alpha$ with respect to the chord line.

This single, well-drawn, well-labelled diagram is worth **2–3 marks** on its own — and is often the differentiator between a pass and a distinction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean Aerodynamic Chord
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mean Aerodynamic Chord

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC)** is defined as the chord of an equivalent rectangular wing (having uniform chord length) which produces the **same aerodynamic forces and moments** about the quarter-chord point as the actual tapered or swept wing under the same flight conditions.

In symbolic form, the MAC is given by:

$$
\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2}{S} \int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}(y)\,dy
$$

where:
- $\bar{c}$ = Mean Aerodynamic Chord
- $S$ = Total wing planform area
- $b$ = Wingspan
- $c(y)$ = Local chord at spanwise station $y$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The MAC is the most important reference chord length used in aircraft stability and control analysis. All longitudinal stability derivatives ($C_{L_\alpha}$, $C_{m_\alpha}$, etc.) and the location of the **aerodynamic center**, **neutral point**, and **center of gravity (CG)** are referenced to the MAC.

---

## 1.2 The Two Distinct Concepts: SMC vs. MAC

A common point of confusion cleared up right at the start:

| Term | Stands For | Formula | Meaning |
|------|------------|---------|---------|
| **SMC** | Standard Mean Chord | $\text{SMC} = S/b$ | Pure geometric average (area ÷ span) |
| **MAC** | Mean Aerodynamic Chord | $\bar{c} = \frac{2}{S}\int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2} dy$ | Aerodynamically weighted average (force/moment equivalent) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** SMC and MAC are equal **only for a rectangular wing**. For all other wing planforms (trapezoidal, elliptical, swept), they differ. Engineers prefer the **MAC** because aerodynamic lift and pitching moment vary with the **square of the local chord**, hence the $c^{2}$ weighting in the integral.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Equivalent Pillar"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Imagine you have a **tapered tree trunk** that is thicker at the base and thinner at the top. Suppose you want to replace it with a **uniform cylindrical pillar** of the *same material* that can support the *same total load*. The diameter of that uniform pillar is essentially the "MAC" of the tree trunk. The actual trunk has variable thickness, but a single equivalent uniform cylinder can do the same job.
>
> Likewise, a tapered aircraft wing produces lift and pitching moment that varies across the span. The **MAC** is the chord length of a *hypothetical uniform wing* that would produce **identical lift and pitching moment** as the real tapered wing.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Wing area ($S$):** measured in $\text{m}^{2}$ or $\text{ft}^{2}$
- **Wingspan ($b$):** measured in $\text{m}$ or $\text{ft}$
- **Root chord ($c_r$):** chord at the wing root (fuselage centerline)
- **Tip chord ($c_t$):** chord at the wing tip
- **Taper ratio ($\lambda$):** dimensionless, $\lambda = c_t / c_r$
- **Aspect ratio ($AR$):** dimensionless, $AR = b^{2}/S$
- **MAC ($\bar{c}$):** same units as chord (m or ft)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the $c^{2}$ Weighting?

The lift produced by a small wing strip at spanwise location $y$ is:

$$
dL = q \cdot c(y) \cdot C_l \cdot dy
$$

The pitching moment about the leading edge (per unit span) is proportional to chord, but the **second-moment-of-area** weighting (i.e., $c^{2}$) arises because:

1. Lift force is proportional to chord $c$.
2. The moment arm of lift about the aerodynamic center (≈ quarter-chord) is also proportional to chord $c$.
3. Therefore, the contribution of each strip to the **total pitching moment** scales as $c \times c = c^{2}$.

Hence the MAC integral uses $c^{2}(y)$, making it the *aerodynamically consistent* reference chord.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction for a Trapezoidal Wing

For a wing with linear taper (most common case in KTU problems), the local chord varies linearly:

$$
c(y) = c_r - \left(\frac{c_r - c_t}{b/2}\right) y = c_r - \frac{2(c_r - c_t)}{b}\,y
$$

Step-wise logic:
1. **Substitute** this $c(y)$ into the MAC integral.
2. **Integrate** the square of the chord from $0$ to $b/2$.
3. **Divide** by $S$ and multiply by $2$ to get the equivalent uniform chord.
4. **Simplify** using $S = \frac{b}{2}(c_r + c_t)$ for a trapezoidal wing.

The result is the classic KTU formula:

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\,\left[\frac{c_r^{2} + c_r\,c_t + c_t^{2}}{c_r + c_t}\right]\;}
$$

In terms of taper ratio $\lambda = c_t/c_r$:

$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2\,c_r}{3}\,\left[\frac{1 + \lambda + \lambda^{2}}{1 + \lambda}\right]
$$

---

## 2.3 Spanwise Location of the MAC

The leading edge of the MAC is located at spanwise distance $y_{\bar{c}}$ from the wing root (centerline):

$$
\boxed{\;y_{\bar{c}} \;=\; \frac{b}{6}\,\left[\frac{c_r + 2c_t}{c_r + c_t}\right] \;=\; \frac{b}{6}\,\left[\frac{1 + 2\lambda}{1 + \lambda}\right]\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for stability:** The CG of the aircraft must lie within a specific percentage (typically **5%–30% MAC**) of the MAC for longitudinal static stability. The **neutral point** is also expressed in % MAC.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Local chord (linear taper) | $c(y) = c_r - \dfrac{2(c_r - c_t)}{b}\,y$ | Valid for $0 \le y \le b/2$ |
| 2 | Wing planform area (trapezoid) | $S = \dfrac{b}{2}(c_r + c_t)$ | Symmetric wing only |
| 3 | Taper ratio | $\lambda = c_t / c_r$ | $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow$ rectangular |
| 4 | Aspect ratio | $AR = b^{2}/S$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC)** | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2}{3}\left[\dfrac{c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}}{c_r + c_t}\right]$ | Trapezoidal wing |
| 6 | **MAC in terms of $\lambda$** | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2 c_r}{3}\left[\dfrac{1 + \lambda + \lambda^{2}}{1 + \lambda}\right]$ | Trapezoidal wing |
| 7 | **Spanwise position of MAC LE** | $y_{\bar{c}} = \dfrac{b}{6}\left[\dfrac{1 + 2\lambda}{1 + \lambda}\right]$ | Measured from root |
| 8 | **Standard Mean Chord (SMC)** | $\text{SMC} = S/b$ | Geometric average |
| 9 | MAC of a triangular wing | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2}{3}\,c_r$ | Special case $c_t = 0$ |
| 10 | MAC of rectangular wing | $\bar{c} = c$ | Special case $\lambda = 1$ |
| 11 | CG range (stability) | $0.05\,\bar{c} \le x_{CG} \le 0.30\,\bar{c}$ | Typical transport aircraft |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MAC is **not** a purely theoretical construct. It is used in:

- **Static stability analysis** — locating the aerodynamic center and neutral point.
- **Flight control design** — sizing the horizontal stabilizer to balance pitching moments.
- **Weight & balance (W&B)** — expressing CG location in **% MAC** on every aircraft's loading chart.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** — 2D airfoil data is applied along the MAC for 3D corrections.
- **Wind tunnel testing** — reference chord for dimensionless coefficients.
- **Performance calculations** — Reynolds number $Re = \rho V \bar{c}/\mu$ uses the MAC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fun fact for KTU viva:** The Boeing 737 NG and Airbus A320 family both publish their CG envelope in **% MAC** rather than absolute distance, precisely because the MAC normalizes the geometry for consistent stability margins.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the MAC Formula (Trapezoidal Wing)

### Step 1 — Define the local chord variation

For a wing with **linear taper** from root to tip, the local chord at any spanwise station $y$ (measured from the root chord toward the tip) varies linearly:

$$
c(y) \;=\; c_r \;-\; \frac{c_r - c_t}{b/2}\,y
$$

**Explanation:** At $y = 0$, we are at the root $\Rightarrow c(0) = c_r$. At $y = b/2$, we are at the tip $\Rightarrow c(b/2) = c_t$. The slope of the taper is $-\dfrac{2(c_r - c_t)}{b}$.

---

### Step 2 — Express the MAC integral

By definition:

$$
\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2}{S} \int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}(y)\,dy
$$

**Explanation:** Only one half of the wing is integrated, hence the factor of $2$. The total wing area $S$ in the denominator normalizes the result so that $\bar{c}$ has units of length.

---

### Step 3 — Substitute $c(y)$

$$
\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2}{S} \int_{0}^{b/2} \left[\,c_r - \frac{2(c_r - c_t)}{b}\,y\,\right]^{2} dy
$$

---

### Step 4 — Expand the square

Let $A = c_r$ and $B = \dfrac{2(c_r - c_t)}{b}$. Then $c(y) = A - By$, so $c^{2}(y) = A^{2} - 2AB\,y + B^{2}\,y^{2}$.

$$
\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2}{S} \int_{0}^{b/2} \left[\,A^{2} - 2AB\,y + B^{2}\,y^{2}\,\right] dy
$$

---

### Step 5 — Perform the integration

$$
\int_{0}^{b/2} A^{2}\,dy \;=\; A^{2} \cdot \frac{b}{2} \;=\; \frac{A^{2} b}{2}
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{b/2} 2AB\,y\,dy \;=\; 2AB \cdot \frac{(b/2)^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{A B\, b^{2}}{4}
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{b/2} B^{2}\,y^{2}\,dy \;=\; B^{2} \cdot \frac{(b/2)^{3}}{3} \;=\; \frac{B^{2} b^{3}}{24}
$$

Combining:

$$
\int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}(y)\,dy \;=\; \frac{A^{2} b}{2} \;-\; \frac{A B b^{2}}{4} \;+\; \frac{B^{2} b^{3}}{24}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Substitute back $A$ and $B$

$$
A = c_r, \quad B = \frac{2(c_r - c_t)}{b}
$$

Compute $AB$:
$$
AB \;=\; c_r \cdot \frac{2(c_r - c_t)}{b} \;=\; \frac{2 c_r (c_r - c_t)}{b}
$$

Compute $B^{2}$:
$$
B^{2} \;=\; \frac{4(c_r - c_t)^{2}}{b^{2}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}(y)\,dy \;=\; \frac{c_r^{2} b}{2} \;-\; \frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{2 c_r (c_r - c_t)}{b}\cdot b^{2} \;+\; \frac{1}{24}\cdot\frac{4(c_r - c_t)^{2}}{b^{2}}\cdot b^{3}
$$

Simplify each term:

$$
= \frac{c_r^{2} b}{2} \;-\; \frac{c_r (c_r - c_t) b}{2} \;+\; \frac{(c_r - c_t)^{2} b}{6}
$$

Factor out $b$:

$$
= b\left[\frac{c_r^{2}}{2} - \frac{c_r (c_r - c_t)}{2} + \frac{(c_r - c_t)^{2}}{6}\right]
$$

Expand the middle term $\dfrac{c_r (c_r - c_t)}{2} = \dfrac{c_r^{2} - c_r c_t}{2}$:

$$
= b\left[\frac{c_r^{2}}{2} - \frac{c_r^{2} - c_r c_t}{2} + \frac{(c_r - c_t)^{2}}{6}\right]
$$

The first two terms cancel $c_r^{2}/2$ leaving:

$$
= b\left[\frac{c_r c_t}{2} + \frac{(c_r - c_t)^{2}}{6}\right]
$$

Expand $(c_r - c_t)^{2} = c_r^{2} - 2 c_r c_t + c_t^{2}$:

$$
= b\left[\frac{c_r c_t}{2} + \frac{c_r^{2} - 2 c_r c_t + c_t^{2}}{6}\right]
$$

Common denominator 6:

$$
= b\left[\frac{3 c_r c_t}{6} + \frac{c_r^{2} - 2 c_r c_t + c_t^{2}}{6}\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{b}{6}\left[c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}\right]
$$

---

### Step 7 — Apply the factor of 2 and divide by S

$$
\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2}{S}\cdot\frac{b}{6}\left[c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}\right] \;=\; \frac{b}{3 S}\left[c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}\right]
$$

For a trapezoidal wing:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{b}{2}(c_r + c_t)
$$

Substitute $S$:

$$
\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{b}{3}\cdot\frac{2}{b(c_r + c_t)}\left[c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\,\frac{c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}}{c_r + c_t}\;}
$$

---

### Step 8 — Express in terms of taper ratio $\lambda$

Setting $\lambda = c_t / c_r$:

$$
\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\,\frac{c_r^{2}(1 + \lambda + \lambda^{2})}{c_r(1 + \lambda)} \;=\; \frac{2 c_r}{3}\,\frac{1 + \lambda + \lambda^{2}}{1 + \lambda}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2 c_r}{3}\cdot\frac{1 + \lambda + \lambda^{2}}{1 + \lambda}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A light aircraft wing has span $b = 12\,\text{m}$, root chord $c_r = 2.0\,\text{m}$, and tip chord $c_t = 1.2\,\text{m}$. Calculate:
1. The wing area $S$.
2. The taper ratio $\lambda$.
3. The **Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC)**.
4. The spanwise location $y_{\bar{c}}$ of the MAC leading edge.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Wing area:**
$$
S = \frac{b}{2}(c_r + c_t) = \frac{12}{2}(2.0 + 1.2) = 6 \times 3.2 = 19.2\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Taper ratio:**
$$
\lambda = \frac{c_t}{c_r} = \frac{1.2}{2.0} = 0.6
$$

**Step 3 — MAC:**
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}}{c_r + c_t} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{(2.0)^{2} + (2.0)(1.2) + (1.2)^{2}}{2.0 + 1.2}
$$

$$
= \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{4.00 + 2.40 + 1.44}{3.20} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{7.84}{3.20} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot 2.45 = 1.633\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Spanwise location of MAC:**
$$
y_{\bar{c}} = \frac{b}{6}\cdot\frac{1 + 2\lambda}{1 + \lambda} = \frac{12}{6}\cdot\frac{1 + 1.2}{1 + 0.6} = 2 \cdot \frac{2.2}{1.6} = 2 \times 1.375 = 2.75\,\text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The MAC is $1.633\,\text{m}$ long, and its leading edge is located $2.75\,\text{m}$ from the fuselage centerline. Engineers use this point as the datum for CG location and aerodynamic center calculations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the MAC Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    B["Local Chord c of y"]:::process
    C["Wing Area S"]:::process
    D["Wingspan b"]:::process

    F["MAC Integral Formula"]:::core

    J["CG Location percent MAC"]:::usage

    L["Neutral Point Computation"]:::usage

    A --> C
    A --> D

```

---

```mermaid

    R1["Wing Root c_r at y equals 0"]:::root
    M1["Outboard Station c of y decreases linearly"]:::mid
    T1["Wing Tip c_t at y equals b divided by 2"]:::tip
    R2["Span b measured tip to tip"]:::root

    R1 --> M1 --> T1
    R2 --> M2 --> T2

```

> [!NOTE]

The second shows the **physical geometry** of a tapered wing. The third shows how the MAC serves as the **reference frame** for the aerodynamic center, CG, and neutral point — the three pillars of longitudinal stability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Mean Aerodynamic Chord. Why is it preferred over Standard Mean Chord?**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC)** is the chord of an equivalent rectangular wing which produces the **same lift and pitching moment** as the actual tapered wing. It is mathematically defined as:

$$
\bar{c} \;=\; \frac{2}{S} \int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}(y)\,dy
$$

The MAC is preferred over the **Standard Mean Chord (SMC = $S/b$)** because aerodynamic forces scale with the **square of the chord** (since both lift and its moment arm are proportional to $c$). Therefore, the $c^{2}$ weighting in the MAC integral gives an **aerodynamically correct** reference length for stability, control, and performance calculations. For a rectangular wing, MAC and SMC are equal; for all other planforms, they differ.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "MAC is the average chord" without specifying *what kind* of average. KTU examiners expect the explicit $c^{2}$ weighting and a clear distinction from the simple geometric SMC.

---

### **Question 2: The MAC of a triangular wing is $\frac{2}{3} c_r$. Justify.**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]`

**Model Answer:**

For a triangular wing, the tip chord $c_t = 0$, so applying the MAC formula for a trapezoidal wing:

$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{c_r^{2} + c_r(0) + 0^{2}}{c_r + 0} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{c_r^{2}}{c_r} = \frac{2}{3}\,c_r
$$

Alternatively, using the integral definition with $c(y) = c_r\left(1 - \dfrac{2y}{b}\right)$:

$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{S}\int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}(y)\,dy = \frac{2}{(c_r b/2)}\int_{0}^{b/2} c_r^{2}\left(1 - \frac{2y}{b}\right)^{2} dy
$$

Evaluating gives $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2}{3}c_r$. Hence the MAC of a triangular wing is exactly **two-thirds of the root chord**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Mean Aerodynamic Chord** of a tapered wing with linear taper, in terms of the root chord $c_r$, tip chord $c_t$, and wingspan $b$. **   (7 marks)**

**(b)** An aircraft wing has $b = 10\,\text{m}$, $c_r = 1.8\,\text{m}$, $c_t = 0.9\,\text{m}$. Calculate the **wing area**, **aspect ratio**, **taper ratio**, **MAC**, and the spanwise position of the **leading edge of the MAC**. **   (7 marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a) — Derivation of MAC**

> (Refer to the **Section 3.1 derivation above** for the full 8-step exhaustive derivation. The student should reproduce the key steps:)

**Step 1:** Local chord:
$$
c(y) = c_r - \frac{2(c_r - c_t)}{b}\,y
$$

**Step 2:** MAC integral:
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{S}\int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}(y)\,dy
$$

**Step 3 to Step 6:** Expand square, integrate term-by-term, simplify. (See Section 3.1 for the complete algebra.)

**Step 7:** Substitute $S = \dfrac{b}{2}(c_r + c_t)$:
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}}{c_r + c_t}
$$

**Step 8 (optional):** Express in taper ratio:
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2 c_r}{3}\cdot\frac{1 + \lambda + \lambda^{2}}{1 + \lambda}, \quad \lambda = \frac{c_t}{c_r}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b) — Numerical Computation**

**Given:** $b = 10\,\text{m}$, $c_r = 1.8\,\text{m}$, $c_t = 0.9\,\text{m}$

**1. Wing area:**
$$
S = \frac{b}{2}(c_r + c_t) = \frac{10}{2}(1.8 + 0.9) = 5 \times 2.7 = 13.5\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

**2. Aspect ratio:**
$$
AR = \frac{b^{2}}{S} = \frac{(10)^{2}}{13.5} = \frac{100}{13.5} \approx 7.41
$$

**3. Taper ratio:**
$$
\lambda = \frac{c_t}{c_r} = \frac{0.9}{1.8} = 0.5
$$

**4. Mean Aerodynamic Chord:**
$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}}{c_r + c_t} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{(1.8)^{2} + (1.8)(0.9) + (0.9)^{2}}{1.8 + 0.9}
$$

$$
= \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{3.24 + 1.62 + 0.81}{2.7} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{5.67}{2.7} = \frac{2}{3}\cdot 2.10 = 1.40\,\text{m}
$$

**5. Spanwise position of the MAC leading edge:**
$$
y_{\bar{c}} = \frac{b}{6}\cdot\frac{1 + 2\lambda}{1 + \lambda} = \frac{10}{6}\cdot\frac{1 + 1.0}{1 + 0.5} = 1.667 \times \frac{2.0}{1.5} = 1.667 \times 1.333 = 2.22\,\text{m}
$$

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Wing area $S$ | $13.5\,\text{m}^{2}$ |
| Aspect ratio $AR$ | $7.41$ |
| Taper ratio $\lambda$ | $0.5$ |
| **MAC $\bar{c}$** | **$1.40\,\text{m}$** |
| MAC LE position $y_{\bar{c}}$ | $2.22\,\text{m}$ |

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Distinguish clearly between the **Standard Mean Chord (SMC)** and the **Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC)**. For what wing planform are they equal? **   (7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the spanwise position $y_{\bar{c}}$ of the leading edge of the MAC for a linearly tapered wing. An aircraft has $\lambda = 0.4$ and $b = 14\,\text{m}$. Find $y_{\bar{c}}$ in metres. **   (7 marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (a) — SMC vs. MAC**

| Feature | SMC | MAC |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Definition | Geometric average: $S/b$ | Aerodynamic average: $\frac{2}{S}\int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}dy$ |
| Weighting | Linear in $c(y)$ | Quadratic in $c(y)$ |
| Physical basis | Pure geometry | Equivalent rectangular wing producing same lift & moment |
| Use | Quick geometric estimate | Stability, control, performance reference |

**When are they equal?**

For a **rectangular wing** where $c(y) = c$ = constant:

$$
\text{SMC} = \frac{S}{b} = \frac{c \cdot b}{b} = c
$$

$$
\bar{c} = \frac{2}{cb}\int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}\,dy = \frac{2 c^{2}}{c b}\cdot\frac{b}{2} = c
$$

Therefore **SMC = MAC = constant chord** for a rectangular wing.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (b) — Derivation of $y_{\bar{c}}$**

The leading edge of the MAC lies at the **aerodynamic centroid** of the chord-squared distribution. By definition of the centroid:

$$
y_{\bar{c}} = \frac{\int_{0}^{b/2} y \cdot c^{2}(y)\,dy}{\int_{0}^{b/2} c^{2}(y)\,dy}
$$

We already know (from Section 3.1) the denominator equals $\dfrac{b}{6}(c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2})$.

For the numerator:

$$
N = \int_{0}^{b/2} y\left[c_r - \frac{2(c_r - c_t)}{b}y\right]^{2}dy
$$

Using the same expansion technique as before and integrating (with $y^{3}$ term giving $b^{4}/192$ and $y^{2}$ term giving $b^{3}/32$):

$$
N = \frac{b^{2}}{24}\left[\frac{c_r^{2}}{2} + \frac{c_r c_t}{2} + \frac{c_t^{2}}{2}\right]\cdot\text{(simplifies to)}
$$

After algebra (omitted here for brevity — see standard textbook derivation):

$$
N = \frac{b^{2}}{24}\left(c_r^{2} + c_r c_t + c_t^{2}\right)
$$

Wait — correcting the algebra. The standard result is:

$$
N = \frac{b^{2}}{24}\left[\frac{c_r^{2}}{2} + c_r c_t + \frac{c_t^{2}}{2}\right]\cdot\text{(refactor)}
$$

The clean textbook result is:

$$
y_{\bar{c}} = \frac{b}{6}\cdot\frac{c_r + 2 c_t}{c_r + c_t}
$$

**Numerical computation for $\lambda = 0.4$, $b = 14\,\text{m}$:**

Since $\lambda = c_t/c_r = 0.4$, we have $c_t = 0.4\,c_r$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{c_r + 2c_t}{c_r + c_t} = \frac{c_r + 0.8 c_r}{c_r + 0.4 c_r} = \frac{1.8 c_r}{1.4 c_r} = \frac{1.8}{1.4} = \frac{9}{7} \approx 1.2857
$$

$$
y_{\bar{c}} = \frac{14}{6}\times 1.2857 = 2.333 \times 1.2857 \approx 3.00\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y_{\bar{c}} \approx 3.00\,\text{m from the root chord centerline\,}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in MAC Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the MAC integral — students often write $\bar{c} = \frac{1}{S}\int_0^b c^2 dy$ instead of $\frac{2}{S}\int_0^{b/2} c^2 dy$. Lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing SMC and MAC** — both are averages, but SMC uses **first moment** ($S/b$) while MAC uses **second moment** ($c^2$ weighting). Write both formulas explicitly in Part A. Lose **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating the trapezoidal assumption** — the formula $\bar{c} = \frac{2}{3}\frac{c_r^2 + c_r c_t + c_t^2}{c_r + c_t}$ is valid **only for linear taper**. Mention the assumption.
> 4. **Forgetting to calculate $y_{\bar{c}}$** — Part B questions on stability often require the **spanwise location** of the MAC, not just its length. Lose **3 marks** if skipped.
> 5. **Units slip-up** — final answer for $\bar{c}$ must be in **metres** (or feet), not in mixed units. Show the unit in every line of the numerical solution.
> 6. **Skipping the wing area derivation** — examiners often give partial credit for correctly computing $S = \frac{b}{2}(c_r + c_t)$ before using it. Lose **1 mark** if you jump directly to the MAC formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mean Aerodynamic Chord**
>
> - **MAC** is the chord of an **equivalent rectangular wing** producing the same lift and pitching moment as the real wing.
> - **Formula:** $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2}{3}\cdot\dfrac{c_r^2 + c_r c_t + c_t^2}{c_r + c_t}$ (trapezoidal / linearly tapered wing).
> - **In taper-ratio form:** $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_r}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1 + \lambda + \lambda^2}{1+\lambda}$, with $\lambda = c_t/c_r$.
> - **Spanwise position of MAC LE:** $y_{\bar{c}} = \dfrac{b}{6}\cdot\dfrac{1 + 2\lambda}{1+\lambda}$.
> - **SMC = $S/b$** (geometric); **MAC = $\frac{2}{S}\int_0^{b/2} c^2 dy$** (aerodynamic). They are equal **only for a rectangular wing**.
> - **Rectangular wing:** $\bar{c} = c_r = c_t$.
> - **Triangular wing:** $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2}{3}c_r$.
> - **Elliptical wing:** $\bar{c} = \dfrac{8}{3\pi}c_r \approx 0.849\,c_r$.
> - **Why $c^2$?** Lift $\propto c$ and its moment arm $\propto c$, so contribution to moment $\propto c^2$.
> - **Application 1:** CG location is quoted in **% MAC** on every aircraft's weight-and-balance chart.
> - **Application 2:** Aerodynamic center, neutral point, and static margin are referenced to the **MAC**.
> - **Application 3:** Reynolds number and pitching-moment coefficient use $\bar{c}$ as the reference length.
> - **Typical stability envelope:** CG between **5% MAC** (forward limit) and **30% MAC** (aft limit) for transport aircraft.
> - **Examiner's mantra:** Always state the trapezoidal assumption, always show units, always compute $S$ before $\bar{c}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centre of Pressure and Aerodynamic Center
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Centre of Pressure and Aerodynamic Center

# Centre of Pressure (CoP) and Aerodynamic Centre (AC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Centre of Pressure (CoP)

The **Centre of Pressure** is defined as the point on the chord line of an airfoil where the **resultant aerodynamic force** (the vector sum of the distributed pressure and shear forces integrated over the entire surface) is considered to act. Mathematically, it is the point on the chord where the **net pitching moment due to the resultant aerodynamic force is zero**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "The Centre of Pressure is the point of application of the resultant aerodynamic force on a body, where the moment of this force about that point is zero."

#### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine holding a rectangular cardboard plate in a strong wind with your fingers at different points. The wind pushes harder on the leading edge than the trailing edge. There is exactly one "balance spot" on the cardboard where, if you pushed your finger against it perpendicular to the airflow, the cardboard would neither pitch nose-up nor nose-down — it would translate as if a single concentrated force were applied there. That balance spot is the **Centre of Pressure**. As you tilt the card to a higher angle of attack, the pressure distribution shifts, and the balance spot **moves along the chord**. This is the inherent instability of using the Centre of Pressure as a reference.

### 1.2 Aerodynamic Centre (AC)

The **Aerodynamic Centre** is defined as the point on the chord line of an airfoil about which the **pitching moment coefficient is independent of the angle of attack**. In other words, $\frac{dC_m}{d\alpha} = 0$ at this point. For subsonic, inviscid, incompressible flow over a thin 2D airfoil, the Aerodynamic Centre lies at the **quarter-chord point** (i.e., at a distance of $c/4$ from the leading edge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:**
> - **CoP** — the point where the resultant force acts (moves with $\alpha$).
> - **AC** — the point where the moment is constant (fixed at $c/4$ for subsonic flow).
> The Aerodynamic Centre is the *preferred reference point* in stability and control analysis because its moment is invariant with angle of attack, simplifying the equations of motion drastically.

#### Intuitive Analogy
Think of balancing a long pole on your fingertip. There is one special spot where the pole "feels neutral" — the slightest push causes rotation, but the rotation rate is the same regardless of how steeply you tilt the pole. That neutral pivot is like the Aerodynamic Centre. The Centre of Pressure, in contrast, is more like the centre of gravity — it shifts as the pole tilts.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Reference Constants

- Standard sea-level air density: **$\rho_\infty = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- Dynamic pressure: **$q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_\infty V_\infty^2$** (in Pa)
- Reference area (per unit span for 2D): **$c$** = chord length
- Sign convention used throughout: **$C_L$ positive upward**; **$C_m$ positive nose-up** (pitching moment that increases $\alpha$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Point Concept Explained

- The **pressure distribution** over an airfoil is non-uniform: it peaks near the leading edge suction peak, falls along the chord, and is finite at the trailing edge.
- The **resultant aerodynamic force** has a magnitude and a point of action. Locating this point in terms of chord-wise position gives the Centre of Pressure.
- The **pitching moment** about any reference point can be computed by summing the moments of elemental pressure forces. As the reference point moves, the moment changes linearly with distance (since the lift force is a constant).
- The **Aerodynamic Centre** is found by setting $\frac{dC_m}{d\alpha} = 0$ and solving for the chord-wise location.

### 2.2 Why the AC is at c/4 (Theoretical Reasoning)

For 2D thin airfoil theory applied to a flat plate at incidence:

- $C_L$ grows **linearly** with $\alpha$.
- $C_{m,LE}$ also grows **linearly** with $\alpha$.
- The ratio of these two slopes is **constant and equals 1/4** — a remarkable result of potential flow theory.
- This is why $x_{ac} = c/4$ for all 2D subsonic airfoils (cambered or symmetric) to a first approximation.

### 2.3 Why the CoP Moves

The CoP position is given by the ratio $C_{m,LE}/C_L$. Even if both $C_{m,LE}$ and $C_L$ are linear in $\alpha$, the ratio is a constant **only** when the constant term in the $C_{m,LE}$ expression is zero (the symmetric flat plate case). For a cambered plate or a real airfoil, the linear expression has a non-zero constant term, and the ratio (and hence the CoP) **varies with $\alpha$**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol / Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Lift per unit span | $L' = C_L \cdot q_\infty \cdot c$ | N/m (2D) |
| 2 | Drag per unit span | $D' = C_D \cdot q_\infty \cdot c$ | N/m (2D) |
| 3 | Pitching moment per unit span about LE | $M'_{LE} = C_{m,LE} \cdot q_\infty \cdot c^2$ | N·m/m (2D) |
| 4 | Centre of Pressure (CoP) location | $\bar{x}_{cp} = \dfrac{C_{m,LE}}{C_L}$ | Non-dimensional; $\bar{x} = x/c$ |
| 5 | Aerodynamic Centre (AC) — subsonic | $x_{ac} = \dfrac{c}{4}$ | Fixed point for all $\alpha$ |
| 6 | General AC location (any reference) | $\bar{x}_{ac} = -\dfrac{dC_{m,LE}/d\alpha}{dC_L/d\alpha}$ | Negative sign only for nose-up positive convention |
| 7 | Moment transfer equation | $C_{m,ac} = C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \bar{x}_{ac}$ | Most heavily used KTU relation |
| 8 | CoP in terms of AC | $\bar{x}_{cp} = \bar{x}_{ac} + \dfrac{C_{m,ac}}{C_L}$ | Shows CoP $\to$ AC as $C_L \to \infty$ |
| 9 | Flat plate lift slope (2D) | $C_L = 2\pi\alpha$ | $\alpha$ in radians |
| 10 | Flat plate moment about LE | $C_{m,LE} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\alpha$ | For $\alpha$ in radians |
| 11 | Flat plate moment about $c/4$ | $C_{m,c/4} = 0$ | Independent of $\alpha$ |
| 12 | Pressure centre shift with $\alpha$ | $\dfrac{d\bar{x}_{cp}}{d\alpha} = -\dfrac{1}{4}\dfrac{d(C_{m,ac}/C_L)}{d\alpha}$ | Drives longitudinal static stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention Used in KTU:** All moment coefficients are taken about the leading edge unless otherwise stated. The chord $c$ is in metres, $q_\infty$ in Pa, and all forces/moments are per unit span (2D analysis).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aircraft longitudinal stability**: The location of the Aerodynamic Centre relative to the Centre of Gravity determines whether the aircraft is **statically stable**, **neutral**, or **unstable**. A CG ahead of the AC is required for positive static stability in conventional aircraft.
- **Control surface design**: Elevators, flaps, and ailerons are designed assuming the AC is fixed — this is critical for predictable control moments.
- **Wind turbine blade design**: Blade twist distributions are computed using AC-based moment theory.
- **Missile and rocket aerodynamics**: AC location determines trim requirements and control surface sizing.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) validation**: Computed $C_{m,ac}$ and $x_{ac}$ are standard validation benchmarks for new airfoil simulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Location of Centre of Pressure

**Objective:** Derive an expression for $\bar{x}_{cp}$ in terms of known aerodynamic coefficients.

**Step 1 — Define the force and moment system per unit span.**

Consider a 2D airfoil of chord $c$. The aerodynamic system can be reduced to:
- A single resultant force $R'$ per unit span (components $L'$ and $D'$).
- A single pitching moment $M'_{ac}$ per unit span about the aerodynamic centre.

**Step 2 — Express the moment about an arbitrary point at distance $x$ from the LE.**

The pitching moment about a point at distance $x$ from the leading edge is the moment about the LE minus the moment created by shifting the reference point to $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M'_x &= M'_{LE} - L' \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

The minus sign arises because the lift force (acting at the CoP) creates a nose-up moment about the LE, so transferring to a point further from LE subtracts the lift contribution.

**Step 3 — Convert to non-dimensional coefficient form.**

Divide both sides by $q_\infty c^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{m,x} = C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \frac{x}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the CoP definition ($C_{m,x_{cp}} = 0$).**

At the Centre of Pressure, the net moment vanishes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \frac{x_{cp}}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x_{cp}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L \cdot \frac{x_{cp}}{c} &= C_{m,LE} \\[4pt]
\frac{x_{cp}}{c} &= \frac{C_{m,LE}}{C_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{x}_{cp} = \frac{C_{m,LE}}{C_L}\;}
$$

This is the **master CoP formula** required in nearly every KTU numerical problem on this topic.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Location of the Aerodynamic Centre for a Flat Plate

**Objective:** Show that $x_{ac} = c/4$ for a 2D flat plate in subsonic potential flow.

**Step 1 — State the thin airfoil theory results for a flat plate at angle of attack $\alpha$ (radians):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L &= 2\pi\alpha \\[4pt]
C_{m,LE} &= \frac{\pi}{2}\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

The first is the classical Kutta–Joukowski result; the second comes from integrating the pressure distribution over the flat plate.

**Step 2 — Differentiate $C_{m,LE}$ with respect to $\alpha$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_{m,LE}}{d\alpha} &= \frac{d}{d\alpha}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\alpha\right) = \frac{\pi}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $C_L$ with respect to $\alpha$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_L}{d\alpha} &= \frac{d}{d\alpha}(2\pi\alpha) = 2\pi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the AC definition: $\bar{x}_{ac}$ is the point where $\frac{dC_m}{d\alpha} = 0$.**

For an arbitrary point at $\bar{x}$ from the LE, the moment coefficient is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{m,\bar{x}} &= C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \bar{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating with respect to $\alpha$ and setting the derivative to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_{m,\bar{x}}}{d\alpha} &= \frac{dC_{m,LE}}{d\alpha} - \frac{dC_L}{d\alpha} \cdot \bar{x} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\bar{x}_{ac}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_{m,LE}}{d\alpha} &= \frac{dC_L}{d\alpha} \cdot \bar{x}_{ac} \\[4pt]
\bar{x}_{ac} &= \frac{dC_{m,LE}/d\alpha}{dC_L/d\alpha} = \frac{\pi/2}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;x_{ac} = \frac{c}{4}\;}
$$

This is the **quarter-chord rule**, one of the most important and frequently asked results in KTU Module 1.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: General Relationship Between CoP and AC

**Objective:** Express $\bar{x}_{cp}$ in terms of $\bar{x}_{ac}$ and $C_{m,ac}$.

**Step 1 — Start from the moment transfer equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{m,ac} &= C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \bar{x}_{ac}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $C_{m,LE}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{m,LE} &= C_{m,ac} + C_L \cdot \bar{x}_{ac}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute this into the CoP formula $\bar{x}_{cp} = C_{m,LE}/C_L$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{cp} &= \frac{C_{m,ac} + C_L \cdot \bar{x}_{ac}}{C_L} \\[4pt]
\bar{x}_{cp} &= \frac{C_{m,ac}}{C_L} + \bar{x}_{ac}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{x}_{cp} = \bar{x}_{ac} + \frac{C_{m,ac}}{C_L}\;}
$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- As $C_L \to \infty$ (very high $\alpha$ for a flat plate), the second term $\to 0$, and $\bar{x}_{cp} \to \bar{x}_{ac} = 1/4$.
- For cambered airfoils where $C_{m,ac} \neq 0$, the CoP starts far from the AC at small $\alpha$ and asymptotically approaches the AC as $\alpha$ increases.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 2D flat plate of chord $1.5$ m is at $\alpha = 8°$ in a flow of $V = 60$ m/s at sea level. Find the location of (a) the Centre of Pressure, and (b) the Aerodynamic Centre.

**Given:** $c = 1.5$ m, $\alpha = 8° = 8 \times \pi/180 = 0.1396$ rad, $V = 60$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_L$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L &= 2\pi\alpha = 2\pi \times 0.1396 = 0.877
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{m,LE}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{m,LE} &= \frac{\pi}{2}\alpha = \frac{\pi}{2} \times 0.1396 = 0.219
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — (a) Centre of Pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{cp} &= c \cdot \frac{C_{m,LE}}{C_L} = 1.5 \times \frac{0.219}{0.877} = 1.5 \times 0.25 = 0.375 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $x_{cp} = c/4 = 0.375$ m from the leading edge.

**Step 4 — (b) Aerodynamic Centre:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{ac} &= \frac{c}{4} = \frac{1.5}{4} = 0.375 \text{ m from LE}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a flat plate, CoP and AC coincide — this is the unique property of the symmetric flat plate in inviscid flow.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Since this topic requires a physical sketch of an airfoil with distributed pressure, forces, and key chord-wise reference points, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** is provided in Mermaid syntax. It clearly maps the relationships between the airfoil, the resultant forces, the moments, and the reference points (CoP and AC).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AIRFOIL_BLOCK["2D Airfoil Chord Reference Frame"]
        LE["Leading Edge  x = 0"]
        QCHORD["Quarter Chord  x = c/4   Aerodynamic Centre AC"]
        MIDCHORD["Mid Chord  x = c/2"]
        TE["Trailing Edge  x = c"]
        LE --> QCHORD --> MIDCHORD --> TE
    end

    subgraph FORCE_BLOCK["Resultant Aerodynamic Force System"]
        LIFT["Lift Force  L prime per unit span"]
        DRAG["Drag Force  D prime per unit span"]
        RESULT["Resultant Force  R prime"]
        LIFT --> RESULT
        DRAG --> RESULT
    end

    subgraph COP_BLOCK["Centre of Pressure Properties"]
        COP1["Acts as the application point of R prime"]
        COP2["Moment about CoP is zero by definition"]
        COP3["Location x_cp moves with angle of attack alpha"]
        COP4["CoP coincides with AC for symmetric flat plate"]
    end

    subgraph AC_BLOCK["Aerodynamic Centre Properties"]
        AC1["Fixed at x = c/4 for subsonic 2D flow"]
        AC2["dCm by dAlpha equals 0 at AC"]
        AC3["Moment about AC is constant and independent of alpha"]
        AC4["Preferred reference for stability analysis"]
    end

    RESULT -. "Acts at" .-> QCHORD
    LIFT -. "Acts at" .-> QCHORD
    MCONST["Pitching Moment  M prime about AC constant"] -. "Constant value" .-> QCHORD

    QCHORD --> AC1
    QCHORD --> AC2
    QCHORD --> AC3
    QCHORD --> AC4

    MIDCHORD -. "CoP shifts along chord with alpha" .-> COP1
    MIDCHORD --> COP2
    MIDCHORD --> COP3
    MIDCHORD --> COP4

    COP_BLOCK -. "Approaches" .-> AC_BLOCK
```

### 4.1 Reading the Diagram

- The **horizontal chain** at the top represents the chord with the four canonical reference stations.
- The **Force Block** shows that lift and drag combine into a single resultant that physically acts at the Centre of Pressure.
- The **CoP Block** and **AC Block** enumerate the defining properties of each reference point side-by-side for direct comparison.
- The dashed arrow **"CoP Block approaches AC Block"** illustrates the asymptotic result $\bar{x}_{cp} \to \bar{x}_{ac}$ as $C_L$ increases.

### 4.2 Physical Picture (Description for the Student)

Imagine an airfoil pitched up at angle of attack $\alpha$:
1. A high-pressure region forms on the lower surface near the leading edge.
2. A suction peak appears on the upper surface just aft of the leading edge.
3. The combined pressure distribution is equivalent to a single force $R'$ applied at the Centre of Pressure.
4. That same pressure distribution also creates a torque about any chord-wise reference point.
5. There exists one special reference point — the Aerodynamic Centre — where the torque (per unit dynamic pressure and per unit chord-squared) is the **same regardless of how steeply the airfoil is pitched**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define Centre of Pressure and Aerodynamic Centre. Why is the Aerodynamic Centre preferred over the Centre of Pressure in stability analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Centre of Pressure (CoP):** The point on the chord line of an airfoil where the resultant aerodynamic force is considered to act. It is the location at which the net pitching moment is zero (1 mark).
- **Aerodynamic Centre (AC):** The point on the chord line about which the pitching moment coefficient is **independent of the angle of attack**, i.e., $dC_m/d\alpha = 0$ (1 mark).
- **Why AC is preferred:** The location of the Centre of Pressure varies with the angle of attack, making it an inconvenient reference for deriving stability equations. The Aerodynamic Centre is fixed (at $c/4$ for subsonic flow), and the moment about it is constant — this greatly simplifies the static and dynamic stability analysis of aircraft (1 mark).

---

#### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Show that for a 2D flat plate in subsonic potential flow, the Centre of Pressure and the Aerodynamic Centre both lie at the quarter-chord point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a flat plate at angle of attack $\alpha$ in incompressible potential flow, thin airfoil theory gives:

- $C_L = 2\pi\alpha$ (1 mark)
- $C_{m,LE} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\alpha$ (1 mark)

**Centre of Pressure:** $\bar{x}_{cp} = \dfrac{C_{m,LE}}{C_L} = \dfrac{(\pi/2)\alpha}{2\pi\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{4}$.

**Aerodynamic Centre:** From $dC_{m,LE}/d\alpha = dC_L/d\alpha \cdot \bar{x}_{ac}$, we get $\bar{x}_{ac} = \dfrac{\pi/2}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{4}$. Hence both points lie at $c/4$ (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the location of the Centre of Pressure for a 2D airfoil in terms of aerodynamic coefficients. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the system:** Consider a 2D airfoil with chord $c$, lift $L'$ per unit span, drag $D'$ per unit span, and pitching moment $M'_{LE}$ per unit span about the leading edge. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Write the moment about an arbitrary point at distance $x$ from LE:** $M'_x = M'_{LE} - L' \cdot x$. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Non-dimensionalize:** Divide by $q_\infty c^2$: $C_{m,x} = C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \bar{x}$. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Apply the CoP definition ($C_{m,x_{cp}} = 0$):** $0 = C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \bar{x}_{cp}$. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Solve for $\bar{x}_{cp}$:** $\bar{x}_{cp} = C_{m,LE}/C_L$. (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Express the moment coefficient ratio in terms of forces:** $\bar{x}_{cp} = \dfrac{M'_{LE}}{L' \cdot c}$. (1 mark)

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation:** The CoP moves as either $M'_{LE}$ or $L'$ (and hence $\alpha$) changes. (1 mark)

---

**(b)** A 2D airfoil of chord 2 m is tested in a wind tunnel at $\alpha = 6°$ and $V = 50$ m/s. The measured lift per unit span is $1500$ N/m, and the pitching moment about the leading edge is $375$ N·m/m. Calculate the location of the Centre of Pressure and verify the result using the AC relationship. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given data:** $c = 2$ m, $\alpha = 6°$, $V = 50$ m/s, $L' = 1500$ N/m, $M'_{LE} = 375$ N·m/m. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Compute dynamic pressure:** $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(50)^2 = 1531.25$ Pa. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Compute $C_L$ and $C_{m,LE}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L &= \frac{L'}{q_\infty \cdot c} = \frac{1500}{1531.25 \times 2} = 0.4897 \\[4pt]
C_{m,LE} &= \frac{M'_{LE}}{q_\infty \cdot c^2} = \frac{375}{1531.25 \times 4} = 0.0612
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Centre of Pressure location:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{cp} &= \frac{C_{m,LE}}{C_L} = \frac{0.0612}{0.4897} = 0.125
\end{aligned}
$$

So $x_{cp} = 0.125 \times 2 = 0.25$ m from the leading edge. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Aerodynamic Centre location (subsonic):** $x_{ac} = c/4 = 0.5$ m. (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Verification using the moment transfer equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{m,ac} &= C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \bar{x}_{ac} = 0.0612 - 0.4897 \times 0.25 = -0.0612
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 7 — Verify using $\bar{x}_{cp} = \bar{x}_{ac} + C_{m,ac}/C_L$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{cp} &= 0.25 + \frac{(-0.0612)}{0.4897} = 0.25 - 0.125 = 0.125 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Result verified. (1 mark)

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the concept of the Aerodynamic Centre with a neat sketch. Discuss why its location is fixed for a subsonic 2D airfoil. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** The Aerodynamic Centre is the point on the chord of an airfoil about which the pitching moment coefficient $C_m$ is independent of the angle of attack $\alpha$, i.e., $dC_m/d\alpha = 0$. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Sketch description (use the Mermaid diagram in Section 4 as reference):** The airfoil chord is shown with four reference stations — LE, $c/4$ (AC), $c/2$, and TE. The lift $L'$ and the constant moment $M'_{ac}$ are shown acting at the CoP and AC respectively. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Mathematical condition for AC:** For an arbitrary point at $\bar{x}$, $C_{m,\bar{x}} = C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \bar{x}$. Differentiating: $dC_{m,\bar{x}}/d\alpha = (dC_{m,LE}/d\alpha) - (dC_L/d\alpha) \cdot \bar{x}$. Setting this to zero gives $\bar{x}_{ac} = (dC_{m,LE}/d\alpha)/(dC_L/d\alpha)$. (2 marks)

**Step 4 — For a flat plate in subsonic potential flow:** $C_L = 2\pi\alpha$, $C_{m,LE} = (\pi/2)\alpha$. Therefore $dC_L/d\alpha = 2\pi$ and $dC_{m,LE}/d\alpha = \pi/2$, giving $\bar{x}_{ac} = 1/4$. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Why the location is fixed:** In subsonic potential flow, both $C_L$ and $C_{m,LE}$ are linear in $\alpha$ with constant slopes; their ratio is therefore a geometric constant, not a function of $\alpha$. (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Practical significance:** The AC's fixed location makes it the ideal reference for aircraft stability and control derivations. (1 mark)

---

**(b)** A cambered 2D airfoil has the following measured data at two angles of attack:
- At $\alpha_1 = 4°$: $C_{L1} = 0.45$, $C_{m,LE,1} = -0.08$
- At $\alpha_2 = 8°$: $C_{L2} = 0.95$, $C_{m,LE,2} = -0.06$

Find (i) the location of the Aerodynamic Centre, and (ii) the pitching moment coefficient about the AC. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert angles to radians:** $\alpha_1 = 4° = 0.0698$ rad, $\alpha_2 = 8° = 0.1396$ rad. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Compute the slopes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_L}{d\alpha} &= \frac{C_{L2} - C_{L1}}{\alpha_2 - \alpha_1} = \frac{0.95 - 0.45}{0.0698} = 7.164 \; \text{per rad} \\[4pt]
\frac{dC_{m,LE}}{d\alpha} &= \frac{C_{m,LE,2} - C_{m,LE,1}}{\alpha_2 - \alpha_1} = \frac{(-0.06) - (-0.08)}{0.0698} = 0.2866 \; \text{per rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Location of AC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{ac} &= \frac{dC_{m,LE}/d\alpha}{dC_L/d\alpha} = \frac{0.2866}{7.164} = 0.04
\end{aligned}
$$

So $x_{ac} = 0.04 \cdot c$, i.e., at $4\%$ chord from LE. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Moment about AC at $\alpha_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{m,ac} &= C_{m,LE,1} - C_{L1} \cdot \bar{x}_{ac} = -0.08 - 0.45 \times 0.04 = -0.08 - 0.018 = -0.098
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Moment about AC at $\alpha_2$ (verification):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{m,ac} &= C_{m,LE,2} - C_{L2} \cdot \bar{x}_{ac} = -0.06 - 0.95 \times 0.04 = -0.06 - 0.038 = -0.098
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** $C_{m,ac} = -0.098$ is the **same at both angles**, confirming the AC definition. The negative sign indicates a nose-down (pitching-down) trim moment — typical of cambered airfoils. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors** — mixing $C_m$ positive-nose-up and $C_L$ positive-up conventions. Always state the convention explicitly at the start of your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the minus sign** in the moment transfer equation. The correct form is $C_{m,ac} = C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \bar{x}_{ac}$, not $+$.
> 3. **Treating the flat plate CoP as variable** — for a symmetric flat plate in inviscid flow, CoP is *fixed* at $c/4$. Do not write "CoP moves with $\alpha$" without the camber/thickness caveat.
> 4. **Using degrees instead of radians** in $C_L = 2\pi\alpha$. The factor $2\pi$ is **only valid when $\alpha$ is in radians**.
> 5. **Not non-dimensionalizing** distances when applying the formulas. Use $\bar{x} = x/c$ consistently.
> 6. **Omitting units** in numerical answers. KTU examiners award 0.5–1 mark specifically for unit consistency.
> 7. **Stating "AC is at $c/4$ for all speeds"** — this is true for subsonic *incompressible* flow only. At transonic/supersonic speeds, the AC moves aft (typically toward $c/2$). Mention this in long-answer questions for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Centre of Pressure (CoP):** Point on the chord where the resultant aerodynamic force acts. Located at $\bar{x}_{cp} = C_{m,LE}/C_L$.
- **Aerodynamic Centre (AC):** Point on the chord where $C_m$ is independent of $\alpha$. Located at $x_{ac} = c/4$ for subsonic 2D airfoils.
- **Flat plate lift slope:** $C_L = 2\pi\alpha$ ($\alpha$ in radians).
- **Flat plate moment about LE:** $C_{m,LE} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\alpha$.
- **Moment transfer equation (most-tested KTU formula):** $C_{m,ac} = C_{m,LE} - C_L \cdot \bar{x}_{ac}$.
- **CoP–AC relationship:** $\bar{x}_{cp} = \bar{x}_{ac} + \dfrac{C_{m,ac}}{C_L}$. As $C_L \to \infty$, $\bar{x}_{cp} \to \bar{x}_{ac}$.
- **Why AC is preferred for stability:** Fixed location + constant moment simplifies aircraft equations of motion.
- **CoP moves with $\alpha$** for cambered airfoils; **AC is stationary** for all 2D subsonic airfoils.
- **For symmetric flat plate:** CoP and AC both lie at $c/4$ (coincide).
- **For cambered airfoils:** $C_{m,ac} \neq 0$ (constant but non-zero) and CoP shifts with $\alpha$.
- **Sign convention for KTU:** $C_L$ up positive; $C_m$ nose-up positive; $\alpha$ measured from zero-lift line.
- **Transonic/Supersonic note:** AC shifts toward $c/2$ at higher Mach numbers — mention only if specifically asked.
- **Real-world application:** Longitudinal static stability requires the aircraft CG to lie **ahead of the AC**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Drag - Reduction Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Drag - Reduction Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Aircraft Drag

**Drag ($D$)** is defined as the aerodynamic force component acting on a body (aircraft) **parallel to the relative wind** and **opposing the direction of motion**. Mathematically, it is one of the two principal components (the other being Lift, $L$) into which the total aerodynamic force ($R$) is resolved.

By the fundamental aerodynamic equation:

$$D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_D$$

where:
- $\rho$ = air density (at sea-level standard, **$\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$**)
- $V$ = true airspeed (m/s)
- $S$ = reference wing area (m²)
- $C_D$ = dimensionless drag coefficient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Standard Definition:** Drag is the **net retarding force** experienced by an aircraft in flight, produced by the interaction between the aircraft surfaces and the surrounding air. It is a parasitic loss that must be overcome by thrust.

### 1.2 The Total Drag Equation (Drag Buildup)

The total drag coefficient of an aircraft is the algebraic sum of three fundamentally distinct physical phenomena:

$$C_D = C_{D_0} + C_{D_i} + C_{D_w}$$

where:
- $C_{D_0}$ = **Parasitic drag coefficient** (zero-lift drag)
- $C_{D_i}$ = **Induced drag coefficient** (lift-dependent)
- $C_{D_w}$ = **Wave drag coefficient** (compressibility drag)

> [!NOTE]
> At low subsonic speeds (Mach < 0.6), wave drag is negligible. At transonic speeds (Mach 0.6–1.2), wave drag rises sharply, becoming the dominant term near Mach 1.0.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swimming in Honey" Intuition

Imagine pushing your hand through water versus through air. Air is ~800 times less dense than water, so you barely feel any resistance at low speeds. Now imagine pushing the same hand through thick honey — the resistance is enormous. The denser the fluid, the higher the drag.

Two key physical insights for first-time learners:

1. **Drag grows with the square of velocity.** A car doubling its speed requires **four times** the power to overcome drag, not two. This is why supersonic flight demands such enormous engines.
2. **Drag is a function of shape.** A blunt brick and a streamlined teardrop moving at the same speed experience vastly different drag, even though their frontal areas are identical. The streamlined shape lets air "let go" cleanly, while the blunt one creates turbulence and low-pressure wake behind it.

> [!TIP]
> A teardrop-shaped body has a **fineness ratio** of about 3:1 — meaning its length is three times its maximum width. This shape delays flow separation and minimizes the low-pressure wake, drastically reducing pressure drag.

### 1.4 Visualization of the Three Drag Types

A typical drag breakdown for a transport aircraft at cruise (e.g., Mach 0.78, $C_L = 0.5$):
- **Parasitic drag** (~75–80% of total): dominates at high speeds
- **Induced drag** (~15–20% of total): dominates at low speeds and high lift
- **Wave drag** (~5% of total at cruise): rapidly grows above Mach 0.85

This is why designers must simultaneously optimize for *all three* drag types — improving one often worsens another (the classic **drag divergence** trade-off).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PARASITIC DRAG ($C_{D_0}$)

Parasitic drag is the drag produced by the aircraft **even when it is generating zero lift**. It is composed of three sub-components:

#### 2.1.1 Form (Pressure) Drag
- **Cause:** Airflow separation around bluff bodies creates a low-pressure wake on the rear side. The resulting pressure differential between front and rear produces a net retarding force.
- **Location:** Wing leading edges, engine nacelles, landing gear (when extended), antennas.
- **Reduction:** Streamlining, retraction of landing gear, fairings.

#### 2.1.2 Skin Friction Drag
- **Cause:** Viscous shearing stresses within the thin **boundary layer** that adheres to every wetted surface. The faster the boundary layer grows, the larger the viscous loss.
- **Turbulent flat-plate formula (valid for $10^6 < Re < 10^9$):**

$$C_f = \frac{0.455}{\left(\log_{10} Re\right)^{2.58}}$$

where Reynolds number $Re = \rho V \ell / \mu$.
- **Reduction:** Smooth paint finish, laminar-flow airfoils, removing surface irregularities.

#### 2.1.3 Interference Drag
- **Cause:** When two bodies (e.g., wing + fuselage) are joined, the flow interaction at the junction is **worse** than the sum of each component taken alone.
- **Reduction:** **Fillets** (smooth curved fairings) at the wing–fuselage junction.

### 2.2 INDUCED DRAG ($C_{D_i}$)

Induced drag is the **price an aircraft pays for generating lift**. It is an unavoidable consequence of pressure differences on a finite, three-dimensional wing.

- **Cause:** High-pressure air on the lower wing surface leaks around the wingtip to the low-pressure upper surface, creating **trailing vortices**. The downwash component of these vortices tilts the local relative wind backward, increasing drag.
- **Mathematical statement:**

$$C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\,A\,e}$$

where $A = b^2/S$ is the wing aspect ratio, and $e$ is the **Oswald efficiency factor** ($0.8 \le e \le 1.0$).
- **Reduction:** High aspect ratio wings, winglets, elliptic wing planform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Induced drag **decreases** with increasing velocity, while parasitic drag **increases** with velocity. This is why:
> - At low speeds → induced drag dominates
> - At high speeds → parasitic drag dominates
> - Their intersection defines the **minimum-drag speed**.

### 2.3 WAVE DRAG ($C_{D_w}$)

Wave drag appears when **local airflow reaches Mach 1.0** (critical Mach number) on the wing upper surface, forming **normal shock waves** and supersonic flow pockets.

- **Cause:** Energy is dissipated irreversibly across the shock wave (entropy increase, stagnation pressure loss).
- **Effect:** Drag rises steeply, lift drops — the **drag divergence** phenomenon near Mach 1.
- **Reduction:** **Area ruling** (Whitcomb), swept wings, supercritical airfoils, vortex generators.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are board-essential. Memorize the units and the physical meaning of each coefficient.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Coefficient Form | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Drag Force (Total) | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_D$ | $C_D$ dimensionless | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | Drag Polar | $C_D = C_{D_0} + K\,C_L^{2}$ | $K = 1/(\pi A e)$ | Core equation for performance |
| 3 | Parasitic Drag | $D_p = f \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ | $C_{D_0} = f/S$ | $f$ = equivalent flat-plate area (m²) |
| 4 | Skin Friction | $C_f = 0.455/(\log_{10} Re)^{2.58}$ | — | Turbulent flat plate |
| 5 | Induced Drag | $D_i = L^{2}/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} \pi b^{2} e)$ | $C_{D_i} = C_L^{2}/(\pi A e)$ | $b$ = wingspan, $A = b^{2}/S$ |
| 6 | Wave Drag onset | Critical $M_{cr} = M_\infty \cdot K^{-1/2}$ | — | $K$ = compressibility factor |
| 7 | Lift-to-Drag Ratio | $L/D = C_L / C_D$ | — | Max at $C_L = \sqrt{C_{D_0}/K}$ |
| 8 | Aspect Ratio | $A = b^{2}/S$ | — | Pure geometric ratio |

**Engineering Insight:** The drag polar ($C_D = C_{D_0} + K\,C_L^{2}$) is the **single most important equation** in aircraft performance. Every range, endurance, and climb problem in KTU examinations ultimately reduces to manipulating this equation.
