| 6 | Stagnation Pressure | $P_0 = P \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$ | Pa | Isentropic relation |
| 7 | Density Ratio | $\rho_0 / \rho = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}}$ | dimensionless | Isentropic |
| 8 | Critical Pressure Ratio | $P^* / P_0 = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$ | dimensionless | $\approx 0.5283$ for air |
| 9 | Critical Temperature Ratio | $T^* / T_0 = \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}$ | dimensionless | $\approx 0.8333$ for air |
| 10 | Dynamic Pressure | $q = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ | Pa | Used in $C_L$, $C_D$ definitions |
| 11 | Approx. Critical Mach | $M_{cr} \approx 1 - \tfrac{\vert C_{p,min} \vert}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}}$ | dimensionless | Thin-airfoil, small dist. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aircraft Design:** Determines wing sweep, thickness-to-chord ratio, and airfoil selection. Transonic aircraft (e.g., Boeing 737, Airbus A320) cruise near $M \approx 0.78 - 0.82$ and use **supercritical airfoils** to delay drag divergence.
- **Propulsion:** Inlet design and engine matching depend heavily on the flight Mach number. Supersonic inlets (ramjets, scramjets) require $M > 1$ to function.
- **Instrumentation:** The **Mach meter** (combined with the Airspeed Indicator in a single Mach/Airspeed Indicator, M ASI) is a primary flight instrument. It uses a **Rayleigh pitot probe** to measure both static and total pressure, and computes Mach from the ratio $P_0 / P$.
- **Structural & Materials:** At $M > 2$, **aerodynamic heating** becomes a materials problem (e.g., Space Shuttle tiles, titanium leading edges on the SR-71).
- **Range of Flight Regimes:** From $M \approx 0.001$ (helicopter) to $M \approx 25$ (Apollo re-entry), Mach number defines the entire envelope.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Local Speed of Sound, $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$

**Physical Premise:** Sound is a small, isentropic pressure disturbance propagating through a fluid. We apply the **conservation equations** (mass, momentum) to a sound wave of small amplitude and derive the propagation speed.

**Step 1 — Continuity (Mass Conservation) for 1-D flow:**

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Momentum (Euler's Equation, inviscid):**

$$\rho \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + \rho u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce small perturbations.** Let $P = P_0 + P'$, $\rho = \rho_0 + \rho'$, and $u = u'$.

The product $\rho u$ in the continuity equation becomes $(\rho_0 + \rho')(u') \approx \rho_0 u'$ to first order (linearisation). The momentum equation similarly reduces to:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u'}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial P'}{\partial x}$$

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial u'}{\partial x} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Combine using the isentropic relation.** For an isentropic process:

$$P' = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s \rho' = a^2 \rho'$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate the continuity equation w.r.t. $x$ and the momentum equation w.r.t. $t$:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t \partial x} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u'}{\partial x^2} = 0$$

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u'}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\partial^2 P'}{\partial t \partial x} = -a^2 \frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t \partial x}$$

**Step 6 — Eliminate the mixed derivative.** Substituting gives the **wave equation** for $u'$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u'}{\partial t^2} = a^2 \frac{\partial^2 u'}{\partial x^2}$$

The general solution is a wave travelling at speed $a$, where:

$$a^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$$

**Step 7 — Apply the ideal gas law, $P = \rho R T$, under isentropic conditions.** For an isentropic process $P / \rho^\gamma = \text{const}$:

$$a^2 = \frac{\gamma P}{\rho} = \gamma R T$$

$$\boxed{\therefore a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is rarely asked in full in KTU exams, but examiners expect students to **state the wave equation** and **identify $a^2 = (\partial P/\partial\rho)_s$** as the key link. Memorize the boxed result and the **physical meaning**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Stagnation Temperature Relation

**Step 1 — Steady, adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) flow with no work other than flow work.** Apply the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE):

$$h_0 = h + \frac{V^2}{2}$$

$$c_p T_0 = c_p T + \frac{V^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express $V$ in terms of Mach number, $V = M a = M \sqrt{\gamma R T}$:**

$$c_p T_0 = c_p T + \frac{M^2 \gamma R T}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Recall that $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$:**

$$\frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} T_0 = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} T + \frac{M^2 \gamma R T}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $\dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} T$:**

$$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{(\gamma - 1)}{2} M^2$$

$$\boxed{T_0 = T \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** This relation is for a **perfect gas undergoing isentropic compression** to a stagnation state. It does **not** apply across a normal shock wave (where entropy increases).

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Examples

#### Example 1 — Speed of Sound at Altitude

**Problem:** Find the local speed of sound at an altitude of 11 km in the standard atmosphere, where $T = 216.5$ K. Also, find the TAS of an aircraft flying at $M = 0.85$ at this altitude.

**Solution:**

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 216.5}$$

Computing the product:

$$1.4 \times 287 = 401.8$$

$$401.8 \times 216.5 = 86\,989.7$$

$$a = \sqrt{86\,989.7} = 294.94 \text{ m/s}$$

TAS:

$$V = M \cdot a = 0.85 \times 294.94 = 250.7 \text{ m/s} = 902.5 \text{ km/h}$$

#### Example 2 — Stagnation Temperature from Mach

**Problem:** An aircraft cruises at $M = 0.82$ at an altitude where the ambient temperature is $-30^\circ$C. Find the stagnation temperature and stagnation pressure ratio. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $P_\infty = 30$ kPa.

**Solution:**

Ambient: $T = 273.15 - 30 = 243.15$ K.

$$T_0 = T \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right) = 243.15 \left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2} \times 0.82^2\right)$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$0.82^2 = 0.6724$$

$$0.2 \times 0.6724 = 0.13448$$

$$1 + 0.13448 = 1.13448$$

$$T_0 = 243.15 \times 1.13448 = 275.85 \text{ K} \approx 276 \text{ K}$$

Stagnation pressure ratio:

$$\frac{P_0}{P} = (1.13448)^{3.5}$$

Compute logarithmically: $\ln(1.13448) = 0.12622$, $\times 3.5 = 0.44178$, $e^{0.44178} = 1.555$.

$$\frac{P_0}{P} \approx 1.555$$

$$P_0 = 1.555 \times 30 = 46.66 \text{ kPa}$$

#### Example 3 — Critical Mach Number (KTU Favourite)

**Problem:** A wing has a maximum local suction peak $C_{p,min} = -0.55$. Estimate the critical Mach number using the thin-airfoil approximation. Take $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Solution:**

$$M_{cr} \approx 1 - \frac{\vert C_{p,min} \vert}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}} = 1 - \frac{0.55}{1 + 0.2} = 1 - \frac{0.55}{1.2}$$

$$= 1 - 0.4583 = 0.5417$$

$$\boxed{M_{cr} \approx 0.54}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Flight Regime Classification by Mach Number

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Aircraft in Flight]) --> Mval{Calculate M = V / a}
    Mval --> M05{M less than 0.3}
    Mval --> M08{M less than 0.8}
    Mval --> M12{M less than 1.2}
    Mval --> M50{M less than 5.0}
    Mval --> Mhigh{M greater than or equal 5.0}

    M05 -->|YES| RegSubLow[Low Subsonic M less than 0.3]
    RegSubLow --> NoteSub1[Incompressible flow OK]
    RegSubLow --> NoteSub2[Light aircraft, helicopters]

    M08 -->|YES 0.3 to 0.8| RegSubHi[High Subsonic M 0.3 to 0.8]
    RegSubHi --> NoteSubA[Compressibility matters]
    RegSubHi --> NoteSubB[Commercial jets cruise here]

    M12 -->|YES 0.8 to 1.2| RegTrans[Transonic M 0.8 to 1.2]
    RegTrans --> NoteT1[Shock waves form on body]
    RegTrans --> NoteT2[Wave drag appears]
    RegTrans --> NoteT3[Critical Mach region]

    M50 -->|YES 1.2 to 5.0| RegSuper[Supersonic M 1.2 to 5.0]
    RegSuper --> NoteS1[Attached oblique shocks]
    RegSuper --> NoteS2[Expansion fans at corners]
    RegSuper --> NoteS3[Fighters, Concorde]

    Mhigh -->|YES M greater than 5.0| RegHyp[Hypersonic M greater than 5.0]
    RegHyp --> NoteH1[Real gas effects, dissociation]
    RegHyp --> NoteH2[Aerodynamic heating dominant]
    RegHyp --> NoteH3[Reentry vehicles, scramjets]

    classDef regime fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000
    class RegSubLow,RegSubHi,RegTrans,RegSuper,RegHyp regime
    class NoteSub1,NoteSub2,NoteSubA,NoteSubB,NoteT1,NoteT2,NoteT3,NoteS1,NoteS2,NoteS3,NoteH1,NoteH2,NoteH3 note
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block Architecture of a Mach-Measuring System (Mach Meter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PitotProbe[Rayleigh Pitot Probe] -->|Total Pressure P0| TransducerA[Pressure Transducer A]
    StaticPort[Static Pressure Port] -->|Static Pressure P| TransducerB[Pressure Transducer B]

    TransducerA --> DiffAmp[Differential Amplifier]
    TransducerB --> DiffAmp

    DiffAmp -->|Pressure Ratio P0 over P| CompUnit[Computation Unit]

    CompUnit -->|Iterative root finding| Algorithm[Algorithm fM equals 0]

    Algorithm -->|Reference P0 over P versus M table| LUT[Look Up Table]

    LUT --> OutputSignal[Mach Number M Display]

    PitotProbe -.->|Heated to prevent icing| Heater[Anti Ice Heater]
    StaticPort -.->|Flush mounted on fuselage| Fuselage[Fuselage Skin]

    classDef sensor fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    class PitotProbe,StaticPort,TransducerA,TransducerB,Heater sensor
    class DiffAmp,CompUnit,Algorithm,LUT process
    class OutputSignal,Fuselage output
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Variation of Local Mach Number Over an Airfoil

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FreeStream[Free Stream M inf] --> Upstream[Approaches Leading Edge]

    Upstream --> UpperSurf[Flow Over Upper Surface]
    Upstream --> LowerSurf[Flow Over Lower Surface]

    UpperSurf --> AccelRegion[Strong Acceleration Region]
    AccelRegion --> MinPress[Minimum Pressure Point]
    MinPress --> PeakM[Local M equals M local max]
    PeakM --> CriticalCheck{Local M equals 1}

    CriticalCheck -->|NO| SubsonicReg[Still Subsonic Over Body]
    CriticalCheck -->|YES| FirstSonic[First Sonic Point Reached]

    FirstSonic --> ShockForm[Shock Wave Forms Further Downstream]
    ShockForm --> Recovery[Pressure Recovery to Subsonic]

    LowerSurf --> MildAccel[Mild Acceleration]
    MildAccel --> NoSonic[Local M remains less than 1]

    PeakM --> McrRelation[This free stream M equals M critical]
    Mfree[Free Stream M less than M cr] --> SubsonicReg
    Mfree2[Free Stream M equals M cr] --> FirstSonic
    Mfree3[Free Stream M greater than M cr] --> ShockForm

    classDef flow fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef event fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef state fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,color:#000
    class Upstream,UpperSurf,LowerSurf,AccelRegion,MildAccel flow
    class PeakM,FirstSonic,ShockForm,MinPress event
    class SubsonicReg,NoSonic,Recovery,McrRelation state
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1] **Define Mach number. Why is it considered a fundamental parameter in high-speed aerodynamics rather than the actual airspeed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mach number is the dimensionless ratio of the true airspeed of a body to the local speed of sound in the surrounding medium: $M = V / a$.

It is fundamental because:

- (1 mark) It directly governs **compressibility effects** — density variations, shock formation, and wave drag all depend on $M$, not on $V$ alone.
- (1 mark) It accounts for the **medium's properties** automatically (since $a$ depends on $T$). The same $V$ at sea level and at altitude gives different flow behaviour; the same $M$ does not.
- (1 mark) Aerodynamic similarity (model testing in wind tunnels) requires matching $M$, not absolute speed. Thus, $M$ is the **true non-dimensional similarity parameter** for compressible flow.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2] **Distinguish between Critical Mach Number ($M_{cr}$) and Free-stream Mach Number ($M_\infty$). What is the significance of $M_{cr}$ in aircraft design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | $M_\infty$ | $M_{cr}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Mach number of the undisturbed free stream far from the body | Free-stream Mach number at which the **local** Mach number first reaches 1 somewhere on the body |
| Location | In the free stream, away from any disturbance | Always on the body surface (usually upper surface of wing) |
| Value | Set by flight condition | A property of the **airfoil shape** and thickness |
| Relation | Independent | $M_{cr} \leq M_\infty$ generally |

**Significance in design (1 mark):** $M_{cr}$ sets the **drag divergence Mach number** — designers must ensure cruise $M_\infty$ stays well below $M_{cr}$ to avoid the wave-drag rise. Transonic aircraft use **swept wings and supercritical airfoils** to raise $M_{cr}$ for a given thickness ratio.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Question Style]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the local speed of sound in a perfect gas. Using this, calculate the speed of sound at an altitude of 7 km where the standard temperature is $T = 241.5$ K. State any assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aircraft is flying at a true airspeed of 920 km/h at an altitude where the ambient temperature is $-15^\circ$C. Determine: (i) the Mach number, (ii) the stagnation temperature, and (iii) the ratio of stagnation to static pressure. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (4 marks) + Calculation (3 marks)**

**Derivation:**

Starting from the 1-D continuity and momentum equations for small perturbations, linearisation yields the wave equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u'}{\partial t^2} = a^2 \frac{\partial^2 u'}{\partial x^2}$$

This implies disturbances propagate at speed $a$, where:

$$a^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$$

For an **isentropic** process in a perfect gas, $P/\rho^\gamma = \text{const}$:

$$a^2 = \frac{\gamma P}{\rho} = \gamma R T$$

$$\boxed{a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Flow is inviscid
- Perturbations are small (linear acoustic waves)
- Process is isentropic (adiabatic and reversible)
- Air behaves as a perfect gas

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 241.5} = \sqrt{97\,035.4} = 311.5 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{a \approx 311.5 \text{ m/s} \approx 1121.5 \text{ km/h}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V = 920$ km/h, $T = -15^\circ$C $= 258.15$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$.

**Step 1 — Convert velocity (1 mark):**

$$V = 920 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 255.56 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Speed of sound (1 mark):**

$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 258.15} = \sqrt{103\,737.5} = 322.08 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Mach number (1 mark):**

$$M = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{255.56}{322.08} = 0.793$$

$$\boxed{M \approx 0.79}$$

**Step 4 — Stagnation temperature (2 marks):**

$$T_0 = T \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)$$

$$M^2 = 0.793^2 = 0.629$$

$$T_0 = 258.15 \left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.629\right) = 258.15 \times 1.1258 = 290.6 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_0 \approx 290.6 \text{ K} \; (\approx 17.5^\circ\text{C})}$$

**Step 5 — Pressure ratio (2 marks):**

$$\frac{P_0}{P} = \left(1.1258\right)^{3.5}$$

Taking $\ln(1.1258) = 0.11865$, multiply by 3.5: $0.41528$, $e^{0.41528} = 1.5148$.

$$\boxed{\frac{P_0}{P} \approx 1.515}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Main Question Style]**

**(a)** Explain the classification of flow regimes around an aircraft based on Mach number, with appropriate sketches and examples of aircraft in each regime. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wing has a maximum thickness-to-chord ratio of 12%. The minimum pressure coefficient on the wing is $C_{p,min} = -0.6$. Estimate the critical Mach number at standard sea-level conditions using the thin-airfoil approximation. If the same aircraft is flying at an altitude of 6 km ($T = 248.15$ K) at a true airspeed of 280 m/s, find the flight Mach number and the stagnation temperature. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Classification (7 marks)**

| Regime | Mach Range | Characteristics | Aircraft Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Subsonic (Low)** | $M < 0.3$ | Incompressible; Bernoulli valid | Cessna 152, helicopters |
| **Subsonic (High)** | $0.3 \le M < 0.8$ | Compressibility noticeable; Prandtl-Glauert correction used | Airbus A320 cruise |
| **Transonic** | $0.8 \le M < 1.2$ | Mixed subsonic/supersonic flow; local shocks; **wave drag** appears; buffet onset | Most commercial jets at high $M$ |
| **Supersonic** | $1.2 \le M < 5.0$ | Entire flow field supersonic; oblique shocks, expansion fans; **sonic boom** heard on ground | F-16, Concorde (M = 2.04) |
| **Hypersonic** | $M \ge 5.0$ | Real gas effects, dissociation, ionization; **aerodynamic heating** dominant | Space Shuttle re-entry, X-15 |

**Key diagrammatic content expected (3 marks):** Sketches of typical streamline pattern over an airfoil in (i) subsonic — smooth compression ahead of body, (ii) transonic — local supersonic pocket with terminating shock, (iii) supersonic — attached oblique shock at leading edge, expansion fan at trailing edge.

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Critical Mach number (3 marks):**

$$M_{cr} \approx 1 - \frac{\vert C_{p,min} \vert}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}} = 1 - \frac{0.6}{1.2} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

$$\boxed{M_{cr} \approx 0.5}$$

(Thickness ratio is given as design context; for thin airfoils, $M_{cr}$ depends primarily on $C_{p,min}$.)

**Step 2 — Speed of sound at 6 km (1 mark):**

$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 248.15} = \sqrt{99\,714.7} = 315.78 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Flight Mach number (1 mark):**

$$M_\infty = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{280}{315.78} = 0.887$$

$$\boxed{M_\infty \approx 0.887 \text{ (transonic regime)}}$$

**Step 4 — Stagnation temperature (2 marks):**

$$T_0 = 248.15 \left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.887^2\right) = 248.15 \left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.7868\right)$$

$$= 248.15 \times 1.1574 = 287.2 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_0 \approx 287.2 \text{ K} \; (\approx 14.0^\circ\text{C})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Mach Number Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin.** A $-15^\circ$C temperature MUST become 258.15 K before plugging into $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$. Losing 1 full mark if not done.
> 2. **Mixing up $V$ units.** If $V$ is given in km/h, divide by 3.6 first; if in knots, divide by 1.944. Many students lose 1 mark by using km/h directly with $a$ in m/s.
> 3. **Using $P_0/P$ relation incorrectly across a shock.** The isentropic relations $T_0 = T(1 + 0.2M^2)$ and $P_0/P = (1 + 0.2M^2)^{3.5}$ are valid only for **isentropic (shock-free)** processes. Across a normal shock, $T_0$ is unchanged but $P_0$ drops.
> 4. **Confusing $M_{cr}$ with the wave-drag Mach number.** $M_{cr}$ is when local flow first becomes sonic. The **drag-divergence Mach** $M_{DD}$ is slightly higher (typically $M_{DD} \approx M_{cr} + 0.05$ to $0.1$). Examiners want $M_{cr}$ when asked.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption statement.** Always state "isentropic, perfect gas, inviscid" when using these formulae. Carries 1 mark in derivation questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mach Number (PCAOT205 Module 1)**

- **Definition:** $M = V / a$ — dimensionless ratio of TAS to local speed of sound. *(Always state this first in any answer.)*
- **Speed of sound:** $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ — depends on **absolute temperature only** for a perfect gas.
- **Standard air values:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $a_0 \approx 340.3$ m/s at sea level.
- **Five flight regimes:** Subsonic ($M < 0.8$), Transonic ($0.8 - 1.2$), Supersonic ($1.2 - 5.0$), Hypersonic ($> 5.0$). *(Sonic at $M = 1$ is a single point, not a regime.)*
- **Compressibility threshold:** $M < 0.3$ — incompressible assumption safe. Above this, use compressible relations.
- **Critical Mach number:** Free-stream $M$ at which **local** $M$ first reaches 1 (usually on upper wing surface). Approx. $M_{cr} \approx 1 - \frac{\vert C_{p,min} \vert}{1 + (\gamma - 1)/2}$.
- **Isentropic stagnation relations:** $T_0 = T(1 + 0.2 M^2)$, $P_0/P = (1 + 0.2 M^2)^{3.5}$, $\rho_0/\rho = (1 + 0.2 M^2)^{2.5}$ (for $\gamma = 1.4$).
- **Common KTU trap:** Use $T$ in **Kelvin** (not Celsius), $V$ in **m/s** (not km/h).
- **Practical engineering use:** Determines wing sweep, engine type, instrumentation (Mach meter), and structural heating limits.
- **Key unit conversions:** $1 \text{ km/h} = 0.2778 \text{ m/s}$, $1 \text{ knot} = 0.5144 \text{ m/s}$, $T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$.
- **Memorize the value $a_0 \approx 340$ m/s** — useful for quick sanity checks on numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aspect Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aspect Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In aeronautical engineering, the **Aspect Ratio (AR)** of a wing is formally defined as the **ratio of the wing span (b) to the mean aerodynamic chord (c̄)** of the wing. It is a dimensionless geometric parameter that fundamentally characterizes the planform shape of a lifting surface.

$$AR = \frac{b}{\bar{c}}$$

An equivalent and frequently more useful expression relates the aspect ratio directly to the wing planform area (S) and the wingspan:

$$AR = \frac{b^2}{S}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, students **must remember both definitions** of aspect ratio. The geometric definition ($b/\bar{c}$) is preferred for design discussions, while the area-based definition ($b^2/S$) is preferred for performance and aerodynamic analysis, as it does not require explicit knowledge of the chord distribution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine holding two rectangular sheets of paper of **identical area**:

- **Sheet A:** Long and narrow (like a ruler) → **High Aspect Ratio**
- **Sheet B:** Almost square → **Low Aspect Ratio**

If you push both through the air (modeling airflow over a wing), **Sheet A** will glide farther and feel "slippery" in the spanwise direction. **Sheet B** will feel "stubby" and produce more turbulence at the wingtips.

**Why does this happen?** Long, narrow wings reduce the strength of the **wingtip vortices**. These vortices are the source of **induced drag** ($C_{D_i}$), which is the unwanted drag created as a byproduct of generating lift. Therefore, **a high aspect ratio = efficient long-range flight**, and a **low aspect ratio = high maneuverability and structural compactness**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples:**
> - **Gliders / Sailplanes:** AR ≈ 25 to 35 (extremely high)
> - **Commercial Transport Aircraft (e.g., Airbus A320, Boeing 737):** AR ≈ 7 to 10
> - **Military Fighters (e.g., F-16):** AR ≈ 2.5 to 3.5
> - **Supersonic Fighter (e.g., Concorde):** AR ≈ 1.5 to 2.5

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Wingspan (b):** Measured in **meters (m)** or **feet (ft)**
- **Mean Aerodynamic Chord (c̄):** Measured in **meters (m)** or **feet (ft)**
- **Wing Planform Area (S):** Measured in **square meters (m²)** or **square feet (ft²)**
- **Aspect Ratio (AR):** **Dimensionless** (no units)
- **Oswald Efficiency Factor (e):** Dimensionless, typically 0.7 – 0.95 for clean wings
- **Induced Drag Coefficient ($C_{D_i}$):** Dimensionless
- **Lift Coefficient ($C_L$):** Dimensionless
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown of Aspect Ratio

The aspect ratio is not just a geometric number — it dictates the **three-dimensional flow field** around the wing. Here is the structured logic:

**Step 1 — Lift Generation Creates Tip Vortices**
A finite wing produces lift by creating a pressure difference: high pressure below, low pressure above. Air from the high-pressure region tends to "leak" around the wingtips toward the low-pressure region, creating spiraling **trailing vortices**.

**Step 2 — Tip Vortices Induce Downwash**
These vortices generate a downward velocity component at the wing called **downwash (w)**. The local flow over any section of the wing is tilted downward by an angle $\varepsilon$ (downwash angle).

**Step 3 — Downwash Reduces Effective Angle of Attack**
The geometric angle of attack ($\alpha$) is reduced by the downwash angle to give the **effective angle of attack** ($\alpha_{eff} = \alpha - \varepsilon$). This means the wing section "sees" a smaller angle of attack than the pilot sets.

**Step 4 — Downwash Tilts the Lift Vector**
Because the lift vector is perpendicular to the local relative airflow (not to the fuselage reference line), the downwash tilts the lift vector **backward by the angle $\varepsilon$**.

**Step 5 — Tilted Lift Vector Produces Induced Drag**
The backward component of the tilted lift vector is the **induced drag ($D_i$)**. This drag is **inversely proportional to aspect ratio**.

**Step 6 — Why High AR Reduces Induced Drag**
A long, slender wing has wingtips that are far apart. The pressure leakage is "stretched" over a long distance, weakening the tip vortices. This reduces downwash, reduces induced drag, and improves aerodynamic efficiency.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for Aspect Ratio (Module 1 — KTU 2024 Scheme):**

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables / Units** |
|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| $AR = b / \bar{c}$ | Geometric aspect ratio | $b$ = span (m), $\bar{c}$ = MAC (m) |
| $AR = b^2 / S$ | Area-based aspect ratio | $b$ = span (m), $S$ = planform area (m²) |
| $C_{D_i} = C_L^2 / (\pi \cdot e \cdot AR)$ | Induced drag coefficient | $C_L$ = lift coeff., $e$ = Oswald factor, $AR$ = aspect ratio |
| $D_i = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \cdot C_{D_i}$ | Induced drag force (N) | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $V$ = velocity (m/s) |
| $e = C_{L_\alpha}^2 / (\pi \cdot AR \cdot C_{D_0}) \cdot \text{some correction}$ | Oswald efficiency factor | function of planform |
| $\varepsilon = C_L / (\pi \cdot e \cdot AR)$ | Downwash angle (radians, small angle) | — |
| $a = a_0 / (1 + a_0/(\pi \cdot e \cdot AR))$ | Finite wing lift curve slope (1/rad) | $a_0$ = 2D section lift slope |
| $\bar{c} = S / b$ | Mean aerodynamic chord for straight tapered wings | $S$ = area, $b$ = span |
| $b = \sqrt{AR \cdot S}$ | Span expressed via AR and area | — |
| $\text{Range factor} \propto \sqrt{AR}$ | Breguet range proportional to $\sqrt{AR}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Note:** The pipe symbol `\|` is **forbidden** inside KTU markdown tables (it breaks the table syntax). All absolute values or conditions in formulas must be written using `\vert` (e.g., $C_L \vert_{max}$).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Aspect Ratio

- **Commercial Aviation:** Designers select AR ≈ 8–10 to balance fuel efficiency (induced drag) with airport gate compatibility (wingspan limits).
- **Sailplane Design:** Sailplanes use AR > 30 to extract maximum range from weak thermal currents.
- **Combat Aircraft:** Fighters use low AR (≈ 3) for high roll rate and structural compactness, accepting higher induced drag.
- **Structural Design:** Higher AR means longer, slender wings, which experience greater **bending moments** at the root under lift. This drives the need for stronger wing spars.
- **Fuel Burn Estimation:** $C_{D_i} \propto 1/AR$ makes AR a direct lever in the Breguet range equation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Geometric to Area-Based Aspect Ratio

We begin with the standard definition of aspect ratio:

$$AR = \frac{b}{\bar{c}}$$

By definition, the **mean aerodynamic chord (MAC)** for a finite wing is the chord of an equivalent rectangular wing that has the same area and the same aerodynamic moments. Mathematically:

$$\bar{c} = \frac{S}{b}$$

This comes from the requirement that an equivalent rectangle of span $b$ and chord $\bar{c}$ must equal the actual wing area $S$, so:

$$S = b \cdot \bar{c} \implies \bar{c} = \frac{S}{b}$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

Step 1 — Substitute $\bar{c} = S/b$ into the definition of AR:

$$
AR = \frac{b}{\bar{c}} = \frac{b}{(S/b)}
$$

Step 2 — Simplify the denominator:

$$
AR = \frac{b}{S / b} = \frac{b \cdot b}{S}
$$

Step 3 — Final form:

$$
AR = \frac{b^2}{S}
$$

This is the **area-based form** of aspect ratio widely used in aerodynamic performance analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Induced Drag Coefficient from Downwash

Consider an infinite wing that produces lift $L'$ per unit span. For a finite wing, the downwash $w$ induced by tip vortices acts on each strip of the wing. The local relative wind is tilted by an angle:

$$\varepsilon = \arctan\left(\frac{w}{V_\infty}\right) \approx \frac{w}{V_\infty} \quad \text{(small angle)}$$

The lift vector $L$ is perpendicular to the local relative wind, so it is tilted backward by $\varepsilon$. Decomposing the tilted lift:

$$
L' = L'_{2D} \cdot \cos\varepsilon \approx L'_{2D}
$$

$$
D_i' = L' \cdot \sin\varepsilon \approx L' \cdot \varepsilon
$$

The lift per unit span relates to the lift coefficient as:

$$
L' = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 \cdot c \cdot C_l
$$

The downwash from lifting-line theory (Prandtl) is proportional to the lift distribution. For an elliptic planform, integrating along the span gives:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{C_L}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}
$$

**Substituting the downwash expression into the induced drag equation:**

Step 1 — Induced drag coefficient definition:

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{D_i}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 S}
$$

Step 2 — Use the relation $D_i = L \cdot \varepsilon$ (from above):

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{L \cdot \varepsilon}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 S}
$$

Step 3 — Note that $L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 S \cdot C_L$, so:

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 S \cdot C_L \cdot \varepsilon}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 S} = C_L \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Step 4 — Substitute $\varepsilon = C_L / (\pi \cdot e \cdot AR)$:

$$
C_{D_i} = C_L \cdot \frac{C_L}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}
$$

Step 5 — Final result:

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}
$$

This is the **Trefftz-plane induced drag equation**, fundamental for KTU numerical problems.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Span of an Aircraft from AR and Wing Area

For a typical transport aircraft preliminary design problem, the span must be determined from AR and area.

**Given:** $AR = 8$, $S = 120 \text{ m}^2$.

Step 1 — Start from the area-based definition:

$$
AR = \frac{b^2}{S}
$$

Step 2 — Rearrange for $b^2$:

$$
b^2 = AR \cdot S
$$

Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:

$$
b^2 = 8 \times 120 = 960 \text{ m}^2
$$

Step 4 — Take the square root:

$$
b = \sqrt{960} \approx 30.98 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:** The wingspan is approximately **31 meters**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Induced Drag at Cruise

**Given:** An aircraft has wing area $S = 30 \text{ m}^2$, $AR = 7$, $C_L = 0.5$, $e = 0.85$, cruises at altitude where $\rho = 0.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at $V = 80 \text{ m/s}$.

Step 1 — Compute the induced drag coefficient:

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR} = \frac{(0.5)^2}{\pi \times 0.85 \times 7}
$$

Step 2 — Evaluate the numerator:

$$
C_L^2 = 0.25
$$

Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator:

$$
\pi \cdot e \cdot AR = 3.1416 \times 0.85 \times 7 = 18.69
$$

Step 4 — Divide:

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{0.25}{18.69} \approx 0.01338
$$

Step 5 — Compute the dynamic pressure:

$$
q = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times (80)^2 = 0.25 \times 6400 = 1600 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

Step 6 — Compute the induced drag force:

$$
D_i = q \cdot S \cdot C_{D_i} = 1600 \times 30 \times 0.01338
$$

Step 7 — Final answer:

$$
D_i = 1600 \times 30 \times 0.01338 \approx 642.2 \text{ N}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Behavior as AR Increases** | **Behavior as AR Decreases** |
|--------------|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Induced drag coefficient $C_{D_i}$ | $C_{D_i} = C_L^2/(\pi e AR)$ | Decreases $\propto 1/AR$ | Increases |
| Lift curve slope $a$ | $a = a_0/(1 + a_0/(\pi e AR))$ | Approaches $a_0$ (infinite wing) | Decreases sharply |
| Wingtip vortex strength | — | Weakens, more spread out | Strengthens, more concentrated |
| Wing root bending moment | — | Increases (longer moment arm) | Decreases |
| Roll rate | — | Decreases (more wing inertia) | Increases (compact wing) |
| Fuel efficiency (range) | — | Improves | Worsens |
| Oswald efficiency $e$ | $e$ | Usually improves for elliptic | Worsens for rectangular |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Aspect Ratio Functional Flow

The following block diagram maps how the geometric parameter **Aspect Ratio (AR)** propagates through aerodynamic analysis into aircraft-level performance outcomes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Geometric Inputs: b, S, MAC] --> A1[Compute AR: b squared divided by S]
    A1 --> A2[Select Oswald factor e from planform]
    A2 --> A3[Compute downwash epsilon: CL over pi e AR]
    A3 --> A4[Compute induced drag coefficient CDi: CL squared over pi e AR]
    A4 --> A5[Compute induced drag force Di: half rho V squared S CDi]
    A5 --> A6[Output: total drag D equals D0 plus Di]
    A1 --> A7[Compute lift curve slope a: a0 over 1 plus a0 over pi e AR]
    A7 --> A8[Output: finite wing CL versus alpha curve]
    A6 --> A9[Performance block: Breguet range]
    A6 --> A10[Performance block: Endurance]
    A6 --> A11[Performance block: Required thrust]
    A8 --> A12[Stability block: Trim and control]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Comparison of High vs Low Aspect Ratio

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HighAR[High Aspect Ratio Aircraft]
        H1[Long slender wings, AR greater than 20] --> H2[Weak tip vortices]
        H2 --> H3[Low induced drag]
        H3 --> H4[High aerodynamic efficiency]
        H4 --> H5[Long range, gliders, transports]
    end
    subgraph LowAR[Low Aspect Ratio Aircraft]
        L1[Short stubby wings, AR less than 5] --> L2[Strong concentrated tip vortices]
        L2 --> L3[High induced drag]
        L3 --> L4[Lower efficiency but better maneuverability]
        L4 --> L5[Fighters, delta wings, supersonic]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Planform Geometry Mapping to AR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Wing Planform Type] --> P1{Choose Planform}
    P1 -->|Rectangular| P2[Chord constant, AR equals b over c]
    P1 -->|Elliptic| P3[Chord varies elliptically, highest e]
    P1 -->|Tapered| P4[Chord reduces toward tip, e near 0.9]
    P1 -->|Delta| P5[Very low AR, high sweep, supersonic use]
    P2 --> Q1[Compute AR from b and S]
    P3 --> Q1
    P4 --> Q1
    P5 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2[Use AR in induced drag formula]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A0`, `H1`, `P2`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names. All labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text without bold, italics, or HTML tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1.a]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Define the term **Aspect Ratio** of an aircraft wing. List any two factors that affect the choice of aspect ratio during aircraft design.

**Model Answer:**

The **Aspect Ratio (AR)** of a wing is defined as the ratio of the wing span ($b$) to the mean aerodynamic chord ($\bar{c}$), or equivalently as the ratio of the square of the wingspan to the wing planform area ($S$).

$$
AR = \frac{b}{\bar{c}} = \frac{b^2}{S}
$$

**Two factors affecting the choice of AR:**

1. **Mission Profile:** Long-range transport aircraft and gliders require high AR (8–35) to minimize induced drag and improve fuel efficiency. Combat aircraft require low AR (2–4) for maneuverability and compactness.

2. **Structural and Aerodynamic Considerations:** High AR wings experience larger root bending moments and require stronger spars. They also occupy more airport gate space, so airport compatibility is a constraint.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q1.b]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** State the formula for **induced drag coefficient** in terms of lift coefficient and aspect ratio. Explain briefly why a glider has a much higher aspect ratio than a fighter aircraft.

**Model Answer:**

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}
$$

**Explanation:**

A glider is designed for **maximum range and endurance** on weak thermal currents. Induced drag is **inversely proportional to AR**, so a high AR (typically 25–35) drastically reduces $C_{D_i}$, giving the glider a very high lift-to-drag ratio ($L/D$). A fighter aircraft, on the other hand, requires **high maneuverability, rapid roll rates, and compactness** for combat. A low AR (2–4) provides structural stiffness, a smaller wingspan, and acceptable roll performance, even at the cost of higher induced drag.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **induced drag coefficient** of a finite wing in terms of lift coefficient, Oswald efficiency factor, and aspect ratio. Clearly state the assumptions used in the derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An aircraft with wing area $S = 50 \text{ m}^2$ has an aspect ratio of 8.5 and an Oswald efficiency factor of 0.82. At a particular flight condition, the lift coefficient is 0.45 and the aircraft is flying at $V = 90 \text{ m/s}$ at an altitude where air density is $\rho = 0.6 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate:
(i) The induced drag coefficient
(ii) The induced drag force

**Model Answer (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The wing operates at a small angle of attack (small perturbation theory).
2. The flow is incompressible and steady.
3. The wing has a high aspect ratio (so lifting-line theory applies).
4. The downwash is uniform across the span (Prandtl's lifting-line approximation).
5. The induced drag is computed at the Trefftz plane (far downstream).

**Derivation:**

The downwash $\varepsilon$ from Prandtl's lifting-line theory is:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{C_L}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}
$$

The induced drag is the component of lift along the flight direction. For small angles, the tilted lift vector has a backward component:

$$
D_i = L \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Dividing by the dynamic pressure and wing area:

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{D_i}{q S} = \frac{L \cdot \varepsilon}{q S} = \frac{(q S C_L) \cdot \varepsilon}{q S} = C_L \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Substituting the downwash:

$$
C_{D_i} = C_L \cdot \frac{C_L}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}
$$

**Model Answer (b):**

**(i) Induced drag coefficient:**

Step 1 — Apply the formula:

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute values: $C_L = 0.45$, $e = 0.82$, $AR = 8.5$.

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{(0.45)^2}{\pi \times 0.82 \times 8.5}
$$

Step 3 — Numerator:

$$
(0.45)^2 = 0.2025
$$

Step 4 — Denominator:

$$
\pi \times 0.82 \times 8.5 = 3.1416 \times 0.82 \times 8.5 = 21.90
$$

Step 5 — Result:

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{0.2025}{21.90} \approx 0.00925
$$

**(ii) Induced drag force:**

Step 1 — Compute dynamic pressure:

$$
q = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.6 \times (90)^2 = 0.3 \times 8100 = 2430 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

Step 2 — Compute drag:

$$
D_i = q \cdot S \cdot C_{D_i} = 2430 \times 50 \times 0.00925
$$

Step 3 — Result:

$$
D_i = 1124 \text{ N (approx)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the concept of **wingtip vortices** and **downwash**. Discuss how these phenomena lead to induced drag, and establish the relationship between induced drag and aspect ratio.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the design considerations of a **high aspect ratio wing** (glider) versus a **low aspect ratio wing** (fighter). Include numerical estimates of AR and a discussion of aerodynamic efficiency.

**Model Answer (a):**

When a finite wing produces lift, a higher pressure is created beneath the wing and a lower pressure above it. Near the wingtips, the higher-pressure air from below tends to spill over and around the tip toward the low-pressure region above. This spillover creates spiraling **trailing vortices** (wingtip vortices) that extend far behind the aircraft. These vortices induce a downward velocity component in the flow over the wing known as **downwash** ($w$). The downwash tilts the local relative wind downward by the small angle $\varepsilon \approx w/V_\infty$.

Because the lift vector is always perpendicular to the local relative wind (not to the aircraft's longitudinal axis), the downwash tilts the lift vector backward by the angle $\varepsilon$. The component of lift in the flight direction is the **induced drag** $D_i$. For a small angle:

$$
D_i \approx L \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Using Prandtl's lifting-line result for elliptic planforms:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{C_L}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}
$$

Substituting, we obtain:

$$
C_{D_i} = \frac{C_L^2}{\pi \cdot e \cdot AR}
$$

Thus, induced drag is **inversely proportional to aspect ratio**. A long, slender wing (high AR) has widely separated tip vortices of weak strength, giving little downwash, less backward tilt of lift, and therefore low induced drag.

**Model Answer (b):**

| **Parameter** | **High AR Wing (Glider)** | **Low AR Wing (Fighter)** |
|---------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Typical AR | 25 – 35 | 2.5 – 3.5 |
| Wing Geometry | Very long, slender, high aspect ratio | Short, stubby, sometimes delta |
| Tip Vortices | Weak and well-separated | Strong and concentrated |
| Induced Drag | Very low ($\propto 1/AR$) | High |
| Aerodynamic Efficiency ($L/D$) | 40 – 60 (excellent) | 8 – 12 (modest) |
| Roll Rate | Low (large wing inertia) | Very high (compact wing) |
| Mission | Soaring, long-range, weak thermals | Dogfighting, maneuverability |
| Structural Penalty | High root bending, strong spar | Lower root bending, stiffer |

**Aerodynamic Discussion:** A glider sacrifices structural complexity and roll agility to maximize $L/D$. A fighter sacrifices aerodynamic efficiency to gain combat agility.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Aspect Ratio Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the two definitions:** Writing only $AR = b/\bar{c}$ without the area form $AR = b^2/S$ costs full credit on KTU 2024 valuations. Always state both.
> 2. **Forgetting the Oswald factor ($e$):** Omitting $e$ in the induced drag formula is the most common mistake. The $e$ factor is mandatory.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Aspect ratio is **dimensionless** — never attach a unit like "m" or "m²". Examiners deduct marks for "AR = 8 m".
> 4. **Induced vs Parasite drag:** Do not confuse $D_i$ (induced) with $D_0$ (parasite/zero-lift). Induced drag is a **function of $C_L$**, parasite drag is not.
> 5. **Numerical substitution:** Show all substitution steps. Examiners reward the work, not just the final number.
> 6. **Sketch Quality in Part B (a):** A 14-mark question asking for explanation "with neat sketches" requires a labeled diagram of the tip vortex. Missing the sketch costs up to 3 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Aspect Ratio (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** $AR = b/\bar{c} = b^2/S$ — **dimensionless**.
- **High AR:** Long, slender wings — gliders, transports — low induced drag.
- **Low AR:** Stubby wings — fighters, supersonic aircraft — high maneuverability.
- **Induced Drag Formula:** $C_{D_i} = C_L^2/(\pi e AR)$ — memorize exactly.
- **Downwash:** $\varepsilon = C_L/(\pi e AR)$ — small angle approximation.
- **Lift Curve Slope (finite wing):** $a = a_0/[1 + a_0/(\pi e AR)]$.
- **Elliptic planform** gives the highest $e$ (theoretically $e = 1$).
- **Rectangular planform** gives the lowest $e$ (around 0.7–0.8).
- **Span Calculation:** $b = \sqrt{AR \cdot S}$ — very common numerical problem.
- **Oswald factor $e$** is typically between 0.7 and 0.95 for clean wings.
- **AR is INVERSELY related to $C_{D_i}$**: doubling AR halves induced drag.
- **Structural penalty:** Higher AR increases root bending moment — drives spar design.
- **Real-world AR values:** Glider 25–35, A320 ≈ 8.5, F-16 ≈ 3.0, Concorde ≈ 1.7.
- **L/D maximum** (aerodynamic efficiency) is achieved when $C_{D_i} = C_{D_0}$ — aspect ratio influences this directly.
- **Always show all substitution steps** in KTU numerical answers — partial credit is given for methodology.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wing Loading (Numericals)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wing Loading (Numericals)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wing Loading** is one of the most fundamental non-dimensional design parameters in aeronautical engineering, defined as the **weight of the aircraft supported per unit area of the wing planform**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$W/S = \frac{W}{S}$$

Where:
- **W** = Maximum gross take-off weight of the aircraft (in **N** or **kgf**)
- **S** = Reference wing planform area (in **m²**)
- **W/S** = Wing loading (in **N/m²** or **kg/m²**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This parameter falls under the broader topic of *Aircraft Performance Parameters* in Module 1 and is considered a **first-order design driver** that influences every phase of flight from take-off to landing, including manoeuvring and gust response.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Snowshoe Principle

Imagine trying to walk on fresh, deep snow. If you wear regular shoes, your entire body weight is concentrated on a tiny area (high "foot loading") — and you sink. The moment you strap on **snowshoes**, the same body weight is distributed over a much larger area (low "foot loading") — and you walk comfortably on top of the snow.

The **wing works exactly the same way**:

- A **low wing loading** (large wings relative to weight) → the aircraft "floats" in the air with low speeds, climbs steeply, and lands slowly. Gliders and STOL aircraft behave this way.
- A **high wing loading** (small wings relative to weight) → the aircraft must fly fast to generate lift, is structurally efficient, has a small radar cross-section, and is less affected by turbulence. Fighters and transport aircraft have high wing loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics to Remember:**
> - Sailplane: **30 to 60 N/m²** (3 to 6 kg/m²)
> - Light general aviation: **600 to 1200 N/m²** (60 to 120 kg/m²)
> - Commercial transport: **2400 to 6000 N/m²** (250 to 600 kg/m²)
> - Fighter aircraft: **3000 to 8000 N/m²** (300 to 800 kg/m²)
> - Hypersonic re-entry: **> 15000 N/m²** (> 1500 kg/m²)

### 1.3 Why Wing Loading is a Master Design Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Wing loading is **not a single value** — it is a *family of design choices* because both W and S are independently variable. For the **same wing loading**, two aircraft can have very different characteristics depending on **wing aspect ratio, planform shape, thickness ratio, sweep, and airfoil section**. This is why KTU examiners expect students to discuss W/S in conjunction with these secondary parameters.

The classical consequences of varying wing loading are summarized in the table below for quick conceptual understanding:

| Parameter Affected | Low Wing Loading (Low W/S) | High Wing Loading (High W/S) |
|---|---|---|
| Stalling Speed | **Low** (safer) | **High** (dangerous) |
| Take-off Run | **Short** | **Long** |
| Landing Run | **Short** | **Long** |
| Rate of Climb | **Better at low speed** | Better at high speed |
| Turn Rate | **Higher** (tight turns) | **Lower** (wide turns) |
| Gust Sensitivity | **High** (bumpy ride) | **Low** (smooth ride) |
| Structural Weight | Higher (large wings) | Lower (compact wings) |
| Useful Load | Lower (wing dominates) | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Behind Wing Loading

To understand *why* W/S governs performance, we start with the fundamental lift equation:

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$$

In **steady, level flight**, the lift must equal the weight:

$$L = W$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L = W$$

$$V^2 = \frac{2 W}{\rho S C_L} = \frac{2 (W/S)}{\rho C_L}$$

This single equation reveals a profound truth — **the velocity required to sustain level flight depends only on the wing loading, the density altitude, and the lift coefficient**. It does *not* depend on the absolute values of W or S individually.

### 2.2 The Master Equation: Wing Loading and Stalling Speed

At the **stall**, the lift coefficient reaches its maximum value $C_{Lmax}$, and the corresponding minimum flight speed is the **stalling speed** $V_s$:

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2 (W/S)}{\rho \, C_{Lmax}}}$$

Rearranging gives the design formula for required wing area:

$$S = \frac{2 W}{\rho V_s^2 C_{Lmax}}$$

### 2.3 Wing Loading and Take-off Performance

The take-off ground run, using the **energy method** (work-energy theorem), gives:

$$s_{LO} = \frac{V_{LOF}^2}{2 \, a_{avg}}$$

Where the average acceleration is:

$$a_{avg} = \frac{T - D_{avg} - \mu (W - L_{avg})}{m}$$

Because $V_{LOF} = 1.2 \, V_{stall}$ and $V_{stall}$ depends on W/S, the **take-off distance increases monotonically with wing loading**. The empirical correlation is:

$$s_{TO} \approx k_1 \left(\frac{W}{S}\right) + k_2 \left(\frac{W}{\sigma T}\right)$$

Where the second term represents the **thrust-to-weight influence** and $\sigma$ is the relative density.

### 2.4 Wing Loading and Landing Performance

The landing distance follows a similar pattern, with the **touchdown speed** $V_T = 1.3 \, V_{stall,L}$ (landing configuration) being the primary driver. An empirical formula widely used in KTU textbooks is:

$$s_L = 0.51 \, V_T^2$$

(With $V_T$ in m/s and $s_L$ in metres, this gives a hard-surface runway, sea-level, no wind condition.)

### 2.5 Wing Loading and Manoeuvring Flight

In a **steady, coordinated turn** with load factor $n = L/W$:

- **Turn rate:** $\omega = \dfrac{g \sqrt{n^2 - 1}}{V}$
- **Turn radius:** $R = \dfrac{V^2}{g \sqrt{n^2 - 1}}$

The **specific excess power** $P_s = (T - D) V / W$ governs the ability to change energy state. For a given engine, a **lower W/S** generally improves instantaneous turn rate at low altitude, which is why dog-fighters are designed with moderate-to-low wing loading.

### 2.6 Wing Loading and Gust Loads

The **gust load factor** due to a vertical gust $u$ is:

$$\Delta n = \frac{K_g \, u \, V_e}{(W/S)}$$

Where $K_g$ is the **gust alleviation factor** (function of air density, speed of sound, and lift-curve slope) and $V_e$ is the equivalent airspeed. **Higher W/S** reduces gust sensitivity — a key reason for the higher wing loading of transport aircraft flying through turbulent air.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** required for KTU Module 1 wing-loading numerical problems. Memorize the units and the exact form of each expression.

| # | Performance Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wing Loading | $W/S$ | N/m² or kg/m² | W in N or kgf, S in m² |
| 2 | Stalling Speed | $V_s = \sqrt{2(W/S) \big/ (\rho C_{Lmax})}$ | m/s | $C_{Lmax}$ at given config |
| 3 | W/S from $V_s$ | $W/S = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_s^2 C_{Lmax}$ | N/m² | Inversion of (2) |
| 4 | Lift-Off Speed | $V_{LOF} = 1.2 \, V_{s,TO}$ | m/s | Empirical factor |
| 5 | Touchdown Speed | $V_T = 1.3 \, V_{s,L}$ | m/s | Empirical factor |
| 6 | Take-off Run (Energy) | $s_{LO} = V_{LOF}^2 \big/ (2 a_{avg})$ | m | $a_{avg}$ in m/s² |
| 7 | Landing Run (Empirical) | $s_L = 0.51 \, V_T^2$ | m | $V_T$ in m/s |
| 8 | Turn Rate | $\omega = g \sqrt{n^2 - 1} \big/ V$ | rad/s | $n \geq 1$ |
| 9 | Turn Radius | $R = V^2 \big/ (g \sqrt{n^2 - 1})$ | m | Steady turn |
| 10 | Specific Excess Power | $P_s = (T - D) V \big/ W$ | m/s | Energy height rate |
| 11 | Gust Load Factor | $\Delta n = K_g u V_e \big/ (W/S)$ | — | $K_g$ has units 1/m |
| 12 | Load Factor (Steady Turn) | $n = L \big/ W = 1 \big/ \cos\phi$ | — | $\phi$ = bank angle |
| 13 | Density at Altitude | $\rho = \rho_0 (1 - L h / T_0)^{(g/RL - 1)}$ | kg/m³ | ISA model |
| 14 | Wing Area from W/S | $S = W \big/ (W/S)$ | m² | Trivial, but tested |

### 2.8 Engineering Utility of Wing Loading

In real aircraft design, wing loading is the **first number a design team fixes** after deciding the mission. For example:

- **Agricultural aircraft** (crop dusters) use **W/S ≈ 600–1000 N/m²** to land in short, rough strips and execute tight turns over fields.
- **Carrier-based fighters** (F/A-18, MiG-29K) use **W/S ≈ 3500–4500 N/m²** to survive catapult launches and arrested landings — too low W/S would cause excessive sink rates.
- **Solar-powered UAVs** (like Solar Impulse) have **W/S < 50 N/m²** to enable ultra-low-speed cruise on minimal power.

This single parameter consequently drives the entire **configuration trade study** of a new aircraft programme.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Derivation: From Lift Equation to Stalling Speed in Terms of W/S

**Starting point:** The fundamental lift equation.

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 1: Apply the steady, level flight condition** where lift must equal the total weight of the aircraft.

$$L = W$$

**Step 2: Substitute $L = W$ into equation (i).**

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L = W$$

**Step 3: Isolate the wing loading term $W/S$.**

Divide both sides by S:

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L = \frac{W}{S}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $V$ in level flight.**

$$V^2 = \frac{2(W/S)}{\rho C_L}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2(W/S)}{\rho C_L}} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5: Apply the stall condition** $C_L = C_{Lmax}$, giving the stalling speed.

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2(W/S)}{\rho C_{Lmax}}}$$

This is the **most tested equation** in the KTU Module 1 examination for any wing-loading numerical. Every other formula in the cheat sheet is derived from this single starting point with appropriate modifications.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Three Solved Numericals (Full KTU Style)

#### NUMERICAL 1 — Basic Wing Loading and Stalling Speed (7-Mark Standard)

**Given Data:**
- Aircraft gross weight $W = 5000$ kg
- Wing reference area $S = 20$ m²
- Maximum lift coefficient $C_{Lmax} = 1.6$
- Sea-level density $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Find:**
- (a) Wing loading in N/m² and in kg/m²
- (b) Stalling speed at sea level
- (c) Stalling speed at 4000 m altitude, where $\rho = 0.8194$ kg/m³

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Wing Loading**

First convert weight to newtons:

$$W = 5000 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 49050 \text{ N}$$

Now compute the wing loading in N/m²:

$$\left(\frac{W}{S}\right) = \frac{49050 \text{ N}}{20 \text{ m}^2} = 2452.5 \text{ N/m}^2$$

In kg/m² (dividing by $g$):

$$\left(\frac{W}{S}\right)_{kg} = \frac{5000 \text{ kg}}{20 \text{ m}^2} = 250 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Part (b): Stalling Speed at Sea Level**

Using the master equation:

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2(W/S)}{\rho_0 C_{Lmax}}}$$

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2452.5}{1.225 \times 1.6}}$$

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{4905}{1.9600}}$$

$$V_s = \sqrt{2502.55}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 50.03 \text{ m/s} \approx 180.1 \text{ km/h}}$$

**Part (c): Stalling Speed at 4000 m Altitude**

Substitute $\rho_{4000} = 0.8194$ kg/m³:

$$V_{s,4000} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2452.5}{0.8194 \times 1.6}}$$

$$V_{s,4000} = \sqrt{\frac{4905}{1.3110}}$$

$$V_{s,4000} = \sqrt{3741.42}$$

$$\boxed{V_{s,4000} = 61.17 \text{ m/s} \approx 220.2 \text{ km/h}}$$

**Percentage Increase in Stalling Speed:**

$$\%\text{ increase} = \frac{61.17 - 50.03}{50.03} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{11.14}{50.03} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{= 22.27\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This problem tests whether the student remembers to convert kg → N before plugging into the SI version of the lift equation. A common error is using $W = 5000$ directly in newtons, giving a stall speed of $\sqrt{2 \times 250 / (1.225 \times 1.6)} = 15.98$ m/s, which is off by a factor of $\sqrt{g}$.

---

#### NUMERICAL 2 — Wing Loading and Take-off Ground Run (7-Mark Standard)

**Given Data:**
- Aircraft weight $W = 44145$ N (4500 kg)
- Wing area $S = 20$ m²
- $C_{Lmax,TO} = 1.8$ (take-off configuration, flaps down)
- Maximum thrust $T = 9000$ N (sea-level, static)
- Rolling friction coefficient $\mu = 0.04$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient at take-off $C_D \approx 0.03$
- Sea-level density $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Empirical factor for safety: $V_{LOF} = 1.2 \, V_{stall}$

**Find:**
- (a) Wing loading in N/m² and stalling speed
- (b) Lift-off speed and average friction force
- (c) Take-off ground run

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Wing Loading and Stalling Speed**

$$\frac{W}{S} = \frac{44145}{20} = 2207.25 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$V_{stall} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2207.25}{1.225 \times 1.8}} = \sqrt{\frac{4414.5}{2.205}}$$

$$V_{stall} = \sqrt{2002.04}$$

$$\boxed{V_{stall} = 44.74 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Part (b): Lift-off Speed and Friction**

$$V_{LOF} = 1.2 \times 44.74 = 53.69 \text{ m/s}$$

The average lift during the ground roll occurs at approximately half the lift-off speed:

$$V_{avg} \approx \frac{V_{LOF}}{2} = 26.85 \text{ m/s}$$

Average lift coefficient during run is $C_{L,avg} \approx C_{Lmax,TO}/2 = 0.9$ (linear assumption).

Average lift:

$$L_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{avg}^2 S C_{L,avg}$$

$$L_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (26.85)^2 \times 20 \times 0.9$$

$$L_{avg} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 720.92 \times 18$$

$$L_{avg} = 7943.4 \text{ N}$$

Average friction force:

$$F_{f,avg} = \mu \, (W - L_{avg}) = 0.04 \times (44145 - 7943.4)$$

$$F_{f,avg} = 0.04 \times 36201.6$$

$$\boxed{F_{f,avg} = 1448.1 \text{ N}}$$

**Part (c): Take-off Ground Run**

Average drag during the run:

$$D_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{avg}^2 S C_D$$

$$D_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (26.85)^2 \times 20 \times 0.03$$

$$D_{avg} = 264.8 \text{ N}$$

Net accelerating force:

$$F_{net} = T - D_{avg} - F_{f,avg}$$

$$F_{net} = 9000 - 264.8 - 1448.1 = 7287.1 \text{ N}$$

Average acceleration:

$$a_{avg} = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{7287.1}{4500} = 1.619 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Take-off ground run (using the work-energy theorem, $V_{LOF}^2 = 2 a_{avg} s_{LO}$):

$$s_{LO} = \frac{V_{LOF}^2}{2 a_{avg}} = \frac{(53.69)^2}{2 \times 1.619}$$

$$s_{LO} = \frac{2882.6}{3.238}$$

$$\boxed{s_{LO} = 890.2 \text{ m}}$$

Total take-off distance to clear a 15 m obstacle (rule of thumb: $s_{TO} \approx 1.44 \, s_{LO}$):

$$s_{TO} = 1.44 \times 890.2 \approx 1281.9 \text{ m}$$

---

#### NUMERICAL 3 — Wing Loading and Landing Distance Constraint (7-Mark Standard)

**Given Data:**
- Aircraft weight $W = 8000$ N
- Wing area $S = 20$ m²
- Landing configuration $C_{Lmax,L} = 1.4$
- Sea-level density $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Empirical landing formula: $s_L = 0.51 \, V_T^2$
- Touchdown speed factor: $V_T = 1.3 \, V_{s,L}$

**Find:**
- (a) Wing loading in N/m²
- (b) Stalling speed and touchdown speed
- (c) Landing ground run
- (d) Required wing loading to land within 500 m

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Wing Loading**

$$\frac{W}{S} = \frac{8000}{20} = 400 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Part (b): Stalling Speed and Touchdown Speed**

$$V_{s,L} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 400}{1.225 \times 1.4}}$$

$$V_{s,L} = \sqrt{\frac{800}{1.715}}$$

$$V_{s,L} = \sqrt{466.47}$$

$$V_{s,L} = 21.60 \text{ m/s}$$

Touchdown speed:

$$V_T = 1.3 \times 21.60 = 28.08 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (c): Landing Ground Run**

$$s_L = 0.51 \times (28.08)^2 = 0.51 \times 788.5$$

$$\boxed{s_L = 402.1 \text{ m}}$$

**Part (d): Wing Loading Required for 500 m Landing**

For $s_L = 500$ m, find required $V_T$:

$$500 = 0.51 \, V_T^2$$

$$V_T^2 = \frac{500}{0.51} = 980.39$$

$$V_T = 31.31 \text{ m/s}$$

Required stalling speed:

$$V_{s,L,req} = \frac{V_T}{1.3} = \frac{31.31}{1.3} = 24.08 \text{ m/s}$$

Required wing loading (using the master equation inverted):

$$\left(\frac{W}{S}\right)_{req} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{s,L,req}^2 C_{Lmax,L}$$

$$\left(\frac{W}{S}\right)_{req} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (24.08)^2 \times 1.4$$

$$\left(\frac{W}{S}\right)_{req} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 579.85 \times 1.4$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{W}{S}\right)_{req} = 497.13 \text{ N/m}^2}$$

**Conclusion:** Since the actual wing loading of **400 N/m²** is less than the maximum allowable of **497.13 N/m²**, the aircraft **satisfies** the 500 m landing distance requirement with a margin of **97.13 N/m²**.

---

### 3.3 The Inverse Problem — Finding Wing Area from a Stalling Speed Constraint

**Problem Statement:** A trainer aircraft is to be designed with a maximum take-off weight of $W = 12000$ N. To ensure safety, the stalling speed must not exceed $V_s = 30$ m/s at sea level. Given $C_{Lmax} = 1.8$, find the required wing area.

**Solution:**

From the master equation:

$$S = \frac{2 W}{\rho V_s^2 C_{Lmax}}$$

$$S = \frac{2 \times 12000}{1.225 \times (30)^2 \times 1.8}$$

$$S = \frac{24000}{1.225 \times 900 \times 1.8}$$

$$S = \frac{24000}{1984.5}$$

$$\boxed{S = 12.09 \text{ m}^2}$$

This is the **minimum wing area** that satisfies the stalling-speed constraint. The actual design wing area is typically chosen **larger** (15–20% margin) to account for real-world losses in $C_{Lmax}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Wing Loading as a Central Design Driver

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates how wing loading functions as the **central node** from which all major aircraft performance parameters branch. The arrows show direction of influence; the icons/colors can be added in modern Mermaid renderers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    WL[NODE_A: Wing Loading W over S] --> STALL[NODE_B: Stalling Speed Vs]
    WL --> TAKEOFF[NODE_C: Take off Run sTO]
    WL --> LANDING[NODE_D: Landing Run sL]
    WL --> CLIMB[NODE_E: Climb Performance RC]
    WL --> TURN[NODE_F: Turn Rate and Radius]
    WL --> GUST[NODE_G: Gust Load Factor]
    WL --> CEIL[NODE_H: Service Ceiling]
    WL --> SPES[NODE_I: Specific Excess Power]

    STALL --> SAF1[NODE_J: Flight Safety Margin]
    TAKEOFF --> SAF1
    LANDING --> SAF1

    TURN --> COMBAT[NODE_K: Combat Manoeuvrability]
    SPES --> COMBAT

    GUST --> COMFORT[NODE_L: Passenger Comfort]
    GUST --> STRUCT[NODE_M: Structural Sizing]

    CLIMB --> MISS1[NODE_N: Mission Profile]
    CEIL --> MISS1
    LANDING --> MISS1

    SAF1 --> DESIGN[NODE_O: Final Wing Area Selection]
    COMBAT --> DESIGN
    COMFORT --> DESIGN
    STRUCT --> DESIGN
    MISS1 --> DESIGN
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **central node** (NODE_A) is Wing Loading.
- Each **branch** represents a *performance trade-off* that the designer must consider.
- All branches **converge** into a single design decision (NODE_O): the final wing area, S.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A common viva question asks, *"If you are told only the wing loading, can you uniquely determine the wing area?"* The correct answer is **no** — you also need the design weight W (or S itself). This is why wing loading is a *ratio* and not an absolute area. The diagram above makes this conceptual point explicit.

### 4.2 Conceptual Schematic — How Wing Loading Trades Off

```mermaid
graph LR
    LOW[NODE_P: Low W over S] -- "Stall Speed LOW" --> SAFER[NODE_Q: Safer Aircraft]
    LOW -- "Take off SHORT" --> SAFER
    LOW -- "Climb STEEP" --> SAFER
    LOW -- "Turn TIGHT" --> SAFER

    LOW -- "Wing BIG" --> HEAVY[NODE_R: Heavy Wing Structure]
    LOW -- "Drag HIGH" --> SLOW[NODE_S: Lower Cruise Speed]
    LOW -- "Bumpy in Turbulence" --> ROUGH[NODE_T: Rough Ride]

    HIGH[NODE_U: High W over S] -- "Stall Speed HIGH" --> RISKY[NODE_V: Risky Low Speed]
    HIGH -- "Wing COMPACT" --> LIGHT[NODE_W: Light Wing Structure]
    HIGH -- "Smooth in Turbulence" --> COMFY[NODE_X: Smooth Ride]
    HIGH -- "Fast Cruise" --> SPEED[NODE_Y: High Speed]
```

This is the **trade matrix** that examiners use to set design-type questions. Be ready to argue for *both* sides depending on the mission.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Define wing loading. Mention the typical range of wing loading for a transport aircraft and a fighter aircraft.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wing loading is defined as the **gross weight of the aircraft divided by the reference wing planform area**, i.e., $W/S$, expressed in **N/m²** or **kg/m²**.

It represents the load carried per unit wing area and is a primary design parameter affecting stalling speed, take-off and landing distances, climb performance, and turn performance.

- **Transport aircraft:** typically $W/S \approx 2500$ to $5000$ N/m²
- **Fighter aircraft:** typically $W/S \approx 3000$ to $8000$ N/m²

---

**Q.A2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**How does an increase in wing loading affect the stalling speed of an aircraft? Justify with a suitable equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **increase in wing loading increases the stalling speed** of the aircraft.

From the stalling-speed equation:

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2 (W/S)}{\rho C_{Lmax}}}$$

Since $V_s$ is **directly proportional to $\sqrt{W/S}$**, doubling the wing loading increases the stalling speed by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

For example, increasing $W/S$ from **200 N/m² to 800 N/m²** (a fourfold increase) raises the stalling speed from 14.3 m/s to 28.6 m/s at sea level, assuming $C_{Lmax} = 1.6$.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 1 (14 marks) will have an **internal choice**. The student attempts **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

## **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

### **Q.A(a)** [7 marks]

**A small commuter aircraft has the following design data:**

- Maximum take-off weight $W = 7000$ kg
- Wing reference area $S = 28$ m²
- Maximum lift coefficient in take-off configuration $C_{Lmax,TO} = 1.8$
- Sea-level density $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Maximum engine thrust $T = 12000$ N
- Rolling friction coefficient $\mu = 0.05$
- Drag coefficient during ground run $C_D = 0.025$
- Lift-off speed factor $V_{LOF} = 1.2 \, V_{stall,TO}$

**Calculate:**
1. The wing loading in N/m²
2. The stalling speed in take-off configuration
3. The lift-off speed

### **Model Solution — Q.A(a)**

**Step 1: Weight in Newtons**

$$W = 7000 \times 9.81 = 68670 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Wing Loading**

$$\frac{W}{S} = \frac{68670}{28} = 2452.5 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Stalling Speed in TO Configuration**

$$V_{stall,TO} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2452.5}{1.225 \times 1.8}}$$

$$V_{stall,TO} = \sqrt{\frac{4905}{2.205}} = \sqrt{2224.49}$$

$$\boxed{V_{stall,TO} = 47.16 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4: Lift-off Speed**

$$V_{LOF} = 1.2 \times 47.16 = 56.60 \text{ m/s}$$

---

### **Q.A(b)** [7 marks]

**Continuing with the same aircraft from Q.A(a), calculate the take-off ground run. Use the energy method with average lift, drag, and friction evaluated at $V_{avg} = V_{LOF}/2$. Comment on whether the aircraft can operate from a 1000 m runway.**

### **Model Solution — Q.A(b)**

**Step 1: Average Speed and Lift Coefficient**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_{LOF}}{2} = 28.30 \text{ m/s}$$

Assuming linear variation, $C_{L,avg} = C_{Lmax,TO}/2 = 0.9$.

**Step 2: Average Lift**

$$L_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (28.30)^2 \times 28 \times 0.9$$

$$L_{avg} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 800.89 \times 28 \times 0.9$$

$$L_{avg} = 12349.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Average Drag**

$$D_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (28.30)^2 \times 28 \times 0.025$$

$$D_{avg} = 343.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Average Friction Force**

$$F_{f,avg} = \mu (W - L_{avg}) = 0.05 \times (68670 - 12349.7)$$

$$F_{f,avg} = 0.05 \times 56320.3 = 2816.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Net Force and Acceleration**

$$F_{net} = T - D_{avg} - F_{f,avg}$$

$$F_{net} = 12000 - 343.0 - 2816.0 = 8841.0 \text{ N}$$

$$a_{avg} = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{8841.0}{7000} = 1.263 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 6: Take-off Ground Run**

$$s_{LO} = \frac{V_{LOF}^2}{2 \, a_{avg}} = \frac{(56.60)^2}{2 \times 1.263}$$

$$s_{LO} = \frac{3203.56}{2.526}$$

$$\boxed{s_{LO} = 1268.2 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 7: Comment on Runway**

Total take-off distance to clear a 15 m obstacle (using $s_{TO} \approx 1.44 \, s_{LO}$):

$$s_{TO} = 1.44 \times 1268.2 = 1826.2 \text{ m}$$

Since **1826.2 m >> 1000 m**, the aircraft **cannot** operate from a 1000 m runway with the given thrust and configuration. To enable 1000 m operation, the designer would need to **increase thrust (T)**, **reduce wing loading (increase S)**, or **improve the lift coefficient (better high-lift devices)**.

---

## **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

### **Q.B(a)** [7 marks]

**Derive the relationship between wing loading and stalling speed starting from the fundamental lift equation. Clearly state all assumptions made.**

### **Model Solution — Q.B(a)**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, level, unaccelerated flight ($L = W$)
2. Standard sea-level conditions (or specified $\rho$)
3. Two-dimensional, incompressible flow (low Mach)
4. Wings operate at the maximum lift coefficient $C_{Lmax}$ at stall
5. Negligible ground effect

**Step 1:** Fundamental lift equation:

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$$

**Step 2:** At the stall, $L = W$ and $C_L = C_{Lmax}$:

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho V_s^2 S C_{Lmax} = W$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate $V_s$:

$$V_s^2 = \frac{2 W}{\rho S C_{Lmax}}$$

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2 W}{\rho S C_{Lmax}}}$$

**Step 4:** Express in terms of wing loading $W/S$:

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2 (W/S)}{\rho C_{Lmax}}}$$

**Step 5:** Physical Interpretation:

- $V_s \propto \sqrt{W/S}$ → **steeper than linear** increase with W/S
- $V_s \propto 1/\sqrt{C_{Lmax}}$ → better high-lift devices reduce $V_s$
- $V_s \propto 1/\sqrt{\rho}$ → at high altitude (low $\rho$), $V_s$ rises (true airspeed)

---

### **Q.B(b)** [7 marks]

**A glider has the following design data:**

- Weight $W = 350$ kg
- Wing area $S = 14$ m²
- Maximum lift coefficient $C_{Lmax} = 1.5$
- Sea-level density $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³
- The glider is cruising at 28 m/s at sea level

**Calculate:**
1. Wing loading in N/m² and in kg/m²
2. The lift coefficient required at cruise
3. The stalling speed
4. The minimum safe approach speed (1.3 times stall)
5. Comment on the safety margin for landing in a small airstrip

### **Model Solution — Q.B(b)**

**Step 1: Wing Loading**

$$W = 350 \times 9.81 = 3433.5 \text{ N}$$

$$\frac{W}{S} = \frac{3433.5}{14} = 245.25 \text{ N/m}^2$$

In kg/m²:

$$\left(\frac{W}{S}\right)_{kg} = \frac{350}{14} = 25 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Required Lift Coefficient at Cruise**

From the lift equation solved for $C_L$:

$$C_L = \frac{2W}{\rho V^2 S}$$

$$C_L = \frac{2 \times 3433.5}{1.225 \times (28)^2 \times 14}$$

$$C_L = \frac{6867}{13445.6}$$

$$\boxed{C_L = 0.511}$$

**Step 3: Stalling Speed**

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 245.25}{1.225 \times 1.5}} = \sqrt{\frac{490.5}{1.8375}}$$

$$V_s = \sqrt{266.94}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 16.34 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4: Approach Speed**

$$V_{app} = 1.3 \times 16.34 = 21.24 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Safety Margin and Comment**

- The cruise lift coefficient $C_L = 0.511$ is only **34%** of $C_{Lmax} = 1.5$, meaning the glider has substantial speed reserve (can fly faster without approaching stall).
- The approach speed of **21.24 m/s ≈ 76.5 km/h** is very low, ideal for short or grass airstrips.
- Using the empirical landing formula $s_L = 0.51 \, V_T^2$ with $V_T = 21.24$ m/s:

$$s_L = 0.51 \times (21.24)^2 = 0.51 \times 451.14$$

$$s_L = 230.1 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** The glider can comfortably land within a **230 m** strip with significant safety margin, and the **low wing loading of 245.25 N/m²** is the enabler of this short-field capability.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Wing-Loading Numericals:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Lift Equation (Most Frequent – Loses 1–2 Marks):** Many students plug $W$ in kgf into the SI version of $L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$. The result is dimensionally wrong and gives an $V_s$ that is off by $\sqrt{g}$. **Always convert weight to newtons** before substituting.
>
> 2. **Confusing Mass-Loading and Weight-Loading:** A $W/S$ of "250 kg/m²" is *mass loading*; the corresponding *weight loading* is $250 \times 9.81 = 2452.5$ N/m². Many KTU textbooks use both forms; check which one the question is asking for.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Density Variation with Altitude:** When the problem says "at 5000 m altitude", students often reuse $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³. This underestimates the stalling speed by a factor of $\sqrt{1.225 / 0.7364} \approx 1.29$.
>
> 4. **Mixing $V_{LOF}$ and $V_{stall}$ in Take-off Run Formula:** Use $V_{LOF} = 1.2 \, V_{stall}$, *not* $V_{stall}$, in $s_{LO} = V_{LOF}^2 / (2 a_{avg})$.
>
> 5. **Sign Error in Gust Load Factor:** The formula $\Delta n = K_g u V_e / (W/S)$ shows that **higher W/S reduces gust sensitivity**, *not* increases it. The misconception is that "a bigger plane is more affected by gusts" — in fact, it is *less* affected per unit weight.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Empirical Safety Factors:** In landing problems, $V_T = 1.3 V_{stall}$ is a *safety margin*. Hardcoding $V_T = V_{stall}$ produces unrealistically short landing distances and loses credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wing Loading (Module 1, KTU 2024):**
>
> - **Definition:** $W/S$ = weight ÷ wing area; expressed in N/m² or kg/m².
> - **Master Equation:** $V_s = \sqrt{2(W/S) \, / \, (\rho C_{Lmax})}$ — memorize in both forms (forward and inverted for design).
> - **Stalling Speed** $\uparrow$ as $\sqrt{W/S} \uparrow$ and as $1/\sqrt{\rho} \uparrow$ (altitude effect).
> - **Take-off Run** $\propto W/S$ (assuming constant thrust-to-weight).
> - **Landing Run** $\propto V_T^2 \propto W/S$ (empirical $s_L = 0.51 V_T^2$).
> - **Turn Performance:** Specific excess power $P_s = (T - D)V / W$ — lower $W/S$ generally improves low-speed turn rate.
> - **Gust Load Factor:** $\Delta n \propto 1/(W/S)$ — higher W/S gives a smoother ride.
> - **Typical Values to Remember:**
>   - Gliders ≈ 30–60 N/m²
>   - Light GA ≈ 600–1200 N/m²
>   - Transports ≈ 2400–5000 N/m²
>   - Fighters ≈ 3000–8000 N/m²
> - **Empirical Factors (must memorize):**
>   - $V_{LOF} = 1.2 \, V_{stall,TO}$
>   - $V_T = 1.3 \, V_{stall,L}$
>   - $V_{app} = 1.3 \, V_{stall}$
>   - $s_L = 0.51 \, V_T^2$ (sea level, hard surface, no wind)
> - **ISA Densities (commonly tested):**
>   - 0 m: 1.225 kg/m³
>   - 2000 m: 1.0066 kg/m³
>   - 4000 m: 0.8194 kg/m³
>   - 6000 m: 0.6601 kg/m³
>   - 8000 m: 0.5258 kg/m³
> - **Trade-off Mnemonic:** "**Low W/S — Low, Low, Long**" (low stall, low run, long wing = heavy structure).
> - **Unit Conversion:** $1 \text{ kg/m}^2 = 9.81 \text{ N/m}^2$; $1 \text{ lb/ft}^2 = 47.88 \text{ N/m}^2$.
> - **Inverse Design:** To find the required wing area, use $S = 2W/(\rho V_s^2 C_{Lmax})$.
> - **Designers' First Question:** What is the *mission*? This fixes W/S, which then drives S.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aircraft Classifications, Airplane Configurations Based on Wing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aircraft Classifications, Airplane Configurations Based on Wing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Aircraft? (KTU 2024 Definition)

An **aircraft** is any machine that derives its support in the atmosphere from the air reactions acting upon its surfaces, or that is designed to navigate or travel within the atmosphere. The KTU 2024 syllabus for *Fundamentals of Aeronautics* further bifurcates this into two grand families based on the fundamental physics of lift generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Canonical Definition**
> The official KTU definition (aligned with ICAO Annex 7 and Indian Aircraft Rules) is: *"Any machine that can derive support in the atmosphere from the reactions of the air other than the reactions of the air against the earth's surface."*
> This single sentence is the foundation of Part A and frequently appears as a 1-mark sub-question in KTU ESE papers.

### The Two Grand Divisions

| Division | Lift Mechanism | Earth Surface Reactions? | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lighter-than-Air (Aerostat)** | Buoyancy (Archimedes' principle on displaced air) | NOT required | Hot-air balloon, Zeppelin, Blimp |
| **Heavier-than-Air (Aerodyne)** | Dynamic lift from airfoil motion (Newton/Bernoulli) | Required (takeoff/landing) | Airplane, Helicopter, Glider, Drone |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Tree of Flying Machines"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the world of aircraft the same way we think of *vehicles* on the road. We classify road vehicles by **fuel type** (petrol/diesel/electric), **usage** (personal/commercial/heavy-truck), **terrain** (on-road/off-road/marine), and **propulsion** (engine/motor/sail). Aircraft are classified on **exactly parallel lines** — by *how they generate lift*, *what mission they perform*, *how they are powered*, and *how they take off*.
> - **"Fuel type" of aircraft = Lift mechanism** (lighter/heavier than air)
> - **"Usage" of aircraft = Mission profile** (civilian/military, transport/fighter/trainer)
> - **"Propulsion" of aircraft = Engine type** (propeller/jet/rocket)
> - **"Chassis layout" of aircraft = Wing configuration** (low-wing, high-wing, monoplane, biplane, swept, delta, etc.)
