So when KTU asks *"Classify aircraft and briefly describe each,"* the examiner is essentially asking you to draw a clean taxonomy tree — just like you would for road vehicles.

## 1.3 Why Wing Configuration is a "First-Class" Classification

The **wing** is the most important lift-producing component of any heavier-than-air aircraft. Its:
- **Number** (one, two, three),
- **Position relative to fuselage** (low/mid/high/parasol),
- **Planform shape** (rectangular, tapered, swept, delta), and
- **Attachment method** (cantilever, braced)

…together determine the airplane's **aerodynamic efficiency, structural weight, stability characteristics, stall behavior, fuel consumption, and even its visible "personality."** A fighter and an airliner are recognisable as such at a glance — the difference is almost entirely in the wing configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Highlight:** *Wing configuration is the single most exam-relevant sub-topic in this module.* It routinely fetches 7 to 14 marks because it combines descriptive theory with quantitative parameters (Aspect Ratio, Taper Ratio, Sweep, MAC) and links directly to Module 3 (Aerodynamics).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Aircraft (Complete KTU Taxonomy)

### 2.1.1 Classification by Lift-Generation Principle
This is the **primary, root-level** classification that the KTU examiner expects you to start with.

**(a) Lighter-Than-Air Aircraft (Aerostats)**
- Lift is produced by **buoyancy**: a lighter-than-air gas (helium, hydrogen, or hot air) displaces a heavier mass of ambient air.
- They do NOT require forward motion to generate lift; they *float*.
- Sub-categories:
  - **Balloons** (free or tethered) — no propulsion, drift with the wind.
  - **Airships** (powered, steerable) — *rigid* (Zeppelin, with internal frame), *semi-rigid*, *non-rigid* (blimps, maintain shape by internal gas pressure).

**(b) Heavier-Than-Air Aircraft (Aerodynes)**
- Lift is produced by the **dynamic reaction** of air against an airfoil moving through it.
- Forward motion (or rotor motion) is mandatory.
- Sub-categories by lift surface:
  - **Fixed-wing** (airplanes)
  - **Rotary-wing** (helicopters, autogyros)
  - **Flapping-wing** (ornithopters — mostly experimental)
  - **Powered-lift / Hybrid** (tilt-rotors like V-22 Osprey, tilt-wings)

### 2.1.2 Classification by Usage / Mission
| Class | KTU Examples | Defining Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport / Commercial** | Boeing 737, Airbus A320 | High capacity, fuel efficiency, long range |
| **Private / General Aviation** | Cessna 172, Piper PA-28 | Light, low cost, 1–4 seats |
| **Military Fighter** | Tejas, F-22, Rafale | High speed, manoeuvrability, weapons |
| **Military Bomber** | B-2 Spirit | High payload, long range, low observability |
| **Trainer** | Pilatus PC-7, Hawk | Tandem or side-by-side seating, robust |
| **Reconnaissance** | U-2, Global Hawk | High altitude, long endurance |
| **Agricultural** | Air Tractor AT-802 | Low altitude, spray equipment |
| **Sport / Aerobatic** | Extra 330, Pitts Special | High G-tolerance, light controls |

### 2.1.3 Classification by Propulsion
- **Propeller-driven**: Piston-engine, Turboprop.
- **Jet-driven**: Turbojet, Turbofan (high-bypass, low-bypass), Turbo-shaft (used in helicopters).
- **Rocket**: Limited to experimental or missile applications.
- **Electric / Solar**: Modern UAVs and record-attempt aircraft (Solar Impulse 2).

### 2.1.4 Classification by Takeoff & Landing Capability
- **CTOL** – Conventional Take-Off and Landing (most common).
- **STOL** – Short Take-Off and Landing (e.g., C-130 Hercules).
- **VTOL** – Vertical Take-Off and Landing (Harrier, F-35B).
- **STOVL** – Short Take-Off, Vertical Landing.
- **VTSTOL** – Vertical/Short Take-Off (tilt-rotors).

### 2.1.5 Classification by Speed Regime (Mach Number, M)
- **Subsonic** : M < 0.8 (almost all civil aircraft).
- **Transonic** : 0.8 ≤ M ≤ 1.2 (cruise regime of most jetliners).
- **Supersonic** : 1.2 < M ≤ 5.0 (Concorde M ≈ 2.02; fighter jets).
- **Hypersonic** : M > 5.0 (SR-71, scramjet research).

## 2.2 Airplane Configurations Based on the Wing

This is the **highest-weightage sub-topic** for KTU Module 2.

### 2.2.1 Configuration A — By Number of Wings

| Type | Description | KTU Examples | Reason for Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monoplane** | A single pair of wings | Almost every modern aircraft | Lightest, least drag |
| **Biplane** | Two wings stacked vertically | Pitts Special, Stearman | High lift in short span (early aviation) |
| **Triplane** | Three wings | Fokker Dr.I (WWI) | Historical; very high lift coefficient |
| **Multiplane** | Four or more wings | Caproni Ca.60 | Historical; obsolete |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why did biplanes go extinct?** Two stacked wings create *interference drag* between them, and the struts/wires add *parasite drag*. A monoplane with a thick cantilever wing achieves the same lift with less drag and less weight. KTU examiners love this "why" question.

### 2.2.2 Configuration B — By Wing Position (Vertical Placement)

| Type | Description | Stability Effect | KTU Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-wing** | Wing root attached to bottom of fuselage | Inherently *unstable* in roll; needs dihedral | Cessna 172, Airbus A320 |
| **Mid-wing** | Wing passes through the middle of the fuselage | Aerodynamically cleanest | F-16, Learjet |
| **High-wing** | Wing on top of fuselage | Inherently *stable* in roll | Cessna 172 High-wing, C-130 |
| **Parasol wing** | Wing mounted on a pylon above fuselage, with fuselage hanging below | Good downward visibility, stable | Early WWII trainers, some UAVs |

### 2.2.3 Configuration C — By Wing Planform Shape
The planform is the shape of the wing when viewed from directly above (bird's-eye view). It is defined by the **Leading Edge Sweep Angle (Λ)**, the **Taper Ratio (λ)**, the **Aspect Ratio (AR)**, and the **Tip Shape**.

| Planform | Sketch Description | Aerodynamic Trait | KTU Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular** | Constant chord, no sweep, no taper | Simplest; uniform stall pattern | Piper Cherokee |
| **Elliptical** | Planform is a true half-ellipse | Theoretically minimum induced drag | Supermarine Spitfire |
| **Tapered** | Chord reduces from root to tip | Better tip relief, reduces tip stall | Boeing 737 |
| **Swept-back** | Leading edge swept aft | Delays drag-divergence at high M | Boeing 747, F-15 |
| **Delta** | Triangular, very high sweep | High-speed, low aspect ratio | Concorde, Mirage 2000 |
| **Variable-Sweep (Swing-wing)** | Sweep changes in flight | Multi-role (low & high speed) | F-14 Tomcat, Tornado |
| **Forward-swept** | Leading edge swept forward | Aggressive manoeuvrability | Grumman X-29 |

### 2.2.4 Configuration D — By Wing Attachment
- **Cantilever Wing** — no external struts or bracing wires; the wing structure itself carries all loads. Modern standard.
- **Semi-cantilever (Braced) Wing** — uses a single strut beneath the wing to reduce bending stress. Common in light aircraft (Cessna 152).
- **Externally Braced** — full wire-bracing and struts (e.g., early biplanes, Piper Cub).

### 2.2.5 Configuration E — By Aspect Ratio
- **Low AR** (AR < 4) — Fighter aircraft, good manoeuvrability.
- **Moderate AR** (4 ≤ AR ≤ 8) — Transport aircraft, balanced.
- **High AR** (AR > 8) — Sailplanes, gliders, U-2, Solar Impulse — maximum aerodynamic efficiency (lift-to-drag).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Use \vert for absolute value to protect markdown)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units (SI) | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wing Area** | $S$ | $S = \dfrac{b}{2}\,(c_{r}+c_{t}) = b \cdot \bar{c}$ | m² | Basic geometry of tapered wing |
| 2 | **Aspect Ratio** | $AR$ | $AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S} = \dfrac{b}{\bar{c}}$ | dimensionless | Classifies wing as low/high AR |
| 3 | **Taper Ratio** | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{c_{t}}{c_{r}}$ | dimensionless | 0 → pointed tip; 1 → rectangular |
| 4 | **Mean Aerodynamic Chord** | $\bar{c}$ or $MAC$ | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+\lambda+\lambda^{2}}{1+\lambda}$ | m | Centre-of-pressure reference |
| 5 | **Leading-Edge Sweep** | $\Lambda_{LE}$ | $\tan\Lambda_{LE} = \dfrac{x_{tip}-x_{root}}{(b/2)}$ | degrees | High-speed (transonic/supersonic) |
| 6 | **Wing Loading** | $W/S$ | $W/S = \dfrac{W_{TO}}{S}$ | N/m² or kg/m² | Takeoff/landing performance |
| 7 | **Lift coefficient (cruise)** | $C_{L}$ | $C_{L} = \dfrac{2W}{\rho V^{2} S}$ | dimensionless | Link to Module 3 (Aerodynamics) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions you must memorise for KTU:**
> - If $\lambda = 1$ (rectangular): $\bar{c} = c_{r}$ and $S = b \cdot c_{r}$. (Use this for sanity check.)
> - If $\lambda = 0.5$ (typical transport): $\bar{c} \approx 0.866\,c_{r}$.
> - If $\lambda \to 0$ (delta-like): $\bar{c} \to \dfrac{2\,c_{r}}{3}$.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Aspect Ratio (AR)** drives the *induced-drag coefficient* $C_{D_i} = \dfrac{C_{L}^{2}}{\pi e AR}$. Long-range aircraft maximise AR to minimise fuel burn.
- **Taper Ratio (λ)** controls the *elliptic lift distribution*; $\lambda \approx 0.4$ approximates the elliptical planform that minimises induced drag.
- **Sweep Angle (Λ)** is the *primary knob* for transonic drag-divergence (the famous "sound barrier" mitigation).
- **Wing Loading (W/S)** dictates the *stall speed* and *runway length* required.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner has a strong preference for derivations. The three most asked are: (1) Wing Area of a tapered wing, (2) Mean Aerodynamic Chord, and (3) Aspect Ratio. We derive all three from first principles below.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wing Area of a Tapered Wing

**Given:** A wing with span $b$ (root to root, so semi-span $= b/2$), root chord $c_{r}$, and tip chord $c_{t}$. The chord varies linearly from root to tip.

**Step 1 — Establish the chord distribution.**
Let $y$ be the distance from the aircraft's centreline along the span. The local chord $c(y)$ is:

$$c(y) = c_{r} + (c_{t} - c_{r})\cdot\dfrac{2y}{b}, \quad 0 \le y \le \dfrac{b}{2}$$

The factor $\frac{2y}{b}$ is a *shape function* that goes from 0 at the root ($y=0$) to 1 at the tip ($y=b/2$).

**Step 2 — Recognise the wing area as the area of a trapezoid in planform.**
The planform of one half of the wing is a trapezoid with:
- Parallel sides $c_{r}$ and $c_{t}$
- Height $b/2$

So the area of one half is $\dfrac{1}{2}(c_{r}+c_{t})\cdot\dfrac{b}{2}$.

**Step 3 — Double it for both halves.**

$$S = 2 \times \dfrac{1}{2}(c_{r}+c_{t})\cdot\dfrac{b}{2}$$

$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{b}{2}\,(c_{r}+c_{t})}$$

**Step 4 — Define the mean geometric chord $\bar{c}$ for sanity check.**
The mean geometric chord is simply the *average* of root and tip:

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{c_{r}+c_{t}}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$S = b \cdot \bar{c}$$

This matches the second form in the formula sheet. **Sanity check:** If $\lambda = 1$ (rectangular), then $c_{r}=c_{t}=c$, so $S = (b/2)(2c) = bc$, which is the familiar rectangle area. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC)

The MAC is the chord of an *equivalent rectangular wing* that has the *same pitching-moment and lift characteristics* as the real wing. The rigorous definition uses the chord-cubed weighting:

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{2}{S}\int_{0}^{b/2} c(y)^{2}\,dy$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the chord distribution.**

$$c(y)^{2} = \left[c_{r} + (c_{t}-c_{r})\cdot\dfrac{2y}{b}\right]^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Non-dimensionalise with $\lambda = c_{t}/c_{r}$.**
Let $u = \dfrac{2y}{b}$, so $du = \dfrac{2}{b}\,dy$, i.e. $dy = \dfrac{b}{2}\,du$. The integral limits stay $0 \to 1$.

$$c(y) = c_{r}\bigl[1 + (\lambda-1)u\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{1} c_{r}^{2}\bigl[1+(\lambda-1)u\bigr]^{2}\,du = c_{r}^{2}\int_{0}^{1}\bigl[1+(\lambda-1)u\bigr]^{2}\,du$$

Let $v = 1+(\lambda-1)u$, so $dv = (\lambda-1)\,du$. Limits: $u=0 \Rightarrow v=1$; $u=1 \Rightarrow v=\lambda$.

$$= \dfrac{c_{r}^{2}}{\lambda-1}\int_{1}^{\lambda} v^{2}\,dv = \dfrac{c_{r}^{2}}{\lambda-1}\cdot\dfrac{v^{3}}{3}\Big\vert_{1}^{\lambda} = \dfrac{c_{r}^{2}}{3(\lambda-1)}\,(\lambda^{3}-1)$$

**Step 4 — Factor the cube-difference.**
Use the algebraic identity $a^{3}-b^{3}=(a-b)(a^{2}+ab+b^{2})$:

$$\lambda^{3}-1 = (\lambda-1)(\lambda^{2}+\lambda+1)$$

So the integral becomes:

$$\dfrac{c_{r}^{2}}{3(\lambda-1)}\cdot(\lambda-1)(\lambda^{2}+\lambda+1) = \dfrac{c_{r}^{2}}{3}(\lambda^{2}+\lambda+1)$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $b/2$ (the $dy$ substitution) and divide by $S/2$.**

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{2}{S}\cdot\dfrac{b}{2}\cdot\dfrac{c_{r}^{2}}{3}(\lambda^{2}+\lambda+1)$$

Using $S = \dfrac{b c_{r}(1+\lambda)}{2}$ from Derivation 1:

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{b c_{r}(1+\lambda)}{2}}\cdot\dfrac{b c_{r}^{2}}{6}(\lambda^{2}+\lambda+1)$$

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{4}{b c_{r}(1+\lambda)}\cdot\dfrac{b c_{r}^{2}}{6}(\lambda^{2}+\lambda+1) = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{\lambda^{2}+\lambda+1}{1+\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+\lambda+\lambda^{2}}{1+\lambda}}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check (rectangular wing, $\lambda=1$).**

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+1+1}{1+1} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{3}{2} = c_{r} \;\checkmark$$

For a triangular (delta-like) wing with $\lambda=0$:

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+0+0}{1+0} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3} \;\checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aspect Ratio (AR)

**Step 1 — Start from the definition.** Aspect ratio is the *ratio of span to mean chord*:

$$AR = \dfrac{b}{\bar{c}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\bar{c} = S/b$ (from Derivation 1).**

$$AR = \dfrac{b}{S/b} = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S}$$

$$\boxed{AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S} = \dfrac{b}{\bar{c}}}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.**
A high AR (long, slender wing) gives low induced drag but penalises roll rate. A low AR (stubby wing) is good for manoeuvrability and structural strength.

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Standard Numerical)

> **Problem [Adapted from KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]:**
> A tapered aircraft wing has a span $b = 20\text{ m}$, root chord $c_{r} = 4\text{ m}$, and tip chord $c_{t} = 1.5\text{ m}$. Find: (i) wing area, (ii) aspect ratio, (iii) mean aerodynamic chord, (iv) taper ratio.

**Step 1 — Taper Ratio.**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{c_{t}}{c_{r}} = \dfrac{1.5}{4} = 0.375$$

**Step 2 — Wing Area.**

$$S = \dfrac{b}{2}(c_{r}+c_{t}) = \dfrac{20}{2}(4+1.5) = 10 \times 5.5 = 55\text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Aspect Ratio.**

$$AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S} = \dfrac{20^{2}}{55} = \dfrac{400}{55} \approx 7.27$$

**Step 4 — Mean Aerodynamic Chord.**

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+\lambda+\lambda^{2}}{1+\lambda}$$

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{2(4)}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+0.375+0.1406}{1+0.375}$$

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{8}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1.5156}{1.375} = 2.6667 \times 1.1022 \approx 2.94\text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check using average chord:**

$$\bar{c}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{c_{r}+c_{t}}{2} = 2.75\text{ m}$$

The MAC (2.94 m) is slightly larger than the simple mean — this is *expected*, because MAC is weighted towards the root where the chord is larger, and root contributes more to pitching moment than tip.

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Taper Ratio $\lambda$ | **0.375** |
| Wing Area $S$ | **55 m²** |
| Aspect Ratio $AR$ | **7.27** |
| Mean Aerodynamic Chord $\bar{c}$ | **2.94 m** |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]
**"Differentiate between lighter-than-air and heavier-than-air aircraft with two examples each."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Lighter-Than-Air (LTA) Aircraft:** These derive their support in the atmosphere from **buoyancy**, i.e. the displaced air weighs more than the aircraft itself. They do not require forward motion to generate lift. They are also called **aerostats**. *Examples:* Hot-air balloon (filled with heated air), and a non-rigid blimp (filled with helium).
>
> **Heavier-Than-Air (HTA) Aircraft:** These derive support from the **dynamic reaction of air against an airfoil** moving through it. They require continuous forward motion (or rotor motion) to generate lift. They are also called **aerodynes**. *Examples:* Commercial transport like Airbus A320, and a helicopter like HAL Dhruv.
>
> **Key distinction:** LTA does NOT require forward motion for lift, HTA DOES. This is the KTU-mandated single-sentence differentiator. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition each + 1 mark for examples each, OR 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for examples)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]
**"What is meant by 'wing planform'? List any four common planform shapes used in modern aircraft."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> The **wing planform** is the **shape of the wing as seen from directly above** (bird's-eye view), defined by the leading edge, trailing edge, and tip shape. *(1 mark)*
>
> Four common planforms: *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
> 1. **Rectangular** — constant chord, no taper (Piper Cherokee).
> 2. **Tapered** — chord decreases from root to tip (Boeing 737).
> 3. **Swept-back** — leading edge swept aft to delay drag-divergence (Boeing 747).
> 4. **Delta** — triangular high-sweep planform (Concorde, Mirage 2000).
>
> *(Acceptable alternatives: Elliptical, Forward-swept, Variable-sweep.)*

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Classify aircraft based on (i) lift-generation principle, (ii) usage, and (iii) propulsion. Give two examples for each main class. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An aircraft wing has span $b = 18\text{ m}$, root chord $c_{r} = 3.6\text{ m}$, and tip chord $c_{t} = 1.2\text{ m}$. Calculate: (i) Wing area, (ii) Aspect ratio, (iii) Taper ratio, (iv) Mean Aerodynamic Chord. State whether the wing is "low AR" or "high AR". *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Classification by lift-generation principle (3 marks):**
1. **Lighter-than-air (aerostats):** Lift by buoyancy. Examples — Hot-air balloon, Zeppelin.
2. **Heavier-than-air (aerodynes):** Lift by dynamic air reaction. Examples — Airbus A320 (airplane), HAL Dhruv (helicopter).

**Classification by usage (2 marks):**
- **Civilian** — Commercial transport (A320), Private/General Aviation (Cessna 172).
- **Military** — Fighter (Tejas Mk2), Bomber (B-2 Spirit), Trainer (PC-7 Mk II).

**Classification by propulsion (2 marks):**
- **Propeller-driven** — Piston (Cessna 152), Turboprop (ATR 72).
- **Jet-driven** — Turbojet (older fighters), Turbofan (modern airliners).
- **Rocket / Electric** — Experimental, drones.

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

**(i) Taper Ratio:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{c_{t}}{c_{r}} = \dfrac{1.2}{3.6} = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**(ii) Wing Area:**

$$S = \dfrac{b}{2}\,(c_{r}+c_{t}) = \dfrac{18}{2}\,(3.6+1.2) = 9 \times 4.8 = \mathbf{43.2\;m^{2}}$$

**(iii) Aspect Ratio:**

$$AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S} = \dfrac{18^{2}}{43.2} = \dfrac{324}{43.2} = \mathbf{7.5}$$

**Classification:** $AR = 7.5$ is in the range $4 \le AR \le 8$, so this is a **moderate AR wing** (typical of a transport / commuter aircraft). It is NOT low AR (that would be < 4) and NOT high AR (that would be > 8). *(1 mark for the judgement.)*

**(iv) Mean Aerodynamic Chord:**

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+\lambda+\lambda^{2}}{1+\lambda}$$

$$\bar{c} = \dfrac{2(3.6)}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+0.333+0.111}{1+0.333} = 2.4 \times \dfrac{1.444}{1.333}$$

$$\bar{c} = 2.4 \times 1.0833 \approx \mathbf{2.60\;m}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Taper Ratio $\lambda$ | **0.333** |
| Wing Area $S$ | **43.2 m²** |
| Aspect Ratio $AR$ | **7.5 (moderate)** |
| Mean Aerodynamic Chord $\bar{c}$ | **2.60 m** |

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, classify airplane configurations based on the wing considering: (i) number of wings, (ii) position of wing, and (iii) planform shape. Give one example of an aircraft for each type. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A trainer aircraft has the following wing parameters: span $b = 10\text{ m}$, average chord $\bar{c} = 1.5\text{ m}$, taper ratio $\lambda = 0.6$. Calculate: (i) Wing area, (ii) Aspect ratio, (iii) Root and tip chords, and (iv) Mean Aerodynamic Chord. Comment on the AR. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Classification Table (with sketches indicated by description):** *(7 marks — 1 mark per major sub-category + 1 mark overall structure)*

| Basis | Type | Example | Sketch Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Number of wings** | Monoplane | Airbus A320 | Single pair of wings, no struts |
| | Biplane | Pitts Special | Two wings, stacked, with struts |
| | Triplane (rare) | Fokker Dr.I (WWI) | Three wings |
| **(ii) Wing position** | Low-wing | Cessna 152 | Wing on bottom of fuselage |
| | Mid-wing | F-16 | Wing through middle |
| | High-wing | Cessna 172 | Wing on top |
| | Parasol | Early WWII trainers | Wing on pylon above fuselage |
| **(iii) Planform** | Rectangular | Piper Cherokee | No taper, no sweep |
| | Tapered | Boeing 737 | Chord reduces towards tip |
| | Swept-back | Boeing 747 | LE swept aft |
| | Delta | Mirage 2000 | Triangular planform |

*(Award full marks if at least 8 types are described with examples.)*

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Given:** $b = 10\text{ m}$, $\bar{c} = 1.5\text{ m}$, $\lambda = 0.6$.

**(i) Wing Area:**

$$S = b \cdot \bar{c} = 10 \times 1.5 = \mathbf{15\;m^{2}}$$

**(ii) Aspect Ratio:**

$$AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S} = \dfrac{10^{2}}{15} = \dfrac{100}{15} \approx \mathbf{6.67}$$

This is a **moderate AR** wing — appropriate for a trainer aircraft that needs good visibility (mild sweep) and reasonable roll rate.

**(iii) Root and Tip Chords:**
We have $\bar{c} = \dfrac{c_{r}+c_{t}}{2} = 1.5 \Rightarrow c_{r}+c_{t} = 3.0$.
We have $\lambda = \dfrac{c_{t}}{c_{r}} = 0.6 \Rightarrow c_{t} = 0.6\,c_{r}$.

Substituting:

$$c_{r} + 0.6\,c_{r} = 3.0 \Rightarrow 1.6\,c_{r} = 3.0 \Rightarrow c_{r} = \mathbf{1.875\;m}$$

$$c_{t} = 0.6 \times 1.875 = \mathbf{1.125\;m}$$

**Sanity check:** $(1.875 + 1.125)/2 = 1.5 = \bar{c}$ ✓

**(iv) Mean Aerodynamic Chord:**

$$\bar{c}_{MAC} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+\lambda+\lambda^{2}}{1+\lambda} = \dfrac{2(1.875)}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+0.6+0.36}{1+0.6}$$

$$= 1.25 \times \dfrac{1.96}{1.6} = 1.25 \times 1.225 = \mathbf{1.531\;m}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Wing Area $S$ | **15 m²** |
| Aspect Ratio $AR$ | **6.67 (moderate — trainer class)** |
| Root Chord $c_{r}$ | **1.875 m** |
| Tip Chord $c_{t}$ | **1.125 m** |
| Mean Aerodynamic Chord $\bar{c}_{MAC}$ | **1.531 m** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $\bar{c}$ (mean geometric chord) with $MAC$ (mean aerodynamic chord).** They are *different quantities* and the MAC formula is $\dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+\lambda+\lambda^{2}}{1+\lambda}$, NOT $\dfrac{c_{r}+c_{t}}{2}$. If the question asks for MAC, you must use the cube-weighted formula. **Loss: 2 marks per question.**
> 2. **Forgetting to specify whether the AR is low / moderate / high.** A bare number $AR = 7.5$ with no engineering interpretation loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Using the wrong unit for span vs. semi-span.** Wing area is computed with *full span* $b$, not semi-span. Many students write $S = b(c_{r}+c_{t})$ which is **off by a factor of 2**. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Not writing the units** in the final answer table. The KTU model answer key deducts 0.5 mark per missing SI unit.
> 5. **Listing classifications without examples.** Part A questions (3 marks) explicitly demand "two examples each" — failure to comply costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Sketches in the wrong plane.** Wing planform is the *top view*; wing section / airfoil is the *cross-sectional view*. Mixing them up is a common KTU trap.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 25 Things You MUST Remember Before Walking Into The KTU Exam Hall**

- [ ] **Definition of aircraft** — support from air reactions, not from ground reactions.
- [ ] **Two grand divisions:** Aerostats (lighter-than-air) and Aerodynes (heavier-than-air).
- [ ] **Three sub-types of aerostats:** Free balloon, Tethered balloon, Airship (rigid / semi-rigid / non-rigid).
- [ ] **Four sub-types of aerodynes:** Fixed-wing, Rotary-wing, Flapping-wing, Powered-lift.
- [ ] **Classification by usage** — Civilian (transport, private, sport) vs. Military (fighter, bomber, trainer, recon).
- [ ] **Classification by propulsion** — Propeller (piston, turboprop), Jet (turbojet, turbofan, turboshaft), Rocket, Electric.
- [ ] **Classification by takeoff/landing** — CTOL, STOL, VTOL, STOVL.
- [ ] **Classification by Mach regime** — Subsonic (<0.8), Transonic (0.8–1.2), Supersonic (1.2–5), Hypersonic (>5).
- [ ] **Wing-number types:** Monoplane (modern standard), Biplane (old, high lift in short span), Triplane (historical).
- [ ] **Wing-position types:** Low-wing (inherently unstable in roll), Mid-wing (clean), High-wing (inherently stable in roll), Parasol (pylon above fuselage).
- [ ] **Planform types:** Rectangular, Elliptical, Tapered, Swept-back, Delta, Forward-swept, Variable-sweep.
- [ ] **Attachment types:** Cantilever (no external bracing), Semi-cantilever (one strut), Braced (full wire-bracing).
- [ ] **Aspect ratio classes:** Low AR < 4 (fighter), Moderate AR 4–8 (transport), High AR > 8 (glider, U-2).
- [ ] **Wing area formula:** $S = \dfrac{b}{2}\,(c_{r}+c_{t}) = b\bar{c}$.
- [ ] **Aspect ratio formula:** $AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S} = \dfrac{b}{\bar{c}}$.
- [ ] **Taper ratio formula:** $\lambda = c_{t}/c_{r}$.
- [ ] **MAC formula (cube-weighted):** $\bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{1+\lambda+\lambda^{2}}{1+\lambda}$.
- [ ] **Sweep formula:** $\tan\Lambda_{LE} = (x_{tip}-x_{root})/(b/2)$.
- [ ] **Wing loading formula:** $W/S = W_{TO}/S$.
- [ ] **Sanity check 1:** $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow \bar{c} = c_{r} = c_{t}$.
- [ ] **Sanity check 2:** $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow \bar{c} = \dfrac{2c_{r}}{3}$.
- [ ] **Sanity check 3:** MAC is always slightly greater than the simple mean chord for tapered wings.
- [ ] **Why biplanes are obsolete:** interference drag between wings + parasite drag from struts.
- [ ] **Why high-AR wings exist:** minimise induced drag (long range, gliders).
- [ ] **Why low-AR wings exist:** maximise roll rate and structural strength (fighters).

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 2, Topic: Aircraft Classifications, Airplane Configurations Based on Wing**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Landing Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Landing Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Landing Gear** is the undercarriage assembly of an aircraft that supports the entire airframe on the ground, absorbs the kinetic energy of landing and taxiing impacts, provides traction for takeoff and braking, and enables directional control during ground operations. It is one of the most structurally and dynamically loaded subsystems of any aircraft, second only to the wings in criticality.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCAOT205 – Module 2):**
> *Landing gear comprises the structural members, wheels, tires, brakes, shock absorbers, retraction mechanisms, and steering systems required to support the aircraft on the ground and absorb landing/taxi loads. Its design integrates structural, mechanical, hydraulic, and pneumatics engineering principles.*

### 1.1.1 Functional Roles of Landing Gear (KTU Board Keywords)

The landing gear is not just a "set of wheels" — it performs **six distinct engineering functions**:

1. **Support** – Carries the static weight of the parked aircraft (MTOW = Maximum Take-Off Weight).
2. **Energy Absorption** – Dissipates the vertical kinetic energy at touchdown without transmitting damaging loads to the airframe.
3. **Braking** – Converts the aircraft's translational kinetic energy into heat via friction during landing rollout.
4. **Steering & Control** – Provides directional stability and nose-wheel steering on the ground.
5. **Retraction/Deployment** – Stows the gear in flight to reduce parasitic drag and deploys it for landing.
6. **Towing & Taxiing** – Permits ground handling by tugs and provides smooth taxi operation.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Human Leg:**
> Think of an aircraft's landing gear exactly like a human runner's legs and shoes. When you run and land from a jump:
> - **The shock absorber (oleo strut)** acts like the cushioning cartilage in your knees — it compresses and slowly releases energy so the skeleton (airframe) is not jarred.
> - **The wheels and tires** are like your shoes, gripping the ground and rolling forward.
> - **The brake discs** are analogous to the friction between your shoe sole and the ground when you decelerate.
> - **The retraction system** is like flexing your knees upward while sprinting to reduce drag — birds tuck their legs in flight for the same aerodynamic reason.
> - **The tricycle geometry (two main + one nose wheel)** gives a runner's three-point stance — stable, level, and easy to steer.

Just as a marathon runner needs shoes engineered for cushioning, grip, and durability, an aircraft needs its landing gear engineered for **specific load cases, runway conditions, and operational speeds**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical Benchmarks used by KTU Examiners:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equivalent vertical sink speed (Tricycle) | $V_s$ | **1.5 m/s to 3.0 m/s** | CS-23 / CS-25 design value |
| Equivalent vertical sink speed (Tailwheel) | $V_s$ | **2.0 m/s to 3.5 m/s** | Higher due to tail-low attitude |
| Coefficient of friction (brake, dry runway) | $\mu_b$ | **0.3 to 0.5** | Used in braking energy calc |
| Tire pressure (commercial airliner) | $p_{tire}$ | **1.0 MPa to 1.5 MPa** | High-pressure tubeless radial |
| Typical oleo strut pressure | $p_{strut}$ | **0.7 MPa to 3.5 MPa** | Charged with dry nitrogen |
| Wheel load factor (CS-25) | $n$ | **2.5 to 3.0 × MTOW** | Static × limit load factor |
| Sink speed at structural limit | $V_{s,lim}$ | **3.05 m/s (10 ft/s)** | FAA regulatory standard |

---

## 1.4 Terminology Glossary (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Oleo Strut:** A telescopic, oil-and-nitrogen shock absorber that combines hydraulic damping with pneumatic spring action.
> - **Trunnion:** The pivot point about which the gear retracts/extends.
> - **Bay (Wheel Bay):** The structural compartment in the wing/fuselage that houses the retracted gear.
> - **Tow Bar / Tow Barless:** Equipment for ground-handling the nose gear.
> - **Torque Links (Scissor Links):** The two hinged arms that prevent the oleo piston from rotating while allowing only linear telescoping motion.
> - **Main Gear:** The principal load-bearing wheels (usually two pairs on transport aircraft).
> - **Nose Gear / Tail Gear:** The third support point providing longitudinal stability on the ground.
> - **Retraction Actuation Time:** Time taken to stow or deploy the gear — typically **8 to 12 seconds** on transport aircraft.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Landing Gear

### 2.1.1 By Configuration (Number & Position of Wheels)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked — Three Principal Configurations:**

| Configuration | Description | Example Aircraft | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional (Tailwheel / Tail-dragger)** | Two main gear forward + small tail wheel aft | Cessna 170, Piper J-3 Cub, Spitfire | Poor directional stability (ground-loop tendency) |
| **Tricycle (Nose-wheel)** | Two main gear aft + nose wheel forward | Boeing 737, Airbus A320, Cessna 172 | Excellent directional stability, prevents ground-loops |
| **Bicycle (Multi-bogie Tandem)** | Two main gear under fuselage, outboard outriggers | B-52, U-2, Concorde | Used for high-wing or carrier operations |

### 2.1.2 By Retractability

- **Fixed Gear:** Permanently extended; simpler, lighter, but high parasitic drag ($C_{D_0}$ penalty of ~0.015–0.025).
- **Retractable Gear:** Stowed in flight in wing or fuselage bays; reduces drag by 70–80%, but adds **weight (~3–5% of MTOW)**, complexity, and **failure modes** (e.g., "gear-up landing").

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Modern Tricycle Landing Gear

```
[Schematic Block — Single Main Gear Assembly]
        ┌──────────────┐
        │  Wheel & Tire │  ◄── Traction + Braking surface
        └──────┬───────┘
               │  (Axle)
        ┌──────┴───────┐
        │ Brake Disc /  │  ◄── Multi-disc carbon/steel heat sink
        │  Stack        │
        └──────┬───────┘
               │
        ┌──────┴───────┐
        │  Oleo Strut   │  ◄── Shock absorber (oil + N₂)
        │  (Upper Cyl.) │
        │      ▲        │
        │      │ Piston │  ◄── Telescopic sliding
        │      ▼        │
        │  (Lower Cyl.) │
        └──────┬───────┘
               │
        ┌──────┴───────┐
        │ Torque Links  │  ◄── Hinged scissors (anti-rotation)
        │  (Scissor)    │
        └──────┬───────┘
               │
        ┌──────┴───────┐
        │ Trunnion /    │  ◄── Retraction pivot
        │  Side Stay    │
        └──────┬───────┘
               │
        ┌──────┴───────┐
        │ Drag Brace /  │  ◄── Locking in extended position
        │  Down-lock    │
        └──────────────┘
```

---

## 2.3 The Oleo-Pneumatic Shock Absorber (Most Tested Sub-Topic)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

The oleo strut is a **two-stage energy-absorber** combining:

1. **Pneumatic Stage (Nitrogen gas):** Stores the vertical kinetic energy of landing as compressed gas potential energy, then returns it slowly. This is the **spring** of the system.
2. **Hydraulic Stage (Silicone/Mineral oil):** Converts the kinetic energy into heat through **orifice throttling** (forced oil flow through small holes). This is the **damper**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why two stages?**
> Pure spring (gas only) → bounces the aircraft back into the air (pogo effect).
> Pure damper (oil only) → aircraft sinks to the bottom of the stroke (bottoming).
> Combination → Smooth, controlled compression with rapid energy dissipation.

### 2.3.2 Working Cycle (Four Phases)

1. **Taxi/Static:** Piston at static equilibrium, gas at initial pressure $p_0$, oil fills upper chamber.
2. **Compression (Touchdown):** Piston moves up into cylinder. Nitrogen gas compresses (rising to peak pressure $p_{max}$). Oil is forced through **metering pin / orifice** from lower to upper chamber at controlled rate.
3. **Recoil (Rebound):** Gas pressure pushes piston back down. Oil flows through **rebound orifice** (smaller than compression orifice) to slow the rebound.
4. **Extension (Stroking):** Oil returns to lower chamber via main orifice; system returns to static position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The compression stroke dissipates ~**70–80%** of landing energy; the rebound stroke dissipates the remaining 20–30%. This **asymmetric damping** prevents oscillation.

---

## 2.4 Retraction & Actuation Systems

| Type | Principle | Aircraft Use | Pros / Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic** | High-pressure oil (2000–3000 psi) drives actuator | All transport aircraft | Reliable, fast, heavy |
| **Electro-mechanical** | Electric motor + gear train + screw jack | A380, some business jets | Lighter, redundant |
| **Mechanical (manual)** | Hand-cranked screw or cable | Light GA aircraft | Backup only |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** "Gear up" emergency extension is often **gravity drop** + aerodynamic force, assisted by mechanical uplocks released by a separate free-fall valve — not the main hydraulic system (which may be inoperative).

---

## 2.5 Steering & Braking

### 2.5.1 Nose-Wheel Steering
- **Tiller controlled** (cockpit, up to ±70° for taxi) and **rudder-pedal controlled** (limited to ±7° for takeoff/landing rollout).
- Modern fly-by-wire aircraft use **electro-hydraulic** or **electro-backup-hydrostatic** steering.

### 2.5.2 Brake Systems
- **Single Disc, Dual Disc, or Multi-Disc Stack** — carbon or steel heat-sink.
- Actuation: **Hydraulic** (most) or **Electric (E-Brake)** — used on Boeing 787 & A350.
- **Anti-Skid System:** Compares wheel deceleration to aircraft deceleration reference, modulates brake pressure to prevent wheel lock and tire flat-spotting.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 examinations. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{abs} = \tfrac{1}{2} m \cdot V_s^2$ | Kinetic energy to be absorbed at landing (per single gear) | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $V_s = 1.3 \cdot V_{sink}$ | Equivalent vertical speed (CS-23) | m/s |
| 3 | $W_{main} = W \cdot \dfrac{b}{c}$ | Static load on main gear (tricycle) | N |
| 4 | $W_{nose} = W \cdot \dfrac{a}{c}$ | Static load on nose gear (tricycle) | N |
| 5 | $E_{brake} = \tfrac{1}{2} m \cdot V_{TD}^2 \cdot \mu_{brake} \cdot \eta$ | Energy absorbed by brakes | J |
| 6 | $F_{brake} = \mu_b \cdot N_{wheel}$ | Maximum braking force per wheel | N |
| 7 | $P_{gas} \cdot V_{gas} = \text{const}$ (isothermal) | Gas law for nitrogen chamber | Pa·m³ |
| 8 | $n_{limit} = \dfrac{P_{gas,max}}{P_{gas,static}}$ | Load factor (gas pressure ratio) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F_{tip} = \dfrac{W \cdot b}{h \cdot \tan\theta}$ | Tip-back / tip-forward force (stability) | N |
| 10 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{E_{brake}}{m_{disc} \cdot c_p}$ | Brake disc temperature rise (adiabatic) | K or °C |
| 11 | $F_{fric} = \mu_{roll} \cdot W$ | Rolling friction force | N |
| 12 | $\eta_{retract} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{D,fixed} - \Delta C_{D,retracted}}{C_{D,fixed}}$ | Drag reduction efficiency | % |

**Where the variables are:**
- $m$ = Aircraft mass (kg); $W$ = weight (N); $V_s$ = vertical sink speed; $V_{TD}$ = touchdown ground speed
- $a$ = distance CG → nose wheel; $b$ = distance CG → main wheel; $c = a + b$ = wheelbase
- $\mu_b$ = brake friction coefficient; $\mu_{roll}$ = rolling resistance coefficient
- $h$ = height of CG above ground; $\theta$ = tip-back angle
- $V_{gas}$ = gas volume; $P_{gas}$ = gas pressure; $n$ = load factor

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Tables:** All absolute value bars, divisions, and fractions use $\vert$ / $\dfrac{}$ — **never the literal `|` symbol** — to preserve the markdown table.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where does this knowledge apply?**
> - **Aircraft Design Firms (HAL, Boeing, Airbus, Lockheed Martin):** Landing gear is sized first, then wing/fuselage geometry is built around it.
> - **Airlines & MROs:** Brake energy limits define landing weight restrictions on hot, high, or contaminated runways.
> - **Pilot Training:** Understanding energy absorption explains why hard landings damage gear; tailwheel handling requires rudder anticipation.
> - **Military/Parachute Drops:** Carrier-based aircraft (F/A-18) need reinforced gear and tailhooks; cargo drops from C-17 use computed touchdown sink rates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Static Load Distribution on Tricycle Gear

**Problem Setup:** An aircraft of total weight $W$ rests on the ground. Let:
- $a$ = horizontal distance from the **nose wheel** to the aircraft's **Center of Gravity (CG)**
- $b$ = horizontal distance from the **main wheel** (axle line) to the CG
- $c = a + b$ = **wheelbase**
- $N_n$ = vertical reaction at nose wheel
- $N_m$ = vertical reaction at main wheel (sum of both sides)

**Equilibrium Equations:**

Sum of vertical forces:
$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies N_n + N_m = W$$

Sum of moments about the main wheel (taking clockwise as positive, considering nose wheel reaction tending to rotate the aircraft forward about main wheel):
$$\sum M_{main} = 0$$

Taking moments about the main gear contact point:
$$N_n \cdot c = W \cdot b$$

Solving for the nose wheel reaction:
$$N_n = \frac{W \cdot b}{c}$$

By substitution into the vertical equilibrium:
$$N_m = W - N_n = W - \frac{W \cdot b}{c} = \frac{W(a + b) - W \cdot b}{c}$$

Simplifying:
$$N_m = \frac{W \cdot a}{c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (KTU Board-Standard Boxed Equations):**
> $$\boxed{W_{nose} = N_n = \frac{W \cdot b}{c} \quad \text{AND} \quad W_{main} = N_m = \frac{W \cdot a}{c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - If CG is **far aft** (large $b$), the **nose wheel bears more load** (tailwheel-converted aircraft at rest are CG-critical).
> - If CG is **far forward** (large $a$), the **main gear bears more load** (typical tricycle layout).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kinetic Energy Absorbed at Landing

**Given:** Aircraft of mass $m$ lands with a vertical sink speed $V_s$ and a horizontal touchdown speed $V_{TD}$. For a single main gear strut:

**Total kinetic energy to be absorbed (per gear pair, by symmetry):**
$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2} m \cdot V_s^2 + \frac{1}{2} m \cdot V_{TD}^2 \cdot (1 - \mu_{roll}^2) - E_{brake}$$

Since the horizontal kinetic energy is mostly absorbed by **brakes and aerodynamic drag**, and the **vertical energy** is what the **shock absorber handles**, we focus on the vertical component per gear:

**Vertical kinetic energy per gear (assuming 2 main gears sharing the load):**
$$E_{abs} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{m}{2} \cdot V_s^2 = \frac{m \cdot V_s^2}{4}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given:
- $m = 10{,}000$ kg (aircraft mass)
- $V_s = 3.0$ m/s (maximum sink speed)
- Two main gears

**Step 1 — Total vertical KE:**
$$E_{total,vert} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 10000 \cdot (3.0)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 10000 \cdot 9 = 45{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Energy per main gear:**
$$E_{per,main} = \frac{45{,}000}{2} = 22{,}500 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Energy per oleo strut (if gear pair has 2 struts):**
$$E_{per,strut} = \frac{22{,}500}{2} = 11{,}250 \text{ J}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** This value (~11 kJ per strut) is realistic for a regional aircraft. A Boeing 737 main gear absorbs ~80–100 kJ per strut; an A380 absorbs ~250 kJ per strut.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Oleo Strut Gas Pressure and Stroke

The strut has:
- Inner cylinder volume $V_1$ (gas at static pressure $p_1$)
- Stroke length $S$
- Piston rod area $A_p$
- Main orifice area $A_o$

**Boyle's Law (isothermal — slow landing compression):**
$$p_1 \cdot V_1 = p_2 \cdot V_2$$

If piston moves up by distance $x$:
$$V_2 = V_1 - A_p \cdot x$$

Therefore:
$$p_2 = \frac{p_1 \cdot V_1}{V_1 - A_p \cdot x}$$

**Load factor on the aircraft at stroke $x$:**
$$n = \frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{V_1}{V_1 - A_p \cdot x}$$

**At maximum stroke $x = S$ (full compression):**
$$n_{max} = \frac{V_1}{V_1 - A_p \cdot S} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{A_p \cdot S}{V_1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** "Why is nitrogen used instead of air in the oleo strut?"
> - **Answer:** Air contains oxygen → under high pressure, O₂ + oil vapor could cause **explosive combustion**. Nitrogen is **inert**, **dry** (no moisture to corrode), and provides **predictable** gas law behavior.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tip-Back / Tip-Forward Stability Angle

When brakes are applied or thrust is reversed, the inertia of the aircraft produces a moment about the main wheels that tends to **rotate the aircraft forward (nose-down)** or **backward (nose-up, tip-back)**.

**Tip-Back Force Analysis:**

Take moments about the **main wheel contact point** at the moment of maximum braking.

- Inertia force $F_i = m \cdot a$ (acting at CG, height $h$)
- Weight $W$ acting at CG
- Reaction $N_m$ at main wheel

For tip-back condition (rear CG, nose-wheel lifts off):
$$F_i \cdot h = W \cdot b$$

Substituting $F_i = m \cdot a = \dfrac{W}{g} \cdot a$:
$$\frac{W}{g} \cdot a \cdot h = W \cdot b$$

Solving for critical deceleration:
$$a_{crit} = \frac{b \cdot g}{h}$$

**Tip-Back Angle:**
$$\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{b}{h}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Students often use $a$ (nose-wheel distance) and $b$ (main-wheel distance) **interchangeably**. The correct moment arm for **tip-back** is $b$ (CG to main wheel); for **tip-forward** (nose-down), it is $a$ (CG to nose wheel).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Brake Energy & Temperature Rise

**Worked Example — Brake Energy Calculation:**

A 5,000 kg aircraft touches down at $V_{TD} = 60$ m/s. Brakes absorb **80%** of the kinetic energy over the rollout. Each main wheel has a brake disc of mass $m_d = 8$ kg and specific heat $c_p = 500$ J/(kg·K).

**Step 1 — Total kinetic energy:**
$$E_{KE} = \frac{1}{2} m V_{TD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 5000 \cdot (60)^2 = 9{,}000{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Energy to be absorbed by brakes:**
$$E_{brake} = 0.80 \cdot 9{,}000{,}000 = 7{,}200{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Energy per brake (assuming 4 main wheels):**
$$E_{per} = \frac{7{,}200{,}000}{4} = 1{,}800{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Temperature rise (adiabatic assumption, no heat loss):**
$$\Delta T = \frac{E_{per}}{m_d \cdot c_p} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{8 \cdot 500} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{4{,}000} = 450 \text{ °C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 450 °C temperature rise in a single stop is severe but within the **transient capability** of carbon brakes (operating up to ~1000 °C). This is why **delayed cooling**, **rejected take-off (RTO)**, and **brake energy limitations** are critical pilot decisions.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Landing Gear Type?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Question Pattern:** "Compare and contrast the conventional and tricycle landing gear arrangements."

| Design Parameter | Tailwheel (Conventional) | Tricycle (Nose-wheel) |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward visibility on ground** | Poor (nose high) | Excellent (nose low) |
| **Directional stability** | Poor (tendency to ground-loop) | Excellent |
| **Crosswind handling** | Difficult | Easier |
| **Brake effectiveness** | Limited (nose can pitch over) | High (level attitude) |
| **Stall/spin on takeoff** | High risk (tail-low attitude) | Lower risk |
| **Wheel size & weight** | Smaller main wheels | Larger nose wheel needed |
| **Manufacturing cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Used on** | Vintage, military trainers, aerobatic | Modern commercial, business jets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Tricycle gear is the **standard for 95%+ of modern aircraft** because of superior ground handling. Tailwheel aircraft are now specialized (agricultural, vintage, military trainers).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Landing Gear Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT[External Environment]
        RUNWAY[Runway Surface<br/>Concrete Asphalt]
        AIRFLOW[Airflow in Flight]
    end

    subgraph LG[Landing Gear Subsystem]
        TIRE[Wheel and Tire<br/>Pneumatic Contact]
        BRAKE[Brake Assembly<br/>Multi Disc Carbon Steel]
        STRUT[Oleo Pneumatic Strut<br/>Oil plus Nitrogen]
        TORQUE[Torque Link Scissor<br/>Anti Rotation]
        TRUNNION[Trunnion<br/>Retraction Pivot]
        RETRACT[Retraction Actuator<br/>Hydraulic 3000 psi]
        STEER[Nose Wheel Steering<br/>Tiller and Rudder Pedal]
        LOCK[Uplock Downlock<br/>Mechanical Safety]
    end

    subgraph AF[Aircraft Airframe]
        WING[Wing and Fuselage<br/>Load Bearing Structure]
        CG[Center of Gravity<br/>Weight Distribution]
    end

    RUNWAY --> TIRE
    AIRFLOW -.->|Drag Reduction| RETRACT
    TIRE --> BRAKE
    BRAKE --> STRUT
    STRUT --> TORQUE
    TORQUE --> TRUNNION
    TRUNNION --> RETRACT
    RETRACT --> LOCK
    STEER --> TIRE
    STRUT --> WING
    LOCK --> WING
    WING --> CG
```

---

```mermaid
    PISTON -->|Rebound Stroke| OCL
    OCL -->|Returns to Static| TOUCH

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

> **Q1.** Define the term "landing gear" and list **any four** functional requirements of a typical aircraft landing gear.

**Model Answer:**

Landing gear is the undercarriage assembly of an aircraft which supports it on the ground, absorbs landing and taxiing loads, enables steering, and provides braking action. The four functional requirements are:

1. **Load Support:** To bear the static and dynamic weight of the aircraft (MTOW) at rest and during operation.
2. **Shock Absorption:** To absorb and dissipate the vertical kinetic energy at landing using a shock-strut system.
3. **Braking:** To decelerate the aircraft during ground rollout and rejected take-off.
4. **Steering & Control:** To provide directional control on the ground, primarily via the nose wheel.

*(Plus, examiners accept: Retraction for drag reduction, Towing capability, Static ground stability.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Question]**

> **Q2.** Differentiate between a **fixed** and a **retractable** landing gear. Mention **one advantage** and **one disadvantage** of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fixed Landing Gear | Retractable Landing Gear |
|---|---|---|
| **Position** | Permanently extended | Stowed in wheel-bay in flight |
| **Drag** | High parasitic drag | Low drag (~80% reduction) |
| **Weight** | Lighter (no actuator) | Heavier (actuator + bay structure) |
| **Reliability** | Very high | Lower (mechanical failure risk) |
| **Advantage** | Simplicity, low maintenance | Higher cruise speed, lower fuel burn |
| **Disadvantage** | Lower top speed, higher fuel consumption | Complex, expensive, "gear-up" risk |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and must have an internal choice. Both choices A and B are provided below for parallel practice.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester Examination]**

> **Q A.** *(a)* Explain the **construction and working principle of an oleo-pneumatic shock absorber** used in aircraft landing gear with a neat schematic. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* An aircraft of mass **12,000 kg** touches down with an equivalent vertical sink speed of **3.0 m/s**. If the energy is equally shared by **two main gear units**, calculate the kinetic energy to be absorbed **per oleo strut** (assume 2 struts per main gear). *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Q A (a) — Oleo Strut Construction & Working (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
An oleo-pneumatic shock absorber consists of:
- **Upper Cylinder (Pressure Cylinder):** Sealed chamber containing compressed **nitrogen gas** at the top.
- **Lower Cylinder (Sliding Piston):** A telescopic piston rod sliding into the upper cylinder.
- **Main Metering Orifice & Metering Pin:** Variable-area opening for oil flow control.
- **Rebound Orifice:** Smaller orifice for rebound damping.
- **Torque Link / Scissor Link:** Two hinged arms to prevent rotation of the piston.
- **Annular Space:** Oil-filled region between piston and cylinder walls.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- On **touchdown**, the piston is forced up into the upper cylinder.
- Nitrogen gas compresses, storing energy as **pneumatic potential energy** (spring effect).
- Oil is forced through the **main orifice** from the lower chamber to the upper chamber, dissipating kinetic energy as **heat** (damper effect).
- During **rebound**, the compressed gas pushes the piston down, and oil flows back via the smaller **rebound orifice** to slow extension.
- The system is **asymmetrically damped**: ~70–80% energy absorbed in compression, ~20–30% in rebound, ensuring rapid stabilization.

#### Solution to Q A (b) — Kinetic Energy Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 12{,}000$ kg
- Sink speed $V_s = 3.0$ m/s
- Two main gear units; 2 struts per main gear → 4 struts total

**Step 1 — Total Vertical KE:**
$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2} m V_s^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 12000 \cdot (3.0)^2$$

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 12000 \cdot 9.0 = 54{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — KE per main gear (2 main gears, equal share):**
$$E_{main} = \frac{54{,}000}{2} = 27{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — KE per oleo strut (2 struts per main gear):**
$$E_{strut} = \frac{27{,}000}{2} = 13{,}500 \text{ J}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** **$E_{strut} = 13{,}500$ J = 13.5 kJ per oleo strut.**

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Examination]**

> **Q B.** *(a)* With a neat sketch, explain the **tricycle landing gear configuration** and compare it with the **conventional (tailwheel) configuration** on **at least four parameters**. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A tricycle-gear aircraft has a CG located **2.5 m aft of the nose wheel** and **1.5 m aft of the main wheel** (wheelbase = 4.0 m). If the total aircraft weight is **50,000 N**, calculate the static reactions at the **nose wheel** and **main wheel**. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Q B (a) — Tricycle vs. Conventional (7 marks)

**Tricycle Configuration (3 marks):**

A tricycle landing gear consists of **two main wheels** located aft of the center of gravity, and **one nose wheel** (or two nose wheels) located forward of the CG. The aircraft sits in a **level, nose-up** attitude on the ground. This configuration is standard on virtually all modern commercial and general aviation aircraft.

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Tricycle Gear | Conventional (Tailwheel) |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward visibility on ground** | Excellent (nose low) | Poor (nose high) |
| **Directional stability** | Excellent (CG ahead of main wheels prevents ground-loops) | Poor (tendency to ground-loop in crosswinds) |
| **Crosswind landing** | Easier | More difficult; requires precise rudder |
| **Braking efficiency** | High (level attitude, no nose-over risk) | Limited (tail-low risks nose-over) |
| **Application** | Modern commercial, business jets | Vintage, aerobatic, agricultural |

#### Solution to Q B (b) — Static Reaction Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $a = 2.5$ m (nose wheel to CG)
- $b = 1.5$ m (main wheel to CG)
- $c = a + b = 4.0$ m (wheelbase)
- $W = 50{,}000$ N

**Step 1 — Nose wheel reaction:**
$$W_{nose} = \frac{W \cdot b}{c} = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot 1.5}{4.0}$$

$$W_{nose} = \frac{75{,}000}{4.0} = 18{,}750 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Main wheel reaction (per main gear pair):**
$$W_{main} = \frac{W \cdot a}{c} = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot 2.5}{4.0}$$

$$W_{main} = \frac{125{,}000}{4.0} = 31{,}250 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Verification (sum must equal $W$):**
$$W_{nose} + W_{main} = 18{,}750 + 31{,}250 = 50{,}000 \text{ N} \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:**
> - **$W_{nose} = 18{,}750$ N** (37.5% of total weight)
> - **$W_{main} = 31{,}250$ N** (62.5% of total weight)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Marks Deduction (Valuation Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Confusing $a$ and $b$:** In moment-arm equations, students frequently swap nose-wheel and main-wheel distances. **Remember:** the reaction is inversely proportional — the **far** wheel carries **less** load.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by 2:** In energy problems with multiple gears or struts, students compute total KE and stop. **Always explicitly state** the per-gear or per-strut division.
> 3. **Units Error:** Sink speed must be in m/s (not km/h), mass in kg. Energy is in **Joules (J)**, not kilojoules unless specifically stated.
> 4. **Oleo Strut Direction:** Saying "the piston moves down on landing" — the piston **moves UP** (into the cylinder) on compression.
> 5. **Static vs. Dynamic Load:** Static load formulas are for **parked** aircraft. During taxi/landing, apply **load factor** ($n \cdot W$, where $n = 2.5$–$3.0$).
> 6. **Nitrogen Justification:** Many students write "air is also used" — wrong. Nitrogen is mandatory to prevent **combustion** and **corrosion**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read Before Exam:**

- **Landing Gear Functions (6):** Support, energy absorption, braking, steering, retraction, towing.
- **Configurations:** Conventional (tailwheel), Tricycle (nosewheel), Bicycle.
- **Tricycle is the modern standard** due to **superior directional stability** and **visibility**.
- **Oleo Strut = Oil + Nitrogen** (pneumatic spring + hydraulic damper).
- **Energy split:** ~70–80% in compression, ~20–30% in rebound — **asymmetric damping**.
- **Why nitrogen?** Inert (no combustion), dry (no corrosion), predictable gas behavior.
- **Static Load Formulas:**
    - $W_{nose} = \dfrac{W \cdot b}{c}$
    - $W_{main} = \dfrac{W \cdot a}{c}$
- **Energy Formula:** $E_{abs} = \dfrac{1}{2} m V_s^2$ (per gear after distribution).
- **Sink Speed Limits:** $V_s = 1.5$–$3.0$ m/s (tricycle), $V_s = 2.0$–$3.5$ m/s (tailwheel).
- **Brake Energy:** $E_{brake} = \tfrac{1}{2} m V_{TD}^2 \cdot \mu_{brake} \cdot \eta$.
- **Tip-Back Critical Deceleration:** $a_{crit} = \dfrac{b \cdot g}{h}$.
- **Retraction Time:** 8–12 seconds (transport aircraft).
- **Brake Disc Material:** Carbon composite (up to 1000 °C) for modern jets; steel for GA.
- **Anti-Skid:** Compares wheel deceleration to reference; releases brake on impending lock.
- **Tire Pressure:** 1.0–1.5 MPa (commercial); low-pressure tires for soft runway operations.
- **Tip-Back Angle:** $\theta = \tan^{-1}(b/h)$.
- **Gasper's Law (Strut):** $p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_2$ (isothermal).
- **Torque Link (Scissor):** Prevents piston **rotation** while allowing linear **telescoping**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engines Attachments, Components of Wing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Engines Attachments, Components of Wing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Engine Attachments (Engine Mounting Systems)

**Formal Definition:**
Engine attachments, commonly called the **engine mounting system** or **powerplant installation**, refer to the structural and aerodynamic assembly that physically secures the aircraft engine to the airframe, transmits thrust loads to the aircraft structure, isolates vibration, provides cooling airflow, and houses the auxiliary accessories required for engine operation.

The system includes the **engine mount, cowling/nacelle, firewall, thrust mounts, spinner, baffles, and accessory drives**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly differentiate between (a) **Nose-mounted installations** (tractor/ tractor-pusher piston engines) and (b) **Wing-mounted (pylon) installations** used in multi-engine aircraft and modern high-bypass turbofans.

**Real-World Analogy — Intuition:**
Think of the engine attachment as the **"engineer's chair with shock absorbers"** at a construction site. The chair (engine mount) holds the heavy tool (engine) in place, the rubber cushions (rubber shock mounts) absorb the shaking (vibration), the protective shell (cowling) keeps debris and rain out, and the firewall (a metal heat shield) protects the operator (pilot) from fire spreading from the tool.

In the same way, an aircraft engine is held by a **rigid steel-tube engine mount**, isolated by **shock mounts**, wrapped in an **aerodynamic cowling/nacelle**, separated from the cabin by a **firewall**, and connected to the propeller via a **spinner**.

**Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics:**

- **FAA Fire Test Standard:** Firewall must withstand **2000 °F (1093 °C)** for at least 15 minutes per FAR 23.1187 / CS-VLA regulations.
- **Engine Mount Material:** Typically **4130 chrome-molybdenum steel tubing** (chrome moly), chosen for its high strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Vibration Isolation Frequency:** Engine mount shock absorbers typically isolate frequencies above **20 Hz** to prevent structural fatigue and pilot discomfort.
- **Nacelle Maximum Diameter (Rule of Thumb):** Nacelle diameter should not exceed **~25–30% of the wing chord** to minimize aerodynamic drag penalty.

### 1.2 Components of Wing (Wing Structure)

**Formal Definition:**
The wing is the principal **lift-generating airfoil-shaped surface** of an aircraft, and its **components of wing** refer to the integrated structural members, aerodynamic surfaces, and control devices that make up the complete wing assembly. These include the **spars, ribs, stringers, skin, leading edge, trailing edge, wing tips, ailerons, flaps, spoilers, and tip tanks/wing-tip extensions** where applicable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Distinction for KTU Examinations:** The wing is a **load-bearing structure** (carries lift, drag, bending, torsion) and a **fuel reservoir** (integral fuel tanks between spars) and a **control surface platform** (ailerons, flaps). Examiners frequently ask students to list "structural" vs "aerodynamic" components separately.

**Real-World Analogy — Intuition:**
Imagine a **bird's wing**: The bones inside the wing (spars) are the main "I-beams" that carry bending loads — like the girders of a building. The thin bones branching out (ribs) hold the shape of the wing, just like the wooden ribs of a paper kite hold the paper's curvature. The skin (membrane/sheet metal) is the smooth surface over which air flows. The feathers at the trailing edge (ailerons/flaps) tilt up or down to control the bird's roll and lift. The leading edge is the "knife edge" that cuts through the air cleanly.

In engineering terms, the wing is a **semi-monocoque (stressed-skin) structure** — the skin carries a portion of the load, but the spars are the primary load-bearers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Focus:** The course specifically requires understanding of (a) **Main Spar and Auxiliary Spar**, (b) **Compression Ribs vs Former Ribs**, (c) **Ailerons and Flaps — function and difference**, and (d) **Wing-Fuselage Attachment methods** (three-point, two-point).

---

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot for Quick Recall

| Subsystem | Type of Mounting | Typical Aircraft Example | Key Component |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nose-Engine (Tractor)** | 4-point steel-tube engine mount behind firewall | Cessna 172, Piper PA-28 | Engine mount + Cowling |
| **Wing-Mounted (Pylon)** | Pylon bolted to wing spar | Beechcraft Baron 58, PA-44 | Nacelle + Pylon |
| **Aft-Fuselage (Pusher)** | 4-point mount ahead of vertical tail | Piper PA-24 Comanche (rare) | Engine mount + Extension shaft |
| **Wing-Tip (Tip-Mounted)** | Cantilever pylon at wingtip | Some WWII aircraft (rare today) | Tip pylon |

---

### 1.4 Visual Functional Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Engine Mount — Detailed Analysis

The **engine mount** is a structural framework, usually made of welded **4130 chrome-molybdenum steel tubing**, that rigidly holds the engine to the airframe. It must:
- Transfer **thrust, torque, weight, and gyroscopic loads** to the airframe
- Isolate **engine vibration** from the fuselage
- Provide **easy access for maintenance**

**Why the Engine Mount is Critical (KTU 14-Mark Favourite):**
The engine produces four major loads:
1. **Thrust load (T)** — forward, along the propeller axis.
2. **Torque reaction** — backward (equal & opposite to engine torque, ~10% of thrust in single-engine).
3. **Weight (W)** — downward (engine + propeller + accessories).
4. **Gyroscopic & Inertia loads** — side-to-side and vertical during maneuvers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why chrome-moly (4130)?** It has a tensile strength of roughly **560 MPa (vs 250 MPa for mild steel)**, yet is only marginally heavier. This gives the highest strength-to-weight ratio for welded steel tubular structures used in light aircraft.

#### 2.1.1 Shock Mounts (Vibration Isolators)
Between the engine mount and the airframe, **elastomeric shock mounts** (natural rubber, neoprene, or silicone) are placed to absorb high-frequency vibration. KTU-defining formula:

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}
$$

where $f_n$ is the natural frequency, $k$ is the spring stiffness (N/m), and $m$ is the mass (kg). The mount is designed such that $f_n$ is **well below** the engine's dominant excitation frequency (e.g., a 4-cylinder engine at 2400 RPM has 40 Hz firing frequency; mount $f_n$ should be $\le$ 20 Hz).

### 2.2 Cowling (Engine Cowling / Nacelle)

The **cowling** is the streamlined, removable metal cover that encloses the engine. Two major functions:
1. **Aerodynamic** — reduces drag, smooths airflow into and around the engine.
2. **Cooling** — directs airflow over the cylinders and out through the cowl flaps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charles Lawther's Cooling Rule:** The cowling is designed to direct cooling air through the engine at a pressure differential of about **ΔP = 0.5 to 1.5 inches of water (≈ 125–375 Pa)** to extract roughly **75% of the total heat of combustion** (which equals fuel energy minus propulsive + exhaust energy).

#### 2.2.1 Cowl Flaps
**Cowl flaps** are small adjustable panels on the lower trailing edge of the cowling. They control the cooling airflow:
- **Open** in takeoff/climb or on hot days (extra cooling).
- **Closed** in cruise to reduce drag and keep the engine warm.

### 2.3 Firewall
A **fire-resistant steel or titanium bulkhead** separating the engine compartment from the cockpit/cabin. It must resist:
- Fire from engine fuel/oil leaks
- Heat from engine bay
- Provide attachment points for engine mount (rear side) and cockpit instruments (front side)

**Standard Requirement:** **FAA FAR 23.1187** specifies firewall must withstand **2000 °F (1093 °C)** for **15 minutes**.

### 2.4 Spinner
The **spinner** is the streamlined cone (often aluminum, sometimes composite) mounted on the propeller hub. It:
- Reduces **propeller hub drag**
- Smooths airflow into the engine
- Houses electrical/ice-detection sensors in some aircraft

### 2.5 Pylons (Wing-Mounted Engines)
A **pylon** is a streamlined structural strut/box that connects a wing-mounted engine to the wing structure. Pylons carry:
- Thrust, weight, side, and torsion loads into the **front and rear wing spars**.
- The pylon shape is airfoil-symmetric to minimize drag.

### 2.6 Components of Wing — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.6.1 Spars (Main Load Carriers)
**Spars** are the principal longitudinal structural members of the wing, running from the root to the tip. They carry:
- **Bending loads** (lift pulls wing up, weight of fuselage pulls down → spar acts like a cantilever beam).
- **Torsion loads** (from aileron/flap deflection and asymmetric loading).

Typical spar types:
- **I-beam spar** — built-up aluminum (extruded) for high bending stiffness.
- **Web + Cap strip** — common in light aircraft, with a thin web (loaded in shear) and cap strips (loaded in compression/tension).

The wing is modeled as a **cantilever beam** fixed at the fuselage. The bending moment at the root is:

$$
M_{root} = W \cdot \frac{L}{2}
$$

where $W$ is the total lift and $L$ is the wing semi-span. (This is a simplified uniformly-distributed-load model used in KTU problems.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In commercial aircraft, spars are the **largest single components of the wing**. The Boeing 787 wing spar is a one-piece carbon-fiber bar over **30 m long**.

#### 2.6.2 Ribs
**Ribs** are transverse structural members that:
- Maintain the **airfoil shape** of the wing.
- Transfer **air loads** from the skin to the spars.
- Provide **mounting points** for ailerons, flaps, and landing gear.

**Types of Ribs (KTU Important):**
- **Compression Rib** — carries compressive chordwise loads (especially in front of the main spar).
- **Former Rib (Contour Rib)** — only maintains the airfoil shape, no significant loads.
- **Auxiliary Rib** — supports flaps/ailerons and landing gear attachments.
- **Fuel Bay Rib** — sealed ribs that form integral fuel tanks.

#### 2.6.3 Stringers (Longitudinal Stiffeners)
**Stringers** are small longitudinal members attached to the ribs, running spanwise. They:
- Stiffen the skin against **buckling** under compression.
- Carry small axial (spanwise) loads.
- Together with the skin, form the **stringer-skin panel** typical of semi-monocoque construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** The "Stringer-Skin Panel" is a primary example of **semi-monocoque construction** — the skin and stringers together resist bending, while the spars and ribs resist shear. The classic **Boeing 737 wing leading edge** is built this way.

#### 2.6.4 Skin
The **skin** is the outer surface of the wing. It is typically:
- **Aluminum alloy** (e.g., 2024-T3, 7075-T6) for general aviation
- **Composite** (CFRP — Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer) for modern jets (e.g., Boeing 787, Airbus A350)

The skin is **stressed-skin** — it carries a portion of the bending and torsion loads, unlike an unstressed fabric covering.

#### 2.6.5 Aerodynamic Components

| Component | Function | Location | Common Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Leading Edge** | Cuts through air; houses pitot tubes, de-icers | Front of wing | Fixed in GA; movable in modern jets (LE slats) |
| **Trailing Edge** | Houses flaps, ailerons, elevators etc. | Rear of wing | Mostly movable |
| **Aileron** | Roll control (differential deflection) | Outer trailing edge | Frise, Plain, Spoileron |
| **Flap** | Increases lift and drag at low speed | Inner trailing edge | Plain, Split, Slotted, Fowler, Double-Slotted |
| **Spoiler** | Reduces lift, increases drag, assists roll | Upper wing surface mid-chord | Plain, Ground spoiler |
| **Wing Tip / Tip Tank** | Reduces wingtip vortex; extends range | Outboard end of wing | Fixed, tip tank, winglet, sharklet |
| **Slats** | Increases lift at high AoA | Leading edge | Fixed slat, movable slat |

#### 2.6.6 Ailerons — Roll Control
**Ailerons** are the primary **roll-control** surfaces, located on the **outer trailing edge** of each wing. They work differentially: the right aileron deflects up while the left deflects down (and vice versa), creating a rolling moment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yaw-Side Effect (Adverse Yaw):** When an aileron deflects down (increases lift), the wing also experiences more induced drag. This causes the aircraft to yaw **opposite** to the roll direction. Modern aircraft use **differential aileron deflection** or **aileron-rudder interconnect** to mitigate this.

#### 2.6.7 Flaps — Lift/Drag Augmentation
**Flaps** are high-lift devices located on the **inner trailing edge** of the wing. They:
- Increase **camber** → increase maximum lift coefficient ($C_{L_{max}}$)
- Increase **drag** → allows steeper approach angles
- Permit **lower approach speeds** → safer landings

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Flap Theory (KTU Formula):** The increase in $C_{L_{max}}$ is approximated by:
$$
\Delta C_{L_{max}} \approx 0.9 \cdot \frac{S_{flap}}{S_{wing}} \cdot \delta_{f}
$$
> where $S_{flap}$ is flap area, $S_{wing}$ is wing area, and $\delta_{f}$ is flap deflection angle in radians. (Approximation; see Hoerner's "Aerodynamics of Wings and Airfoils".)

#### 2.6.8 Wing-Fuselage Attachment
Wing-fuselage attachment is critical because the wing must transmit **bending, shear, and torsion** to the fuselage. The two main configurations are:

1. **Two-Point Attachment:** Used in light aircraft (Cessna 150/152). Two bolts (front and rear) carry shear; bending moment is reacted by a "carry-through" spar inside the fuselage.
2. **Three-Point Attachment:** Used in cantilever-wing aircraft (Piper Cherokee). Two **front lugs** carry bending, one **rear lug** carries shear and torsion.

---

| 1 | Engine Mount Natural Freq. $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $f_n$=natural freq., $k$=stiffness, $m$=mass | Vibration isolation design | Hz |
| 2 | Bending Moment at Wing Root $M_{root} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2}$ (UDL) | $W$=lift, $L$=semi-span | Wing spar sizing | N·m |
| 3 | Lift Equation $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$ | $\rho$=air density, $V$=velocity, $S$=wing area, $C_L$=lift coefficient | Lift generation | N |

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Cantilever Wing Spar Bending Stress

**Problem Context:** A light aircraft wing with semi-span $L = 5$ m, total lift distributed uniformly $W = 6000$ N. The main spar is a rectangular tube with width $b = 50$ mm, height $h = 120$ mm. Calculate the maximum bending stress at the root.

**Step 1 — Identify the Loading Model**
The wing is treated as a **cantilever beam** fixed at the fuselage root, with a uniformly distributed load (lift) $w$ over the semi-span $L$.

$$
w = \frac{W}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Distributed Load**
Substitute $W = 6000$ N and $L = 5$ m:

$$
w = \frac{6000\,\text{N}}{5\,\text{m}} = 1200\,\text{N/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Bending Moment Distribution**
For a cantilever beam with uniform load, the bending moment at distance $x$ from the root is:

$$
M(x) = \frac{w \cdot (L - x)^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Maximum Bending Moment at the Root**
At $x = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{root} &= \frac{w \cdot L^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{1200 \times 5^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{1200 \times 25}{2} \\
&= \frac{30000}{2} \\
&= 15000\,\text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is consistent with the simplified formula $M_{root} = \frac{W L}{2} = \frac{6000 \times 5}{2} = 15000$ N·m. ✓

**Step 5 — Compute the Second Moment of Area of the Rectangular Spar**
For a hollow rectangular tube (outer width $b_o = 50$ mm, outer height $h_o = 120$ mm; inner width $b_i = 40$ mm, inner height $h_i = 110$ mm):

$$
I = \frac{b_o h_o^3 - b_i h_i^3}{12}
$$

Substitute the values (in mm, will convert to m at the end):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{50 \times 120^3 - 40 \times 110^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 1728000 - 40 \times 1331000}{12} \\
&= \frac{86400000 - 53240000}{12} \\
&= \frac{33160000}{12} \\
&= 2.763 \times 10^6\,\text{mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to m⁴:

$$
I = 2.763 \times 10^6 \times (10^{-3})^4 = 2.763 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^4
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Maximum Distance from Neutral Axis**

$$
y_{max} = \frac{h_o}{2} = \frac{120\,\text{mm}}{2} = 60\,\text{mm} = 0.06\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Bending Equation**
The flexure formula:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{root} \cdot y_{max}}{I}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{15000 \times 0.06}{2.763 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{900}{2.763 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 3.257 \times 10^8\,\text{Pa} \\
&\approx 325.7\,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This value is **below the yield strength of 2024-T3 aluminum (~345 MPa)**, so the spar is safe with a margin of about **6%**. A real design would have a higher safety factor (~1.5), suggesting the spar should be thickened.

### 3.2 Derivation: Engine Mount Vibration Natural Frequency

**Problem:** An engine + mount assembly has effective mass $m = 120$ kg, and four shock mounts each with stiffness $k = 5 \times 10^4$ N/m in parallel. Compute the natural frequency.

**Step 1 — Total Stiffness of Parallel Mounts**

$$
k_{total} = 4 \times k = 4 \times 5 \times 10^4 = 2 \times 10^5\,\text{N/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Natural Frequency Formula**

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{total}}{m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_n &= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{120}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{1666.67} \\
&= \frac{40.82}{6.283} \\
&\approx 6.5\,\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret**
A typical 4-cylinder piston engine at 2400 RPM has a dominant firing frequency of $f = \frac{2400 \times 4}{120} = 80$ Hz. The mount's $f_n = 6.5$ Hz is well below 80 Hz, so **vibration isolation is effective** (since transmissibility drops sharply when excitation >> $f_n$).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Load-Carrying Members of a Wing

| Structural Member | Primary Load | Type of Stress | Critical Section | Material Typical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Main Spar (Cap)** | Bending | Tension (bottom) & Compression (top) | Wing root (max moment) | 2024-T3, 7075-T6 Al |
| **Spar Web** | Shear, Torsion | Shear | Wing root (max shear) | Al alloy sheet |
| **Compression Rib** | Chordwise compression | Buckling, Compression | Front of main spar | Al alloy |
| **Forme Rib** | Air-pressure shape | Bending (small) | Throughout | Al alloy |
| **Stringer** | Axial (spanwise) | Tension/Compression | Wing root | Al alloy extrusions |
| **Skin** | Aerodynamic + torsion | Shear, Biaxial | Throughout | Al alloy 2024-T3 |
| **Aileron Hinge** | Inertia + aerodynamic | Shear at hinge pin | Hinge location | Steel/Composite |
| **Flap Track** | Flap loads | Bending, Shear | Inner wing | Steel/Al |
| **Wing-Fuselage Attach** | Bending, Shear, Torsion | Mixed | All three lugs | High-strength steel |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Analysis: Aileron Deflection Effect

**Setup:** A light aircraft with wing area $S = 16$ m², air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, velocity $V = 60$ m/s, lift coefficient $C_L = 0.4$, aileron deflection $\delta_a = 15°$ (one wing up, one wing down).

**Step 1 — Compute Lift**

$$
L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 \times 16 \times 0.4
$$

$$
L = 14112\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Aileron Effectiveness Formula (simplified)**

$$
\Delta C_L \approx C_{L_{\delta_a}} \cdot \delta_a
$$

where $C_{L_{\delta_a}}$ is the aileron control derivative (typical value for GA aircraft: $C_{L_{\delta_a}} \approx 0.5$ per radian). Convert $15°$ to radians:

$$
\delta_a = 15° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.262\,\text{rad}
$$

So:

$$
\Delta C_L \approx 0.5 \times 0.262 = 0.131
$$

**Step 3 — Differential Lift**

$$
\Delta L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \cdot \Delta C_L = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 \times 16 \times 0.131
$$

$$
\Delta L = 4622\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Rolling Moment**
The rolling moment is approximately (with $b$ = wing span, e.g., $b = 10$ m):

$$
L_{roll} \approx \Delta L \cdot \frac{b}{2} = 4622 \times 5 = 23110\,\text{N·m}
$$

> This is enough rolling moment to bank a light aircraft in roughly 2-3 seconds.
