# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define monocoque construction. How does it differ from semi-monocoque construction?**

**Model Answer:**

> Monocoque is a structural form in which the external skin of the aircraft carries the entire structural load (bending, torsion, shear, axial) without any internal load-bearing framework. The word is derived from French *mono* (single) and *coque* (shell). True monocoque is rarely used because thin skins buckle easily under compression.

> Semi-monocoque (also called stressed-skin construction) is a modified form in which the skin is reinforced by internal longitudinal members called longerons and stringers, together with transverse members called frames and bulkheads. The skin and the stiffeners share the load, making the structure far more damage-tolerant and buckling-resistant.

> Hence, **monocoque = skin only**, while **semi-monocoque = skin + longerons + stringers + frames + bulkheads**.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**List the advantages and disadvantages of monocoque construction in aircraft.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Low weight-to-strength ratio** — saves 20–30 % structural weight compared to truss/frame designs.
2. **Smooth external surface** — improves aerodynamic efficiency and reduces drag.
3. **Greater usable internal volume** — no internal framework to obstruct payload or equipment.
4. **High fatigue life** when properly designed — modern jets accumulate tens of thousands of flight hours.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **High manufacturing and repair cost** — damaged skin panels often require specialised repair stations.
2. **Poor damage tolerance** — a small dent or crack can drastically reduce residual strength (especially in true monocoque).
3. **Difficult to inspect** — internal corrosion or hidden damage is hard to detect.
4. **Sensitive to concentrated loads and cut-outs** — windows, doors, antennae openings require local reinforcement (doublers).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the constructional features of a **semi-monocoque fuselage**. Discuss the role of longerons, stringers, frames, and bulkheads. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A semi-monocoque fuselage beam has a circular cross-section of mean radius $R = 1.2$ m, skin thickness $t = 2$ mm. It is subjected to a bending moment of $M = 8 \times 10^{5}$ N·m. Calculate:
  (i) the second moment of area,
  (ii) the maximum bending stress in the skin,
  (iii) the location where this maximum occurs. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Constructional Features:**

A semi-monocoque fuselage consists of the following load-carrying members working together:

1. **Skin** — the outer covering, typically aluminium alloy (Al 2024-T3) or CFRP in modern aircraft. The skin carries **in-plane shear and membrane stresses** and provides the aerodynamic shape. It is thin (≈ 1–2 mm) to keep weight low.

2. **Longerons** — heavy longitudinal stiffeners that run the full length of the fuselage along the top, sides, and bottom. They are the **primary load carriers in bending**, taking the axial tension and compression when the fuselage bends.

3. **Stringers** — smaller, more numerous longitudinal stiffeners, located between longerons. They **subdivide the skin into small panels** to prevent local buckling.

4. **Frames (Formers)** — transverse members spaced regularly along the fuselage. They **maintain the cross-sectional shape**, distribute concentrated loads circumferentially, and provide attachment points for stringers and skin.

5. **Bulkheads** — heavy transverse partitions at critical stations (e.g., wing attachment, tail attachment, cockpit pressure bulkhead). They **transfer concentrated loads** from wings, tail, or engines into the skin and longerons.

> The complete load path: external loads (lift, weight, thrust, drag) → bulkheads at attachment points → frames (distribute loads around circumference) → skin + longerons + stringers (carry distributed loads) → reactions at supports.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $R = 1.2$ m, $t = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $M = 8 \times 10^{5}$ N·m.

**(i) Second moment of area of a thin circular tube:**

$$I = \pi R^{3} t = \pi \times (1.2)^{3} \times (2 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$I = 3.1416 \times 1.728 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0857 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^{4}$$

**(ii) Maximum bending stress** (occurs at $y = R$):

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \cdot R}{I} = \frac{8 \times 10^{5} \times 1.2}{1.0857 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{9.6 \times 10^{5}}{1.0857 \times 10^{-2}} = 8.842 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = 88.42\ \text{MPa}$$

**(iii) Location:** Maximum bending stress occurs at the **top and bottom of the fuselage** (points farthest from the neutral axis), i.e., at $y = +R$ and $y = -R$ measured from the centroid.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students often forget to **convert mm to m** when computing $I$, leading to a $10^{3}$ error.
> 2. Some students incorrectly write $I = \pi R^{3}\,t$ for a *solid* shaft. Always justify: *"thin-walled approximation, $I \approx \pi R^{3} t$ since $R_o \approx R_i \approx R$ for $t \ll R$."*
> 3. Maximum stress is at $y = R$, not at the centroid. Marks are deducted if the student writes $\sigma = M/I$.

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **critical buckling stress of a flat skin panel** in terms of thickness, stiffener spacing, and material properties. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A thin-walled circular tube of mean radius $R = 1.0$ m and wall thickness $t = 1.5$ mm is subjected to a torque $T = 5 \times 10^{4}$ N·m. Calculate:
  (i) the shear flow in the skin using Bredt's formula,
  (ii) the shear stress in the skin,
  (iii) the angle of twist per unit length, given $G = 27$ GPa. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Critical Buckling Stress:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. The skin panel is a flat rectangular plate of length $a$, width $b$, and thickness $t$, with $a \gg b$.
> 2. The four edges are **simply supported** (no in-plane restraint, free to rotate out of plane).
> 3. The plate is loaded by a uniform in-plane compressive force $N_x$ per unit length.
> 4. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
> 5. Deflections are small compared to thickness (linear buckling theory).
> 6. No initial imperfections.

The governing differential equation for plate buckling is:

$$D \left( \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + 2\,\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2}\,\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}} \right) + N_x \,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} = 0$$

where $D = \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})}$ is the flexural rigidity of the plate.

Assume a sinusoidal buckling mode:

$$w(x,y) = w_0 \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi x}{a} \right) \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi y}{b} \right)$$

Substituting and applying the boundary conditions yields:

$$N_{x,cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} D}{b^{2}} \left( \frac{b}{a} + \frac{a}{b} \right)^{2}$$

For a long panel ($a/b \to \infty$), the term $(b/a)$ is negligible, giving:

$$N_{x,cr} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} D}{b^{2}} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} E t^{3}}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})\,b^{2}}$$

Dividing by $t$ to obtain the critical stress:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{N_{x,cr}}{t} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} E}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})} \left( \frac{t}{b} \right)^{2}$$

Or in general form:

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k\,\pi^{2}\,E}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2}\,}$$

where $k$ is the buckling coefficient ($k = 4$ for a long simply-supported plate, higher for clamped edges).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $R = 1.0$ m, $t = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $T = 5 \times 10^{4}$ N·m, $G = 27 \times 10^{9}$ Pa.

**(i) Shear flow using Bredt's formula:**

Mean enclosed area:

$$A_m = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times (1.0)^{2} = 3.1416\ \text{m}^{2}$$

Shear flow:

$$q = \frac{T}{2\,A_m} = \frac{5 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times 3.1416} = 7.958 \times 10^{3}\ \text{N/m}$$

**(ii) Shear stress in skin:**

$$\tau = \frac{q}{t} = \frac{7.958 \times 10^{3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.305 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 5.305\ \text{MPa}$$

**(iii) Angle of twist per unit length:**

Mean perimeter $s_m = 2 \pi R = 2 \pi \times 1.0 = 6.283$ m.

Since thickness is uniform, $\displaystyle\oint \frac{ds}{t} = \frac{s_m}{t} = \frac{6.283}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4189$ m⁻¹.

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{4\,A_m^{2}\,G} \oint \frac{ds}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{4}}{4 \times (3.1416)^{2} \times 27 \times 10^{9}} \times 4189$$

$$= \frac{5 \times 10^{4}}{1.0659 \times 10^{12}} \times 4189 = 1.965 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad/m}$$

Converting to degrees per metre:

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = 1.965 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.01126\ \text{°/m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students often confuse the formula for $q$ in *single-cell* tubes (Bredt) with that of multi-cell tubes. For a single closed cell only, $q = T/(2 A_m)$.
> 2. **Sign convention is irrelevant** for magnitude, but students sometimes include incorrect units. Always state *N/m* for shear flow and *Pa* or *MPa* for stress.
> 3. For part (b)(iii), the line integral $\oint ds/t$ must be **explicitly evaluated** — writing it without substituting the geometry loses 1 mark.
> 4. In derivation questions, **state the assumptions first** and **mention the boundary conditions** — these alone carry 1–2 marks in the KTU valuation key.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Monocoque (Module 2, PCAOT205)**

- [x] **Definition:** Monocoque = "single shell" — load carried entirely by the skin. Semi-monocoque = skin + internal stiffeners.
- [x] **Key components of semi-monocoque:** Skin, longerons (heavy longitudinal), stringers (light longitudinal), frames/formers (transverse), bulkheads (heavy transverse at load-introduction points).
- [x] **Bending stress formula:** $\sigma = M y / I$ — apply to built-up section using parallel-axis theorem.
- [x] **Bredt's shear flow (single closed cell):** $q = T / (2 A_m)$.
- [x] **Twist angle (uniform thickness):** $\theta = T L \, s_m / (4 A_m^{2}\, G\, t)$.
- [x] **Critical skin buckling (Bryan):** $\sigma_{cr} = k \pi^{2} E (t/b)^{2} / [12(1-\nu^{2})]$, with $k = 4$ for long simply-supported panels.
- [x] **Design rule:** $\sigma_{cr} \propto (t/b)^{2}$ — **stringer spacing** is the most effective design lever for buckling control.
- [x] **Why semi-monocoque is preferred:** Buckling resistance + damage tolerance + repairability.
- [x] **Typical materials:** Al 2024-T3 (older), Al 7075-T6, CFRP composites (modern).
- [x] **Closed cross-section** is mandatory for efficient torque transmission (Bredt's formula requires a closed cell).
- [x] **Safety factor** of 1.5 is standard for ultimate load design in aircraft structures.
- [x] **Unit vigilance:** mm → m conversion is the most common source of error; always check before substituting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semi-Monocoque, Control Surfaces and Maneuvers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semi-Monocoque, Control Surfaces and Maneuvers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Semi-Monocoque Fuselage Structure

### Formal Definition
The **semi-monocoque** (also called *modified monocoque* or *reinforced shell*) is a **load-bearing aircraft fuselage construction** in which the external **skin** carries a portion of the structural loads, while an internal skeleton of **longitudinal members (longerons and stringers)**, **transverse members (frames and bulkheads)**, and the **skin** acting together as a stressed-skin structure resist bending, shear, and torsional loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Monocoque** (French: "single shell") relies almost entirely on the skin to carry loads and is rarely used in modern aircraft because it buckles easily. **Semi-monocoque** solves this by reinforcing the skin with an internal framework, distributing the load between the skin and the frame.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Egg and the Bamboo Cage"
Imagine a hollow egg (a pure monocoque): it is light and smooth, but press on its side and it cracks instantly. Now, glue a delicate cage of bamboo strips (longitudinal longerons, stringers, and circular rings) inside that eggshell. The bamboo **shares** the load with the shell. The egg still looks smooth outside, but it is now strong, stiff, and can be repaired by replacing individual bamboo pieces. **That is the semi-monocoque philosophy.**

### Why It Matters
- The semi-monocoque **transmits bending loads through the longerons**, **shear through the skin**, and **shape through the frames**.
- The skin handles **torsional (twisting) shear stress**, which a pure monocoque cannot manage efficiently.
- It allows **cut-outs** (windows, doors, access panels) without catastrophic loss of strength, because the surrounding frames and longerons carry the redistributed load.

---

## 1.2 Aircraft Control Surfaces

### Formal Definition
**Control surfaces** are **hinged or movable aerodynamic surfaces** attached to the trailing (or leading) edges of an aircraft's wings, horizontal stabilizer, and vertical stabilizer. They are deflected by the pilot (or autopilot) to generate **aerodynamic moments** about the aircraft's three principal axes, thereby controlling its attitude and flight path.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Kayak's Rudder and Trim Tabs"
A kayak has a small fin at the back (rudder) — tilting it steers the boat. A rowing boat has small trim tabs on the rear of the keel — adjusting them lets the boat run straight without constant effort. Aircraft control surfaces work on the same principle: **small hinged panels deflect the airflow, the resulting asymmetric force creates a torque, and the aircraft rotates accordingly.**

### Classification (KTU 2024 Module 2 highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Control Surfaces** — Essential for controlled flight; loss makes the aircraft unflyable:
> - **Ailerons** → roll control (lateral axis)
> - **Elevator** (or **Stabilator** / **Canard**) → pitch control (transverse axis)
> - **Rudder** → yaw control (vertical axis)
>
> **Secondary (Auxiliary) Control Surfaces** — Improve performance; not strictly required for safe flight:
> - **Flaps, Slats, Slots** → increase lift at low speeds
> - **Spoilers / Speedbrakes** → reduce lift / increase drag
> - **Trim Tabs, Balance Tabs, Anti-Balance Tabs, Servo Tabs** → relieve stick forces
> - **Leading-Edge Devices** → modify stall behaviour

---

## 1.3 Aircraft Maneuvers

### Formal Definition
A **flight maneuver** is any deliberate change of the aircraft's **attitude**, **flight path**, **speed**, or **altitude** from a steady reference state (typically straight-and-level flight). Maneuvers are governed by the **moments** generated by the control surfaces and the **aerodynamic and inertial forces** acting on the aircraft.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Skater on Ice"
A skater gliding straight on ice (straight-and-level flight) leans inward to turn (banking), crouches and pushes the skate to climb a slope (climb), and arches the back to spin faster (increase rate). The pilot, by tilting the lift vector, banking, and pulling the stick, performs the **aerodynamic equivalent** of these motions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Maneuvering flight always increases the load factor (n) above 1**, because the lift must exceed the weight to accelerate the aircraft along a curved path. This is why sharp maneuvers impose higher structural loads.

### Common KTU-Syllabus Maneuvers
- **Straight-and-level flight** (n = 1)
- **Climb and descent** (steady and unaccelerated, vertical component of L and T)
- **Level (banked) turn** (n > 1, L tilted by bank angle φ)
- **Climbing and descending turns**
- **Pull-up and push-over** (changes in flight path curvature)
- **Loops, rolls, and stalls** (advanced / aerobatic)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Semi-Monocoque Construction — Component Roles

The semi-monocoque fuselage is a **load-sharing assembly** of five main elements:

| Component | Geometry | Primary Structural Role | Stress Carried |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Skin** | Thin sheet (aluminium alloy, composites) | Aerodynamic shape, carries shear | Shear, in-plane normal |
| **Longerons** | Heavy longitudinal beams (typically 4–8 around the fuselage) | Primary load-carrying longitudinal members | Bending (axial) |
| **Stringers** | Smaller longitudinal stiffeners between longerons | Stabilise skin against buckling, share axial load | Compressive (stabilising) |
| **Frames** (Formers) | Light transverse rings | Maintain cross-sectional shape, transfer loads to longerons | Local bending |
| **Bulkheads** | Heavy transverse partitions (closed sections) | Concentrate and redistribute concentrated loads (engines, wings, tail) | Concentrated reaction loads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule (KTU 2024):** The skin alone, unsupported, will **buckle at a far lower stress** than its material yield strength. Stringers and frames raise this **buckling stress** dramatically by reducing the unsupported panel width — this is the key efficiency of the semi-monocoque.

### Load Transfer Mechanism (Step-by-Step)
1. **Aerodynamic pressure** acts on the skin.
2. The skin transmits this pressure as **in-plane shear** to the longerons and stringers.
3. **Bending loads** (due to lift distribution, fuselage weight, inertia) are carried primarily by the **longerons as axial tension/compression**.
4. **Concentrated loads** (wing-fuselage junction, tail attachment, engine mount) are spread into the skin by **bulkheads**.
5. **Frames** keep the cross-section rigid so that the whole box behaves as a **thin-walled closed beam** in torsion.

### Advantages of Semi-Monocoque over Monocoque
- **Higher strength-to-weight ratio** (skin does not buckle).
- **Tolerates cut-outs** (windows, doors) without major redesign.
- **Easier inspection and repair** of internal members.
- **Allows larger fuselage diameters** (modern wide-bodies use it exclusively).
- **Better fatigue life** because loads are distributed over many load paths.

### Disadvantages
- More **complex and labour-intensive** to manufacture.
- Requires **more fasteners and rivets** → more points of fatigue and corrosion.
- Heavier than an ideal monocoque if not optimised.

---

## 2.2 Primary Control Surfaces — Detailed Analysis

### (a) Ailerons
- **Location:** Outboard trailing edge of each wing.
- **Operation:** Differential deflection — left aileron up + right aileron down (or vice versa).
- **Effect:** Creates **roll moment** about the longitudinal (roll) axis.
- **Side effect — Adverse Yaw:** The down-deflected aileron increases both lift *and* induced drag on its wing, dragging that wing backwards. The aircraft yaws **toward the lifted wing**. This is countered by **coordinated rudder input** or by using **differential ailerons / Frise ailerons**.

### (b) Elevator (and Stabilator / Canard)
- **Location:** Trailing edge of the **horizontal stabilizer**.
- **Operation:** Both halves move together — trailing-edge up reduces tail-down force → nose pitches **up** (pulling back on the stick); trailing-edge down pitches **down** (pushing forward).
- **Effect:** **Pitching moment** about the lateral (pitch) axis.
- **Variants:** **Stabilator** (all-moving horizontal tail) — no separate elevator; **Canard** — control surface ahead of the wing.

### (c) Rudder
- **Location:** Trailing edge of the **vertical stabilizer** (fin).
- **Operation:** Deflection to left or right.
- **Effect:** **Yawing moment** about the normal (yaw) axis; also produces a small roll due to **roll-yaw coupling**.
- **Use:** Primarily for **coordinated turns** and to counter adverse yaw; not the primary turning control in normal flight.

---

## 2.3 Secondary Control Surfaces

| Surface | Function | Typical Use Phase |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain Flap** | Increases camber → higher $C_L$ | Take-off / Landing |
| **Split Flap** | Increases effective camber, high drag | Landing |
| **Fowler Flap** | Increases wing **area** and camber | Take-off / Landing |
| **Slats / Slots** | Energise boundary layer → delay stall | Take-off / Landing |
| **Spoilers** | Destroy lift on one wing (roll assist) or both (speed brake) | Roll / Descent |
| **Speedbrakes** | Increase drag without lift change | Descent |
| **Trim Tabs** | Small hinged tab deflected to set control surface to balance aerodynamic moment | Cruise (relieves stick force) |
| **Balance / Servo Tabs** | Aerodynamically balance or actuate the main surface | Various |

---

## 2.4 Aircraft Maneuvers — Theory

### 2.4.1 Steady Climb
In a steady climb at climb angle $\gamma$:
- Lift balances weight only in the vertical component: $L = W\cos\gamma$.
- Thrust overcomes drag and the weight component along the flight path: $T = D + W\sin\gamma$.
- **Rate of climb (RoC):** $RC = V\sin\gamma = V \cdot \dfrac{T - D}{T} \cdot \sin\gamma$, simplified for small $\gamma$ as $RC \approx V\gamma$.
- **Climb gradient:** $\tan\gamma = \dfrac{T - D}{W}$ (for small $\gamma$, $\gamma \approx (T-D)/W$).

### 2.4.2 Steady Descent
Same equations with **throttle reduced**, possibly a negative net thrust. Aircraft may use **idle thrust** and **gear/flaps extended** to maintain a controlled $3^\circ$ glideslope.

### 2.4.3 Steady Level (Banked) Turn
The most important maneuver for the KTU exam. In a **coordinated, steady, level turn** of bank angle $\phi$ and true airspeed $V$:
- Lift vector is **tilted** by $\phi$ from the vertical.
- **Vertical equilibrium:** $L\cos\phi = W$.
- **Centripetal force balance (horizontal):** $L\sin\phi = \dfrac{mV^2}{R} = \dfrac{WV^2}{gR}$.
- **Load factor:** $n = \dfrac{L}{W} = \dfrac{1}{\cos\phi}$.
- **Turn radius:** $R = \dfrac{V^2}{g\tan\phi}$.
- **Turn rate (angular velocity):** $\omega = \dfrac{V}{R} = \dfrac{g\tan\phi}{V}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The tighter the bank (large $\phi$) and the slower the speed, the smaller the turn radius — but the load factor and stalling speed both **rise**, and structural stress increases.

### 2.4.4 Pull-Up and Push-Over
- **Pull-up:** Elevator up → flight path curves **upward** → $L > W$ → $n > 1$.
- **Push-over:** Elevator down → flight path curves **downward** → $L < W$ → $n < 1$ (can approach 0 in a free-fall push).

### 2.4.5 Stalling Speed in a Turn
Because lift must equal $nW$ in a turn, the stalling speed increases as $V_{s,\text{turn}} = V_s\sqrt{n}$. At $60^\circ$ bank, $n = 2$ and $V_{s,\text{turn}} = V_s\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 V_s$ — a **41% increase** in stall speed. This is a major safety consideration.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable in the KTU 2024 Scheme PCAOT205 End-Semester Examination.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L\cos\phi = W$ | Vertical force balance in level turn | N, N |
| 2 | $L\sin\phi = \dfrac{WV^2}{gR}$ | Centripetal force balance in level turn | N, N |
| 3 | $n = \dfrac{L}{W} = \dfrac{1}{\cos\phi}$ | Load factor in level turn | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{V^2}{g\tan\phi}$ | Turn radius in level turn | m |
| 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{g\tan\phi}{V}$ | Turn rate (angular velocity) | rad/s |
| 6 | $V_{s,\text{turn}} = V_s\sqrt{n}$ | Stalling speed in a turn | m/s |
| 7 | $\tan\gamma = \dfrac{T - D}{W}$ | Climb angle (small $\gamma$) | dimensionless / rad |
| 8 | $RC = V\sin\gamma$ | Rate of climb | m/s |
| 9 | $\dfrac{L}{D}_{\max}$ | Determines glide ratio (best glide angle) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tau = \rho_\infty V_\infty$ | Dynamic pressure (often $q$) | N/m² |

> **Note on notation:** $V$ is true airspeed (TAS), $V_s$ is straight-flight stall speed, $\phi$ is bank angle, $\gamma$ is flight-path (climb) angle, $R$ is turn radius, $\omega$ is turn rate, $n$ is load factor, $T$ is thrust, $D$ is drag, $L$ is lift, $W$ is weight, $g \approx 9.81$ m/s².

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Aircraft structural design:** Load factor envelope ($V$–$n$ diagram) defines the airspeed limits between $V_{S1}$ (stall) and $V_{NE}$ (never-exceed), and the **design manoeuvring speed $V_A$** below which full deflection is allowed.
- **Airline operations:** Bank-angle limits (typically $25^\circ$–$30^\circ$ standard, $45^\circ$ maximum) and load-factor limits (e.g., $+2.5\,g$ / $-1\,g$ for airliners) are baked into operating procedures.
- **Combat aircraft:** Operate routinely at $9\,g$ with reinforced semi-monocoque airframes and computer-controlled g-limits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 1 below is the most heavily tested derivation in Module 2.** It is essential to reproduce every algebraic step exactly as shown in KTU board valuation.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Steady Coordinated Level (Banked) Turn

### Aim
To derive the **turn radius $R$**, **turn rate $\omega$**, and **load factor $n$** for an aircraft executing a steady, coordinated, level turn at true airspeed $V$ and bank angle $\phi$.

### Free-Body Setup
Consider an aircraft in a steady level turn. Four forces act on it:
- **Lift $L$** — perpendicular to the wings, tilted by $\phi$ from the vertical.
- **Weight $W$** — vertical, downward.
- **Thrust $T$** — along the flight path (assumed horizontal in a level turn, so $T = D$ to maintain constant speed).
- **Drag $D$** — opposite to the flight path (also horizontal; balances $T$).

The lift vector makes an angle $\phi$ with the vertical. It therefore has:
- A **vertical component** $L\cos\phi$ balancing $W$.
- A **horizontal component** $L\sin\phi$ providing the centripetal force for the circular path.

### Step 1 — Vertical Force Equilibrium
The aircraft is in level flight, so the vertical acceleration is zero.

$$
L\cos\phi = W
$$

Solving for the lift required in the turn:

$$
L = \frac{W}{\cos\phi}
$$

### Step 2 — Horizontal (Centripetal) Force Balance
The aircraft moves in a circle of radius $R$ with speed $V$. Its centripetal acceleration is $V^2 / R$ directed horizontally toward the centre of the turn. By Newton's second law, the horizontal component of lift must supply this centripetal force.

$$
L\sin\phi = \frac{mV^2}{R}
$$

Substituting $m = W / g$:

$$
L\sin\phi = \frac{W V^2}{g R}
$$

### Step 3 — Definition of Load Factor
The **load factor** $n$ is the ratio of lift to weight:

$$
n = \frac{L}{W}
$$

Substituting $L$ from Step 1:

$$
n = \frac{W / \cos\phi}{W} = \frac{1}{\cos\phi}
$$

**This is the canonical KTU result for the level-turn load factor.** At $\phi = 60^\circ$, $n = 1/\cos 60^\circ = 1/0.5 = 2$ (i.e. **$2\,g$**).

### Step 4 — Turn Radius
Divide the horizontal equation (Step 2) by the vertical equation (Step 1):

$$
\frac{L\sin\phi}{L\cos\phi} = \frac{W V^2 / (gR)}{W}
$$

The $L$ and $W$ cancel:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{V^2}{g R}
$$

Solving for $R$:

$$
R = \frac{V^2}{g \tan\phi}
$$

**Result:** The turn radius is proportional to the square of speed and inversely proportional to $\tan\phi$. Slow aircraft make tight turns; high bank angles also tighten the turn.

### Step 5 — Turn Rate (Angular Velocity)
The turn rate is the angular speed at which the aircraft's heading changes, $\omega = V / R$:

$$
\omega = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{V}{\dfrac{V^2}{g \tan\phi}} = \frac{g \tan\phi}{V}
$$

**Result:** Turn rate is proportional to $\tan\phi$ and inversely proportional to speed. To turn *fast*, bank steeply and fly *slowly* (subject to stall margins — see Section 3.2).

### Step 6 — Alternative Form Using Load Factor
Since $\tan\phi = \sqrt{n^2 - 1}$ (from $n = 1/\cos\phi$), the radius and rate can be written as:

$$
R = \frac{V^2}{g\sqrt{n^2 - 1}}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{g\sqrt{n^2 - 1}}{V}
$$

This form is convenient when the KTU problem gives $n$ instead of $\phi$.

### Summary of Derived Quantities
| Quantity | Symbol | Final Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Load factor | $n$ | $1 / \cos\phi$ |
| Turn radius | $R$ | $V^2 / (g\tan\phi)$ |
| Turn rate | $\omega$ | $g\tan\phi / V$ |
| Radius (in terms of $n$) | $R$ | $V^2 / (g\sqrt{n^2 - 1})$ |
| Turn rate (in terms of $n$) | $\omega$ | $g\sqrt{n^2 - 1} / V$ |

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Stalling Speed in a Turn

### Aim
To derive the relationship between the stalling speed in a level turn and the stalling speed in straight-and-level flight.

### Step 1 — Stall Condition in Straight-and-Level Flight
At the stall, the wing reaches its maximum lift coefficient $C_{L,\max}$. In straight-and-level flight, $L = W$:

$$
\frac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2 S \, C_{L,\max} = W
$$

Solve for $V_s$:

$$
V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho S C_{L,\max}}}
$$

### Step 2 — Stall Condition in a Level Turn of Bank Angle $\phi$
In a level turn, the lift required is $L = nW$ with $n = 1/\cos\phi$. Setting $L = L_{\max}$:

$$
\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{s,\text{turn}}^2 S \, C_{L,\max} = nW
$$

Solve for $V_{s,\text{turn}}$:

$$
V_{s,\text{turn}} = \sqrt{\frac{2nW}{\rho S C_{L,\max}}}
$$

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio
Square both stall-speed expressions and take the ratio:

$$
\frac{V_{s,\text{turn}}^2}{V_s^2} = \frac{2nW / (\rho S C_{L,\max})}{2W / (\rho S C_{L,\max})} = n
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{s,\text{turn}}^2 = n V_s^2
$$

$$
V_{s,\text{turn}} = V_s \sqrt{n}
$$

### Step 4 — Sanity Check at $\phi = 60^\circ$
At $\phi = 60^\circ$, $n = 1/\cos 60^\circ = 2$. Hence $V_{s,\text{turn}} = V_s \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 V_s$. The aircraft stalls at a **41% higher airspeed** in a steep $60^\circ$ bank than in straight flight.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why high-performance pilots slow down *before* entering a steep bank, not during it — to maintain a safe margin above $V_{s,\text{turn}}$.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Climb Angle and Rate of Climb

### Step 1 — Force Balance Along the Flight Path
In a steady climb at flight-path angle $\gamma$, the net force along the flight path is zero:

$$
T = D + W\sin\gamma
$$

### Step 2 — Climb Angle
Rearrange:

$$
\sin\gamma = \frac{T - D}{W}
$$

For small $\gamma$, $\sin\gamma \approx \tan\gamma \approx \gamma$ (in radians):

$$
\gamma \approx \frac{T - D}{W}
$$

### Step 3 — Rate of Climb
The rate of climb is the vertical component of the velocity:

$$
RC = V \sin\gamma \approx V \gamma \quad \text{(small } \gamma \text{)}
$$

Substituting $\gamma$:

$$
RC = V \cdot \frac{T - D}{W}
$$

Since $W = mg$, this may also be written:

$$
RC = \frac{V(T - D)}{W}
$$

### Step 4 — Best Climb Performance
$RC$ is maximised at the airspeed where the **excess power** $V(T - D)$ is greatest. This defines $V_{Y}$ (best rate-of-climb speed) and is found by plotting $(T - D)$ vs $V$ and finding the tangent from the origin to the power-required curve.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** An aircraft weighing $W = 60\,000$ N executes a steady coordinated level turn at $V = 150$ m/s with a bank angle $\phi = 45^\circ$. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². Find the load factor, turn radius, and turn rate.

### Solution

**Load factor:**

$$
n = \frac{1}{\cos 45^\circ} = \frac{1}{0.7071} \approx 1.414
$$

**Turn radius:**

$$
R = \frac{V^2}{g \tan\phi} = \frac{150^2}{9.81 \times \tan 45^\circ} = \frac{22\,500}{9.81 \times 1} \approx 2293.6 \text{ m} \approx 2.29 \text{ km}
$$

**Turn rate:**

$$
\omega = \frac{g \tan\phi}{V} = \frac{9.81 \times 1}{150} = 0.0654 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Convert to degrees per second:

$$
\omega = 0.0654 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 3.75^\circ / \text{s}
$$

**Time for a $360^\circ$ turn (turn period):**

$$
t_{360} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{0.0654} \approx 96.1 \text{ s} \approx 1 \text{ min } 36 \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The same procedure — apply $n = 1/\cos\phi$, then $R = V^2/(g\tan\phi)$ and $\omega = g\tan\phi/V$ — solves **every** KTU banked-turn problem. Commit it to memory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams below use **Mermaid block diagrams** to represent the physical structure of the semi-monocoque fuselage, the aircraft control surfaces, and the force system in a banked turn. Mermaid cannot render literal mechanical drawings, so the relationships and functional architecture are shown in their place.

## 4.1 Semi-Monocoque Fuselage — Structural Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    SM[Semi-Monocoque Fuselage]
    SM --> SK[Skin]
    SM --> LG[Longerons]
    SM --> ST[Stringers]
    SM --> FR[Frames]
    SM --> BH[Bulkheads]
    SK --> SK1[Outer load-bearing shell]
    SK --> SK2[Carries torsional shear]
    SK --> SK3[Provides aerodynamic shape]
    LG --> LG1[Heavy longitudinal beams]
    LG --> LG2[Carry axial bending loads]
    LG --> LG3[Typically 4 to 8 around fuselage]
    ST --> ST1[Smaller longitudinal stiffeners]
    ST --> ST2[Prevent skin buckling]
    ST --> ST3[Distribute axial load]
    FR --> FR1[Light transverse rings]
    FR --> FR2[Maintain cross section]
    FR --> FR3[Transfer load to longerons]
    BH --> BH1[Heavy transverse partitions]
    BH --> BH2[Redistribute concentrated loads]
    BH --> BH3[Used at wing tail and engine junctions]
    LG --> LT[Load Transfer]
    SK --> LT
    ST --> LT
    FR --> LT
    BH --> LT
    LT --> LB[Bending]
    LT --> LS[Shear]
    LT --> LT2[Torsion]
```

## 4.2 Aircraft Control Surfaces — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    CS[Control Surfaces]
    CS --> PC[Primary]
    CS --> SC[Secondary]
    PC --> AL[Aileron]
    PC --> EL[Elevator]
    PC --> RD[Rudder]
    AL --> AL1[Outboard trailing edge of wing]
    AL --> AL2[Differential deflection]
    AL --> AL3[Roll about longitudinal axis]
    AL --> AL4[Adverse yaw as side effect]
    EL --> EL1[Trailing edge of horizontal tail]
    EL --> EL2[Symmetric deflection]
    EL --> EL3[Pitch about lateral axis]
    RD --> RD1[Trailing edge of vertical tail]
    RD --> RD2[Deflection to left or right]
    RD --> RD3[Yaw about normal axis]
    SC --> FL[Flaps]
    SC --> SL[Slats]
    SC --> SP[Spoilers]
    SC --> TR[Trim Tabs]
    FL --> FL1[Increase camber and CL]
    FL --> FL2[Takeoff and landing]
    SL --> SL1[Leading edge high lift device]
    SL --> SL2[Delay stall]
    SP --> SP1[Destroy or reduce lift]
    SP --> SP2[Speed brake or roll spoiler]
    TR --> TR1[Relieve stick forces]
    TR --> TR2[Set trimmed flight condition]
```

## 4.3 Banked Turn — Force System Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    BT[Steady Coordinated Level Turn]
    BT --> F1[Lift L tilted by phi]
    BT --> F2[Weight W vertical downward]
    BT --> F3[Thrust T along flight path]
    BT --> F4[Drag D opposite to flight path]
    F1 --> FE1[Vertical component L cos phi balances W]
    F1 --> FE2[Horizontal component L sin phi provides centripetal force]
    FE1 --> EQ1[L cos phi equals W]
    FE2 --> EQ2[L sin phi equals m V squared over R]
    EQ1 --> NF[Load Factor n equals 1 over cos phi]
    EQ2 --> NF
    EQ1 --> TR1[Turn radius R equals V squared over g tan phi]
    EQ2 --> TR1
    EQ1 --> TR2[Turn rate omega equals g tan phi over V]
    EQ2 --> TR2
    NF --> ST1[At phi 60 degrees n equals 2]
    TR1 --> ST2[Tighter bank gives smaller radius]
    TR2 --> ST3[Slower speed gives higher rate]
```

## 4.4 Three-Axis Control Mapping (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TB
    AX[Aircraft Three Axes]
    AX --> AX1[Roll axis longitudinal]
    AX --> AX2[Pitch axis lateral]
    AX --> AX3[Yaw axis normal]
    AX1 --> CS1[Controlled by Ailerons]
    AX2 --> CS2[Controlled by Elevator]
    AX3 --> CS3[Controlled by Rudder]
    CS1 --> M1[Roll moment]
    CS2 --> M2[Pitch moment]
    CS3 --> M3[Yaw moment]
    M1 --> MN1[Couples into yaw as adverse yaw]
    M3 --> MN2[Couples into roll as roll yaw coupling]
```

## 4.5 Flight Maneuver Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    FL[Flight Maneuver]
    FL --> ST[Steady Maneuvers]
    FL --> UN[Unsteady Maneuvers]
    ST --> ST1[Straight and level]
    ST --> ST2[Climb]
    ST --> ST3[Descent]
    ST --> ST4[Level turn banked]
    ST --> ST5[Climbing turn]
    ST --> ST6[Descending turn]
    UN --> UN1[Pull up]
    UN --> UN2[Push over]
    UN --> UN3[Spin]
    UN --> UN4[Loop]
    UN --> UN5[Aerobatic rolls]
    ST1 --> L1[n equals 1]
    ST4 --> L2[n greater than 1]
    ST2 --> L3[T exceeds D plus W sin gamma]
    UN1 --> L4[Instantaneous n greater than 1]
    L2 --> VS[Stall speed rises as V s turn equals V s sqrt n]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The question bank below mirrors the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for PCAOT205. Part A carries 3 marks per question; Part B carries 14 marks per question (with a typical internal choice), split as 7 + 7. All questions are tagged with simulated past-year references for familiarity.

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. Differentiate between monocoque and semi-monocoque structures. (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question Bank, PCAOT205)]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Monocoque | Semi-Monocoque |
|---|---|---|
| Structural framework | Skin alone carries all loads | Skin + longerons, stringers, frames, bulkheads |
| Load sharing | Only the skin | Distributed between skin and internal frame |
| Cut-outs | Not feasible (weakens the structure) | Possible (load redistributed by frame) |
| Strength-to-weight | Low (skin buckles) | High (skin stabilised by stringers) |
| Repair | Difficult | Easier (replace individual members) |
| Application | Rare, small aircraft | All modern transport, fighter, trainer aircraft |

> A **monocoque** ("single shell") is a stressed-skin structure where the skin alone resists all loads. It is light but buckles easily. A **semi-monocoque** reinforces the skin with an internal skeleton of longerons, stringers, frames, and bulkheads, making it far stronger and the universal choice in modern aircraft. **(3 marks)** — one mark per major differentiation point.

---

### Q2. Define load factor. State its significance in aircraft design and maneuvers. (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (PCAOT205 Model Paper)]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** The **load factor** $n$ is the ratio of the lift produced by the wing to the weight of the aircraft: $n = L / W$. It is dimensionless.
- **In a level turn:** $n = 1/\cos\phi$, where $\phi$ is the bank angle.
- **Significance in maneuvers:** $n$ quantifies the **additional stress** the structure must withstand during a maneuver. Sharp turns and pull-ups raise $n$ (and therefore structural load) well above the cruise value of 1.
- **Significance in design:** The $V$–$n$ (maneuvering envelope) diagram plots $n$ against airspeed. The boundaries of this envelope — $V_S$ (stall), $V_A$ (design manoeuvring speed), $V_C$ (cruise), $V_{NE}$ (never-exceed), and the $n$-limit lines — define the **operational and structural design limits** of the aircraft. **(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for $n = 1/\cos\phi$, 1 mark for design/manoeuvre significance.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, internal choice provided)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — (14 marks)

#### Q3(a) Explain with a neat sketch the semi-monocoque construction of an aircraft fuselage. List its advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The semi-monocoque (or *reinforced shell*) is the standard fuselage construction in modern aircraft. It is a load-sharing structure in which the external **skin** and an internal **skeleton of longitudinal and transverse members** together resist bending, shear, and torsional loads.

**Components (3 marks — 0.5 mark each):**
1. **Skin:** Thin sheet of aluminium alloy (e.g., 2024-T3) or composite (CFRP in modern aircraft). Forms the outer aerodynamic surface and carries in-plane shear and a share of the bending stress.
2. **Longerons:** Heavy longitudinal stringers (typically four in a circular fuselage) running the full length. They carry the major share of bending loads as axial tension/compression.
3. **Stringers:** Smaller longitudinal stiffeners spaced between the longerons. Their main job is to **stabilise the skin against buckling** under compression.
4. **Frames (Formers):** Light transverse ring sections spaced along the fuselage. They maintain the cross-sectional shape and transfer concentrated loads from the skin to the longerons.
5. **Bulkheads:** Heavy transverse partitions of closed cross-section. Used at points of high concentrated load (engine mount, wing-fuselage junction, tail attachment, landing-gear bay) to spread the load into the skin.
6. **Floor beams and doublers:** Additional reinforcements at the floor and around major cut-outs.

*(For a diagram, refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 — and in the answer script, draw a labelled cross-section showing skin, four longerons, several stringers, and a frame.)*

**Load Transfer Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
- **Bending** is carried by the **longerons** as axial stress.
- **Torsion** is carried by the **closed-cell skin** (acting as a thin-walled closed tube).
- **Shear** is carried by both the **skin** and the **longerons**.
- **Concentrated loads** (e.g., wing reactions) are spread by **bulkheads** into the skin.
- **Frames** keep the cross-section undeformed so that the structure behaves as a unit.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Highest strength-to-weight ratio among practical fuselage types.
- Skin does not buckle (stringers + frames stabilise it).
- Cut-outs (windows, doors) are easily accommodated.
- Easier inspection, repair, and modification.
- Long fatigue life (load distributed over many members).
- Forms the basis of **fail-safe** and **damage-tolerant** design.

**Disadvantages (0.5 mark):**
- Manufacturing complexity and labour cost.
- Many rivets/fasteners → corrosion and fatigue concerns.
- Slightly heavier than an idealised monocoque at very small sizes.

---

#### Q3(b) Describe the primary and secondary control surfaces of an aircraft, with diagrams, and explain how they generate moments about the three axes. (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Adapted)]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
Control surfaces are hinged panels on the trailing (or leading) edges of wings and tail surfaces. They deflect the airflow to create aerodynamic moments, giving the pilot control about the aircraft's three principal axes. They are classified as **primary** (essential for flight) and **secondary** (performance-enhancing).

**Primary Control Surfaces (3 marks):**

1. **Ailerons** *(refer to the diagram in Section 4.2 — draw on the outboard trailing edge of the wing in the plan view):*
   - Hinged on the **outboard trailing edges** of the wings.
   - Deflected **differentially** — left up, right down (or vice versa).
   - The wing with the down-aileron produces **more lift**, rolling the aircraft about the **longitudinal (roll) axis**.
   - **Side effect:** Adverse yaw (the down-aileron wing has more induced drag) → counter-rudder required for coordination.
   - **Variants:** Plain aileron, Frise aileron (protrudes below wing to reduce adverse yaw), differential aileron.

2. **Elevator:**
   - Hinged on the **trailing edge of the horizontal stabilizer**.
   - Both halves move **symmetrically** (together).
   - Trailing-edge up → tail load decreases → nose pitches **up** (pull on stick).
   - Trailing-edge down → tail load increases → nose pitches **down** (push on stick).
   - Generates pitching moment about the **lateral (pitch) axis**.

3. **Rudder:**
   - Hinged on the **trailing edge of the vertical stabilizer (fin)**.
   - Deflection to the left or right.
   - Generates sideforce on the tail, producing a **yawing moment** about the **normal (yaw) axis**.
   - Used primarily for **coordination** in turns and to counter adverse yaw; not the primary turning control in normal flight.

**Secondary Control Surfaces (2 marks):**

| Surface | Function | Use Phase |
|---|---|---|
| **Flaps** (plain, split, fowler) | Increase wing camber and/or area → higher $C_{L,\max}$ | Take-off, landing |
| **Slats / Slots** | Energise boundary layer at high angles of attack | Take-off, landing |
| **Spoilers** | Destroy lift on one wing (roll spoiler) or both (speed brake) | Roll assist, descent |
| **Trim tabs** | Small adjustable tab on the control surface to trim out stick forces | Cruise |
| **Speedbrakes** | Increase drag without major lift change | Descent, landing rollout |
| **Servo / Balance tabs** | Reduce hinge moment or actuate the main surface | All phases |

**Moment Generation About the Three Axes — Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Axis | Name | Control Surface | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal | Roll | Aileron (differential) | Left/right wing |
| Lateral | Pitch | Elevator (symmetric) | Nose up/down |
| Normal | Yaw | Rudder | Nose left/right |

**Coupling Effects (0.5 mark):**
- **Adverse yaw** — aileron deflection creates unwanted yaw, corrected by rudder.
- **Roll–yaw coupling in turns** — dihedral effect and high-wing configurations.
- **Dutch roll** — oscillatory coupling of roll and yaw in swept-wing aircraft.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — (14 marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]

#### Q4(a) Define a flight maneuver. With neat sketches, describe straight-and-level flight, climb, and descent. (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **flight maneuver** is any deliberate change in an aircraft's attitude, speed, altitude, or flight path from a reference (typically straight-and-level) state. It is achieved by the pilot deflecting the control surfaces, which generates aerodynamic moments and changes the force equilibrium.

**1. Straight-and-Level Flight (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Flight at **constant altitude and constant heading** with the wings level.
- **Force balance:**
  - Lift equals weight: $L = W$.
  - Thrust equals drag: $T = D$.
  - Load factor: $n = L/W = 1$.
- **Condition:** Throttle set so $T = D$; elevator trimmed for $\gamma = 0$.
- **Diagram:** *(In the answer script, sketch an aircraft with horizontal wings, level flight path, and four labelled force vectors.)*

**2. Climb (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Flight in which the **flight path makes a positive angle $\gamma$** with the horizontal.
- **Force balance:**
  - Lift balances the **vertical component** of weight: $L = W\cos\gamma$.
  - Thrust overcomes drag **and** the **flight-path component of weight**: $T = D + W\sin\gamma$.
  - Climb angle: $\tan\gamma = (T - D)/W$ (small $\gamma$).
  - Rate of climb: $RC = V\sin\gamma$.
- **Best rate-of-climb speed $V_Y$:** airspeed at which the **excess power** $V(T - D)$ is maximum.
- **Diagram:** *(Sketch showing the flight path inclined at angle $\gamma$ above the horizontal, with $L$ tilted slightly back and $T$ along the flight path.)*

**3. Descent (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Flight in which the **flight path makes a negative angle $-\gamma$** below the horizontal.
- **Force balance:**
  - $L = W\cos\gamma$ (same as climb, with $\gamma$ now the magnitude of the descent angle).
  - Net thrust is **negative** (idle or partial thrust): $T < D$, so the aircraft descends at constant speed when $T - D = -W\sin\gamma$.
  - **Glide** (engine idle): $L/D$ determines the glide ratio; best glide speed $V_{L/D,\max}$ gives the **flattest** glide (smallest $\gamma$).
- **Diagram:** *(Sketch similar to climb but with the flight path inclined downward.)*

**Summary Table (built into answer):**

| Maneuver | Path Angle $\gamma$ | Thrust Condition | Load Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Straight-and-level | $0$ | $T = D$ | $n = 1$ |
| Climb | $\gamma > 0$ | $T > D$ | $n = \cos\gamma$ (perpendicular) |
| Descent | $\gamma < 0$ | $T < D$ (often idle) | $n = \cos\gamma$ |

---

#### Q4(b) Derive the expressions for load factor, turn radius, and turn rate in a steady coordinated banked turn. Also derive the stalling speed in a turn. (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (PCAOT205 Model Paper)]**

**Model Answer:**

*Assumptions:* Steady, level, coordinated turn. Airspeed $V$ constant. Thrust balances drag. Bank angle $\phi$.

*Free-Body Diagram:* *(Refer to Section 4.3 — in the answer script, draw an aircraft in a banked turn with $L$ tilted by $\phi$, $W$ vertical, $T$ and $D$ horizontal and opposing.)*

**Step 1 — Vertical equilibrium (1 mark):**

$$
L\cos\phi = W
$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal (centripetal) equilibrium (1 mark):**

$$
L\sin\phi = \frac{mV^2}{R} = \frac{W V^2}{g R}
$$

**Step 3 — Load factor (1 mark):**

$$
n = \frac{L}{W} = \frac{1}{\cos\phi}
$$

**Step 4 — Turn radius (1.5 marks):**
Divide the equations: $\tan\phi = V^2 / (gR)$, hence

$$
R = \frac{V^2}{g \tan\phi}
$$

**Step 5 — Turn rate (1.5 marks):**

$$
\omega = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{g\tan\phi}{V}
$$

*Alternative forms in terms of $n$ (using $\tan\phi = \sqrt{n^2 - 1}$):*

$$
R = \frac{V^2}{g\sqrt{n^2 - 1}}, \qquad \omega = \frac{g\sqrt{n^2 - 1}}{V}
$$

**Step 6 — Stalling speed in a turn (1 mark):**
At the stall, $C_L = C_{L,\max}$. In straight flight: $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2 S C_{L,\max} = W$. In a turn: $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{s,\text{turn}}^2 S C_{L,\max} = nW$. Therefore:

$$
V_{s,\text{turn}} = V_s \sqrt{n}
$$

**Numerical Insight (optional, 0.5 mark):**
At $\phi = 60^\circ$, $n = 2$ and $V_{s,\text{turn}} = 1.414\,V_s$ — a **41 % increase** in stall speed. This is a critical safety consideration during steep turns.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic in the KTU board exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the bank angle with the flight-path angle.** Bank angle $\phi$ is in the *vertical plane containing the wings and the lift vector*. Flight-path angle $\gamma$ is in the *vertical plane containing the velocity vector*. They are not the same. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using the wrong one.
>
> 2. **Writing $L = W$ in a banked turn.** A turn ALWAYS requires $L > W$ (specifically $L = W/\cos\phi$). Many students mistakenly use $L = W$ and end up with $R = V^2 / (g\phi)$, which is dimensionally wrong and earns zero for the radius.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{n^2 - 1}$ form.** When the question gives $n$ instead of $\phi$, students should rewrite $\tan\phi$ as $\sqrt{n^2-1}$. Failure to do so costs a full mark in the substitution step.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units.** KTU valuation is strict: $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $R$ in **metres**, $V$ in **m/s**. Mixing knots with m/s is a common error and attracts a 0.5 mark penalty.
>
> 5. **Omitting the load factor in the stall-speed derivation.** Students often write $V_{s,\text{turn}} = V_s\sqrt{2}$ for a 60° bank without showing the intermediate $n$ step. The KTU answer key explicitly awards 1 mark for stating $n = 1/\cos\phi$ before substituting.
>
> 6. **Skipping the free-body diagram.** A labelled free-body diagram is worth **1 full mark** in the long-answer section. Omitting it is the single most common avoidable loss.
>
> 7. **Conflating flap types.** Plain flap ≠ split flap ≠ fowler flap. Examiners want the specific characteristic of each (camber change vs. area change vs. drag change).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Semi-Monocoque
- [ ] **Five components** — skin, longerons, stringers, frames, bulkheads.
- [ ] **Skin carries shear; longerons carry bending; frames hold shape; bulkheads spread concentrated loads; stringers prevent buckling.**
- [ ] Semi-monocoque **replaces the pure monocoque** in all modern aircraft because it tolerates cut-outs and resists buckling.
- [ ] **Closed-cell cross-section** is critical for torsional rigidity.

### Control Surfaces
- [ ] **Primary = Aileron (roll) + Elevator (pitch) + Rudder (yaw).**
- [ ] **Ailerons are differential; elevator and rudder deflection is on the tail.**
- [ ] **Adverse yaw** is the aileron's main side effect, countered by rudder.
- [ ] **Secondary surfaces** include flaps, slats, spoilers, speedbrakes, trim tabs, servo tabs, balance tabs.
- [ ] **Fowler flap** is the only flap that increases wing **area**, not just camber.

### Maneuvers
- [ ] **Straight-and-level:** $L = W$, $T = D$, $n = 1$.
- [ ] **Climb angle:** $\tan\gamma = (T - D)/W$; **rate of climb** $RC = V\sin\gamma$.
- [ ] **Level turn:** **Load factor** $n = 1/\cos\phi$; **radius** $R = V^2 / (g\tan\phi)$; **rate** $\omega = g\tan\phi / V$.
- [ ] **Stalling speed in turn:** $V_{s,\text{turn}} = V_s\sqrt{n}$. At 60° bank, it rises by **41 %**.
- [ ] **Tighter bank + slower speed = smaller radius but higher $n$ and higher stall speed.**
- [ ] **Design manoeuvring speed $V_A$** is the maximum speed at which full control deflection is permitted without structural damage — below $V_A$, the aircraft stalls before it breaks.

### Key Numerical Values to Memorise
- [ ] $g = 9.81$ m/s² (use $9.81$, not $10$, unless the problem specifies).
- [ ] $n = 1/\cos\phi$ → at $\phi = 30^\circ$: $n = 1.155$; at $45^\circ$: $n = 1.414$; at $60^\circ$: $n = 2.0$.
- [ ] $\tan\phi$ → at $30^\circ = 0.577$; at $45^\circ = 1$; at $60^\circ = 1.732$.

### Most-Tested KTU Question Types
1. Sketch + label the semi-monocoque components. (4–7 marks)
2. Function of primary control surfaces + axis mapping. (7 marks)
3. Derive the three banked-turn equations. (7 marks)
4. Compute $n$, $R$, $\omega$ given $V$ and $\phi$ (numerical). (7 marks)
5. Stalling speed in a turn. (3–7 marks)
6. Climb gradient / rate of climb. (3–7 marks)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pitot Static System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pitot Static System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition
> The **Pitot-Static System** is an integrated network of pressure-sensing instruments and pneumatic lines used in an aircraft to measure three critical flight parameters: **airspeed, altitude, and vertical speed (rate of climb/descent)**. It functions by simultaneously sensing two distinct pressures in the surrounding airflow — the **stagnation (total) pressure** $P_t$ via a Pitot tube, and the **ambient (static) pressure** $P_s$ via flush-mounted static ports — and routing these pressures to three independent flight instruments: the **Airspeed Indicator (ASI)**, the **Altimeter**, and the **Vertical Speed Indicator (VSI)**.

The differential pressure $\Delta P = P_t - P_s$ is known as the **dynamic pressure** ($q$) and forms the fundamental basis of all airspeed computations in subsonic, incompressible flow regimes.

## 1.2 System Components — Block Inventory

A complete Pitot-Static System consists of the following physical hardware elements:

| S.No | Component | Function |
|:----:|:----------|:---------|
| 1 | **Pitot Tube (Pressure Head)** | Faces the oncoming airflow; measures stagnation/total pressure $P_t$ |
| 2 | **Static Port (Flush-Mounted Orifice)** | Senses undisturbed ambient static pressure $P_s$ perpendicular to flow |
| 3 | **Pitot Drain Hole** | Removes water/moisture accumulated in the Pitot line |
| 4 | **Pneumatic Tubing (Static Line)** | Conveys $P_s$ to instruments through airtight, flexible lines |
| 5 | **Airspeed Indicator (ASI)** | Displays Indicated Airspeed (IAS) from $\Delta P$ |
| 6 | **Altimeter** | Displays altitude from $P_s$ using barometric calibration |
| 7 | **Vertical Speed Indicator (VSI)** | Displays rate of climb from the time derivative of $P_s$ |
| 8 | **Alternate Static Source Valve** | Backup $P_s$ source used if primary static port is blocked |
| 9 | **Heated Pitot Tube** | Electric heating element to prevent icing at low altitudes |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Drinking Straw Experiment

> [!NOTE] Real-World Intuition
> Think of a **drinking straw placed in a moving river**. If you point the opening directly into the flow, the water pushes inside the straw and rises higher than the water level outside — this is exactly what happens in a **Pitot tube**, where the airstream is brought to rest (stagnation), and the pressure rises above ambient. Now imagine a **second straw placed sideways (perpendicular to the flow)** in the same river — the water level inside this straw is the same as the river surface, undisturbed. This sideways measurement is what a **static port** does — it senses the ambient pressure of the undisturbed atmosphere. The **difference** in water levels (pressures) between the two straws is your **dynamic pressure**, which tells you how fast the river (aircraft) is moving relative to the air.

The aircraft is essentially "flying through" a stationary river of air, and these two "straws" convert the invisible airflow into measurable pressure signals.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Atmosphere Metrics

- **Sea-level standard pressure:** $P_0 = 101325 \text{ Pa} = 29.92 \text{ in.Hg} = 1013.25 \text{ hPa}$
- **Sea-level standard temperature:** $T_0 = 288.15 \text{ K} = 15 \text{ °C} = 59 \text{ °F}$
- **Sea-level standard density:** $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Lapse rate (troposphere):** $L = 0.0065 \text{ K/m}$
- **Universal gas constant for air:** $R = 287.05 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$

> [!IMPORTANT] Syllabus Highlight
> The **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)** model assumes a linear temperature decrease with altitude up to **11 km (Tropopause)** and is the universal reference for all altimetry, airspeed calibration, and aircraft performance calculations in KTU board examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Physical Principle — Bernoulli's Equation

The Pitot-Static System fundamentally operates on **Daniel Bernoulli's principle for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow** along a streamline. When the aircraft moves through still air at a true airspeed $V_{TAS}$, the airflow can be modeled in the **aircraft's reference frame** as an oncoming stream of velocity $V_{TAS}$:

- **Far upstream (undisturbed air):** static pressure $P_s$, velocity $V_{TAS}$
- **At the Pitot tube inlet (stagnation point):** pressure $P_t$, velocity $0$

Applying Bernoulli's equation between these two points (along the same streamline, same elevation):

$$P_s + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{TAS}^2 = P_t + \frac{1}{2} \rho (0)^2$$

Solving for the dynamic pressure:

$$q \equiv P_t - P_s = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{TAS}^2$$

This is the **fundamental equation of the Pitot-Static System**, and every downstream instrument is a direct or indirect measurement of either $P_t$, $P_s$, or their difference.

## 2.2 The Three Pressure Concepts — Logical Breakdown

1. **Static Pressure $P_s$** — The **thermodynamic pressure** of the air that would exist whether or not the aircraft is moving. Measured perpendicular to the flow direction by static ports. Varies with altitude.
2. **Dynamic Pressure $q$** — The **kinetic energy per unit volume** of the moving airstream, given by $q = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$. This is what the ASI ultimately measures.
3. **Stagnation (Total) Pressure $P_t$** — The pressure the air would reach if brought to rest **isentropically**. Equal to the sum $P_t = P_s + q$. Measured by the Pitot tube.

## 2.3 The Three Airspeed Definitions — Hierarchical Logic

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Pressure Basis |
|:---------|:------:|:-----------|:---------------|
| **Indicated Airspeed** | $V_{IAS}$ | Speed shown directly on ASI dial | Uses sea-level density $\rho_0$ |
| **Calibrated Airspeed** | $V_{CAS}$ | IAS corrected for **instrument/position error** | Still uses $\rho_0$ |
| **Equivalent Airspeed** | $V_{EAS}$ | CAS corrected for **compressibility error** (subsonic) | Uses $\rho_0$ |
| **True Airspeed** | $V_{TAS}$ | Actual speed of aircraft relative to air mass | Uses local $\rho$ |
| **Mach Number** | $M$ | $V_{TAS} / a_{\text{local}}$ | Ratio (dimensionless) |

> [!NOTE] Compressibility Note
> For Mach numbers $M < 0.3$ (which corresponds to roughly 200 knots at sea level), the flow is treated as **incompressible** and the simple Bernoulli equation is valid. Above $M = 0.3$, compressibility corrections using the **Rayleigh Pitot formula** become mandatory.

## 2.4 Indicated Airspeed Derivation Logic

The ASI is calibrated at **sea-level standard density** $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, irrespective of the actual flight density. The dial mechanism solves Bernoulli's equation backwards:

$$V_{IAS} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot (P_t - P_s)}{\rho_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_0}}$$

> [!WARNING] Critical KTU Pitfall
> Students often confuse **IAS** with **TAS**. Always remember: **IAS uses sea-level density, TAS uses local density.** Therefore $V_{IAS} < V_{TAS}$ at altitude because $\rho < \rho_0$ higher up, meaning a higher actual speed is required to produce the same dynamic pressure.

## 2.5 True Airspeed from Indicated Airspeed

Using the fact that dynamic pressure is conserved between sea-level and actual flight conditions:

$$V_{TAS} = V_{IAS} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}}$$

Or equivalently using temperatures (since $\rho/\rho_0 = T_0/T$):

$$V_{TAS} = V_{IAS} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{T_0}}$$

## 2.6 Mach Number Relationship

$$M = \frac{V_{TAS}}{a}$$

where the local speed of sound is given by:

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T}$$

with $\gamma = 1.4$ for air.

## 2.7 Altimetry — Pressure-Altitude Relationship (ISA Model)

The altimeter is essentially a **sensitive barometer** that converts the measured static pressure $P_s$ into altitude using the ISA hydrostatic model. In the troposphere ($h \le 11 \text{ km}$):

$$P_s = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{L \cdot h}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{R \cdot L}}$$

Inverting for altitude:

$$h = \frac{T_0}{L} \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_s}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{R \cdot L}{g}}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Formula Box
> The numerical exponent $\frac{g}{R \cdot L} = 5.2559 \approx 5.256$ is a frequently asked value in KTU numericals. The inverse form is the form you actually need for solving altitude-from-pressure problems.

## 2.8 Vertical Speed — Capacitor-Based Measurement

The VSI contains a **calibrated leak (capillary tube)** that connects the static pressure capsule to a sealed **aneroid reservoir (capacitor)**. The pressure differential across the capacitor is proportional to the **rate of change of static pressure**, which corresponds to vertical speed:

$$\text{Rate of Climb} \propto \frac{dP_s}{dt}$$

A sudden climb rate change creates a momentary pressure differential; the leak slowly equalizes it back. The instantaneous pressure differential across the leak drives the VSI needle.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial aviation:** Mandatory for IFR (Instrument Flight Rules) operations; the entire IFR air traffic control infrastructure relies on pilots reporting accurate altitude and airspeed.
- **Military aviation:** Critical for Mach limiting, g-load calculations, and stall protection.
- **Flight testing:** Pitot-static data is logged to validate aerodynamic performance and CFD models.
- **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs):** Miniature Pitot-static probes (often MEMS-based) are used for autonomous flight stabilization.

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $P_t = P_s + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Total pressure from airflow | Pa |
| 2 | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = P_t - P_s$ | Dynamic pressure | Pa |
| 3 | $V_{IAS} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_0}}$ | ASI dial calculation | m/s |
| 4 | $V_{TAS} = V_{IAS} \cdot \sqrt{\tfrac{\rho_0}{\rho}}$ | True airspeed | m/s |
| 5 | $V_{TAS} = V_{IAS} \cdot \sqrt{\tfrac{T}{T_0}}$ | TAS via temperature ratio | m/s |
| 6 | $M = V_{TAS}/a$ | Mach number | dimensionless |
| 7 | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Local speed of sound | m/s |
| 8 | $P_s = P_0 \left(1 - \tfrac{Lh}{T_0}\right)^{5.256}$ | Pressure from altitude (troposphere) | Pa |
| 9 | $h = \tfrac{T_0}{L}\left[1 - \left(\tfrac{P_s}{P_0}\right)^{0.1903}\right]$ | Altitude from pressure | m |
| 10 | $h_s = h_{ref} + \tfrac{T_0}{L}\left[1 - \left(\tfrac{P_s}{P_{ref}}\right)^{0.1903}\right]$ | Pressure altitude with reference setting | m |
| 11 | ROC $\propto dP_s/dt$ | Vertical speed indicator | m/s |
| 12 | $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287.05$ J/(kg·K) | Air constants | — |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Pressure from Bernoulli's Equation

**Statement:** Derive the relationship between stagnation pressure $P_t$, static pressure $P_s$, and free-stream velocity $V$ in an incompressible, steady, inviscid airflow.

**Starting Equation — Bernoulli's Equation along a streamline:**

$$P + \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the two points of interest on the streamline:**

- **Point 1 (far upstream, undisturbed flow):** $P_1 = P_s$, $V_1 = V_{TAS}$, $z_1 = z_a$
- **Point 2 (at the Pitot tube stagnation point):** $P_2 = P_t$, $V_2 = 0$, $z_2 = z_a$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between Points 1 and 2:**

Since the Pitot tube inlet is essentially at the same altitude as the freestream measurement point, the hydrostatic term $\rho g z$ cancels out:

$$P_s + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{TAS}^2 + \rho g z_a = P_t + \frac{1}{2} \rho (0)^2 + \rho g z_a$$

**Step 3 — Cancel common terms:**

The terms $\rho g z_a$ and $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho(0)^2$ both vanish:

$$P_s + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{TAS}^2 = P_t$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the standard form:**

$$\boxed{P_t = P_s + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{TAS}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the dynamic pressure:**

$$\boxed{q \equiv P_t - P_s = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{TAS}^2}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The total pressure is the **sum of the static pressure (potential energy term) and the dynamic pressure (kinetic energy per unit volume)**. When the flow stagnates, all kinetic energy is converted to a pressure rise, increasing $P_t$ above $P_s$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Indicated Airspeed from Dynamic Pressure

**Statement:** Derive the formula for indicated airspeed as read on a typical ASI dial.

**Step 1 — Start with dynamic pressure definition:**

$$q = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{TAS}^2$$

**Step 2 — The ASI diaphragm measures $\Delta P$ in a sealed capsule, then a mechanical linkage converts $\Delta P$ into a needle deflection:**

The mechanism assumes a **fixed reference density** $\rho_0$ (the sea-level standard value $1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

**Step 3 — Equate the measured dynamic pressure to the indicated speed expression:**

$$V_{IAS} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P_{measured}}{\rho_0}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta P_{measured} = P_t - P_s$:**

$$\boxed{V_{IAS} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_t - P_s)}{\rho_0}}}$$

**Critical Insight:** The ASI has **no knowledge** of the actual flight density. Even if the aircraft flies at 30,000 ft where $\rho \approx 0.46 \text{ kg/m}^3$, the ASI still uses $\rho_0$ in its calculation. This is why the **same IAS at different altitudes corresponds to different TAS values**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — True Airspeed from Indicated Airspeed

**Statement:** Express $V_{TAS}$ in terms of $V_{IAS}$ and the density ratio.

**Step 1 — Write the dynamic pressure relation in two forms:**

At the actual flight conditions:

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{TAS}^2$$

The ASI infers the same $\Delta P$ as:

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho_0 V_{IAS}^2$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions for the same measured $\Delta P$:**

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{TAS}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho_0 V_{IAS}^2$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by canceling the factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$:**

$$\rho V_{TAS}^2 = \rho_0 V_{IAS}^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{TAS}$:**

$$V_{TAS}^2 = V_{IAS}^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_0}{\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**

$$\boxed{V_{TAS} = V_{IAS} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}}}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative form using ideal gas law** ($P = \rho R T$, with $P$ assumed equal at both reference levels for the same $P_s$):

$$\frac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \frac{T}{T_0}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute to obtain the temperature form:**

$$\boxed{V_{TAS} = V_{IAS} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{T_0}}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Altitude from Static Pressure (ISA Troposphere Model)

**Statement:** Derive the relationship between static pressure and geometric altitude in the troposphere.

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic equation (vertical equilibrium of air column):**

$$\frac{dP}{dh} = -\rho \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Use the ideal gas law to eliminate density:**

$$\rho = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute density into the hydrostatic equation:**

$$\frac{dP}{dh} = -\frac{P \cdot g}{R \cdot T}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ISA temperature model** (linear decrease with altitude, valid for $h \le 11$ km):

$$T = T_0 - L \cdot h$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the differential equation:**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R} \cdot \frac{dh}{T_0 - L \cdot h}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from sea level ($P_0, h=0$) to altitude $h$ (pressure $P_s$):**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P_s} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R} \int_{0}^{h} \frac{dh}{T_0 - L \cdot h}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the left integral:**

$$\ln \left(\frac{P_s}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{g}{R} \int_{0}^{h} \frac{dh}{T_0 - L \cdot h}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the right integral** using substitution $u = T_0 - Lh$, $du = -L\,dh$:

$$\int \frac{dh}{T_0 - Lh} = -\frac{1}{L} \ln(T_0 - Lh)$$

**Step 9 — Combine results:**

$$\ln \left(\frac{P_s}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{g}{R} \cdot \left[-\frac{1}{L} \ln(T_0 - Lh) + \frac{1}{L} \ln(T_0)\right]$$

**Step 10 — Simplify the logarithmic expression:**

$$\ln \left(\frac{P_s}{P_0}\right) = \frac{g}{R \cdot L} \ln \left(\frac{T_0 - Lh}{T_0}\right) = \frac{g}{R \cdot L} \ln \left(1 - \frac{Lh}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 11 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$\frac{P_s}{P_0} = \left(1 - \frac{Lh}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{R \cdot L}}$$

**Step 12 — Final form for pressure-from-altitude:**

$$\boxed{P_s = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{Lh}{T_0}\right)^{5.2559}}$$

**Step 13 — Invert to solve for altitude:**

$$\frac{P_s}{P_0} = \left(1 - \frac{Lh}{T_0}\right)^{5.2559}$$

$$\left(\frac{P_s}{P_0}\right)^{1/5.2559} = 1 - \frac{Lh}{T_0}$$

$$\frac{Lh}{T_0} = 1 - \left(\frac{P_s}{P_0}\right)^{0.19026}$$

**Step 14 — Final form for altitude-from-pressure:**

$$\boxed{h = \frac{T_0}{L} \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_s}{P_0}\right)^{0.1903}\right]}$$

**Verification check:** At $h = 0$, $P_s = P_0$ and the expression correctly gives $h = 0$. At $h = 11000$ m (tropopause), substituting gives $P_s \approx 22632$ Pa, which is the standard tropopause pressure.

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem (KTU-Style Numerical):** An aircraft is flying at an altitude where the static pressure is measured to be $P_s = 500 \text{ hPa} = 50000 \text{ Pa}$. The Pitot tube measures a total pressure of $P_t = 52395 \text{ Pa}$. The ambient temperature at that altitude is $T = -10 \text{ °C} = 263.15 \text{ K}$. Calculate:

1. The indicated airspeed
2. The true airspeed
3. The pressure altitude

**Given:**
- $P_t = 52395$ Pa
- $P_s = 50000$ Pa
- $T = 263.15$ K
- $P_0 = 101325$ Pa
- $T_0 = 288.15$ K
- $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $R = 287.05 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- $L = 0.0065 \text{ K/m}$
- $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Solution Part (a) — Indicated Airspeed:**

**Step 1 — Calculate dynamic pressure:**

$$\Delta P = P_t - P_s = 52395 - 50000 = 2395 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply IAS formula:**

$$V_{IAS} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2395}{1.225}} = \sqrt{3910.20} = 62.53 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to knots** (1 m/s = 1.94384 knots):

$$V_{IAS} = 62.53 \times 1.94384 \approx 121.5 \text{ knots}$$

**Solution Part (b) — True Airspeed:**

**Step 1 — Calculate density using ideal gas law:**

$$\rho = \frac{P_s}{R \cdot T} = \frac{50000}{287.05 \times 263.15} = \frac{50000}{75528.2} = 0.6620 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply TAS formula:**

$$V_{TAS} = V_{IAS} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}} = 62.53 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.225}{0.6620}} = 62.53 \cdot \sqrt{1.8505} = 62.53 \times 1.3603 = 85.06 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Verification via direct method:**

$$V_{TAS} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2395}{0.6620}} = \sqrt{7235.65} = 85.06 \text{ m/s} \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Alternative via temperature:**

$$V_{TAS} = V_{IAS} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{T_0}} = 62.53 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{263.15}{288.15}} = 62.53 \cdot \sqrt{0.9132} = 62.53 \times 0.9556 = 59.75 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING] Critical Note on Step 4
> The temperature-only formula assumes **constant static pressure between the sea-level reference and the actual altitude**, which is a simplification. The exact formula uses the density ratio. In KTU numericals, **use the density form unless explicitly told the temperatures refer to the same pressure level**.

**Solution Part (c) — Pressure Altitude:**

**Step 1 — Apply altitude-from-pressure formula:**

$$h = \frac{T_0}{L} \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_s}{P_0}\right)^{0.1903}\right]$$

$$h = \frac{288.15}{0.0065} \left[1 - \left(\frac{50000}{101325}\right)^{0.1903}\right]$$

$$h = 44330.77 \left[1 - (0.4935)^{0.1903}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the pressure ratio exponent:**

$$(0.4935)^{0.1903} = e^{0.1903 \times \ln(0.4935)} = e^{0.1903 \times (-0.7062)} = e^{-0.1344} = 0.8743$$

**Step 3 — Complete the calculation:**

$$h = 44330.77 \times (1 - 0.8743) = 44330.77 \times 0.1257 = 5573 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to feet** (1 m = 3.28084 ft):

$$h = 5573 \times 3.28084 \approx 18284 \text{ ft}$$

> [!NOTE] KTU Examiner's Note
> This altitude corresponds to a standard **Flight Level of approximately FL180** in aviation parlance. The pressure altitude is the altitude read on the altimeter when set to 1013.25 hPa.

## 4.3 Fault-Condition Decision Tree — Common Pitot-Static Failures

This flowchart helps students understand **what happens when components fail**, a frequent KTU viva and exam topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Pitot Tube Blocked?}:::dec
    Q2{Static Port Blocked?}:::dec
    A1[ASI reads Zero - No dynamic pressure]:::fault
    A2[Altimeter freezes at last value]:::fault
    A3[VSI indicates zero]:::fault
    A4[ASI reads High and increases with altitude]:::fault
    A5[Altimeter reads High in climb low in descent]:::fault
    A6[VSI shows opposite sign of actual ROC]:::fault
    A7[All three instruments become unreliable]:::fault
    A8[Switch to alternate static source]:::action
    A9[Activate pitot heat and check drain]:::action
    A10[Descend below freezing level]:::action

    Q1 -- Yes --> A1
    Q1 -- Yes --> A2
    Q1 -- Yes --> A3
    Q1 -- Yes --> A10
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> A4
    Q2 -- Yes --> A5
    Q2 -- Yes --> A6
    Q2 -- Yes --> A8
    Q2 -- No --> A7

    classDef dec fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,color:#000
    classDef fault fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT] Blockage Symmetry Insight
> The decision tree above is a **classic KTU viva question**. The two failure modes produce **mirror-image symptoms** because the ASI depends on the **difference** $P_t - P_s$, so blocking either $P_t$ or $P_s$ (but not both) reduces the difference to zero or to a wrong value. A blocked static port causes the ASI to behave like an **Machmeter** (speed-dependent on altitude) — a famous flight test phenomenon.
