# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define pressure gauge. List any four types of pressure gauges used in aircraft.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A pressure gauge is an instrument used to measure the pressure of a fluid (gas or liquid) by converting the pressure-induced force into a measurable mechanical or electrical output.

**Four types used in aircraft:**
1. **Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge** — used for engine oil, hydraulic, and fuel pressure.
2. **Diaphragm Pressure Gauge** — used for low-pressure and differential measurements.
3. **Capsule (Aneroid) Pressure Gauge** — used in altimeters and vertical speed indicators.
4. **Bellows Pressure Gauge** — used in pressure switches and regulators.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning the **specific aircraft application** of each type fetches full 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between absolute pressure and gauge pressure. Why is absolute pressure used in altimeters?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Absolute Pressure** | **Gauge Pressure** |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Perfect vacuum (zero) | Local atmospheric pressure |
| Formula | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} + P_{\text{gauge}}$ | $P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}$ |
| Altitude usage | Always used in altimeters | Used in engine and hydraulic gauges |
| Sign | Always positive | Can be negative (vacuum) |

**Why altimeter uses absolute pressure:** Altimetry is based on the **ISA model**, which gives pressure as a function of geometric height above mean sea level. Since altitude is independent of the *local* weather, altimeters must reference a **fixed datum (vacuum)** to give correct altitude readings. The sealed **aneroid capsule** in the altimeter is evacuated and responds to the *absolute* pressure of the atmosphere.

---

## 📝 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **C-type Bourdon tube pressure gauge**. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and aircraft applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A U-tube manometer is used to measure the pressure of oxygen in an aircraft cylinder. The manometric fluid is mercury with density **13,600 kg/m³**, and the height difference is **0.35 m**. Find the gauge pressure in kPa. If the well-type variant is used (well area = 40 cm², tube area = 0.5 cm²), what is the corrected reading? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A C-type Bourdon tube consists of:
- A **flattened, curved metal tube** of elliptical cross-section (a × b, with a > b).
- One end is **fixed and sealed** to the pressure inlet.
- The other end is **free** and connected via a **linkage and gear mechanism** to a pointer.
- The whole assembly is housed in a case with a **calibrated dial**.

**Working (3 marks):**
- The pressurized fluid enters the fixed end.
- Internal pressure acts on the **inner walls** of the elliptical cross-section.
- The pressure tends to **circularize** the cross-section (because a circle encloses the maximum area for the same perimeter).
- As the cross-section becomes more circular, the tube tries to **straighten**, displacing the free end.
- This **small linear displacement** is amplified by the **sector-and-pinion gear train** to rotate the pointer through a large angular deflection.
- The pointer reads the pressure directly on a calibrated dial.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Simple, rugged, and reliable.
- Wide pressure range (vacuum to ~7000 bar).
- Reasonable accuracy (±0.5% FS).
- Direct reading, low cost.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Hysteresis and temperature errors.
- Not ideal for highly viscous or corrosive fluids.
- Sensitive to mechanical shock and vibration.

**Aircraft Applications:** Engine oil pressure, hydraulic system pressure, fuel line pressure, oxygen cylinder monitoring.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $\rho_m = 13,600$ kg/m³
- $h = 0.35$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Basic U-tube manometer pressure**

$$P = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h = 13,600 \times 9.81 \times 0.35$$

$$P = 46,696.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 46.70 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Well-type correction factor**

$$h_{\text{true}} = h \cdot \left(1 + \frac{a}{A}\right)$$

Converting areas: $A = 40 \text{ cm}^2 = 40 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $a = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²

$$\frac{a}{A} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-4}}{40 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.5}{40} = 0.0125$$

$$h_{\text{true}} = 0.35 \cdot (1 + 0.0125) = 0.35 \cdot 1.0125 = 0.3544 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Corrected pressure**

$$P_{\text{corrected}} = 13,600 \times 9.81 \times 0.3544$$

$$P_{\text{corrected}} = 47,278 \text{ Pa} \approx 47.28 \text{ kPa}$$

**Result:**
- U-tube reading: **46.70 kPa**
- Well-type corrected: **47.28 kPa**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **diaphragm pressure gauge** with a labelled diagram. Derive the expression for the maximum deflection at the centre of a clamped circular diaphragm. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A dead-weight pressure tester has a piston of effective area $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m². What total mass (in kg) must be loaded to generate a pressure of **2.5 bar**? If the piston rotates during operation, what is the actual pressure if the friction torque correction reduces the effective force by 0.3%? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Construction (1.5 marks):**
- A **thin circular metallic disc** (phosphor bronze, stainless steel, or beryllium copper) is clamped between two flanges.
- For low pressures, the disc is **corrugated** to increase sensitivity.
- The lower side is exposed to the process pressure.
- A **push rod** at the centre transmits the diaphragm deflection to a **lever and pointer mechanism**.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
- Pressure $P$ acts on one side of the diaphragm.
- The diaphragm **deflects** by an amount $y$, maximum at the centre, zero at the clamped edge.
- The deflection is linearly proportional to $P$ (for small deflections, within the elastic limit).
- The push rod moves the lever, which rotates the pointer on a calibrated dial.

**Derivation of centre deflection (3 marks):**

For a clamped circular diaphragm of radius $a$, thickness $t$, subjected to uniform pressure $P$:

The classical plate theory (Timoshenko, 1959) gives the deflection at radius $r$ as:

$$y(r) = \frac{3P(1-\nu^2)}{16Et^3} \cdot (a^2 - r^2)^2$$

This is obtained by solving the **biharmonic equation** for plate bending with clamped boundary conditions:

$$\nabla^4 w = \frac{P}{D}$$

where the **flexural rigidity** is:

$$D = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

At the **centre** ($r = 0$):

$$y_{\max} = \frac{3P(1-\nu^2)}{16Et^3} \cdot a^4 = \frac{P \cdot a^4}{5.33 \cdot D}$$

(Exact form: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{Pa^4}{64D}$, which matches the expression above.)

$$\boxed{y_{\max} = \frac{3 \cdot P \cdot a^4 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)}{16 \cdot E \cdot t^3}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The 1/64D form is what the official answer key uses. Always write both forms in the exam.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $A_e = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $P = 2.5$ bar = $2.5 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Find mass for nominal pressure**

$$P = \frac{m \cdot g}{A_e} \implies m = \frac{P \cdot A_e}{g}$$

$$m = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-4})}{9.81} = \frac{25}{9.81}$$

$$m = 2.548 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply friction correction (0.3% reduction in effective weight)**

The rotation creates friction that **partially supports the weight**, so the pressure is **lower** than calculated.

$$P_{\text{actual}} = P_{\text{nominal}} \times (1 - 0.003)$$

$$P_{\text{actual}} = 2.5 \times 0.997 = 2.4925 \text{ bar}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students forget to **convert bar to Pa** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa).
> 2. Students confuse the **direction of friction correction** — friction *reduces* effective weight, hence reduces actual pressure.
> 3. The **0.3% is small** — but examiners love testing if you understand the physics behind it.
> 4. Always mention the **re-entrant piston correction** if asked about a *real* dead-weight tester.

---

## 🧠 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in Module 3 (Pressure Gauges):**
> 1. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure** — A single mistake here can lose 2-3 marks in any numerical.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Pressure must always be in Pa, kPa, bar, or psi consistently. Mixing units = full mark loss.
> 3. **Skipping diagrams** — A Bourdon or diaphragm gauge question *without* a labelled diagram = -2 marks minimum.
> 4. **Not mentioning aircraft applications** — Examiner looks for **aeronautical context**. Always tie the gauge to an aircraft system.
> 5. **Memorizing without understanding the working** — Students who memorize the Bourdon formula without understanding the "circularization" principle lose marks on "Why does the tube straighten?" type questions.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 — Pressure Gauges — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Bourdon Tube** is the most common aircraft pressure gauge. Works on the principle that an elliptical cross-section tends to become circular under pressure, straightening the curved tube.
- ✅ **Tip displacement** of a C-type Bourdon: $\delta = \dfrac{P \cdot b(a-b) \cdot R^3 \cdot \phi_0^2}{E \cdot I}$
- ✅ **Diaphragm gauge** deflection is linear with pressure at small deflections: $y_{\max} = \dfrac{3Pa^4(1-\nu^2)}{16Et^3}$
- ✅ **Bellows** have high axial sensitivity — used in pressure regulators and switches.
- ✅ **Capsule (Aneroid)** is the heart of the **altimeter and VSI** — uses **absolute** pressure reference.
- ✅ **U-tube manometer**: $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$. **Mercury** for high pressure, **water** for low pressure.
- ✅ **Inclined manometer** enhances sensitivity by a factor of $1/\sin\theta$.
- ✅ **Well-type manometer** correction: $h_{\text{true}} = h(1 + a/A)$.
- ✅ **Dead-weight tester** is the **primary standard** for pressure calibration: $P = mg/A_e$, accuracy ±0.01%.
- ✅ **Absolute pressure** = gauge pressure + atmospheric pressure. **Altimeters use absolute.**
- ✅ **Engine and hydraulic gauges** use **gauge pressure** (referenced to atmosphere).
- ✅ **ISA standard atmosphere** pressure at sea level = **101,325 Pa = 1.01325 bar**.
- ✅ **Standard gravity** $g = 9.80665$ m/s² for all pressure calculations.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip (Module 3):** If you can draw a labelled Bourdon tube, write the tip-displacement formula, state the manometer equation, and explain the dead-weight tester principle — you have covered ~80% of the marks that can be asked in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tachometer: Mechanical and Electrical, Modern Control Systems - Fly by Wire Systems and Auto Pilot Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tachometer: Mechanical and Electrical, Modern Control Systems - Fly by Wire Systems and Auto Pilot Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 AIRCRAFT TACHOMETER — THE FUNDAMENTAL DEFINITION

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Tachometer** is a precision flight instrument designed to measure, display, and transmit the *rotational speed* of an aircraft engine's main shaft or rotor system. The indicated value is conventionally expressed in **percentage of maximum rated revolutions per minute (% RPM)** for piston engines, and as **RPM (N₁, N₂)**, **Ng (gas generator RPM)**, or **Nf (fan RPM)** for turbojet, turbofan, and turboshaft engines.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework, tachometers are classified into two fundamental families based on their energy conversion mechanism:

| Family | Energy Conversion Path | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Tachometers** | Rotational motion → Centrifugal force or Spring tension → Mechanical pointer | Piston-engine aircraft, older turboprops |
| **Electrical Tachometers** | Rotational motion → EMF / Magnetic flux variation → Voltage signal → Pointer / Digital readout | All modern aircraft, including FBW platforms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Within Module 3, the tachometer is positioned as a *transducer* — a device that bridges a mechanical domain (rotational speed) with an indication domain (pointer or digital readout). The same transducer principle underpins engine-governor feedback loops, autothrottle systems, and Fly-By-Wire thrust-management computers.

---

## 1.2 INTUITIVE ANALOGY — "THE BICYCLE SPEEDOMETER REIMAGINED"

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of a bicycle speedometer. As the wheel spins, a small magnet attached to a spoke passes a pickup coil — the faster you pedal, the more pulses per second you generate, and the gauge needle climbs. An aircraft tachometer is simply the *engineering-grade, aviation-certified* version of this exact idea, but instead of a bicycle wheel, it senses the main **engine crankshaft** or **turbine shaft** turning at thousands of revolutions per minute.

In a mechanical tachometer, the "magnet and pickup" is replaced by spinning **centrifugal flyweights** or a **clock-spring governor**. In an electrical tachometer, the bicycle's magnet is upgraded to a multi-pole **permanent-magnet rotor** or a **drag cup** driven by eddy currents, generating a precise alternating or direct voltage proportional to RPM.

---

## 1.3 FLY-BY-WIRE (FBW) SYSTEM — DEFINITION

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Fly-By-Wire (FBW) system** is a digitally mediated flight control architecture in which the pilot's mechanical input at the control column / side-stick is converted into **electrical signals** by position transducers (Linear Variable Differential Transformers, LVDTs; or Rotary Variable Differential Transformers, RVDTs), processed by redundant **Flight Control Computers (FCCs)**, and used to command electro-hydraulic or electro-mechanical **actuators** that physically move the aerodynamic control surfaces.

The literal meaning of "Fly-By-Wire" is that the control command travels to the surface **by means of an electrical wire** rather than by a direct mechanical linkage (cables, rods, push-pull tubes) as in conventional aircraft.

---

## 1.4 AUTO-PILOT SYSTEM — DEFINITION

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An **Automatic Flight Control System (AFCS)**, popularly known as the **Auto-Pilot**, is an integrated closed-loop control system that automatically stabilises an aircraft about its three rotational axes (roll, pitch, yaw) and executes programmed flight-path commands using feedback from gyroscopic, accelerometric, and air-data sensors driving servo-actuated control surfaces.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Cruise Control for Aircraft":**
> Imagine driving a modern car on a highway with **Adaptive Cruise Control**. The car uses radar and cameras to *sense* its surroundings, a computer *decides* the throttle and steering corrections, and actuators *apply* them — all without your feet touching the pedals. The aircraft auto-pilot is precisely this idea extended to three dimensions, using attitude gyros, heading references, and altitude sensors to "drive" the aircraft while the pilot monitors.

---

## 1.5 KEY PHYSICAL CONSTANTS, STANDARDS & METRICS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics to Memorise:**
> - **Earth's gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$ — *use this for acceleration conversions in autopilot error budgets.*
> - **Standard sea-level pressure:** $P_0 = 1013.25 \text{ hPa}$
> - **Avionics data bus standards:** **MIL-STD-1553B** (military FBW), **ARINC 429** (commercial transport), **ARINC 664 / AFDX** (A380, B787, A350)
> - **Tachometer standard outputs:** AC voltage ~$0-100$ V at **400 Hz** (military) or **$26$ V** at variable frequency (civil)
> - **FBW redundancy standard:** No fewer than **3 or 4 dissimilar Flight Control Computers** operating in parallel (Triple Modular Redundancy, TMR; or Quadruple Modular Redundancy, QMR)
> - **Sweep frequency for autopilot gain analysis:** typically **$0.1$ to $10$ rad/s**

---

## 1.6 WHY THIS TOPIC MATTERS IN KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Anchor Note:**
> The triad of *Tachometer → FBW → Auto-Pilot* forms a logical chain: the **tachometer** is the engine-state *sensor*; the **auto-pilot** is a *system-level* control loop using aircraft-state sensors; and **Fly-By-Wire** is the *technology backbone* that allows both engine FADECs and autopilot computers to share a digital backbone. Mastering all three is essential for the **CO3** of PCAOT205 ("Apply the principles of aircraft instrumentation and control systems to real-world avionics").

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 MECHANICAL TACHOMETERS — OPERATIONAL THEORY

### 2.1.1 Centrifugal (Pendulum-Type) Tachometer

> This is the classic mechanical RPM gauge found in light aircraft (Cessna 172, Piper PA-28 families).

**Operating Principle:**
The tachometer is mechanically coupled to the engine crankshaft via a flexible drive cable (Tachometer drive — typically a **0.0625 inch square** shaft rotating at engine speed or **1/2 engine speed** depending on the engine type). Inside the instrument, the drive shaft spins a pair of **centrifugal flyweights** pivoted on links.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. The drive shaft rotates a **rotor plate** carrying two flyweights held by link pins.
2. As RPM increases, the **centrifugal force** on each flyweight ($F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$) pulls the weights radially outward against the restraining force of a calibrated **helical control spring**.
3. The outward radial motion is converted, via a **linkage mechanism**, into an axial translation of a **sliding collar** on the drive shaft.
4. The collar's translation rotates a **magnetically coupled pointer** (or in some instruments, directly drives the pointer shaft through gearing).
5. The pointer indicates RPM on a calibrated dial — usually **$0$ to $3500$ RPM** for piston engines, scaled with a **green arc** for normal operating range and a **red line** for maximum permissible RPM.

**Physical Law Governing the Design:**

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = m \cdot \left(\frac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)^2 \cdot r$$

The spring restraining force is linear in displacement: $F_s = k \cdot x$. Equating at equilibrium:

$$k \cdot x = m \cdot \left(\frac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)^2 \cdot r$$

Solving for the indicator displacement $x$:

$$x = \frac{m \cdot r}{k} \cdot \left(\frac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)^2$$

This **quadratic dependence** of $x$ on $N$ is why the dial markings are non-linear (compressed at low RPM, spread at high RPM).

> [!TIP]
> **Why "quadratic"? — Real-world significance:**
> Because the restoring force is linear (Hooke's law) but the disturbing force is quadratic in $\omega$, the dial must be hand-calibrated or pre-distorted during manufacture. Designers choose the spring constant $k$ to keep $x$ within the geometric limits of the pointer travel — typically $\pm 90°$ for a robust instrument.

### 2.1.2 Clock-Type (Chronometric) Tachometer

> Used in large piston radial engines and as a portable hand-cranked RPM gauge.

**Operating Principle:**
This instrument uses a **clockwork escapement mechanism**. The drive shaft winds a calibrated **spring governor** that, combined with a centrifugal element, controls the rate at which a clock-driven pointer completes one revolution.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Drive shaft rotates a small **gear train** that drives a **centrifugal governor**.
2. The governor regulates a **clock escapement** such that the pointer completes one full revolution in a time inversely proportional to engine speed.
3. The pointer reads directly in RPM because its angular velocity is calibrated against the engine RPM.

**Mathematical Relation:**

$$N = \frac{60 \cdot k_{\text{cal}}}{T_{\text{rev}}}$$

where $T_{\text{rev}}$ is the time for one pointer revolution and $k_{\text{cal}}$ is the calibration constant of the escapement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why used less today:** Mechanical clock-tachometers are accurate but bulky, expensive, and vulnerable to vibration. The electrical tachometer has effectively replaced them on modern aircraft.

### 2.1.3 Friction / Chronometric Hand-Held Tachometer

> Common in maintenance hangars. A rubber cone is pressed against the rotating shaft, friction drives an internal gear train, and a centrifugal governor regulates the displayed value.

---

## 2.2 ELECTRICAL TACHOMETERS — OPERATIONAL THEORY

### 2.2.1 DC Tachometer (DC Generator / Tachogenerator)

**Operating Principle:**
A **permanent-magnet DC generator** is mechanically coupled to the engine shaft. The induced EMF is, by Faraday's law, exactly proportional to shaft rotational speed.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. The rotating armature (or, in modern designs, the rotating permanent-magnet field) cuts the magnetic flux lines of a stationary or rotating field structure.
2. The induced EMF is rectified (if the field rotates) or is naturally DC (if the armature rotates in a stationary field with a commutator).
3. The DC voltage is fed to a **moving-coil voltmeter** (d'Arsonval movement) calibrated directly in RPM.
4. The polarity indicates direction of rotation (used in twin-engine aircraft to verify both engines are turning the same way).

**The Governing Equation:**

$$E = K \cdot \Phi \cdot N$$

where:
- $E$ = generated EMF (volts)
- $K$ = machine constant (depends on armature winding turns, number of poles, and construction)
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux per pole (Webers) — **held constant** by permanent magnets
- $N$ = shaft speed (rev/sec; multiply by 60 for RPM)

For a permanent-magnet machine, $\Phi$ is constant, so $E \propto N$ — a **linear** relationship, which is a major advantage over the centrifugal tachometer's quadratic law.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> The DC tachogenerator is still used as a **servo rate feedback** element in autopilot servo loops because its output voltage is a clean, linear analogue of rotation rate. It is also used in **engine-propeller synchroniser** systems to match propeller RPMs on multi-engine aircraft.

### 2.2.2 AC Tachometer (AC Alternator / Magneto-Type)

**Operating Principle:**
A small **permanent-magnet rotor** with multiple poles (typically 4, 6, 8, or 12) is driven by the engine. The stator carries a three-phase or single-phase winding. The induced AC voltage has a **frequency directly proportional to RPM** and an **amplitude also proportional to RPM**.

**Frequency Equation:**

$$f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120}$$

where $P$ = number of poles, $N$ = RPM, and $f$ = electrical frequency in Hz.

**Voltage Equation:**

$$E_{\text{rms}} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_{\text{turns}} \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where are AC tachometers used?**
> They are the heart of the **engine speed and power** system in modern turbofan aircraft. The **N₁ fan RPM** and **N₂ compressor RPM** signals in a CFM56, PW4000, or GE90 engine are derived from AC tachometer pickups (variable-reluctance or Hall-effect) feeding the **FADEC (Full Authority Digital Engine Controller)**.

### 2.2.3 Drag-Cup (Induction) Tachometer

> This is the standard *EDM-style* electrical tachometer found in light-aircraft instrument panels.

**Operating Principle:**
A two-phase stator winding creates a **rotating magnetic field** when energised from a 400 Hz supply. An **aluminium (or copper) drag cup** is mounted on the engine shaft. As the rotating field drags the conductive cup, **eddy currents** are induced in the cup, and the resulting torque on the cup is opposed by a control spring. The cup's angular displacement is proportional to engine speed.

**Governing Equation:**

$$\theta = K_{\text{cup}} \cdot \omega_{\text{rotor}}$$

where $\theta$ is the drag-cup angular displacement and $K_{\text{cup}}$ is the calibration constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl — The Three Tachometer "Families":**
> 1. **Centrifugal (mechanical)** — quadratic, flyweight + spring, $x \propto N^2$
> 2. **Magneto / Drag-cup (electrical-AC)** — linear, magnetic coupling, $V \propto N$
> 3. **DC Tachogenerator (electrical-DC)** — linear, Faraday EMF, $E = K \Phi N$

---

## 2.3 FLY-BY-WIRE (FBW) SYSTEMS — DEEP THEORY

### 2.3.1 The Four-Block FBW Architecture

A modern FBW system is composed of four distinct functional blocks:

1. **Sensing Block (Pilot Input Transducers):** Converts cockpit control-column / side-stick displacement into electrical signals. Typical transducers: **LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) for travel, **RVDT** (Rotary VDT) for rotation, and **strain gauges** for force-feel.
2. **Computation Block (Flight Control Computer — FCC):** Multiple redundant digital processors running *control laws* — the algorithms that translate "pilot wants 30° bank" into "elevator deflection $\delta_e$ and aileron deflection $\delta_a$ as a function of Mach, altitude, $C_L$, $n_z$, and load factor limit."
3. **Actuation Block:** **Electro-hydraulic servo actuators (EHA)** or **electro-backup hydraulic actuators (EBHA)** that physically deflect the control surface.
4. **Feedback Block (Surface Position Sensors):** **LVDTs** on the actuator ram report the *actual* surface position back to the FCC to close the loop.

### 2.3.2 Why FBW Replaced Mechanical Linkage

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Justification — The Five Drivers of FBW Adoption:**
> 1. **Weight reduction:** Replacing 1 km of cables, rods, and pulleys with copper wires saves **hundreds of kilograms** in large transports.
> 2. **Relaxed static stability:** FBW allows *inherently unstable* airframes (F-16, F-117, Rafale, Su-57) to be flown safely — the computer provides artificial stability, dramatically improving manoeuvrability.
> 3. **Carefree manoeuvring:** Envelope protection (AoA limit, $g$-limit, overspeed protection) is implemented in software.
> 4. **Reduced maintenance:** No mechanical wear, no cable tensioning, no lubrication points.
> 5. **Automatic reconfiguration:** A jammed or damaged surface is automatically compensated by redistributing commands to remaining surfaces (control allocation).

### 2.3.3 FBW Control Laws — The Mathematics

A simplified *longitudinal* control law (analogous to those in the A320 / F-16) is:

$$\delta_e = K_p \cdot (n_z_{\text{cmd}} - n_z) + K_q \cdot q + K_{\theta} \cdot (\theta_{\text{ref}} - \theta) + K_i \int (n_z_{\text{cmd}} - n_z) \, dt$$

where:
- $\delta_e$ = commanded elevator deflection
- $n_z$ = measured normal load factor (g)
- $n_{z_{\text{cmd}}}$ = pilot-commanded load factor
- $q$ = pitch rate (rad/s)
- $\theta$ = pitch attitude (rad)
- $K_p, K_q, K_{\theta}, K_i$ = control-law gains
- The integral term eliminates steady-state error (auto-trim)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aircraft Generations by Control Type:**
> - **Gen 1 (Pre-1960s):** Mechanical control (cables + rods)
> - **Gen 2 (1960s-1980s):** Fly-By-Wire analogue (e.g., Apollo Lunar Module, F-16A — first production digital FBW, 1973)
> - **Gen 3 (1980s-2000s):** Digital FBW with full envelope protection (A320, 1988)
> - **Gen 4 (2000s-now):** Fly-By-Optics, distributed IMA (Integrated Modular Avionics), and **Power-By-Wire (PBW)** eliminating centralised hydraulics (F-35, A380, B787)

---

## 2.4 AUTO-PILOT SYSTEMS — DEEP THEORY

### 2.4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Basic Auto-Pilot

A single-axis (wing-leveler) autopilot consists of:

1. **Reference Gyro** (e.g., a vertical gyro for pitch and roll) — provides the *desired* attitude reference.
2. **Sensing Gyro** (rate gyro) — measures the *actual* rate of attitude change.
3. **Error Detector / Computer** — computes the difference (error) between desired and actual.
4. **Servo Amplifier** — boosts the error signal to drive the servo.
5. **Servo Motor** — physically deflects the aileron (or elevator / rudder).
6. **Feedback Potentiometer / LVDT** on the control surface — closes the inner servo loop.

### 2.4.2 Auto-Pilot Modes of Operation

| Mode | Function | Sensor Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Wing Leveler** | Maintains wings horizontal | Vertical / rate gyro |
| **Heading Hold** | Holds a selected compass heading | Directional gyro + flux valve |
| **Altitude Hold** | Maintains barometric altitude | Aneroid capsule / static port |
| **Vertical Speed Hold** | Holds constant rate of climb/descent | Vertical speed sensor |
| **VOR Coupling** | Tracks a VOR radial | VOR receiver + CDI |
| **Localiser Coupling** | Tracks ILS localiser | ILS receiver |
| **Glideslope Coupling** | Tracks ILS glideslope | ILS receiver |
| **Autoland (Cat III)** | Automatic landing to flare | Dual ILS + radio altimeter |
| **Yaw Damper** | Dampens Dutch Roll | Rate gyro in yaw axis |

### 2.4.3 Autopilot Transfer Function (Classical Control Form)

For a roll-axis autopilot, the closed-loop transfer function from aileron command $\delta_a$ to roll rate $p$ is:

$$\frac{p(s)}{\delta_a(s)} = \frac{K_a \cdot L_p}{(s + L_p)(s + \tau_a^{-1})}$$

The corresponding *roll-angle* response is:

$$\frac{\phi(s)}{\delta_a(s)} = \frac{K_a \cdot L_p}{s(s + L_p)(s + \tau_a^{-1})}$$

For a *good* autopilot design, the **roll subsidence mode** $L_p$ is the dominant fast pole, and the **actuator time constant** $\tau_a$ is small (typically $\leq 0.1$ s for hydraulic servos).

---

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET / CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every formula you must know for Module 3 of PCAOT205. Memorise these and you can solve 95% of numerical / derivation problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units | Domain of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal force on flyweight | $F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, r$ | $m$=mass, $\omega$=rad/s, $r$=radius | N | Mechanical tachometer |
| 2 | Flyweight angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | $N$ in RPM | rad/s | All rotating systems |
| 3 | Centrifugal Tach displacement | $x = \frac{m r}{k} \left(\frac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)^2$ | $k$ = spring constant | m | Quadratic, $x \propto N^2$ |
| 4 | DC Tachogenerator EMF | $E = K \Phi N$ | $K$=machine constant, $\Phi$=flux, $N$=rev/s | V | Linear, $E \propto N$ |
| 5 | AC Tacho frequency | $f = \frac{P \, N}{120}$ | $P$=poles, $N$=RPM | Hz | AC alternator tachometer |
| 6 | AC EMF (RMS) | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N_t \, \Phi \, k_w$ | $N_t$=turns, $k_w$=winding factor | V | AC machines |
| 7 | Drag-cup displacement | $\theta = K_{\text{cup}} \cdot \omega$ | $K_{\text{cup}}$ = cup constant | rad | Induction tachometer |
| 8 | Roll subsidence mode | $L_p = -\frac{C_{l_p}}{C_{l_{\delta_a}}} \cdot \frac{\rho V S b}{2 I_{xx}}$ | Stability derivative ratio | 1/s | Roll dynamics |
| 9 | Spiral mode (Dutch roll) | $L = \frac{g}{V} \left(\frac{C_{l_\beta} C_{n_r} - C_{n_\beta} C_{l_r}}{C_{l_p} C_{n_r} - C_{l_r} C_{n_p}}\right)$ | Stability derivatives | 1/s | Yaw-roll coupled |
| 10 | FBW longitudinal law | $\delta_e = K_p \Delta n_z + K_q q + K_\theta \Delta \theta + K_i \int \Delta n_z \, dt$ | PID control law | rad | FBW pitch channel |
| 11 | Autopilot transfer function | $\frac{p(s)}{\delta_a(s)} = \frac{K_a L_p}{(s + L_p)(s + 1/\tau_a)}$ | $K_a$ = actuator gain | rad/s/rad | Roll channel |
| 12 | Centrifugal accuracy (worst case) | $\epsilon = \pm \frac{\Delta k}{k} \pm 2 \frac{\Delta r}{r} \pm 2 \frac{\Delta m}{m}$ | Tolerance stack-up | — | Sensitivity analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **Units Mnemonic — "PRMFS":**
> Pressure, RPM, Force, Mass, Speed are the five things you must always verify in any tachometer or autopilot problem. The KTU examiner will often fail an answer purely on missing units.

---

## 2.6 REAL-WORLD UTILITY — WHY THIS MATTERS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Significance of the Module 3 Triad:**
> - **Tachometers** are mandatory flight instruments under **FAR 23 / CS-23** and **FAR 25 / CS-25** regulations — a literal certificate of airworthiness depends on a calibrated tachometer being installed.
> - **Fly-By-Wire** is the enabling technology of every modern combat aircraft (F-22, F-35, Rafale, Eurofighter, Su-57, J-20) and every modern transport (A320 family, A330, A350, B777, B787, C919).
> - **Auto-Pilots** have reduced commercial-jet fatal accident rates by more than **80%** in the en-route phase, according to ICAO safety reports. Long-range twin-engine operations (ETOPS) are *only* possible because of the reliability of modern autopilots.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — CENTRIFUGAL TACHOMETER DISPLACEMENT LAW

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2-mark variant):**
*"A centrifugal tachometer has flyweights of mass $0.020$ kg each, mounted at a radius of $30$ mm from the spindle centre. The control spring has a stiffness of $0.85$ N/m. The linkage is such that one full pointer revolution ($360°$) corresponds to a flyweight radial displacement of $8$ mm. Determine the RPM at which the pointer will read 75% of full-scale, and the full-scale RPM."*

**Step 1 — Identify the physical law.**
Each flyweight is acted upon by two forces: a *centrifugal force* pulling it outward, and a *spring force* pulling it inward. At equilibrium, these are equal in magnitude.

$$F_c = F_s$$

**Step 2 — Write the centrifugal force in terms of the angular velocity.**

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

**Step 3 — Express the spring force as a function of the radial displacement $x$.**

$$F_s = k \cdot x$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two and solve for $x$ as a function of $\omega$.**

$$m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = k \cdot x$$

$$x = \frac{m \, r}{k} \, \omega^2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$ to obtain $x$ in terms of $N$ (RPM).**

$$x = \frac{m \, r}{k} \cdot \left(\frac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)^2$$

**Step 6 — Simplify by computing the constant $\frac{m \, r}{k} \cdot \left(\frac{2 \pi}{60}\right)^2$.**

Let

$$C = \frac{m \, r}{k} \cdot \left(\frac{2 \pi}{60}\right)^2$$

Substituting the given values: $m = 0.020$ kg, $r = 30 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $k = 0.85$ N/m:

$$C = \frac{(0.020)(30 \times 10^{-3})}{0.85} \cdot \left(\frac{2 \pi}{60}\right)^2$$

Step 6a — Compute $\left(\frac{2 \pi}{60}\right)^2$:

$$\left(\frac{2 \pi}{60}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{6.2832}{60}\right)^2 = (0.10472)^2 = 1.0966 \times 10^{-2}$$

Step 6b — Compute $\frac{m \, r}{k}$:

$$\frac{m \, r}{k} = \frac{0.020 \times 0.030}{0.85} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.85} = 7.0588 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

Step 6c — Multiply:

$$C = 7.0588 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0966 \times 10^{-2} = 7.7406 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/RPM}^2$$

Step 6d — Take the inverse square-root to obtain $N(x)$:

$$N = \frac{1}{2 \pi / 60} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k \cdot x}{m \cdot r}} = \frac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.85 \cdot x}{0.020 \times 0.030}}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the *full-scale* displacement $x_{fs}$.**
The full-scale displacement is the radial movement corresponding to a pointer of $360°$ rotation. Given that $8$ mm of radial movement drives a $360°$ pointer rotation:

$$x_{fs} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the *full-scale* RPM using the inverse relation derived in Step 6d.**

$$N_{fs} = \frac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.85 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

Step 8a — Compute the ratio inside the square root:

$$\frac{0.85 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{6.8 \times 10^{-3}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 11.333$$

Step 8b — Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{11.333} = 3.3667$$

Step 8c — Multiply by $\frac{60}{2 \pi} = 9.5493$:

$$N_{fs} = 9.5493 \times 3.3667 = 32.15 \text{ RPM} \quad \text{(this is full-scale)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall!** The 32.15 RPM is correct in this *idealised* model. Real tachometers use a gear-up drive to the flyweight spindle (often 1:1 with engine crankshaft for a 4-stroke piston, sometimes 1:2), so the *engine* RPM at full-scale is 2× or more of the spindle RPM. The examiner deducts marks if you do not clearly state the **gear ratio between the engine crankshaft and the tachometer spindle**.

**Step 9 — Compute the *75% of full-scale* RPM.**
Because the relation is **quadratic**, 75% of pointer deflection is **not** 75% of RPM. The 75% deflection corresponds to a displacement of $x = 0.75 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

$$N_{75\%} = \frac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.85 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

Step 9a — Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{0.85 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{5.1 \times 10^{-3}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.5$$

Step 9b — Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{8.5} = 2.9155$$

Step 9c — Multiply:

$$N_{75\%} = 9.5493 \times 2.9155 = 27.84 \text{ RPM} \quad \text{(spindle)}$$

Step 9d — Express the result as a percentage of full-scale RPM:

$$\frac{N_{75\%}}{N_{fs}} = \frac{27.84}{32.15} = 0.866 = 86.6\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight — the "non-linear dial" problem:**
> A 75% pointer position corresponds to 86.6% of full-scale RPM. The dial is therefore **compressed at the low end and expanded at the high end** relative to a linear scale. This is precisely why mechanical tachometer dials look "spread out" near the red line.

**Step 10 — Generalised sensitivity formula.**
Differentiating the displacement relation with respect to $N$:

$$\frac{dx}{dN} = \frac{m \, r}{k} \cdot 2 \left(\frac{2 \pi}{60}\right)^2 \cdot N$$

$$\frac{dx}{dN} = \frac{2 \, m \, r}{k} \cdot \left(\frac{2 \pi}{60}\right)^2 \cdot N$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sensitivity Insight:**
> The sensitivity $\frac{dx}{dN}$ is **itself proportional to $N$** — the instrument is *more sensitive* at high RPM. This is the reason mechanical tachometers are most accurate at the high end of the scale and least accurate at idle, where small changes in $N$ produce imperceptibly small pointer movements.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — DC TACHOGENERATOR OUTPUT VOLTAGE & LOADING

**Problem Statement:**
*"A DC tachogenerator is rated at 100 V at 3000 RPM. The armature resistance is $R_a = 50 \; \Omega$ and the field flux is constant at $\Phi = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb. A voltmeter of internal resistance $R_v = 950 \; \Omega$ is connected across the armature. Find the indicated voltage at (a) 1500 RPM, (b) 2400 RPM. Comment on the loading error."*

**Step 1 — Determine the machine constant $K$.**
Using the EMF equation $E = K \Phi N$ (with $N$ in rev/sec):

At 3000 RPM, $N = 50$ rev/sec and $E = 100$ V:

$$K = \frac{E}{\Phi \, N} = \frac{100}{5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 50} = \frac{100}{0.25} = 400 \text{ V·s/Wb·rev}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the open-circuit voltage at 1500 RPM.**
At $N = 25$ rev/sec:

$$E_{1500} = K \Phi N = 400 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 50 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Account for the loading by the voltmeter.**
The voltmeter $R_v$ is in parallel with the armature internal resistance $R_a$. The terminal voltage $V_t$ is the open-circuit EMF $E$ reduced by the voltage drop across the internal resistance due to the load current.

$$V_t = E \cdot \frac{R_v}{R_a + R_v}$$

Substituting $R_a = 50 \; \Omega$ and $R_v = 950 \; \Omega$:

$$V_t = E \cdot \frac{950}{50 + 950} = E \cdot \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95 \, E$$

**Step 4 — Compute the *indicated* voltage at 1500 RPM:**

$$V_{t,1500} = 0.95 \times 50 = 47.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the open-circuit voltage at 2400 RPM.**
At $N = 40$ rev/sec:

$$E_{2400} = 400 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 40 = 80 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the indicated voltage at 2400 RPM:**

$$V_{t,2400} = 0.95 \times 80 = 76.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the loading error at each point.**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{E - V_t}{E} \times 100\% = (1 - 0.95) \times 100\% = 5.0\%$$

The loading error is a **constant 5%** because the ratio $R_v/(R_a + R_v)$ is independent of $E$ and $N$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Lesson:**
> This is why practical DC tachogenerators are specified with a **minimum load resistance** (or a high-impedance buffer amplifier in the indicator) — to keep the loading error below a defined accuracy class, e.g. **±1% of full scale** for aviation use.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — ROLL-AXIS AUTOPILOT TRANSFER FUNCTION

**Problem Statement:**
*"A roll-axis autopilot has an actuator modelled as a first-order lag with time constant $\tau_a = 0.05$ s and DC gain $K_a = 2.5$ rad/rad. The aircraft roll subsidence derivative is $L_p = 1.8$ /s. Derive the closed-loop transfer function from aileron command $\delta_a$ to roll rate $p$, and compute the roll-rate time constant."*

**Step 1 — Write the aircraft's bare-airframe roll dynamics.**
The bare airframe roll rate $p$ obeys:

$$\dot{p} = L_p \, p + L_{\delta_a} \, \delta_a$$

Taking the Laplace transform with zero initial condition:

$$s \, P(s) = L_p \, P(s) + L_{\delta_a} \, \Delta_a(s)$$

$$P(s) \, (s - L_p) = L_{\delta_a} \, \Delta_a(s)$$

$$\frac{P(s)}{\Delta_a(s)} = \frac{L_{\delta_a}}{s - L_p}$$

Since $L_p < 0$ (stable airframe), we write $s + \vert L_p \vert$:

$$\frac{P(s)}{\Delta_a(s)} = \frac{L_{\delta_a}}{s + \vert L_p \vert}$$

**Step 2 — Recognise the effective roll-mode constant.**
The roll time constant is $\tau_r = 1/\vert L_p \vert$. With $L_p = 1.8$ /s (and noting the sign convention in many texts treats $L_p$ as positive in the *stable* sense), we have $\tau_r = 1/1.8 = 0.556$ s.

**Step 3 — Write the actuator transfer function.**
A first-order actuator is:

$$\frac{\Delta_a(s)}{\Delta_{a_{\text{cmd}}}(s)} = \frac{K_a}{1 + \tau_a s} = \frac{K_a}{\tau_a s + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Cascade the actuator and the airframe.**

$$\frac{P(s)}{\Delta_{a_{\text{cmd}}}(s)} = \frac{K_a}{\tau_a s + 1} \cdot \frac{L_{\delta_a}}{s + L_p}$$

$$= \frac{K_a \, L_{\delta_a}}{(\tau_a s + 1)(s + L_p)}$$

Expanding the denominator to standard form by dividing by $\tau_a$:

$$= \frac{(K_a \, L_{\delta_a}) / \tau_a}{(s + 1/\tau_a)(s + L_p)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values.**
Take $K_a = 2.5$, $L_{\delta_a} = 5.0$ /s² (representative value), $\tau_a = 0.05$ s, $L_p = 1.8$ /s:

$$\frac{P(s)}{\Delta_{a_{\text{cmd}}}(s)} = \frac{(2.5)(5.0)}{(0.05 s + 1)(s + 1.8)} = \frac{12.5}{(0.05 s + 1)(s + 1.8)}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 20 to standardise:

$$= \frac{250}{(s + 20)(s + 1.8)}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the dominant (slow) pole.**
The two poles are at $s = -20$ and $s = -1.8$. The pole at $-1.8$ is the **roll subsidence pole** (slow) and the pole at $-20$ is the **actuator pole** (fast).

The **roll-rate time constant** is the inverse of the magnitude of the slow pole:

$$\tau_{p,\text{closed}} = \frac{1}{\vert L_p \vert} = \frac{1}{1.8} = 0.556 \text{ s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Pearl — "Pole-Separation Rule":**
> A well-designed autopilot has its **actuator pole at least 5–10× faster** than the *bare airframe* pole. Here, the actuator pole is at 20 /s and the airframe pole is at 1.8 /s — a ratio of **11.1**, satisfying the rule. If the ratio were less than 5, the autopilot would *fight* the airframe and produce oscillatory ("phugoid-like") roll behaviour.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — FLY-BY-WIRE LOAD FACTOR COMMAND LAW

**Problem Statement:**
*"An FBW pitch control law is given by $\delta_e = K_p (n_{z_{\text{cmd}}} - n_z) + K_q \, q + K_\theta (\theta_{\text{ref}} - \theta)$. Given $K_p = 0.6$ rad/g, $K_q = -0.4$ rad·s/rad, $K_\theta = 1.2$ rad/rad, and steady-state trim where $n_{z_{\text{cmd}}} - n_z = 0$, $q = 0$, $\theta_{\text{ref}} - \theta = -0.05$ rad, find the steady-state elevator deflection."*

**Step 1 — Recognise the trim condition.**
At steady-state trim, the integral and derivative terms vanish (or balance), and the static $\theta$ error drives the elevator.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

$$\delta_e = (0.6)(0) + (-0.4)(0) + (1.2)(-0.05)$$

$$\delta_e = 0 + 0 - 0.06$$

$$\delta_e = -0.06 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to degrees.**

$$\delta_e = -0.06 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} = -3.44°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> A nose-down (negative) elevator deflection of $3.44°$ is required to hold the aircraft at the trim attitude. The negative $K_q$ (negative sign on the pitch-rate term) is a **stability augmenter** — it damps the short-period pitch oscillation by commanding elevator *against* the pitch rate.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5 — SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS OF CENTRIFUGAL TACHOMETER

**Step 1 — Write the design equation.**

$$N = \frac{1}{2 \pi / 60} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k \, x}{m \, r}}$$

$$N = \frac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k}{m \, r}} \cdot \sqrt{x}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative of $N$ with respect to each parameter.**

$$\frac{\partial N}{\partial k} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{m \, r \, k}} \cdot \sqrt{x} = \frac{N}{2 k}$$

$$\frac{\partial N}{\partial m} = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k}{r}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{x}}{m^{3/2}} = -\frac{N}{2 m}$$

$$\frac{\partial N}{\partial r} = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{x}}{r^{3/2}} = -\frac{N}{2 r}$$

$$\frac{\partial N}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{60}{2 \pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k}{m \, r}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}} = \frac{N}{2 x}$$

**Step 3 — Combine into the total differential (worst-case tolerance).**

$$\frac{\Delta N}{N} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\Delta k}{k} - \frac{\Delta m}{m} - \frac{\Delta r}{r} + \frac{\Delta x}{x} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
Suppose $k$ is $\pm 2\%$, $m$ is $\pm 1\%$, $r$ is $\pm 0.5\%$, and $x$ is $\pm 1\%$ (resolution of the pointer readout):

$$\frac{\Delta N}{N} = \frac{1}{2} (0.02 - 0.01 - 0.005 + 0.01) = \frac{1}{2} (0.015) = 0.0075 = 0.75\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The *percent* error in $N$ is exactly **half** the algebraic sum of the percent errors in the design parameters. This is the classical *square-root sensitivity* result. The $1/2$ factor is the *tachometer's intrinsic error attenuation* — a mechanical tachometer with $\pm 5\%$ component tolerances gives only $\pm 2.5\%$ RPM error.
