# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 MERMAID DIAGRAM 1 — CENTRIFUGAL TACHOMETER FUNCTIONAL ARCHITECTURE

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "Block-Level Functional Flow" rather than a physical cross-section?**
> The internal mechanics of a centrifugal tachometer involve pivots, links, and gears that are best understood through functional blocks. The flow below mirrors the energy path: rotational input → centrifugal force → spring restraint → pointer displacement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ENGINE CRANKSHAFT] --> B[TACH DRIVE CABLE]
    B --> C[ROTOR PLATE SPINDLE]
    C --> D[FLYWEIGHT A]
    C --> E[FLYWEIGHT B]
    D --> F[CENTRIFUGAL FORCE FC]
    E --> F
    F --> G[RADIAL OUTWARD MOTION X]
    G --> H[LINKAGE MECHANISM]
    H --> I[SLIDING COLLAR]
    I --> J[POINTER SHAFT]
    J --> K[CALIBRATED DIAL RPM]
    L[CONTROL SPRING K] --> G
    G --> M[EQUILIBRIUM CONDITION]
    style A fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style E fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style F fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
    style I fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style K fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style L fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style M fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
```

**Block-by-Block Interpretation:**
- `A` → `B`: The engine's mechanical rotation is transferred to the instrument via a flexible **drive cable** (square-shaft Bowden cable).
- `B` → `C`: The drive cable spins the internal **spindle / rotor plate**.
- `C` → `D`, `E`: The rotor plate carries two symmetric **flyweights** on pivoted links.
- `D`, `E` → `F`: The flyweights generate **centrifugal force** $F_c \propto \omega^2$.
- `F` → `G`: The flyweights move radially **outward** by displacement $x$.
- `L` → `G`: A **control spring** opposes this motion, creating an equilibrium point.
- `G` → `H` → `I` → `J`: The radial motion is converted, through a **linkage → sliding collar → pointer shaft** chain, into pointer rotation.
- `J` → `K`: The pointer reads **RPM on a calibrated dial** with a red line for max RPM.

---

## 4.2 MERMAID DIAGRAM 2 — DC TACHOGENERATOR SIGNAL FLOW

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ENGINE SHAFT] --> B[DC TACH ARMATURE]
    C[PERMANENT MAGNET POLE PIECES] --> B
    B --> D[INDUCED EMF E]
    D --> E[ARMATURE RESISTANCE RA]
    E --> F[TERMINAL VOLTAGE VT]
    F --> G[VOLTMETER MOVING COIL DIAL]
    H[INDICATED RPM] --> G
    style A fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style C fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
    style F fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style H fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
```

**Interpretation:**
- `A` → `B`: Engine rotation drives the DC machine armature.
- `C` → `B`: Stationary **permanent magnets** provide a constant field $\Phi$.
- `B` → `D`: Armature conductors cut the field flux, inducing **EMF $E = K \Phi N$**.
- `D` → `E` → `F`: The internal resistance $R_a$ causes a voltage drop; the remaining voltage is the **terminal voltage $V_t$**.
- `F` → `G` → `H`: The voltmeter (a d'Arsonval movement) is calibrated in **RPM**.

---

## 4.3 MERMAID DIAGRAM 3 — FLY-BY-WIRE SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[PILOT LEFT SIDE STICK] --> S1[LVDT RVDT PILOT INPUT SENSORS]
    P2[PILOT RIGHT SIDE STICK] --> S1
    S1 --> FCC1[FLIGHT CONTROL COMPUTER 1 FCC1]
    S1 --> FCC2[FLIGHT CONTROL COMPUTER 2 FCC2]
    S1 --> FCC3[FLIGHT CONTROL COMPUTER 3 FCC3]
    S1 --> FCC4[FLIGHT CONTROL COMPUTER 4 FCC4]
    FCC1 --> VOTER[SOFTWARE VOTER MID VALUE SELECT]
    FCC2 --> VOTER
    FCC3 --> VOTER
    FCC4 --> VOTER
    VOTER --> ACT1[ACTUATOR LEFT ELEVATOR]
    VOTER --> ACT2[ACTUATOR RIGHT ELEVATOR]
    VOTER --> ACT3[ACTUATOR AILERON LEFT]
    VOTER --> ACT4[ACTUATOR AILERON RIGHT]
    VOTER --> ACT5[ACTUATOR RUDDER]
    ACT1 --> SF1[SURFACE POSITION LVDT]
    ACT2 --> SF2[SURFACE POSITION LVDT]
    ACT3 --> SF3[SURFACE POSITION LVDT]
    ACT4 --> SF4[SURFACE POSITION LVDT]
    ACT5 --> SF5[SURFACE POSITION LVDT]
    SF1 --> FCC1
    SF2 --> FCC2
    SF3 --> FCC3
    SF4 --> FCC4
    SF5 --> FCC1
    BUS1[MIL STD 1553B OR ARINC 664 AFDX DATA BUS] --> FCC1
    BUS1 --> FCC2
    BUS1 --> FCC3
    BUS1 --> FCC4
    FADEC[FADEC ENGINE CONTROL BUS] --> BUS1
    style P1 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style S1 fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style FCC1 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style FCC2 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style FCC3 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style FCC4 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style VOTER fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
    style ACT1 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style ACT2 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style ACT3 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style ACT4 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style ACT5 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style SF1 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style SF2 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style SF3 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style SF4 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style SF5 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style BUS1 fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style FADEC fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
```

**Block-by-Block Interpretation:**
- `P1`, `P2`: Two **pilot side-sticks** (capt and fo) — the *input* devices.
- `S1`: **LVDT / RVDT** position transducers convert the mechanical motion into analogue electrical signals.
- `FCC1`–`FCC4`: **Quadruple-redundant Flight Control Computers** running *identical but independent* control laws.
- `VOTER`: A **mid-value-select voter** discards any FCC whose output disagrees with the others (dissimilar redundancy concept).
- `ACT1`–`ACT5`: **Electro-hydraulic actuators** that physically move the **elevator, aileron, and rudder** control surfaces.
- `SF1`–`SF5`: **Surface position LVDTs** provide *feedback* to the FCCs, closing the inner loop of each actuator.
- `BUS1`: A **redundant digital data bus** (e.g. **ARINC 664 / AFDX** for A380, B787; **MIL-STD-1553B** for F-16, F/A-18) interconnects all the FCCs, FADEC, and other avionics.
- `FADEC`: The **Full Authority Digital Engine Controller** shares the same bus, enabling *integrated* flight-and-propulsion control.

---

## 4.4 MERMAID DIAGRAM 4 — AUTOPILOT CLOSED-LOOP ARCHITECTURE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[SELECTED REFERENCE GYRO] --> ERR_SUM
    ACT[ACTUAL ATTITUDE GYRO] --> ERR_SUM
    ERR_SUM[ERROR DETECTOR COMPARATOR] --> GAIN[P GAIN AMPLIFIER]
    GAIN --> INT[INTEGRAL TERM KI]
    INT --> SUM2[SUMMING JUNCTION]
    GAIN --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> RATE[DERIVATIVE RATE TERM KD]
    RATE --> SERVO[SERVO AMPLIFIER]
    SERVO --> SERVOM[SERVO MOTOR HYDRAULIC]
    SERVOM --> SURF[CONTROL SURFACE AILERON OR ELEVATOR]
    SURF --> POT[SURFACE FEEDBACK POTENTIOMETER OR LVDT]
    POT --> SERVO
    SURF --> AIR[AIRCRAFT ATTITUDE]
    AIR --> ACT
    style REF fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style ACT fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style ERR_SUM fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style GAIN fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style INT fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style SUM2 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style RATE fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style SERVO fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
    style SERVOM fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
    style SURF fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style POT fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style AIR fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
```

**Block-by-Block Interpretation:**
- `REF` and `ACT`: The **desired** attitude (from the pilot's heading/altitude selector) is **compared** with the **actual** attitude from the vertical/directional gyro.
- `ERR_SUM`: The error detector outputs an **error signal** proportional to the difference.
- `GAIN`, `INT`, `RATE`: A **PID controller** (Proportional + Integral + Derivative) shapes the response — P provides stiffness, I eliminates steady-state offset, D damps overshoot.
- `SERVO` and `SERVOM`: The **servo amplifier** drives a **hydraulic servo motor** that produces the muscle to move the surface.
- `SURF`: The **control surface** is deflected, producing an aerodynamic moment.
- `AIR`: The aircraft begins to rotate, changing its attitude, which is sensed by the **actual attitude gyro** — closing the *outer* loop.
- `POT`: The **surface position LVDT/pot** closes the *inner* servo loop, ensuring the commanded surface position is achieved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Loops, One Goal:**
> The diagram clearly shows the **inner servo loop** (POT → SERVO) and the **outer autopilot loop** (ACT → ERR_SUM). A well-designed autopilot has an **inner-loop bandwidth at least 5–10× higher** than the outer-loop bandwidth — the same *pole-separation* principle we saw in §3.3.

---

## 4.5 MERMAID DIAGRAM 5 — COMPARATIVE OVERVIEW: TACHOMETER vs FBW vs AUTOPILOT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TACH[TACHOMETER SUBSYSTEM]
        T1[ENGINE SHAFT] --> T2[SENSOR MECHANICAL OR ELECTRICAL]
        T2 --> T3[POINTER OR DIGITAL READOUT]
    end
    subgraph FBW[FLY BY WIRE SUBSYSTEM]
        F1[PILOT INPUT SIDE STICK] --> F2[FCC CONTROL LAW]
        F2 --> F3[ACTUATOR]
        F3 --> F4[CONTROL SURFACE]
    end
    subgraph AP[AUTOPILOT SUBSYSTEM]
        A1[NAVIGATION REFERENCE] --> A2[AFCS COMPUTER]
        A2 --> A3[SERVO]
        A3 --> A4[SURFACE]
    end
    T2 -.SHARES POWER BUS.-> F2
    F2 -.SHARES DATA BUS.-> A2
    T1 -.PROVIDES ENGINE STATE.-> F2
    style TACH fill:#FFF8DC
    style FBW fill:#E0FFFF
    style AP fill:#FFE4E1
```

**Cross-Subsystem Integration Insight:**
- The **tachometer subsystem** generates the **engine state** signal used by the FADEC.
- The **FBW subsystem** consumes the FADEC's *thrust* data to perform **auto-throttle** integrated with the autopilot.
- The **autopilot** *commands* the FBW system to fly the trajectory.
- All three share a common **redundant data bus**, making the modern aircraft a *federated* (or in newer aircraft, an *Integrated Modular Avionics*) system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — SHORT-ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, and require crisp 3-4 line answers. Marks are awarded for (a) correct definition, (b) one supporting equation, (c) one real-world example.

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PCAOT205]`

**Q:** *With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a centrifugal tachometer used in light aircraft. State the governing equation.*

**Model Answer:**

A centrifugal tachometer is mechanically coupled to the engine via a flexible **tach drive cable** that rotates a **spindle** inside the instrument. Two **flyweights** are pivoted on the spindle and swing outward under the action of **centrifugal force** as speed increases. This outward motion is opposed by a **calibrating spring**. The equilibrium displacement is transmitted through a **linkage** to a **pointer** that indicates RPM on a calibrated dial.

The governing equation is:

$$F_c = F_s \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; m \, \omega^2 \, r = k \, x \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x = \frac{m \, r}{k} \left(\frac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)^2$$

Because $x \propto N^2$, the dial markings are **quadratically spaced**, compressed at low RPM and expanded near the red-line.

**Real-world example:** **Cessna 172R** — uses a mechanical centrifugal tachometer (0–3500 RPM) driven at half engine speed.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q:** *List the advantages of an electrical tachometer over a mechanical tachometer.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Advantage | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear scale** | $E = K \Phi N$ gives $E \propto N$ directly, easing dial calibration. |
| 2 | **Remote indication** | The electrical signal can drive an indicator in the cockpit located far from the engine. |
| 3 | **High accuracy** | No mechanical hysteresis, no spring ageing; ±0.5% achievable. |
| 4 | **Interface to FADEC** | The same signal feeds the digital engine controller. |
| 5 | **Lower friction** | No rotating linkages, only magnetic coupling → less wear. |
| 6 | **Direction sensing** | Polarity of DC tachogenerator indicates rotation direction (used for multi-engine synchrophasers). |

---

## 5.2 PART B — LONG-ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH — INTERNAL CHOICE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student attempts **ONE** of two alternatives. Show all derivations in **neat steps** with units. The KTU examiner deducts **2 marks per missing unit** and **3 marks per missing diagram**.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 MARKS)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Paper PCAOT205]`

#### **Question A(a) (7 Marks): Mechanical Tachometer — Centrifugal Type**

**Q:** A centrifugal tachometer has two flyweights, each of mass $0.025$ kg, mounted at a pivot radius of $35$ mm. The control spring stiffness is $1.0$ N/m, and the linkage ratio is such that $1$ mm radial displacement produces a $45°$ pointer rotation. Calculate:

(i) The full-scale RPM for a pointer that swings through $270°$.
(ii) The percentage RPM error if the spring stiffness drifts by $+5\%$.
(iii) The pointer angle at $60\%$ of full-scale RPM.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the full-scale radial displacement.**
A $270°$ pointer rotation corresponds to $\frac{270°}{45°/\text{mm}} = 6$ mm of radial motion.
$$x_{fs} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the displacement equation.**

$$x = \frac{m \, r}{k} \left(\frac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)^2$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$N = \frac{60}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{k \, x}{m \, r}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values for the full-scale RPM.**

$$N_{fs} = \frac{60}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.025 \times 0.035}}$$

**Step 3a — Compute the ratio inside the square root:**

$$\frac{1.0 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.025 \times 0.035} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{8.75 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.857$$

**Step 3b — Take the square root:**

$$\sqrt{6.857} = 2.6185$$

**Step 3c — Multiply by $\frac{60}{2\pi} = 9.5493$:**

$$\boxed{N_{fs} = 9.5493 \times 2.6185 = 25.00 \text{ RPM (spindle)}}$$

If the engine is a 4-stroke piston with a **1:2 gear-up** from crankshaft to spindle, the engine RPM is $50$ RPM, which is unrealistically low. This indicates the numerical values in the question are **dimensionally illustrative**; in practice, real instrument values are calibrated for engine RPM around 2000–3500 RPM. The KTU examiner will accept the calculation **as the spindle RPM**.

**Step 4 — Compute the error from a $+5\%$ spring drift.**

Using the sensitivity relation derived in §3.5:

$$\frac{\Delta N}{N} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta k}{k} = \frac{1}{2} (0.05) = 0.025 = 2.5\%$$

A $5\%$ increase in spring stiffness produces a $2.5\%$ *increase* in the indicated RPM.

**Step 5 — Compute the pointer angle at $60\%$ of full-scale RPM.**
At $60\%$ of $N_{fs}$, the actual $N = 0.6 \times 25.00 = 15.00$ RPM.

The corresponding radial displacement is (using $x \propto N^2$):

$$x = \frac{m \, r}{k} \left(\frac{2 \pi \times 15}{60}\right)^2 = 7.0588 \times 10^{-4} \times (1.5708)^2 = 7.0588 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.4674 = 1.7415 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$x = 1.74 \text{ mm}$$

The corresponding pointer angle:

$$\theta = 1.74 \text{ mm} \times 45°/\text{mm} = 78.3°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Many students erroneously write "60% RPM corresponds to 60% pointer angle = 162°". This is WRONG because the law is **quadratic**, not linear. The correct answer is **78.3°**. A student who writes 162° loses **5 of 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A(b) (7 Marks): Fly-By-Wire Architecture**

**Q:** *Explain the architecture of a modern Fly-By-Wire flight control system. List the four main blocks and describe the role of each. What are the advantages of FBW over conventional mechanical control?*

**Model Answer:**

A modern Fly-By-Wire system replaces the conventional mechanical linkages (cables, rods, pulleys) between the pilot's cockpit controls and the aerodynamic control surfaces with an **electrical and digital signal path**.

**The Four Main Blocks:**

| # | Block | Components | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pilot Input Sensors** | LVDTs, RVDTs, force transducers | Convert mechanical stick motion into electrical signals |
| 2 | **Flight Control Computers (FCCs)** | 3 or 4 redundant digital processors | Implement control laws, envelope protection, fault detection |
| 3 | **Actuators** | EHA, EBHA, EHA-FA | Convert electrical command into hydraulic/mechanical surface motion |
| 4 | **Surface Position Feedback** | LVDTs on actuators | Close the inner servo loop |

**Supporting Infrastructure:** A **redundant data bus** (ARINC 664 / AFDX for civil, MIL-STD-1553B for military) interconnects all the FCCs, FADECs, and the cockpit displays.

**Control Law (simplified pitch channel):**

$$\delta_e = K_p (n_{z_{cmd}} - n_z) + K_q q + K_\theta (\theta_{ref} - \theta) + K_i \int (n_{z_{cmd}} - n_z) \, dt$$

**Five Advantages of FBW:**
1. **Weight saving** — eliminates heavy cables and pulleys (typical saving: 200–500 kg on a transport).
2. **Enables relaxed static stability** — unstable airframes can be flown safely (F-16, F/A-22).
3. **Envelope protection** — software limits AoA, $g$, and airspeed to safe values.
4. **Carefree handling** — pilot cannot accidentally depart controlled flight.
5. **Easy maintenance and reconfiguration** — software updates, automatic fault-tolerance, fewer lubrication points.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world example:** The **Airbus A320** (first commercial digital FBW aircraft, 1988) and the **Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II** (most advanced production FBW aircraft today).

---

### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 MARKS)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

#### **Question B(a) (7 Marks): DC Tachogenerator — Numerical**

**Q:** A DC tachogenerator has a constant field flux of $6 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb and an armature constant of $K_a = 350$ V·s/Wb. The armature resistance is $R_a = 80 \; \Omega$, and the indicator voltmeter has $R_v = 920 \; \Omega$.

(i) Determine the open-circuit voltage at $2000$ RPM and at $3000$ RPM.
(ii) Determine the indicated voltage in each case.
(iii) What is the percentage loading error?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $N$ in rev/sec.**

$$N_{2000} = \frac{2000}{60} = 33.33 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$N_{3000} = \frac{3000}{60} = 50 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2 — Open-circuit voltage from $E = K_a \, \Phi \, N$.**

$$E_{2000} = 350 \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 33.33 = 350 \times 0.2 = 70.0 \text{ V}$$

$$E_{3000} = 350 \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 = 350 \times 0.3 = 105.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Terminal voltage accounting for the voltmeter load.**

$$V_t = E \cdot \frac{R_v}{R_a + R_v} = E \cdot \frac{920}{80 + 920} = E \cdot \frac{920}{1000} = 0.92 \, E$$

**Step 4 — Indicated voltage at 2000 RPM.**

$$V_{t,2000} = 0.92 \times 70 = 64.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Indicated voltage at 3000 RPM.**

$$V_{t,3000} = 0.92 \times 105 = 96.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Loading error.**

$$\text{Error} = (1 - 0.92) \times 100\% = 8.0\%$$

The error is a **constant 8%** of the open-circuit voltage, independent of speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Note:** To reduce the loading error below $1\%$, the voltmeter resistance should be at least $99 \times R_a$, i.e. $R_v \geq 7920 \; \Omega$ for the given armature. Modern indicators use a high-impedance buffer amplifier (FET input, $R_{in} > 10 \; M\Omega$) to eliminate the loading error altogether.

---

#### **Question B(b) (7 Marks): Auto-Pilot — Block Diagram and Working**

**Q:** *Draw the block diagram of a single-axis (roll) autopilot system. Explain the function of each block. What are the principal modes of operation of a modern auto-pilot?*

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.4 of this document for the detailed Mermaid diagram; replicate as a hand-drawn figure in the exam).**

**Block-by-Block Description:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **1. Reference Gyro** | Provides the *desired* attitude (e.g. wings level) as a stable reference. |
| **2. Attitude Gyro** | Senses the *actual* aircraft attitude. |
| **3. Error Detector** | Computes the difference (error) between desired and actual. |
| **4. Servo Amplifier** | Boosts the error signal to a level capable of driving the servo motor. |
| **5. Servo Motor** | Physically moves the control surface. |
| **6. Surface Position Feedback** | Potentiometer / LVDT on the surface — closes the *inner* loop. |
| **7. Aircraft Dynamics** | The aircraft responds to the surface deflection by rotating in roll. |

**Closed-Loop Equation:**

$$\phi(s) = \frac{K_a \, L_p}{s(s + L_p)(s + 1/\tau_a)} \cdot \phi_{ref}(s)$$

**Principal Modes of Operation of a Modern Auto-Pilot:**

| Mode | Function |
|---|---|
| **Wing Leveler** | Maintains wings horizontal (uses vertical gyro). |
| **Heading Hold** | Holds a compass heading (uses directional gyro + flux valve). |
| **Altitude Hold** | Holds barometric altitude (uses aneroid capsule). |
| **Vertical Speed Hold** | Holds a constant rate of climb/descent. |
| **VOR / Localiser Coupling** | Tracks a VOR radial / ILS localiser. |
| **Glideslope Coupling** | Tracks an ILS glideslope. |
| **Autoland (Cat III)** | Fully automatic landing to flare and rollout. |
| **Yaw Damper** | Damps Dutch-roll oscillations. |
| **Autothrottle** | Maintains a commanded airspeed or Mach number. |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Example:** The **Garmin G1000** integrated avionics system used in the Cessna 172/182/208 and Diamond DA42 includes a 2-axis (roll + pitch) autopilot with heading, altitude, vertical-speed, NAV, and approach-coupling modes — a textbook example of a modern general-aviation autopilot.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — PITFALL CALLOUTS

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Points on this Topic (compiled from KTU 2022, 2023, 2024 answer scripts):**
> 1. **Confusing *quadratic* and *linear* tachometer laws.** Students write $x = kN$ for a centrifugal tachometer. The correct relation is $x \propto N^2$. **Loss: 2–3 marks per error.**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s** in the centrifugal-force formula. The student writes $F_c = m N^2 r$ instead of $F_c = m (2\pi N/60)^2 r$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Mixing up DC and AC tachometer equations.** DC: $E = K\Phi N$ (linear in rev/s). AC: $f = PN/120$ (frequency proportional to RPM). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Drawing FBW as just "no cables" without naming the four blocks.** The examiner expects *Sensors → FCC → Actuator → Feedback*. **Loss: 3 marks.**
> 5. **Writing "FBW uses a computer"** without specifying *redundancy level* (3 or 4 computers, mid-value-select voter). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 6. **Confusing autopilot "modes" with "axes".** A mode is what the autopilot *does* (heading hold); an axis is the *degree of freedom* (roll, pitch, yaw). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 7. **Missing units in numerical problems.** KTU deducts $0.5$ to $1$ mark per missing unit. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 8. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g. "assuming the spring is linear," "assuming constant flux"). **Loss: 1 mark per missing assumption.**
> 9. **Skipping diagrams.** A 7-mark question without a labelled diagram gets a maximum of **5/7**.
> 10. **Confusing "envelope protection" with "autopilot."** Envelope protection is a *function of FBW*, not of the autopilot per se. **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and pin it to your study wall. Each tick-mark is one mark in the KTU exam.**

### **A. Tachometers — Mechanical**
- ☐ **Centrifugal (pendulum) tachometer** is the most common in piston-engine aircraft. Uses **flyweights + control spring**.
- ☐ Governing equation: $x = \frac{mr}{k} \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2$ — **quadratic** in $N$.
- ☐ Sensitivity: $\frac{dx}{dN} \propto N$ — most accurate at high RPM, least at idle.
- ☐ Dial markings are **compressed at low end, expanded at high end** (non-linear).
- ☐ **Clock-type (chronometric) tachometer** uses a clockwork escapement. Used in large radial engines and as portable hand-cranked gauges.
- ☐ **Friction-type hand tachometer** uses a rubber cone on the shaft. Common in maintenance hangars.

### **B. Tachometers — Electrical**
- ☐ **DC Tachogenerator:** $E = K \Phi N$. Linear, simple, used as servo rate-feedback. Direction indicated by polarity.
- ☐ **AC Tachometer:** $f = PN/120$. Frequency proportional to RPM. The standard for engine N₁ and N₂ in modern turbofans.
- ☐ **Drag-Cup Induction Tachometer:** Aluminium cup, eddy currents, 400 Hz excitation. Common in light-aircraft EDM panels.
- ☐ **Loading error in DC tachogenerator:** $V_t = E \cdot \frac{R_v}{R_a + R_v}$; high-impedance indicator minimises this.
- ☐ AC tachometer is **immune to loading error** in frequency-domain reading (frequency is unaffected by amplitude loading).

### **C. Fly-By-Wire (FBW)**
- ☐ Definition: pilot's mechanical input → **electrical signal** → computer → actuator → surface.
- ☐ Four blocks: **Sensors → FCC → Actuator → Surface Feedback**.
- ☐ Redundancy: **TMR or QMR** (3 or 4 computers) with **mid-value-select voter**.
- ☐ Data buses: **ARINC 429** (older), **ARINC 664 / AFDX** (modern), **MIL-STD-1553B** (military).
- ☐ **Control law (pitch):** $\delta_e = K_p \Delta n_z + K_q q + K_\theta \Delta \theta + K_i \int \Delta n_z \, dt$.
- ☐ **Envelope protection:** AoA, $g$-limit, overspeed — implemented in software.
- ☐ **Examples:** A320 (first civil FBW, 1988), F-16 (first military FBW, 1973), F-35, A380, B787, Rafale.
- ☐ Advantages: **weight, manoeuvrability, carefree handling, reliability, easy maintenance**.

### **D. Auto-Pilot (AFCS)**
- ☐ Definition: closed-loop control of aircraft **attitude, heading, altitude**, and **flight path**.
- ☐ **Two nested loops:** *inner servo loop* (surface position feedback) and *outer autopilot loop* (attitude feedback).
- ☐ **Pole-separation rule:** inner loop $\geq 5-10\times$ faster than outer loop.
- ☐ **Modes:** wing leveler, heading hold, altitude hold, V/S hold, VOR coupling, ILS coupling, autoland, yaw damper, autothrottle.
- ☐ **Sensors:** vertical gyro, directional gyro, rate gyro, aneroid capsule, VOR/ILS receivers.
- ☐ **Servo actuator:** hydraulic (conventional) or electro-hydrostatic (modern FBW).
- ☐ **Roll subsidence time constant** $\tau_r = 1/\vert L_p \vert$ — the dominant autopilot time constant.
- ☐ **Examples:** Garmin G1000 (GA), Collins Pro Line 21 (regional jets), Honeywell Primus Epic (A380), B777 Autoland.

### **E. Cross-Cutting Mnemonics**
- ☐ **"PRMFS"** — Pressure, RPM, Force, Mass, Speed — the five things to always check.
- ☐ **"SFAF"** — Sensors, FCC/FADEC, Actuator, Feedback — the canonical four-block FBW architecture.
- ☐ **"SQUAD"** — Sensitivity, Quadratic, Units, Accuracy, Drive-cable ratio — the five things to verify for any centrifugal tachometer numerical.
- ☐ **"B-WORD"** — Bus (data), Wire (electrical), Output (to actuator), Redundancy (TMR/QMR), Demanded (control law) — the five things to mention in any FBW theory question.

---

## 5.5 QUICK-FIRE FORMULA CARD (FOR THE LAST-NIGHT REVISION)

$$\boxed{F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, r \quad\quad E_{\text{DC}} = K \Phi N \quad\quad f_{\text{AC}} = \frac{PN}{120}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FBW:} \; \delta_e = K_p \Delta n_z + K_q q + K_\theta \Delta \theta + K_i \int \Delta n_z \, dt}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Autopilot roll TF:} \;\; \frac{p(s)}{\delta_a(s)} = \frac{K_a L_p}{(s + L_p)(s + 1/\tau_a)}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Sensitivity:} \;\; \frac{\Delta N}{N} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\Delta k}{k} - \frac{\Delta m}{m} - \frac{\Delta r}{r} + \frac{\Delta x}{x}\right)}$$

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — TACHOMETER, FLY-BY-WIRE, AND AUTO-PILOT**
> **Total estimated study time for mastery: 6–8 hours (lectures + 2 past papers + 1 numerical drill set)**
> **Best paired KTU reference text:** *Collinson, R.P.G., "Introduction to Avionics Systems," Springer (3rd ed., 2011)*, Chapter 6 (Flight Control Systems) and Chapter 7 (Inertial Sensors and Navigation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Head Up Display
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Head Up Display # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Head Up Display (HUD)** is a flight-critical transparent optical display system that presents essential flight, navigation, weapons, and aircraft performance information directly in the pilot's forward line of sight, enabling the pilot to view the data and the outside world simultaneously without refocusing the eyes or shifting gaze from the primary flight path. In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the HUD is classified under the domain of **Electronic Flight Information Systems (EFIS) — Display Subsystems** and consists of three functional blocks: 1. **Symbol Generator (SG)** – The digital computer that builds the symbology. 2. **Display Unit / Overhead Unit (OHU)** – The cathode-ray tube or solid-state source that produces the image. 3. **Combiner Unit (CU)** – The optically collimated glass that reflects the image into the pilot's eyes while remaining transparent to the outside world. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A HUD is *not* just a screen; it is a **collimated optical system**. The image is projected at optical infinity so that the pilot's eyes remain focused on the runway during the critical landing phase. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. Your eyes are focused on the road ahead, perhaps 30–50 meters away. Suddenly, your speedometer drops to your lap. To read it, your eyes must **refocus** (from far to near), which takes about **0.5–0.8 seconds** — and during that time you are legally "blind" to the road. Now, imagine the speedometer number is magically floating in the air, perfectly in focus with the road, projected onto a small piece of clear glass on your windshield. **That is exactly what a Head Up Display does for a pilot.** A pilot landing a fighter jet or airliner in low visibility has **zero time** to look down at instruments. The HUD makes the data appear *overlaid on the runway*, allowing the pilot to: - See airspeed, altitude, attitude, and heading - *and* see the outside world - *at the same time*, *in the same focal plane* > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** A HUD = a "smart, semi-transparent projection screen" placed between the pilot and the windshield that floats flight data over the real world. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Standard Value (Civil) | Standard Value (Military) | |---|---|---| | Total Field of View (TFOV) | ~**30° × 24°** | ~**30° × 25°** to **40° × 30°** | | Instantaneous FOV (IFOV) | **15°** circular (typical) | **15°** to **30°** | | Luminance | **3,000–15,000 fL** | **>10,000 fL** (sunlight readable) | | Display Source | CRT, LCD, OLED | CRT, Laser, Holographic | | Combiner Transmittance | >**70%** | >**60%** | | Combiner Reflectance | >**25%** | >**30%** | | Eye Relief | ~**610 mm (24 inches)** | ~**250–400 mm** | | Design Eye Box | ~**5–6 inches** diameter | ~**4 inches** diameter | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" A HUD works on a single overarching principle: **collimate the image to optical infinity.** This is the *why* behind every component. The *how* is broken into 7 structured logic steps: ### Step 1 — Data Acquisition The onboard avionics bus (**ARINC 429** in civil aircraft, **MIL-STD-1553B** in military aircraft) supplies real-time data: - Airspeed ($V$), Indicated Airspeed ($V_{IAS}$), True Airspeed ($V_{TAS}$) - Pressure Altitude ($h_p$), Radio Altitude ($h_r$) - Pitch ($\theta$), Roll ($\phi$), Heading ($\psi$) - Angle of Attack ($\alpha$), Mach Number ($M$) > [!NOTE] > **Why so many buses?** The HUD is a *display* — it does not compute flight data. It merely renders whatever the Flight Management System (FMS) or Air Data Computer (ADC) provides. ### Step 2 — Symbol Generation A dedicated **Symbol Generator Computer (SGC)** — typically a MIL-spec ruggedized processor — converts raw data into standardized **stroke-written** symbols: - Pitch ladder - Heading tape - Velocity vector (the "flying" symbol) - Flight path marker - Digital readouts > **Why stroke-written (not raster)?** Stroke symbology is *vector-based*; lines are drawn with single bright strokes for **maximum brightness in direct sunlight** — critical for daytime HUD readability. ### Step 3 — Image Source (Overhead Unit) The SGC drives a high-luminance display source mounted **above the pilot's head** (overhead). Common sources: - **CRT (Cathode Ray Tube)** — legacy, high brightness, monochrome green - **LCD (Liquid Crystal Display)** — modern civil, with backlight - **OLED (Organic LED)** — emerging, very high contrast - **Laser/CRT hybrid** — modern military (F-35 Gen III HUD) ### Step 4 — Relay Optics The image leaves the overhead unit and passes through a **relay lens assembly** that: - Folds the optical path (to fit inside the cockpit) - Adjusts focus - Controls field of view geometry ### Step 5 — Collimation (The Core Trick) A **collimating lens** converts diverging light rays from the symbol into **parallel rays**. To the pilot's eye, parallel rays appear to come from **infinity** — meaning the eye does *not* need to refocus. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why infinity focus matters:** A pilot's eyes are tuned to a 3–10 km runway during landing. A normal instrument at 0.5 m requires 2 diopters of refocus, which takes ~**0.5 s** of "blind time." The HUD eliminates this blind time. ### Step 6 — Combiner Reflection The parallel (collimated) rays strike the **Combiner** — a specially coated, optically flat piece of glass mounted between the pilot and the windshield. The combiner has two competing optical properties: - **High specular reflectance** (for the wavelength of the display, e.g., green 545 nm) - **High transmittance** (for all other wavelengths, so the runway is clearly visible) This is achieved through: - **Multi-layer thin-film dielectric coatings** (modern HUDs) - **Holographic Optical Elements (HOE)** (Gen II / Gen III military HUDs) ### Step 7 — Pilot's Eye Receives Collimated Image + Real World The pilot's eye simultaneously receives: - **Real world** (transmitted through the combiner) - **HUD image** (reflected off the combiner) - Both in the **same focal plane** (infinity) → no eye refocus needed. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total Field of View | $TFOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{D}{2f}\right)$ | degrees | $D$ = combiner width, $f$ = collimator focal length | | 2 | Instantaneous FOV | $IFOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$ | degrees | $d$ = display source active diameter | | 3 | Eye Relief | $ER = \dfrac{h_{eye}}{2 \tan(\alpha/2)}$ | mm | $h_{eye}$ = eye box height, $\alpha$ = half-angle | | 4 | Boresight Angle | $\theta_b = \arctan\left(\dfrac{y}{L}\right)$ | degrees | $y$ = vertical offset, $L$ = distance to datum | | 5 | Combiner Reflectance (thin film) | $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | $n_1, n_2$ = refractive indices | | 6 | Eye Box Diameter | $D_{EB} = f \cdot \tan(\theta_{conv})$ | mm | $\theta_{conv}$ = eye box convergence | | 7 | Luminance Required | $L_{req} = L_{amb} \cdot K_{sunch}$ | $\text{cd/m}^2$ | $K_{sunch}$ = sun-load coefficient (10–1000×) | | 8 | Angular Subtence | $\omega = \dfrac{h_{obj}}{R_{slant}}$ | rad | $h_{obj}$ = object height, $R_{slant}$ = slant range | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Pip Rule:** When using formulas in KTU exams, always state the **units explicitly** and verify the **boundary conditions** — examiners allocate 1 mark for unit correctness. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why HUD is Used | |---|---| | **Airline Cat-III Landing** | Allows pilot to monitor autoland + see runway in zero visibility | | **Fighter Aircraft Aiming** | Weapons reticle stays on target during high-G maneuvers | | **Helicopter Degraded Visual Environment (DVE)** | Brownout/whiteout: HUD remains readable when outside is lost | | **Helmet-Mounted Display (HMD)** | F-35, Apache — fully 360° cued symbology | | **Enhanced Flight Vision System (EFVS)** | Combines FLIR/IR image overlaid on HUD | | **Synthetic Vision System (SVS)** | Database-rendered terrain on HUD (e.g., Gulfstream EVS) | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Field of View (TFOV) from Combiner Geometry ### Statement Derive the expression for the Total Field of View of a HUD in terms of the combiner width ($D$) and the collimator focal length ($f$). ### Given - Combiner active width: $D$ (mm) - Collimating lens focal length: $f$ (mm) - Pilot's eye at the design eye point (DEP) ### Step-by-Step Derivation By the thin-lens collimation geometry, the edge of the combiner, the center of the collimating lens, and the pilot's eye form a triangle. The half-angle subtended at the eye by the combiner edge is: $$ \tan\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \dfrac{D/2}{f} $$ This is because the combiner edge lies at a perpendicular distance equal to the focal length $f$ (since the combiner sits at the front focal plane of the collimating lens), and the half-width is $D/2$. The total field of view is the *full angle*, not the half-angle, so we multiply by 2: $$ \theta_{TFOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{D}{2f}\right) $$ Converting to degrees for KTU paper presentation: $$ \boxed{\theta_{TFOV} = \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{D}{2f}\right) \;\; [\text{degrees}]} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example A civil transport HUD has a combiner of width **$D = 220$ mm** and a collimator focal length **$f = 110$ mm**. $$ \theta_{TFOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{220}{2 \times 110}\right) $$ $$ = 2 \cdot \arctan(1.0) $$ $$ = 2 \cdot 45^{\circ} $$ $$ \boxed{\theta_{TFOV} = 90^{\circ}} $$ > **Commentary:** A 90° TFOV is extremely wide. Practical HUDs use narrower fields (~30°–40°) because the combiner must be physically mounted inside the cockpit without obscuring the pilot's forward view. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Instantaneous FOV (IFOV) from Display Source Geometry ### Statement Derive the IFOV — the angular size of the actual image as seen by the pilot. ### Given - Display source (CRT/LCD) diagonal or active diameter: $d$ (mm) - Collimator focal length: $f$ (mm) ### Derivation By the same thin-lens reasoning, but now applied to the **physical display source** (which sits at the back focal plane of the collimator): $$ \tan\left(\dfrac{\phi_{IFOV}}{2}\right) = \dfrac{d}{2f} $$ $$ \boxed{\phi_{IFOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right) \;\; [\text{degrees}]} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example A military HUD uses a CRT with active diagonal **$d = 76$ mm (3 inches)** and a collimator focal length **$f = 76.2$ mm**. $$ \phi_{IFOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{76}{2 \times 76.2}\right) $$ $$ = 2 \cdot \arctan(0.4987) $$ $$ = 2 \cdot 26.51^{\circ} $$ $$ \boxed{\phi_{IFOV} \approx 53^{\circ}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Fundamental HUD Relation: TFOV vs IFOV ### Key Engineering Relationship The Total Field of View is **not** equal to the IFOV. The TFOV is the geometric window through which the pilot can look; the IFOV is the *angular extent of the actual symbology*. The IFOV must always be **≤ TFOV** for the pilot to see the full image. By combining the two previous derivations: $$ \dfrac{TFOV}{IFOV} = \dfrac{\arctan(D/2f)}{\arctan(d/2f)} $$ The ratio of the linear dimensions of the combiner and the source: $$ \boxed{\dfrac{TFOV}{IFOV} \approx \dfrac{D}{d} \quad \text{(small-angle approximation)}} $$ This is the **Optical Leverage Ratio** of the HUD — and explains why military HUDs use **magnifying relay optics** ($D \gg d$) to achieve wide FOVs from physically small CRTs. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Design Eye Box (DEB) Geometry The **Design Eye Box** is the 3-D volume where the pilot's eye must be placed to see the entire HUD image without vignetting. ### Derivation The eye box is bounded by the convergence angle of rays from the top and bottom of the display source: $$ h_{EB} = f \cdot \tan\left(\dfrac{\phi_{IFOV}}{2}\right) $$ For a circular eye box of diameter $D_{EB}$: $$ D_{EB} = \dfrac{f \cdot d}{f} = d \quad \text{(at the collimator)}$$ When projected forward to the pilot's eye position by the combiner optics, the **actual eye box** is: $$ D_{EB,actual} = d \cdot \dfrac{L_{combiner-to-eye}}{f} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A larger eye box = better pilot comfort, but reduces brightness. Military HUDs use **automatic shimmy damping** to keep the pilot's head inside the eye box despite vibration. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Gen I HUD (Optical Lens) | Gen II HUD (Holographic) | Gen III HUD (Wide FOV / HMD) | |---|---|---|---| | Field of View | ~20° × 15° | ~30° × 25° | >40° × 30° (or full helmet) | | Combiner | Glass + dielectric coat | Holographic Optical Element | Helmet visor | | Brightness | ~3,000 fL | ~10,000 fL | >10,000 fL | | Source | CRT | CRT / LCD | Laser + DLP | | Weight | 15–20 kg | 8–12 kg | <5 kg (HMD) | | Civil Example | Boeing 737 Classic | Boeing 777, A320 | — | | Military Example | F-15 (early) | F/A-18, F-16 | F-35 Gen III HMDS | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a HUD System ```mermaid graph TD A1[ARINC 429 Bus / MIL-STD-1553B] --> B1[Air Data Computer ADC] A2[Inertial Reference Unit IRU] --> B2[Attitude Heading Reference System AHRS] A3[Radio Altimeter] --> B3[Navigation System FMS GPS] A4[Weapons System MIL] --> B4[Flight Control System FCC] B1 --> C1[Symbol Generator Computer SGC] B2 --> C1 B3 --> C1 B4 --> C1 C1 --> D1[Overhead Display Unit OHU - CRT or LCD] D1 --> E1[Relay Optics - Folding Mirrors and Lenses] E1 --> F1[Collimating Lens Group] F1 --> G1[Combiner Unit CU - Coated Glass or HOE] H1[Outside World - Runway Horizon Sky] --> G1 G1 --> I1[Pilot Eye at Design Eye Box DEB] I1 --> J1[Brain - Fused Real World and Symbology Perception] ``` ## 4.2 Optical Path / Ray Diagram (Block Form) ```mermaid graph LR A[Step 1: Data Input
Flight Parameters] --> B[Step 2: Symbol Generator
Stroke Writing] B --> C[Step 3: Display Source
CRT or LCD at FOV Plane] C --> D[Step 4: Relay Lens
Path Folding] D --> E[Step 5: Collimating Lens
Focal Length f] E --> F[Step 6: Combiner
Reflects Collimated Rays] F --> G[Step 7: Pilot Eye
Sees Image at Infinity] H[Real World View] -.Transmitted.-> F F --> G ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchy of HUD Generations ```mermaid graph TD A0[Aircraft HUD Evolution] --> B0[Gen I: Optical Lens HUD
1970s to 1980s] A0 --> C0[Gen II: Holographic HUD
1980s to 2000s] A0 --> D0[Gen III: Wide FOV and Helmet Mounted
2000s to Present] B0 --> B1[Glass Combiner with Dielectric Coat] B0 --> B2[Narrow FOV 20 x 15 deg] B0 --> B3[CRT Source] B0 --> B4[Example: F-15A, B-727] C0 --> C1[Holographic Optical Element Combiner] C0 --> C2[Medium FOV 30 x 25 deg] C0 --> C3[High Luminance CRT or LCD] C0 --> C4[Example: F-16, A320, B-777] D0 --> D1[Dual Combiner or HMD Visor] D0 --> D2[Wide FOV greater than 40 x 30 deg] D0 --> D3[Laser or DLP Source] D0 --> D4[Example: F-35 HMDS, Sniper Pod] ``` ## 4.4 Cockpit Mounting and Geometry ```mermaid graph TD A1[Pilot Eye Position] --> A2[Eye Relief Distance ER] A2 --> A3[Combiner Unit CU] A3 --> A4[Field of View Cone TFOV] A4 --> A5[Overhead Unit OHU above Pilot] A5 --> A6[Symbol Generator SG behind Avionics Bay] A1 -.Boresight Axis.-> A3 A3 -.Reflected Image.-> A1 A3 -.Transmitted Outside World.-> A1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU 2024):** In a 14-mark question, drawing a labeled block diagram of the HUD architecture and identifying the **3 main functional blocks** (Symbol Generator, Display Unit, Combiner) fetches **3–4 marks** even before you write any theory. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define a Head Up Display. Mention any two advantages of a HUD over conventional head-down instruments. > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme | Module 4]** **Model Answer:** A **Head Up Display (HUD)** is a transparent optical display system that presents critical flight information directly in the pilot's forward line of sight, allowing simultaneous viewing of flight data and the outside world without head movement or eye refocus. **Two Advantages:** 1. **Reduced Pilot Workload:** Pilot can monitor airspeed, altitude, attitude, and heading while maintaining visual contact with the outside world — especially critical during approach and landing. 2. **Faster Reaction Time:** The pilot does not need to look down (3–5° head-down angle) and refocus (0.5–0.8 seconds), reducing **transition time** and increasing **situational awareness**, particularly in poor visibility. --- ### Q2. What is the function of the combiner in a Head Up Display? > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme | Module 4]** **Model Answer:** The **combiner** is a semi-transparent, optically flat glass element positioned in the pilot's forward line of sight. Its functions are: 1. To **reflect** the collimated image from the overhead display unit into the pilot's eyes. 2. To **transmit** the outside-world view (runway, horizon) with minimal distortion. 3. To ensure the reflected image appears at **optical infinity** so the pilot's eyes do not need to refocus. The combiner is treated with **multi-layer dielectric coatings** (Gen I/II) or **Holographic Optical Elements (HOE)** (Gen II/III) to achieve selective reflectance at the display wavelength (typically green, 545 nm) while maintaining high transmittance for the visible outside scene. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question has an internal choice — attempt either OR. Both alternatives are framed below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Head Up Display system. Identify and describe the three main functional blocks. (7 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** **Model Answer:** The functional architecture of a HUD consists of three main blocks, supported by a data acquisition subsystem: **Block 1 — Symbol Generator (SG):** - A digital computer that receives raw flight data from the **Air Data Computer (ADC)**, **Attitude Heading Reference System (AHRS)**, **FMS/GPS**, and (in military aircraft) the **Weapons System**. - Converts this data into **stroke-written vector symbology** (pitch ladder, heading tape, velocity vector, digital readouts). - Operates at high refresh rates (60–120 Hz) to avoid flicker. **Block 2 — Display Unit (Overhead Head Unit — OHU):** - Mounted **above and ahead of the pilot**. - Contains a high-luminance **CRT, LCD, or laser** source. - Produces a bright image that can be read against the **>100,000 lux** background of direct sunlight. **Block 3 — Combiner Unit (CU):** - A coated glass or HOE located in the pilot's line of sight. - Reflects the image to the pilot's eye while transmitting the outside world. - Sits at the **front focal plane** of a collimating lens, ensuring the image appears at optical infinity. **Data Flow Block Diagram:** ``` [ADC / AHRS / FMS] → [Symbol Generator] → [Display Unit OHU] → [Relay + Collimator] → [Combiner] → [Pilot Eye] ``` > *(A well-labeled block diagram on the answer sheet fetches full marks — examiners expect at least the 3 named blocks + 2 connecting arrows.)* --- #### (b) Explain the concept of collimation in a HUD. Why is collimation essential? Derive the relationship between the Total Field of View and the combiner width. (7 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** **Model Answer:** **Concept of Collimation:** Collimation is the optical process of converting **divergent light rays** from a nearby display source into **parallel rays**, such that they appear to the observer to originate from **optical infinity**. In a HUD, a **collimating lens** (focal length $f$) is placed between the display source and the combiner. The display source is positioned at the **back focal plane** of this lens. By the thin-lens equation: $$ \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f} $$ For an object at $u = -f$ (back focal plane), the image is formed at $v = \infty$. Thus, the rays emerging from the lens are parallel. **Why Collimation is Essential:** 1. **Eye Refocus Elimination:** During landing, the pilot's eyes are focused on the runway (3–10 km away). A standard instrument at 0.5 m requires a refocus of 2 diopters, which takes **0.5–0.8 s** of "blind time." Collimation places the HUD image at the same focal plane as the runway, eliminating this transition. 2. **Head Movement Tolerance:** A collimated image remains visible even if the pilot moves his head slightly within the **Design Eye Box**, because parallel rays entering the pupil from different angles still appear to come from the same point at infinity. 3. **Parallax-Free Viewing:** Critical for accurate aiming (e.g., weapons reticle). **Derivation of TFOV:** Let $D$ be the combiner width and $f$ the collimator focal length. The half-angle subtended at the eye by the combiner edge is: $$ \tan\left(\frac{\theta_{TFOV}}{2}\right) = \frac{D/2}{f} $$ Multiplying by 2 to obtain the full angle: $$ \boxed{\theta_{TFOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{D}{2f}\right)} $$ **Numerical Example (for KTU paper):** For a combiner of width $D = 200$ mm and focal length $f = 100$ mm: $$ \theta_{TFOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan(1.0) = 90^{\circ} $$ Such a wide FOV is impractical; real HUDs use $D/f$ ratios corresponding to 30°–40° FOVs. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### (a) Compare and contrast Generation I, II, and III HUD systems. Mention at least 4 comparison parameters and give one civil and one military example for each generation. (7 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper | OR Choice]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Gen I (Optical Lens) | Gen II (Holographic) | Gen III (Wide FOV / HMD) | |---|---|---|---| | **Combiner Technology** | Glass with dielectric coating | Holographic Optical Element (HOE) | Dual combiner / HMD visor | | **Field of View** | 20° × 15° (narrow) | 30° × 25° (medium) | >40° × 30° (wide) | | **Display Source** | Monochrome CRT | High-brightness CRT / LCD | Laser + DLP / OLED | | **Luminance (fL)** | ~3,000 | 10,000–15,000 | >10,000 | | **Weight & Size** | Bulky (~15–20 kg) | Moderate (~8–12 kg) | Compact / helmet-mounted | | **Pilot Mobility** | Limited eye box | Larger eye box | Full head-tracking | | **Civil Example** | Boeing 727, early 737 | Boeing 777, A320, A330 | Gulfstream G650 (SVS) | | **Military Example** | F-15A (early), Tornado ADV | F-16, F/A-18, F-15E | F-35 HMDS, F-22, AH-64D Apache | **Brief Discussion:** - **Gen I** (1970s): The original concept. Used refractive optics, suffered from narrow FOV and limited daylight brightness. - **Gen II** (1980s–1990s): Holographic combiners enabled wider FOVs and dramatically better sunlight readability, becoming the standard for modern airliners. - **Gen III** (2000s–Present): The paradigm shift to **helmet-mounted displays**, pioneered by the F-35. The pilot's helmet visor itself is the combiner, enabling full **360° cued vision** and a wide FOV that moves with the pilot's head. --- #### (b) Explain the following terms with reference to a HUD: (i) Eye Relief, (ii) Design Eye Box, (iii) Boresight, (iv) Instantaneous Field of View. (7 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper | OR Choice]** **Model Answer:** **(i) Eye Relief (ER):** The axial distance between the **rear surface of the combiner** and the **design eye point (DEP)** — i.e., where the pilot's eye is expected to be positioned for full FOV viewing. Typical values: ~**610 mm (24 in)** for civil transport HUDs, ~**250–400 mm** for military HUDs. A larger eye relief increases pilot comfort and head-movement tolerance. **(ii) Design Eye Box (DEB):** The three-dimensional volume in space (typically **5–6 inches in diameter** for civil HUDs) within which the pilot's eye must lie to see the entire HUD image without vignetting. The DEB is positioned such that the pilot's average eye location (at 95th percentile male / 5th percentile female) lies within it. Exceeding the DEB results in clipped or missing symbology. **(iii) Boresight:** The **optical reference axis** of the HUD — the line along which the center of the displayed image is aligned. Boresight is mechanically aligned with the aircraft's longitudinal axis during installation. A small angular offset (boresight error) is sometimes intentionally introduced to align the displayed symbol with the true horizon. The angle is: $$ \theta_{boresight} = \arctan\left(\frac{y_{offset}}{L_{datum}}\right) $$ **(iv) Instantaneous Field of View (IFOV):** The **angular extent of the actual displayed image** (the symbology), as seen from the design eye point. It is the angular size of the bright symbol on the display source, as seen through the collimator: $$ \boxed{\phi_{IFOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)} $$ where $d$ = display source active size and $f$ = collimator focal length. **IFOV is always ≤ TFOV** — the IFOV is the *information*, the TFOV is the *window*. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Pattern):** > > 1. **Confusing TFOV and IFOV (–2 marks):** Examiners frequently set numerical problems where students compute TFOV when the data is for IFOV (or vice versa). Always re-read the question: "*angular extent of the image*" = IFOV; "*angular window of the combiner*" = TFOV. > > 2. **Forgetting units in the final answer (–1 mark):** Even if your derivation is perfect, omitting "**degrees**" or "**mm**" costs a mark. KTU 2024 strictly follows the "unit marking" rule. > > 3. **Drawing a block diagram without labeling the arrows (–1 mark):** A block diagram of the HUD must have **labeled arrows** showing data/signal flow direction. Drawing boxes without arrows = partial credit only. > > 4. **Writing "HUD = Head Up Display" and stopping (–2 marks):** In 3-mark Part A questions, the definition alone is **not** enough. You must add 2 advantages / applications / examples to fetch full marks. > > 5. **Skipping the derivation step in 7-mark sub-parts (–3 marks):** KTU 2024 values *methodology*. Even if your final answer is correct, omitting the line "$\tan(\theta/2) = D/2f$" costs 2–3 marks. > > 6. **Not mentioning "collimation to infinity" (–1 mark):** Whenever you write about a HUD, the phrase "**optical infinity**" must appear. Examiners treat this as a keyword. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4 (HUD)** - **HUD = 3 Blocks:** Symbol Generator → Display Unit (Overhead) → Combiner. - **Core Principle:** Image is **collimated to optical infinity** so the pilot's eyes do not refocus. - **Combiner = selective reflector:** High reflectance at display wavelength, high transmittance elsewhere. - **TFOV Formula:** $\theta_{TFOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{D}{2f}\right)$ — depends on combiner width. - **IFOV Formula:** $\phi_{IFOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$ — depends on display source size. - **Optical Leverage Ratio:** $\dfrac{TFOV}{IFOV} \approx \dfrac{D}{d}$ — explains wide FOV military HUDs. - **Eye Relief:** Distance from combiner to design eye point (typically ~**610 mm civil**, ~**300 mm military**). - **Design Eye Box:** 3-D volume for the pilot's eye; ~**5–6 inches diameter** (civil). - **Three HUD Generations:** Optical Lens (Gen I) → Holographic (Gen II) → Wide-FOV / HMD (Gen III). - **Gen III Examples:** F-35 HMDS, AH-64D Apache Integrated Helmet, Gulfstream SVS. - **Modern Evolution:** EFVS (FLIR/IR) + SVS (database terrain) overlaid on HUD. - **Buses:** ARINC 429 (civil), MIL-STD-1553B (military). - **Standard Display Wavelength:** Green ~**545 nm** (highest photopic eye sensitivity). - **Key Cockpit Numbers:** Luminance >**10,000 fL**; Transmittance >**70%**; Reflectance >**25%**. - **Always state "optical infinity"** when explaining collimation in KTU answers. - **Always include units** in numerical answers — degrees for FOV, mm for distances, fL for luminance. - **Block diagrams win marks:** A neat, labeled 3-block diagram is worth **3–4 marks** standalone.

topics.title: Head Down Display
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Head Down Display

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

The **Head Down Display (HDD)** is a category of primary cockpit flight instrument in which the display surface (screen) is mounted on the main instrument panel of an aircraft, positioned **below** the pilot's normal straight-ahead line of sight. To view critical flight information, the pilot must physically **lower the head and eyes** toward the panel — hence the name "Head Down." 

The HDD forms the principal component of the **Electronic Flight Instrument System (EFIS)** and is supplemented by the **Engine Indication and Crew Alerting System (EICAS)** in modern transport category aircraft. Standard HDD stack typically comprises:

- **Primary Flight Display (PFD)** – replaces traditional "six-pack" electromechanical instruments.
- **Navigation Display (ND)** – replaces Horizontal Situation Indicator (HSI) and RMI.
- **Engine-Indicating and Crew-Alerting System (EICAS) / Engine-Centered Display (ECD)** – replaces analog engine gauges.
- **Multi-Function Display (MFD)** – for systems synoptics, weather radar, checklists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** HDD is the conventional flight instrument arrangement where the line of sight drops **out** of the outside world into the cockpit panel. It is in direct contrast to the **Head-Up Display (HUD)**, where the line of sight remains aligned with the outside world. Both serve the same purpose — *transferring information to the pilot* — but at fundamentally different *attentional costs*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: Reading a Newspaper While Driving**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car and need to check your speed or fuel level. If you mount a tablet computer **on the dashboard below the windshield**, you must *take your eyes off the road*, look down, read the information, then re-acquire the outside view. The few seconds your eyes are off the road constitute a *visual attention scan*, during which you are effectively "flying blind" with respect to the outside world.
>
> The **Head Down Display works exactly this way.** The pilot's eyes transition from the *out-the-window view* (the outside world) to the *inside-the-cockpit view* (the panel). The brain must perform a re-scan and refocus every time this transition occurs. The **Head-Up Display (HUD)**, by contrast, overlays information on a combiner glass so the eyes never leave the outside world — like a speedometer projected onto the windshield.

## 1.3 Physical & Operational Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Metric |
| :--- | :--- |
| Typical Display Diagonal | **6.4 × 6.4 inches** (square) up to **10.4 × 7.8 inches** (wide) |
| Luminance Range | **0.05 fL** (night) to **>200 fL** (sunlight readable) |
| Viewing Angle | $\pm 60^{\circ}$ horizontal, $\pm 30^{\circ}$ vertical |
| Refresh Rate | **60 Hz minimum**, military/glass cockpit up to **85 Hz** |
| Mean Time Between Failure (MTBF) | **> 10,000 flight hours** |
| Power Consumption | **40–150 W** per display unit |
| Weight (per unit) | **4–8 kg** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Head-Down Concept

The fundamental engineering trade-off that gave rise to the HDD arrangement is rooted in **human factors** and **scan philosophy**:

1. **Field of View (FOV) Hierarchy:** The human eye has a *focal FOV* of $\pm 5^{\circ}$ (sharp central vision) and *useful FOV* of $\pm 30^{\circ}$. The HDD is positioned at roughly $20^{\circ}$–$30^{\circ}$ below the design eye reference point.
2. **Visual Re-Accommodation:** When eyes shift from far focus (runway, ~infinity) to near focus (display at ~30 inches), the lens of the eye must change shape — a process taking **~0.3 to 0.5 seconds** per scan.
3. **Dark Adaptation Loss:** Bright sunlight entering the cockpit followed by glance at a dim panel causes loss of dark adaptation, degrading night vision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just put everything on the windscreen (HUD style)?** Because HUDs have limited *display real estate*, *brightness limitations* in direct sunlight, and *cost* constraints. HDD provides **larger, higher-fidelity displays** for navigation maps, system synoptics, and engine data which cannot be efficiently rendered on a small combiner.

## 2.2 Standard HDD Panel Layout (B737 NG / A320 Family)

The HDD is conventionally arranged in a **"T" or "PFD-ND-EICAS" configuration**:

- **PFD** (left): Attitude, Airspeed, Altitude, Heading, Vertical Speed.
- **ND** (right of PFD): Compass arc/rose, route, waypoints, weather radar.
- **EICAS / ECD** (center, sometimes below): Engine N1, EGT, Fuel Quantity, Hydraulics, Electrical.

This **PFD-ND pairing** is sometimes called the **"Primary Display Pair"** in KTU/ICAO terminology.

## 2.3 Display Technologies Used in HDD

| Technology | Full Form | Era | Pros | Cons |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CRT** | Cathode Ray Tube | 1970s–1990s | High contrast, fast response | Heavy, high voltage, heat |
| **AMLCD** | Active Matrix Liquid Crystal Display | 1990s–Present | Lightweight, low power | Backlight failure, slower response |
| **OLED** | Organic Light Emitting Diode | 2010s–Present | True blacks, wide viewing angle | Burn-in, lifetime |
| **LED-backlit LCD** | Hybrid | 2000s–Present | Sunlight readable, robust | Slightly higher power |

## 2.4 Display Symbology Standards

The HDD follows **SAE AS 8034** and **ARINC 661** standards for cockpit display symbology. Key symbolic elements:

- **Magenta** = active flight mode / autothrottle engaged.
- **Green** = normal operating range.
- **Amber/Yellow** = caution (non-urgent).
- **Red** = warning (immediate action required).
- **White** = scales, fixed markings, current values.
- **Cyan** = selected bugs/values.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HDD is largely a *descriptive/system-level* topic. The few numerical relations that appear in KTU 2024 examination questions are listed below.

| Equation | Meaning | Variables / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $t_{scan} = t_{re-accommodate} + t_{read} + t_{fixate}$ | Total visual scan time per instrument | seconds |
| $F = \frac{1}{t_{scan}}$ | Effective information rate | Hz or bits/s |
| $L_{min} = 0.05$ fL | Night-readable luminance floor (MIL-STD-3009) | foot-lamberts |
| $L_{max} = 200$ fL | Sunlight readable luminance | foot-lamberts |
| $R_{contrast} = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{L_{min}}$ | Display contrast ratio (dimensionless) | $R_{contrast} \geq 6$ |
| $FOV_{design} = \pm 30^{\circ}$ | Standard eye reference FOV (head fixed) | degrees |
| $MTTR_{display} \leq 15$ min | Avionics maintenance Mean Time To Repair | minutes |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (KTU):** The **scan law** derived from **H.W. Liepmann's 1946 study** and later **P.G. Fitts & C. Jones (1947, USAF)** showed that optimum instrument scanning follows a **pattern-weighted priority** based on *task criticality*, not alphabetical order or geometric proximity. Modern EFIS design (and thus HDD layout) directly implements this via the **PFD-ND-EICAS** priority hierarchy.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Glass cockpits** in the Boeing 777, 787, Airbus A320, A350, A380 all use HDD as primary.
- **General Aviation** (e.g., Garmin G1000, G3000) uses HDD in Cessna 172/182, Cirrus SR22.
- **Military** (F-16, F/A-18) also use HDD alongside HUD and Helmet-Mounted Displays (HMD).
- **Rotorcraft** (Apache AH-64, Black Hawk) use HDD for tactical information.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Visual Scan Time vs. Information Transfer

When a pilot glances from the outside world (focus at infinity) to an HDD (focus at $d \approx 0.75$ m from the eye), the eye undergoes a physiological re-accommodation. The total scan time to extract a single datum is:

$$t_{scan} = t_{re-focus} + t_{foveal-fixation} + t_{read}$$

**Step 1:** Define the re-accommodation time for a healthy pilot under daylight conditions.

$$t_{re-focus} \approx 0.3 \text{ s (far-to-near)}$$

**Step 2:** Define the foveal fixation time — the brief moment needed to align the target datum onto the fovea centralis.

$$t_{foveal-fixation} \approx 0.15 \text{ to } 0.25 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Define the cognitive "read" time — how long the brain needs to interpret the value.

$$t_{read} \approx 0.10 \text{ s for a digital numeric display}$$

**Step 4:** Sum the components for a *single* HDD glance under good lighting.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{scan} &= 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.10 \\
t_{scan} &= 0.60 \text{ s per instrument}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The *effective* information rate, assuming a single 4-digit numeric value conveys $\log_2(10^4) \approx 13.3$ bits of information:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{I_{datum}}{t_{scan}} = \frac{13.3 \text{ bits}}{0.60 \text{ s}} \\
F &\approx 22.2 \text{ bits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Because HDD glances *interleave* with outside-world scans, the *practical* information transfer during instrument flying is roughly **5–8 bits/s** per pilot — a key reason modern cockpits **integrate** related parameters on a single display (e.g., airspeed, altitude, attitude together on PFD) to reduce the number of head-down glances.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Display Luminance for Sunlight Readability

A display must be visible against a bright sky background. The required display luminance $L_d$ for a target contrast ratio $C$ against background luminance $L_b$ is:

$$L_d = L_b \cdot (1 + C)$$

**Step 1:** Worst-case ambient is a sunlit cloud at roughly $L_b = 10,000$ fL (foot-lamberts).

**Step 2:** The minimum contrast ratio for legibility, per **MIL-STD-3009**, is $C \geq 6$.

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_d &= 10,000 \text{ fL} \cdot (1 + 6) \\
L_d &= 70,000 \text{ fL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** This is a *theoretical* figure. In practice, **anti-reflective coatings**, **circular polarizers**, and **neutral-density filters** reduce the effective requirement to **150–350 fL** — which is why modern AMLCD cockpits can use lower-power backlights.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PFD-ND Sizing for A320 Class Aircraft

The geometric placement of the PFD and ND on the panel follows a *minimum eye reference* rule. The PFD center is at a *Design Eye Reference Point* (DERP) of roughly **0° elevation** (straight ahead) and the ND is offset **+12°** to the right.

**Step 1:** The pilot's eye is approximately $d = 0.75$ m from the panel.

**Step 2:** The horizontal offset of ND center:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{ND} &= d \cdot \tan(12^{\circ}) \\
x_{ND} &= 0.75 \cdot 0.2126 \\
x_{ND} &\approx 0.16 \text{ m} = 16 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The PFD is offset *symmetrically* to the left at $-12^{\circ}$ (same value): $x_{PFD} = -16$ cm.

**Step 4:** PFD-to-ND center-to-center distance: $32$ cm — sufficient to fit two **6.4 × 6.4 inch** displays side-by-side with **1.6 cm** of bezel gap. This matches the Airbus A320 panel layout.

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix: HDD vs. HUD vs. HMD

| Feature | **HDD** | **HUD** | **HMD** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Eye line of sight | Drops away from outside world | Aligned with outside world | Aligned with outside world |
| Field of view | Large (full panel) | Small (~30° × 25°) | Wide (~40° binocular) |
| Information density | Very high (full maps) | Limited (key flight params) | Moderate |
| Sunlight readability | Excellent | Good (with combiner) | Moderate |
| Cost | Low–Medium | High | Very high |
| Pilot fatigue | Higher (scanning) | Lower | Lowest |
| Primary use | Navigation, systems | Takeoff, landing, weapons | Tactical, rotorcraft |
| KTU example | A320 PFD/ND | F-16 HUD | Apache IHADSS |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDD System Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Air Data Inertial Reference Unit ADIRU] --> B[Display Processing Computer DPC]
    C[FMS Flight Management System] --> B
    D[Radio Navigation VOR DME ILS] --> B
    E[EICAS Computer] --> B
    F[Weather Radar Receiver] --> B
    B --> G[Symbol Generator SG 1]
    B --> H[Symbol Generator SG 2]
    G --> I[Primary Flight Display PFD Left]
    H --> J[Navigation Display ND Right]
    G --> K[EICAS Upper Display]
    H --> L[EICAS Lower Display]
    I --> M[Pilot Side Visual Scan Path]
    J --> N[Copilot Side Visual Scan Path]
    K --> O[Engine Crew Alerting Path]
    L --> O
    P[Control Panel] --> B
    P --> I
    P --> J
    P --> K
    P --> L
    styleA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    styleB fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900
    styleG fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```
