topics.title: Very High Omnidirectional Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Very High Omnidirectional Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **VOR (VHF Omnidirectional Range)** is a short-range radio navigation system operating in the Very High Frequency (VHF) band of **108.00 MHz to 117.95 MHz**, which enables an aircraft receiver to determine its **magnetic bearing (radial)** relative to a fixed ground-based beacon with a **standard accuracy of ± 2°** under normal operating conditions.

According to the **International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO)** standard, VOR is officially designated as a **standard en-route short-distance navigation aid (Short Range NAVAID)** and serves as the principal navigational reference for **Visual Flight Rules (VFR)** and **Instrument Flight Rules (IFR)** operations in national and continental airspace networks.

| Parameter | ICAO / KTU Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Frequency Band** | 108.00 MHz – 117.95 MHz |
| **Channel Spacing** | 50 kHz (with 100 kHz sub-channels for Doppler VOR) |
| **Operating Range** | 40 NM (low altitude) to 200 NM (high altitude) |
| **Bearing Accuracy** | ± 2° (95% probability) |
| **Modulation Frequencies** | 30 Hz (reference + variable) and 9960 Hz (subcarrier) |
| **Polarization** | Horizontal (H) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lighthouse of the Sky" 💡

Imagine standing in the middle of a giant circular pond with a lighthouse in the exact center:

1. The **lighthouse beam** rotates at a constant speed (e.g., 30 revolutions per second → 30 Hz), sweeping across the entire 360° horizon — this is the **variable (rotating) signal** of the VOR.
2. At the **exact same moment** the beam points toward **Magnetic North**, a separate **omnidirectional "ping"** is broadcast equally in all directions — this is the **reference signal**.
3. An aircraft floating on the pond measures the **time delay** (or phase difference) between the omni-ping and the lighthouse sweep arriving at its position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Intuition:** A bearing from the VOR station is simply the **phase difference** between two 30 Hz signals measured at the aircraft. **0° phase difference = Magnetic North (000° Radial)**. The phase increases clockwise just like a compass rose.

This elegant phase-comparison principle is what makes VOR a **"omnidirectional"** range — unlike older ADF/NDB systems, VOR gives an unambiguous magnetic bearing regardless of the aircraft's direction from the station, hence the name **Omni**directional Range.

---

## 1.3 Why VOR is a Pillar of Aviation Navigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** VOR is covered in Module 4 of PCAOT205 because it is the **most widely deployed short-range NAVAID in the world**, with over **3,000 ICAO-listed ground stations** providing a redundant, ground-based navigation network that complements modern GNSS systems.

The four foundational properties that make VOR indispensable are:

1. **Omnidirectionality** — A single ground station defines **360 radials** (one for every magnetic degree).
2. **Magnetic Referencing** — Radials are referenced to Magnetic North, not True North, eliminating pilot mental math.
3. **Line-of-Sight (LOS) Operation** — VHF signals travel in straight lines, so the range is limited by the radio horizon (~1.23 × √h in NM, where h is in feet).
4. **Phase-Based Robustness** — Phase comparison is largely immune to amplitude fading, static, and thunderstorm noise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Phase Comparison Method

The VOR system broadcasts **two simultaneous 30 Hz signals** on the same VHF carrier frequency. The aircraft receiver measures the **electrical phase angle (φ)** between them, and converts this phase into a **magnetic bearing (θ)**.

### 2.1.1 The Two 30 Hz Signals Explained

| Signal | Generation Method | Radiation Pattern | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reference Signal** | Frequency Modulated (FM) on a 9960 Hz sub-carrier, then AM-modulated on the VHF carrier. | **Omnidirectional** — same amplitude in all 360° directions. | Provides the "time zero" reference pulse aligned with Magnetic North. |
| **Variable Signal** | Generated by a rotating **Alford Loop antenna** at **30 revolutions/second** (30 Hz). | **Cardioid (heart-shaped)** pattern that rotates 360° in 1/30th of a second. | Its amplitude at the aircraft depends on the aircraft's bearing from the station. |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Trick:** When the cardioid lobe of the variable signal points **directly at the aircraft**, the phase of the variable signal **coincides** with the reference signal at the aircraft receiver. This bearing is defined as **0° (Magnetic North)**.

---

### 2.1.2 Signal Generation Chain — Step-by-Step

The complete signal flow in a Conventional VOR (CVOR) ground station follows this precise sequence:

1. **30 Hz Reference Oscillator** generates a stable 30 Hz sine wave.
2. This 30 Hz reference is **frequency-modulated** onto a **9960 Hz sub-carrier** (with a deviation of ± 480 Hz, giving a total swing of 9.48 kHz to 10.44 kHz).
3. The FM sub-carrier is then **amplitude-modulated** (AM) onto the main VHF carrier (108–117.95 MHz) at a depth of **30%**.
4. A separate **30 Hz Variable Signal** is generated by mechanically rotating a directional **Alford Loop antenna** at 1800 rpm (30 rev/sec).
5. The variable signal is amplitude-modulated onto the same VHF carrier at a depth of **30%**.
6. The two 30 Hz modulations (one on the sub-carrier, one on the main carrier envelope) are **locked in phase** at the **Magnetic North** radial.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **9960 Hz sub-carrier frequency** is chosen because it is the lowest frequency in the VHF audio band that is **inaudible to human voice communication**, preventing interference with ATC voice channels multiplexed on the same carrier.

---

## 2.2 Frequency Allocation & Channel Structure

The VOR band is **108.00 MHz – 117.95 MHz**, with **50 kHz channel spacing**, yielding:

$$N_{channels} = \frac{117.95 - 108.00}{0.05} + 1 = 200 \text{ channels}$$

Of these 200 channels, **40 are paired with ILS Localizers** (108.10–111.95 MHz in odd-decimal steps), leaving **160 dedicated VOR channels**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency Pairing:** Each VOR frequency is paired with a **paired DME (Distance Measuring Equipment)** or **TACAN** channel. This combined station is called a **VOR/DME** or **VORTAC**, providing both **bearing AND range** simultaneously — a 2D position fix.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula, constant, and unit** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for VOR problems.

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Description & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bearing from Phase Difference** | $\theta = \phi_{ref} - \phi_{var}$ | Bearing θ in **degrees (Magnetic)**, phase φ in **degrees/radians** |
| **Phase-to-Time Conversion** | $\phi = 360° \times f \times \Delta t$ | With f = 30 Hz, φ = 10800 × Δt (in seconds) |
| **Period of 30 Hz Signal** | $T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{30} \approx 33.33 \text{ ms}$ | One full 360° phase rotation |
| **Time per Degree** | $\Delta t_{1°} = \frac{T}{360°} = \frac{33.33}{360} \approx 92.6 \text{ μs/°}$ | Microseconds per degree of phase |
| **Line-of-Sight Range** | $R_{LOS} = 1.23 \times (\sqrt{h_{AC}} + \sqrt{h_{GS}})$ | R in NM, h in feet; GS = ground station |
| **Bearing Accuracy** | $\pm 2°$ | ICAO standard for CVOR |
| **Sub-carrier Frequency** | $f_{sub} = 9960 \text{ Hz}$ | FM sub-carrier with ±480 Hz deviation |
| **Modulation Depths** | Reference AM = 30%, Variable AM = 30% | Sum ≤ 60% to avoid overmodulation |
| **Antenna Rotation Speed** | $\omega = 1800 \text{ RPM} = 30 \text{ rev/sec}$ | Equivalent to 30 Hz |
| **Doppler VOR Bearing Error** | $\epsilon \propto \frac{D}{\lambda}$ | D = antenna array diameter, λ = wavelength |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The phase-comparison principle used in VOR is **not just a textbook curiosity**. It underpins the following real engineering applications:

- **Civil Aviation Route Structure:** VOR airways (called **"Victor Airways"** below 18,000 ft in the US, and **"ATS Routes"** globally) connect VOR stations into a network of predefined corridors.
- **Holding Patterns:** Aircraft hold over VOR stations using specific **inbound/outbound radials** (e.g., "Hold west of XYZ VOR on the 270° radial, inbound course 090°, right turns").
- **Approach Procedures:** **VOR Approach** and **VOR/DME Approach** are published terminal procedures allowing landings in low-visibility conditions.
- **Military Tactical Air Navigation:** **TACAN** is the military version of VOR, providing both bearing and range in a single system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Relevance:** Even in the **GNSS / RNAV era**, VOR is mandated by ICAO as a **mandatory backup navigation aid** at most airports. All KTU aeronautical engineers must understand its operation for **CNS/ATM (Communication, Navigation, Surveillance / Air Traffic Management)** career roles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Phase Difference to Magnetic Bearing

The fundamental VOR equation relates the measured phase difference at the aircraft receiver to the magnetic bearing of the aircraft from the ground station.

### Step 1: Define the Reference Signal

The reference signal is **omnidirectional** and time-invariant in phase. It can be expressed as:

$$v_{ref}(t) = A_{ref} \cdot \sin(2\pi f t + \phi_{0})$$

where $f = 30 \text{ Hz}$ is the reference frequency and $\phi_0$ is a constant initial phase locked to the moment the variable antenna points to **Magnetic North**.

### Step 2: Define the Variable Signal

The variable signal at the aircraft receiver has an amplitude that depends on the aircraft's bearing θ, AND a phase that shifts by θ relative to the reference:

$$v_{var}(t) = A_{var}(\theta) \cdot \sin(2\pi f t + \phi_{0} + \theta)$$

**Explanation:** When the rotating cardioid lobe points directly at the aircraft (bearing θ from North), the signal amplitude $A_{var}(\theta)$ is maximum, and its **electrical phase has advanced by exactly θ degrees** compared to the reference signal.

### Step 3: Compute the Phase Difference

The phase difference φ measured by the aircraft receiver's phase detector is:

$$\phi = \phi_{var} - \phi_{ref} = (\phi_0 + \theta) - \phi_0 = \theta$$

**Result:** The measured phase difference **equals the magnetic bearing** in degrees.

$$\boxed{\theta_{magnetic} = \phi_{var} - \phi_{ref}}$$

### Step 4: Convert Phase Difference from Time Delay

If the receiver measures a time delay $\Delta t$ between the reference and variable signal peaks, then:

$$\theta = 360° \times f \times \Delta t$$

Substituting $f = 30 \text{ Hz}$:

$$\theta = 360 \times 30 \times \Delta t = 10800 \times \Delta t \quad (\Delta t \text{ in seconds})$$

**Numerical Example:** If $\Delta t = 16.67 \text{ ms} = 0.01667 \text{ s}$:

$$\theta = 10800 \times 0.01667 = 180.0°$$

This corresponds to the **South Radial (180°)** — the aircraft is due south of the VOR station.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Line-of-Sight Range Calculation

The radio horizon (and hence VOR range) is determined by the curvature of the Earth. The geometric range between a ground station at height $h_{GS}$ and an aircraft at height $h_{AC}$ is:

### Step 1: Define the Geometric Setup

Consider the Earth as a perfect sphere of radius $R = 3440 \text{ NM}$ (mean Earth radius in nautical miles). The line-of-sight horizon distance for a single station at height $h$ is:

$$d = \sqrt{2Rh + h^2}$$

For $h \ll R$ (true for aircraft altitudes), $h^2$ is negligible:

$$d \approx \sqrt{2Rh}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Earth Radius Constant

$$d \approx \sqrt{2 \times 3440 \times h} = \sqrt{6880 \times h} \text{ NM}$$

$$d \approx 82.95 \times \sqrt{h} \text{ NM}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Empirical ICAO Correction Factor

Empirically, **atmospheric refraction** bends VHF rays slightly downward, effectively extending the range. The **ICAO standard** multiplies the result by a factor of **1.23** (this empirically derived constant accounts for standard atmospheric refraction):

$$R_{LOS} \approx 1.23 \times 82.95 \times \sqrt{h} \approx 1.23 \sqrt{h} \text{ NM (with h in feet, coefficient 1.23)}$$

More precisely, when **both** the ground station and aircraft contribute to the horizon:

$$R_{LOS} = 1.23 \times (\sqrt{h_{AC}} + \sqrt{h_{GS}}) \text{ NM}$$

where $h_{AC}$ and $h_{GS}$ are in **feet**.

### Step 4: Numerical Example

An aircraft at **25,000 ft** is receiving a VOR from a station at **50 ft** AGL:

$$R_{LOS} = 1.23 \times (\sqrt{25000} + \sqrt{50})$$

$$R_{LOS} = 1.23 \times (158.11 + 7.07) = 1.23 \times 165.18$$

$$\boxed{R_{LOS} \approx 203.2 \text{ NM}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sub-Carrier Frequency Modulation Analysis

The reference signal is **frequency-modulated** onto the 9960 Hz sub-carrier. The instantaneous frequency of the FM sub-carrier is:

$$f_{inst}(t) = f_c + \Delta f \cdot \sin(2\pi f_{mod} t)$$

where:
- $f_c = 9960 \text{ Hz}$ (sub-carrier center frequency)
- $\Delta f = 480 \text{ Hz}$ (peak frequency deviation)
- $f_{mod} = 30 \text{ Hz}$ (modulating reference frequency)

### Step 1: Compute the Modulation Index

The Carson's rule modulation index β is:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_{mod}} = \frac{480}{30} = 16$$

### Step 2: Apply Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_{mod}) = 2(480 + 30) = 1020 \text{ Hz}$$

The sub-carrier sweeps from $9960 - 480 = 9480 \text{ Hz}$ to $9960 + 480 = 10440 \text{ Hz}$, occupying a total bandwidth of **1020 Hz**.

### Step 3: Why This Matters in Practice

This 1 kHz FM sub-carrier bandwidth is **well below** the 50 kHz VOR channel spacing, ensuring that the **adjacent channel interference** is minimal and **Voice communication** (300 Hz – 3 kHz) can be multiplexed on the same carrier without disturbing the navigation reference signal.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of VOR Variants

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to know the **three main VOR variants** and their engineering trade-offs.

| Parameter | **CVOR** (Conventional VOR) | **DVOR** (Doppler VOR) | **TVOR** (Terminal VOR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Full Form** | Conventional VHF Omnidirectional Range | Doppler VHF Omnidirectional Range | Terminal VOR |
| **Antenna Type** | Rotating Alford Loop | Static circular array (30+ antennas), electronically switched | Rotating Alford Loop (low-power) |
| **Variable Signal Generation** | Mechanical rotation of cardioid pattern | Simulated rotation via Doppler frequency shift on switched elements | Same as CVOR but smaller scale |
| **Bearing Accuracy** | ± 2° | ± 1° (better due to Doppler effect) | ± 2° (lower-power, shorter range) |
| **Range** | 40–200 NM | 40–200 NM | 25 NM (terminal use) |
| **Site Error Sensitivity** | High (multipath from nearby terrain) | Low (Doppler principle rejects multipath) | Moderate |
| **Maintenance Cost** | High (mechanical rotation, brushes, motors) | Low (solid-state, no moving parts) | High |
| **Typical Application** | En-route navigation | Precision approach & en-route | Airport terminal area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Key Insight:** **DVOR achieves higher accuracy by exploiting the Doppler Effect.** When the aircraft is east of the station, the simulated antenna motion towards the aircraft produces a positive Doppler shift on the variable signal — a phase shift that the receiver decodes as bearing. This Doppler principle makes DVOR **immune to most site-error multipath**, a major limitation of CVOR.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VOR Ground Station Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal flow inside a Conventional VOR (CVOR) ground station, from reference oscillator generation to antenna radiation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    oscRef["30 Hz Reference Oscillator"] --> fmMod["9960 Hz FM Sub-carrier Modulator"]
    oscSub["9960 Hz Sub-carrier Oscillator"] --> fmMod
    fmMod --> amMod1["30% AM Modulator Stage 1"]
    
    motor["1800 RPM Drive Motor"] --> alford["Rotating Alford Loop Antenna"]
    alford --> cardioid["Cardioid Radiation Pattern 30 Hz"]
    cardioid --> amMod2["30% AM Modulator Stage 2"]
    
    vhfOsc["VHF Carrier Oscillator 108 to 117.95 MHz"] --> amMod1
    vhfOsc --> amMod2
    amMod1 --> combiner["RF Signal Combiner"]
    amMod2 --> combiner
    combiner --> pa["Power Amplifier 50 to 200 W"]
    pa --> antFeed["Antenna Feed Network"]
    antFeed --> vhfAnt["Omnidirectional VHF Antenna"]
    antFeed --> alford
    
    magSensor["Magnetic North Sensor"] --> phaseLock["Phase Lock Circuit"]
    oscRef --> phaseLock
    phaseLock --> alford
    
    style oscRef fill:#FFE08A
    style vhfOsc fill:#9CD3F4
    style pa fill:#FF9E9E
    style vhfAnt fill:#A8E6A3
    style alford fill:#A8E6A3
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **Yellow nodes** represent low-frequency signal sources (30 Hz and 9960 Hz).
- **Blue node** represents the VHF carrier oscillator.
- **Red node** is the high-power RF amplifier (CVOR stations typically transmit 50–200 W).
- **Green nodes** are the radiating elements.
- The **Phase Lock Circuit** synchronizes the Alford Loop's "North-pointing moment" with the reference signal phase — this is the **physical embodiment of the 0° radial definition**.

---

## 4.2 VOR Aircraft Receiver Signal Processing Block Diagram

The aircraft receiver performs the reverse operation: it extracts the two 30 Hz modulations and measures their phase difference.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ant["Aircraft VHF Antenna"] --> preamp["Low Noise Preamplifier"]
    preamp --> mixer["Mixer with Local Oscillator"]
    lo["Tunable Local Oscillator 108 to 117.95 MHz"] --> mixer
    mixer --> ifAmp["IF Amplifier 10.7 MHz or 21.4 MHz"]
    ifAmp --> amDet["AM Envelope Detector"]
    ifAmp --> fmDet["FM Sub-carrier Discriminator 9960 Hz"]
    
    amDet --> filter30a["30 Hz Bandpass Filter Variable Signal"]
    fmDet --> demod30["30 Hz Demodulator Reference Signal"]
    demod30 --> filter30b["30 Hz Bandpass Filter Reference Signal"]
    
    filter30a --> phaseDet["Phase Comparator Circuit"]
    filter30b --> phaseDet
    
    phaseDet --> micro["Microprocessor Bearing Calculation"]
    micro --> display["CDI or RMI Display"]
    micro --> obs["OBS Knob Selected Radial"]
    
    style ant fill:#A8E6A3
    style phaseDet fill:#FFE08A
    style micro fill:#FF9E9E
    style display fill:#9CD3F4
```

**Key Functional Stages:**

1. **Mixer + Local Oscillator** — Standard superheterodyne down-conversion.
2. **AM Detector** — Recovers the **variable 30 Hz** from the carrier envelope.
3. **FM Discriminator** — Recovers the **reference 30 Hz** from the 9960 Hz sub-carrier.
4. **Phase Comparator** — Outputs a DC voltage proportional to the **phase difference (i.e., the magnetic bearing)**.
5. **CDI/RMI Display** — Course Deviation Indicator / Radio Magnetic Indicator shows the bearing to the pilot.

---

## 4.3 VOR Bearing Geometry — Radial Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "VOR Ground Station - Center"
        stn["VOR Beacon"]
    end
    
    stn --- north["000 Radial North"]
    stn --- east["090 Radial East"]
    stn --- south["180 Radial South"]
    stn --- west["270 Radial West"]
    stn --- ne["045 Radial NE"]
    stn --- sw["225 Radial SW"]
    
    style stn fill:#FF6B6B
    style north fill:#A8E6A3
    style east fill:#A8E6A3
    style south fill:#A8E6A3
    style west fill:#A8E6A3
    style ne fill:#FFE08A
    style sw fill:#FFE08A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Convention:** A **"radial"** is the magnetic bearing **FROM the station TO the aircraft**. An aircraft on the "090° radial" is located **due East** of the VOR. An aircraft on the "270° radial" is **due West**. The pilot selects a desired radial using the **Omni-Bearing Selector (OBS)** knob on the CDI.

---

## 4.4 VOR Airborne Equipment Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Cockpit Instruments"
        cdi["CDI Course Deviation Indicator"]
        rmi["RMI Radio Magnetic Indicator"]
        hsi["HSI Horizontal Situation Indicator"]
    end
    
    subgraph "Avionics Rack"
        navBox["VOR Navigation Receiver"]
        antSelector["Antenna Coupler and Selector"]
    end
    
    subgraph "Antennas"
        antVHF["VOR VHF Antenna 108 to 118 MHz"]
        antGPS["Optional GPS Antenna Backup"]
    end
    
    subgraph "Power and Data Bus"
        dcBus["28 V DC Aircraft Bus"]
        arinc["ARINC 429 Data Bus"]
    end
    
    antVHF --> antSelector
    antGPS -.Backup.-> antSelector
    antSelector --> navBox
    navBox --> cdi
    navBox --> rmi
    navBox --> hsi
    navBox <--> arinc
    dcBus --> navBox
    
    style cdi fill:#9CD3F4
    style navBox fill:#FFE08A
    style antVHF fill:#A8E6A3
    style dcBus fill:#FF9E9E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The **ARINC 429 data bus** is the industry-standard avionics communication protocol. The VOR receiver outputs **bearing, identifier (Morse code), and station-selection validity** as ARINC 429 labels, which are shared with **FMS (Flight Management System)**, **autopilot**, and **glass-cockpit displays**.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]

**Define VOR. State the operating frequency band and standard bearing accuracy of a Conventional VOR (CVOR) system.**

#### Model Answer:

> **VOR (VHF Omnidirectional Range)** is a short-range radio navigation system that provides an aircraft with its **magnetic bearing** relative to a ground-based beacon, using **phase comparison** between two 30 Hz signals.
>
> - **Operating Frequency Band:** 108.00 MHz to 117.95 MHz (VHF).
> - **Standard Bearing Accuracy:** ± 2° (95% probability) as per ICAO Annex 10.
> - **Operating Principle:** The phase difference between an **omnidirectional reference signal** and a **rotating variable signal** is measured at the aircraft; this phase difference directly equals the magnetic bearing in degrees.

**Mark Distribution (Typical KTU):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Frequency band: 1 mark
- Accuracy & principle: 1 mark

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**What is the purpose of the 9960 Hz sub-carrier in a VOR signal? Why is this specific frequency chosen?**

#### Model Answer:

> The **9960 Hz sub-carrier** carries the **30 Hz reference signal** as a **frequency-modulated** (FM) waveform, which is then **amplitude-modulated** onto the VHF carrier.
>
> **Purpose:** It separates the reference signal from the variable signal (which sits directly on the AM envelope), allowing the aircraft receiver to extract the two 30 Hz components independently and measure their **phase difference**.
>
> **Why 9960 Hz specifically?**
> - It lies in the VHF audio band but is **above the human voice range (300–3000 Hz)**.
> - This allows **ATC voice communication (typically 300–3000 Hz)** and the **1020 Hz FM sub-carrier** to coexist on the **same VHF carrier** without mutual interference — a concept called **multiplexing**.
> - The 9960 Hz center with ±480 Hz deviation (sweeping 9480–10440 Hz) keeps the navigation signal well clear of voice channels.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Purpose: 1.5 marks
- Frequency justification: 1.5 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering VOR numerical problems, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting to convert **magnetic bearing to true** (or vice versa) when the question specifies a magnetic variation.
> 2. Confusing the **omnidirectional reference signal** with the **variable signal** — remember: the **REFERENCE is omnidirectional**, the **VARIABLE is directional**.
> 3. Using the wrong unit (feet vs. meters) in the **line-of-sight range formula** — KTU strictly expects **feet** for the coefficient 1.23.
> 4. Skipping the "**Why?**" explanation in derivation questions — KTU 2024 scheme rewards **conceptual reasoning**, not just final numbers.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat block diagrams the **working principle of a VOR ground station**. Describe the role of the Alford Loop antenna and the 30 Hz reference signal in the phase comparison process.

#### Model Solution:

A VOR ground station generates two 30 Hz signals locked in phase at the **Magnetic North** radial:

1. **30 Hz Reference Signal Generation:**
   - A highly stable 30 Hz oscillator produces a sine wave.
   - This signal is frequency-modulated onto a 9960 Hz sub-carrier (with ±480 Hz deviation, total swing 9480–10440 Hz).
   - The FM sub-carrier is then amplitude-modulated onto the VHF carrier at 30% depth.
   - The reference signal is radiated **omnidirectionally** — same phase and amplitude in all 360° directions.

2. **30 Hz Variable Signal Generation:**
   - A 1800 RPM motor rotates a **directional Alford Loop antenna** at 30 revolutions per second.
   - The Alford Loop produces a **cardioid (heart-shaped) radiation pattern** with a sharp null in the opposite direction.
   - As the loop rotates, the cardioid lobe **sweeps through all 360° directions** at 30 Hz.
   - The signal is amplitude-modulated onto the same VHF carrier at 30% depth.
   - The phase of this signal at the aircraft receiver depends on the **bearing** of the aircraft from the station.

3. **Phase Locking Mechanism:**
   - A **magnetic north sensor** detects the moment the Alford Loop points to True/Magnetic North.
   - A **phase lock circuit** synchronizes the 30 Hz reference signal to this moment.
   - This ensures that the **0° phase difference** radial corresponds exactly to the **Magnetic North bearing**.

4. **Block Diagram of the System:**

```
Reference Oscillator (30 Hz) → FM Modulator → AM Modulator → RF Combiner → Power Amplifier → VHF Antenna
                                                            ↑
VHF Carrier Oscillator (108–117.95 MHz) ---------------------↑

Alford Loop (rotating at 30 rev/s via motor) → 30 Hz AM Modulator → RF Combiner
                                              ↑
                                Magnetic North Sensor (Phase Lock)
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Reference signal generation: 2 marks
- Variable signal and Alford Loop: 2 marks
- Phase locking and synchronization: 1.5 marks
- Block diagram: 1.5 marks

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An aircraft flying at **FL 250** (25,000 ft) is receiving signals from a VOR ground station located at an elevation of **150 ft AMSL**. The station antenna is **50 ft AGL**. Calculate the **maximum line-of-sight range** between the aircraft and the VOR station.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the heights**

- Aircraft altitude: $h_{AC} = 25{,}000 \text{ ft}$
- Ground station elevation AMSL: 150 ft
- Ground station antenna AGL: 50 ft
- Total ground station height AMSL: $h_{GS} = 150 + 50 = 200 \text{ ft}$

**Step 2: Apply the ICAO Line-of-Sight Formula**

$$R_{LOS} = 1.23 \times (\sqrt{h_{AC}} + \sqrt{h_{GS}}) \text{ NM}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values**

$$R_{LOS} = 1.23 \times (\sqrt{25000} + \sqrt{200})$$

$$R_{LOS} = 1.23 \times (158.114 + 14.142)$$

$$R_{LOS} = 1.23 \times 172.256$$

$$\boxed{R_{LOS} \approx 211.87 \text{ NM} \approx 212 \text{ NM}}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

The aircraft can receive usable VOR signals up to a maximum distance of approximately **212 NM** under standard atmospheric conditions. Beyond this range, the VHF signal is blocked by the Earth's curvature.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Formula selection: 1 mark
- Height calculation: 2 marks
- Substitution and arithmetic: 2 marks
- Final answer and interpretation: 2 marks

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **CVOR, DVOR, and TVOR** systems. Highlight the key engineering advantage of DVOR over CVOR and explain how the **Doppler effect** is exploited in DVOR.

#### Model Solution:

| Feature | CVOR | DVOR | TVOR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Generation Method** | Mechanically rotating Alford Loop | Static antenna array with electronic switching (simulated rotation) | Mechanically rotating Alford Loop, low power |
| **Bearing Accuracy** | ± 2° | ± 1° (better) | ± 2° |
| **Multipath Rejection** | Poor (site errors common) | Excellent (Doppler immunity) | Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | High (moving parts) | Low (solid-state) | High |
| **Range** | 40–200 NM | 40–200 NM | 25 NM (terminal use) |
| **Application** | En-route | En-route + Approach | Terminal area |

**The Doppler Principle in DVOR:**

In a DVOR, the ground station has a **circular array of typically 30 to 52 individual antennas** arranged around a central reference antenna. A high-speed electronic switch **sequentially activates** the outer antennas in succession, simulating a single antenna rotating around the circle at a very high rate.

This simulated motion creates a **Doppler frequency shift** on the variable signal:

$$\Delta f_{Doppler} = \frac{v_{sim}}{\lambda} \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

where:
- $v_{sim}$ is the simulated antenna velocity (very high, since switching occurs in microseconds).
- $\lambda$ is the wavelength at the VOR carrier frequency.
- $\theta$ is the bearing of the aircraft from the station.

**Key Engineering Advantage of DVOR:**

The **large effective antenna aperture** of DVOR (the diameter of the antenna ring, typically **13.5 meters** = 4 wavelengths) makes the variable signal **very sensitive to bearing** but **insensitive to amplitude variations from multipath reflections**. In CVOR, the variable signal is generated by a physically small Alford Loop (~1 wavelength), so multipath reflections from buildings, hills, or hangars can distort the cardioid pattern and cause **site errors of up to ± 10°** in extreme cases. DVOR reduces these errors to within the ± 1° ICAO specification.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Comparison table: 3 marks
- Doppler equation: 1.5 marks
- Explanation of DVOR advantage: 2.5 marks

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An aircraft on the **210° radial** of a VOR station measures a **time delay of 19.44 ms** between the reference and variable signal peaks. Verify whether the receiver is operating correctly, and calculate the **radial on which the aircraft is actually located**.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the bearing corresponding to the measured time delay**

Using the VOR phase-time formula:

$$\theta = 360° \times f \times \Delta t = 10800 \times \Delta t \quad (\text{with } f = 30 \text{ Hz})$$

$$\theta = 10800 \times 0.01944 \text{ s}$$

$$\theta = 209.95° \approx 210°$$

**Step 2: Verification**

The measured time delay corresponds to a bearing of **210°**, which matches the selected radial. **The receiver is operating correctly.**

**Step 3: State the radial**

The aircraft is on the **210° radial** (Magnetic), which is the **South-Southwest (SSW)** bearing from the VOR station.

**Step 4: Validation using phase angle**

The phase angle corresponding to 210° is:

$$\phi = 210° = \frac{210 \times \pi}{180} \approx 3.665 \text{ radians}$$

Checking with time:

$$\Delta t = \frac{\phi}{2\pi f} = \frac{3.665}{2\pi \times 30} = 0.01944 \text{ s} = 19.44 \text{ ms} \checkmark$$

**Mark Distribution:**
- Formula application: 2 marks
- Arithmetic correctness: 2 marks
- Verification reasoning: 1.5 marks
- Radial identification: 1.5 marks

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — VOR**

- ✅ **VOR** stands for **VHF Omnidirectional Range**; frequency band is **108.00 – 117.95 MHz**.
- ✅ The system uses **phase comparison** between two **30 Hz signals** (one reference, one variable).
- ✅ **Reference Signal** = omnidirectional, FM on a **9960 Hz sub-carrier**, AM on the VHF carrier at 30% depth.
- ✅ **Variable Signal** = rotating cardioid pattern (Alford Loop, 1800 RPM = 30 Hz), AM on the VHF carrier at 30% depth.
- ✅ **Phase difference = Magnetic bearing** (in degrees). 0° phase = 0° radial (Magnetic North).
- ✅ A **radial** is the bearing **from the station to the aircraft**, measured clockwise from Magnetic North.
- ✅ **ICAO Accuracy:** CVOR = ± 2°, DVOR = ± 1°.
- ✅ **Line-of-Sight Range formula:** $R_{LOS} = 1.23 \times (\sqrt{h_{AC}} + \sqrt{h_{GS}}) \text{ NM}$ (h in **feet**).
- ✅ **DVOR** uses the **Doppler effect** with a static antenna array — gives better accuracy, lower multipath errors, no moving parts.
- ✅ **TVOR (Terminal VOR)** is a low-power VOR used at airports, range ~25 NM.
- ✅ **VOR/DME** and **VORTAC** combine VOR bearing with **DME distance** for 2D position fixing.
- ✅ VOR is designated as a **mandatory ICAO backup NAVAID** even in the GNSS era.
- ✅ **Alford Loop** is a square-loop antenna with four feed points that creates a cardioid pattern.
- ✅ The **9960 Hz sub-carrier** is chosen to be **above the voice band** (300–3000 Hz) for multiplexing.
- ✅ VOR signals are **horizontally polarized**; vertical polarization causes bearing errors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Pitfall Reminder:** In numerical problems, always verify the **units of the LOS range formula** (feet, not meters) and always **state the magnetic variation** if a True bearing is involved. The 1.23 factor is **empirically derived** for standard atmosphere — do not derive it from first principles unless the question explicitly asks for it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Direction Finder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automatic Direction Finder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automatic Direction Finder (ADF)**: An aircraft radio navigation receiver system that automatically and continuously determines the **bearing** (i.e., the horizontal angle measured clockwise from the aircraft's nose/heading) of a ground-based **Non-Directional Beacon (NDB)** operating in the Low Frequency (LF) and Medium Frequency (MF) bands.

The system comprises three main functional blocks:
1. **NDB (Ground Transmitter Station)**: An omnidirectional, non-directional radio beacon radiating equally in all azimuth directions.
2. **Aircraft Receiving Antenna Assembly**: Consisting of a **Loop Antenna** (directional) and a **Sense (Omnidirectional) Antenna** mounted on the airframe.
3. **On-Board ADF Receiver & Indicator**: A superheterodyne receiver coupled to a servo-driven loop goniometer and an **RMI (Radio Magnetic Indicator)** or **RBI (Relative Bearing Indicator)** for cockpit display.

The standard operational frequency band is **190 kHz to 1750 kHz** (LF/MF), with selected channels spaced at **500 Hz** intervals.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Talking Lighthouse"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Imagine standing in a dense fog at sea, holding a radio that receives sound from a single horn-blowing lighthouse that blows equally in all directions (an NDB). The ADF is your special hearing device that can instantly tell you **the exact compass direction** the lighthouse is from you, *and it updates that direction continuously as you move*.

- The **Lighthouse horn** = the **NDB ground station** (radiates in all directions equally).
- The **shape of your ears** (one pointed, one round) = the **Loop + Sense antenna** combination that "shapes" the hearing direction.
- The **compass needle on a display** = the **RMI pointer** showing the bearing.
- The **moving fog and the lighthouse horn never stopping** = real-time, continuous bearing updates.

This is, in essence, a **"radio compass"** — a compass that points not to the Earth's magnetic north, but to a chosen radio beacon.

## 1.3 Why ADF? Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is ADF still taught despite GPS?** ADF is mandated as a **back-up long-range navigation aid**, immune to satellite failure, jamming, and is independent of inertial reference systems. It is essential for **en-route navigation over oceanic/remote terrain** and for **non-precision instrument approaches**.

Explicit physical constants and standard metrics:
- Speed of radio waves: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- Operating band: **190 kHz – 1750 kHz** (LF/MF)
- Channel spacing: **500 Hz**
- Typical NDB power: **50 W to 2 kW** (locator) up to **25 kW** (en-route)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — The Three Subsystems

The ADF system is logically partitioned into:

### A. Ground Segment — The NDB
- A simple **vertical monopole** transmitting antenna, with the **earth acting as a perfect reflecting plane**, producing a **horizontally polarized** wave with a perfectly **omnidirectional azimuthal pattern**.
- Identification is via **Morse-coded two- or three-letter callsigns** (e.g., *BBI* – Bhubaneswar NDB).
- The NDB transmits a **carrier wave modulated by a 1020 Hz tone** for automatic receiver identification in modern systems.

### B. Airborne Antenna Assembly
- **Loop Antenna**: A rectangular or circular loop of wire, mechanically or electronically rotated. Two outputs are 180° out of phase — the key to direction finding.
- **Sense (Omni) Antenna**: A small vertical whip or loop providing a non-directional reference signal.
- **Goniometer**: A precision inductive coupler that subtracts and combines the loop and sense signals electronically (modern ADF systems are fully solid-state — no rotating mechanical loops in the cockpit).

### C. Receiver & Indicator
- **Superheterodyne receiver** with **automatic gain control (AGC)** and a **servo-driven balance loop**.
- Display: **RMI (Radio Magnetic Indicator)** — a combined compass card that shows the magnetic heading of the aircraft *and* the relative bearing of the NDB simultaneously via a single needle.

## 2.2 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind the Cardioid

### Step 1: Loop Antenna Pattern (Figure-8)
A loop antenna does **not** respond equally to signals from all directions. When a vertically polarized wave (E-field vertical) arrives, the induced EMF depends on the angle $\theta$ between the **plane of the loop** and the **direction of wave propagation**.

The induced EMF in a loop antenna is given by:
$$E_{\text{loop}} = k \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

This produces a **figure-8 (bidirectional) polar pattern** with two maxima and two nulls. The two nulls are **180° apart**, creating the famous **"180° ambiguity"** — a loop alone cannot tell whether the station is in front of or behind the aircraft.

### Step 2: Sense Antenna (Omnidirectional)
A small sense antenna responds equally in all azimuth directions, producing:
$$E_{\text{sense}} = k' \cdot (\text{constant})$$

This is a perfect **circular pattern** in the polar diagram.

### Step 3: Combining — The Cardioid
When the loop and sense signals are **added with appropriate phase and amplitude**, the resultant field strength becomes:
$$E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{loop}} + E_{\text{sense}} = A\cos(\theta) + A = A[\cos(\theta) + 1]$$

This is the equation of a **cardioid** (heart-shaped) pattern. The cardioid has a **single maximum** and a **single minimum**, which **resolves the 180° ambiguity** of the loop alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 180° ambiguity is resolved by the sense antenna** — this is the single most important concept tested in KTU on ADF. The phase relationship between the loop and sense signal selects the "front" lobe of the cardioid, pointing toward the NDB.

## 2.3 Bearing Nomenclature — The Three "Norths" Problem

A KTU-favourite question. The bearing of the NDB from the aircraft can be expressed in **three different reference frames**:

| Bearing Type | Reference Direction | Equation |
|--------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Relative Bearing (RB)** | Aircraft's Nose (0° clockwise) | $RB = \theta$ (loop output) |
| **Magnetic Bearing (MB)** | Magnetic North (0° clockwise) | $MB = RB + MH$ |
| **True Bearing (TB)** | True (Geographic) North (0° clockwise) | $TB = RB + TH$ |

Where:
- $MH$ = Magnetic Heading of aircraft
- $TH$ = True Heading of aircraft
- Variation (WCA) = $MH - TH$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 — Memorize These:**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $E_{\text{loop}} = A\cos(\theta)$ | Loop antenna directional EMF (figure-8 pattern) |
| 2 | $E_{\text{total}} = A[\cos(\theta) + 1]$ | Combined cardioid response (loop + sense) |
| 3 | $MB = RB + MH$ | Magnetic bearing of NDB |
| 4 | $TB = RB + TH$ | True bearing of NDB |
| 5 | $TB = MB + V$ | True bearing = Magnetic bearing + Variation |
| 6 | $V = MH - TH$ | Variation (East positive, West negative) |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength of NDB signal |
| 8 | $f_{\text{IF}} = f_{\text{LO}} - f_{\text{RF}}$ | Superheterodyne intermediate frequency |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f}$ | Quality factor of receiver tuned circuits |
| 10 | $E = \dfrac{V}{d}$ (qualitative) | Field strength falls with distance (1/d for ground wave) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use** the vertical pipe `|` symbol for absolute value inside a KTU exam answer. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ to avoid markdown rendering issues.

## 2.5 The Cockpit Display — RMI

The **Radio Magnetic Indicator (RMI)** is the integrated display showing:
- **Outer Compass Card**: Rotates to display the aircraft's magnetic heading under the lubber line.
- **Single/Double Needle**: 
  - **Single needle (thin)** → ADF #1 (or VOR #1 in some installations).
  - **Double needle (thick)** → ADF #2 (or VOR #2).
- The **head of the needle** indicates the **magnetic bearing to the NDB**, while the **tail of the needle** shows the **reciprocal magnetic bearing** (the bearing from the NDB to the aircraft).

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Air Traffic Control (ATC)**: Allows controllers to vector aircraft using NDB bearings.
- **Non-Precision Approach (NPA)**: Used for final approach to runways not equipped with ILS.
- **Holding Patterns**: Aircraft hold over NDBs at specific radial/bearing intersections.
- **Over-water/Polar routes**: Backup navigation when GNSS/INS is denied.
- **Search and Rescue (SAR)**: ELT (Emergency Locator Transmitter) signals are tracked via airborne ADF.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Loop Antenna EMF and the Figure-8 Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal**: Show rigorously that the EMF induced in a small loop antenna is proportional to $\cos(\theta)$, producing the bidirectional figure-8 pattern.

**Setup**: Consider a single-turn rectangular loop of dimensions $a \times b$, oriented in the $x$-$y$ plane, with the **direction of arrival of the radio wave** lying in the $x$-$y$ plane at an azimuth angle $\theta$ from the loop's $x$-axis.

Let the incoming plane wave have:
- Electric field vector: $\vec{E} = E_0 \sin(\omega t - \beta z) \hat{z}$ (vertically polarized, propagating along $+z$).
- The magnetic field vector (from $\vec{H} = \dfrac{1}{\eta}\hat{k} \times \vec{E}$): $\vec{H} = \dfrac{E_0}{\eta}\sin(\omega t - \beta z)(-\hat{y})\cos(\theta) + \dfrac{E_0}{\eta}\sin(\omega t - \beta z)\hat{x}\sin(\theta)$

This is messy — let us use a **simpler, equivalent approach** using the **Faraday Induction Principle**.

### Method: Vector Potential Approach

The **magnetic flux density** of the wave can be expressed via the magnetic field strength $H_0$. For a vertical loop in a horizontally travelling vertical-polarization wave, the relevant magnetic field is the **horizontal component perpendicular to the direction of propagation**.

The **flux linking the loop** is given by integrating $\vec{B} \cdot d\vec{A}$ over the loop area. For a small loop (loop dimensions $\ll \lambda$), the EMF induced is:

$$\text{EMF} = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\int \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{A}$$

For a small loop of area $A = a \times b$, the effective flux is $B \cdot A \cdot \cos(\theta)$ where $\theta$ is the angle between the loop's normal vector and the direction of the magnetic field of the wave.

$$\Phi(t) = B_0 \sin(\omega t) \cdot A \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

Differentiating:

$$\text{EMF} = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -\omega A B_0 \cos(\omega t)\cos(\theta)$$

The peak EMF is:

$$E_{\text{loop, peak}} = \omega A B_0 \cos(\theta) = k\cos(\theta)$$

where $k = \omega A B_0$ is a constant for a given wave frequency and loop geometry.

\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{loop, peak}} &= k\cos(\theta) \\
\text{At } \theta = 0° &: E_{\text{loop}} = +k \quad (\text{Maximum}) \\
\text{At } \theta = 90° &: E_{\text{loop}} = 0 \quad (\text{Null}) \\
\text{At } \theta = 180° &: E_{\text{loop}} = -k \quad (\text{Maximum, opposite phase}) \\
\text{At } \theta = 270° &: E_{\text{loop}} = 0 \quad (\text{Null})
\end{aligned}

The sign reversal at $\theta = 180°$ represents a **180° phase shift**, which is the basis of the ambiguity. ✓ **Figure-8 pattern derived.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combining Loop and Sense — The Cardioid

The sense antenna produces a **non-directional** EMF of constant magnitude $A$ (in phase with the wave):

$$E_{\text{sense}} = A \cdot (\text{sign adjusted to match the } \theta = 0° \text{ phase of the loop})$$

Let the sense antenna output be:

$$E_{\text{sense}} = A \quad \text{(independent of } \theta \text{)}$$

We deliberately **phase-align the sense signal** to match the **positive lobe** of the loop (i.e., the lobe where the NDB is located when the loop output is positive).

Total field strength (proportional to receiver input voltage):

$$E_{\text{total}}(\theta) = E_{\text{loop}}(\theta) + E_{\text{sense}}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{\text{total}}(\theta) = k\cos(\theta) + k = k[\cos(\theta) + 1]$$

Letting $k = 1$ for normalization:

$$E_{\text{total}}(\theta) = \cos(\theta) + 1$$

This is the **cardioid equation in polar form**. The polar plot $r = \cos(\theta) + 1$:

\begin{aligned}
\text{At } \theta = 0° &: r = \cos(0°) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \quad (\text{Maximum, points to NDB}) \\
\text{At } \theta = 90° &: r = \cos(90°) + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1 \quad (\text{Intermediate}) \\
\text{At } \theta = 180° &: r = \cos(180°) + 1 = -1 + 1 = 0 \quad (\text{Single null}) \\
\text{At } \theta = 270° &: r = \cos(270°) + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1 \quad (\text{Intermediate})
\end{aligned}

**Single null at $\theta = 180°$** — the 180° ambiguity is **resolved**. The single maximum at $\theta = 0°$ unambiguously indicates the bearing to the NDB.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the cardioid points the "right" way**: In a real ADF, the receiver's servo system physically (or electronically via the goniometer) rotates the loop such that the **null of the cardioid points away from the station** and the **maximum points toward the station**. The RMI needle is mechanically or electronically coupled to this null.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bearing Conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup**: An aircraft is flying with a magnetic heading of $MH = 080°$. The ADF indicates a relative bearing of $RB = 135°$ to the NDB. The local variation is $V = 5°\text{W}$. Find the **magnetic bearing** and **true bearing** of the NDB from the aircraft.

**Step 1**: Compute the Magnetic Bearing (MB).

\begin{aligned}
MB &= RB + MH \\
MB &= 135° + 080° \\
MB &= 215°
\end{aligned}

**Step 2**: Compute the True Bearing (TB).

\begin{aligned}
TB &= MB + V_{\text{east}} \quad \text{or} \quad TB = MB - V_{\text{west}} \\
\text{Since variation is West, we subtract:} \quad TB &= MB - V \\
TB &= 215° - 5° \\
TB &= 210°
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 (Cross-check using true heading)**:

\begin{aligned}
TH &= MH - V_{\text{west}} = 080° - 5° = 075° \\
TB &= RB + TH = 135° + 075° = 210° \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **The two methods must agree.** KTU examiners often give variation with the sign convention; always state: *"Variation East, Magnetic Best (MB is greater than TB); Variation West, Magnetic Worst (MB is less than TB)"* — the famous **Cadet mnemonic "East is least, West is best"** when comparing MB to TB is the inverse of the heading case — be careful.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Error Sources in ADF

| # | Error Type | Cause | Mitigation |
|---|------------|-------|------------|
| 1 | **Night Effect (Skywave Interference)** | LF/MF waves reflect off the ionosphere at night, creating interference between ground and sky waves, causing bearing fluctuations. | Use NDBs with restricted power; fly at higher altitudes; use during day. |
| 2 | **Mountain/Terrain Effect (Quadrantal Error)** | Re-radiation of NDB signal from mountain slopes, causing bearing errors of up to ±90° in some quadrants. | Fly at altitudes providing adequate terrain clearance; avoid mountainous NDBs at low altitude. |
| 3 | **Coastal Refraction** | Signal travels faster over sea than land, causing the wavefront to bend near coastlines, shifting the apparent bearing. | Use NDBs located inland; cross coastlines at oblique angles. |
| 4 | **Station Interference** | Multiple NDBs on similar frequencies cause overlapping signals. | Use selective receivers; check NOTAM for frequency changes. |
| 5 | **Bank Effect** | Aircraft banking causes the loop antenna plane to tilt, introducing a bearing error proportional to bank angle. | Limit bank angle below 10° during ADF tracking. |
| 6 | **Atmospheric Interference (Static)** | Lightning, electrical storms create noise bursts in LF/MF. | Use ADF in clear weather; switch to INS/GPS in storms. |
| 7 | **Loop Alignment Error** | Improper mechanical alignment of the loop antenna with the aircraft's longitudinal axis. | Periodic calibration checks. |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ADF Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[NDB Ground Station\nLF/MF 190-1750 kHz\nVertical Monopole Antenna] -->|Vertically Polarized\nGround Wave + Skywave| A2[Aircraft Antenna Assembly]
    A2 --> A3[Loop Antenna\nDirectional Figure-8\nPattern]
    A2 --> A4[Sense Antenna\nOmnidirectional\nWhip or Loop]
    A3 --> A5[Goniometer\nElectronic Combiner\nResolves 180 degree Ambiguity]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[RF Amplifier\nTuned LF/MF Stage]
    A6 --> A7[Mixer and Local Oscillator\nSuperheterodyne Conversion]
    A7 --> A8[IF Amplifier\n1020 Hz ID Tone Pass]
    A8 --> A9[Detector and AGC\nAudio Output]
    A9 --> A10[Servo Amplifier\nDrives Loop to Null]
    A9 --> A11[Audio Amplifier\nPilot ID Headphones]
    A10 --> A5
    A5 --> A12[Synchro Output to RMI]
    A12 --> A13[RMI Cockpit Display\nMagnetic Compass Card\nBearing Needle]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Polar Pattern Diagram — Conceptual

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Loop Antenna Pattern] -->|Figure 8| B2[Two Maxima 180 degree Apart]
    B1 -->|180 degree Ambiguity| B3[Cannot Tell Front From Back]
    B4[Sense Antenna Pattern] -->|Perfect Circle| B5[Omnidirectional Reference]
    B2 --> B6[Add Sense Signal with\nCorrect Phase]
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7[Cardioid Pattern\nSingle Maximum\nSingle Null]
    B7 --> B8[180 degree Ambiguity Resolved]
    B7 --> B9[Servo Drives Loop to Null Position]
    B9 --> B10[RMI Shows Magnetic Bearing to NDB]
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow Matrix (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

| Stage | Block | Function | Typical Component |
|-------|-------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | NDB | Transmit omnidirectional LF/MF signal | T-antenna, 50W–25kW TX |
| 2 | Loop Antenna | Receive directional signal | Rotating/shaded-loop |
| 3 | Sense Antenna | Receive omnidirectional reference | Fixed whip or loop |
| 4 | Goniometer | Combine signals to resolve ambiguity | Static or rotary transformer |
| 5 | RF Amplifier | Amplify weak LF/MF signal | Tuned L-C amplifier |
| 6 | Mixer + LO | Convert to intermediate frequency (IF) | Superheterodyne stage, IF ≈ 455 kHz |
| 7 | IF Amplifier | Provide most of the gain and selectivity | Ceramic/IF filter |
| 8 | Detector + AGC | Demodulate A1A (telegraphy) signal | Envelope detector |
| 9 | Servo Amplifier | Drive loop to null position | DC amplifier, motor |
| 10 | RMI | Display magnetic bearing | Synchro-driven indicator |
