# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the term "Non-Directional Beacon (NDB)" and state the frequency band in which it operates.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
A **Non-Directional Beacon (NDB)** is a ground-based radio transmitter used as a navigation aid for aircraft, which radiates **equally in all azimuth directions** (i.e., non-directionally) in the **Low Frequency (LF) and Medium Frequency (MF)** bands. The frequency range is **190 kHz to 1750 kHz**. The NDB transmits a carrier wave modulated by a 1020 Hz identification tone. **[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for frequency range, 1 for modulation type]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q: What is the 180° ambiguity of a loop antenna, and how is it resolved in an ADF system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
A loop antenna alone has a **figure-8 (bidirectional) polar pattern** with two maxima 180° apart. This creates an ambiguity: the receiver cannot distinguish whether the incoming signal is arriving from $\theta = 0°$ (front) or $\theta = 180°$ (rear). This is called the **180° ambiguity** of the loop antenna. **[1 mark]**

In an ADF system, the **sense antenna** (an omnidirectional antenna) is added in **phase** with the loop signal. The combined output $E_{\text{total}} = k[\cos(\theta) + 1]$ is a **cardioid pattern** with a single maximum and a single null, which **resolves the 180° ambiguity**. **[2 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule**: Part B questions in ESE (End Semester Examination) carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**. You must answer **one full question** (either Q A or Q B), not both.

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an Automatic Direction Finder (ADF) system. Also explain how the 180° ambiguity of the loop antenna is resolved using the sense antenna.**

#### (a) **Block Diagram and Working Principle** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

An Automatic Direction Finder (ADF) determines the bearing of a ground-based **Non-Directional Beacon (NDB)** relative to the aircraft. The system consists of three main sections:

1. **Ground Station (NDB)**: An LF/MF transmitter (190 kHz – 1750 kHz) with a vertical monopole antenna, radiating **equally in all azimuth directions** (omnidirectional pattern). The transmitted signal is a carrier wave modulated by a 1020 Hz Morse-code identification tone. **[1 mark]**

2. **Airborne Antenna Assembly**: Consists of two antennas:
   - **Loop Antenna**: A directional antenna with a **figure-8 polar pattern**, with two maxima and two nulls 180° apart.
   - **Sense Antenna**: An omnidirectional antenna with a **circular polar pattern**.
   
   Both signals are fed to a **Goniometer**, an inductive coupler that combines them electronically. **[2 marks]**

3. **Receiver and Indicator**: A **superheterodyne receiver** with RF amplifier, mixer, IF amplifier (IF ≈ 455 kHz), detector, and AGC. The receiver's output drives:
   - A **servo motor** that rotates the goniometer (or virtual loop) until the **null of the cardioid pattern points away from the NDB**.
   - An **RMI (Radio Magnetic Indicator)** display, which shows the magnetic bearing of the NDB. **[2 marks]**

4. **Working Sequence**:
   - The NDB transmits a continuous omnidirectional LF/MF signal.
   - The loop and sense antennas on the aircraft receive this signal.
   - The goniometer combines them into a cardioid pattern.
   - The servo system orients the cardioid such that the **single null points to 180° (away from the station)**, ensuring the **maximum points toward the NDB**.
   - The synchro output drives the RMI needle, which displays the magnetic bearing. **[2 marks]**

#### (b) **180° Ambiguity Resolution Using Sense Antenna** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

The loop antenna alone has a polar pattern $E_{\text{loop}} = k\cos(\theta)$, which is a **figure-8 pattern** with:
- **Maxima** at $\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 180°$ (equal in magnitude, opposite in phase).
- **Nulls** at $\theta = 90°$ and $\theta = 270°$.

This pattern has two equally strong maxima 180° apart. The receiver cannot determine from the loop output alone whether the NDB is at $\theta = 0°$ (front of the aircraft) or $\theta = 180°$ (behind the aircraft). This is the **180° ambiguity** of the loop antenna. **[2 marks]**

The **sense antenna** is a small omnidirectional antenna that produces a **non-directional** signal of constant amplitude:
$$E_{\text{sense}} = A \quad (\text{constant, in phase with the } \theta = 0° \text{ lobe of the loop})$$

The goniometer **adds** the loop and sense signals in the correct phase:
$$E_{\text{total}}(\theta) = E_{\text{loop}}(\theta) + E_{\text{sense}} = k\cos(\theta) + k = k[\cos(\theta) + 1]$$

This is the equation of a **cardioid** in polar coordinates. The cardioid has:
- A **single maximum** at $\theta = 0°$, $E_{\text{total}} = 2k$.
- A **single null** at $\theta = 180°$, $E_{\text{total}} = 0$.
- Intermediate values elsewhere. **[2 marks]**

Since the cardioid has only **one maximum** and **one null**, the receiver can unambiguously determine that the NDB is in the direction of the maximum (i.e., $\theta = 0°$). The 180° ambiguity is **resolved**. **[1 mark]**

The receiver's servo system then rotates the goniometer (or the virtual loop) so that the **null of the cardioid is at 180° relative to the loop's normal**, ensuring the loop's $\theta = 0°$ axis points toward the NDB. The angular position of the loop's reference axis gives the **relative bearing** of the NDB. **[2 marks]**

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: An aircraft is flying on a magnetic heading of $MH = 240°$. The ADF shows a relative bearing of $RB = 315°$ to an NDB. The local variation is $8°\text{E}$. Find the magnetic bearing (MB) and the true bearing (TB) of the NDB from the aircraft. Also explain the various errors encountered in ADF systems.**

#### (a) **Bearing Calculation** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

**Given Data**:
- Magnetic Heading: $MH = 240°$
- Relative Bearing: $RB = 315°$
- Variation: $V = 8°\text{E}$

**Step 1: Compute the Magnetic Bearing (MB) of the NDB**.

The magnetic bearing is the angle, measured clockwise from magnetic north, to the NDB. From the geometry of the headings:

$$MB = RB + MH$$

> **Note**: When $RB + MH > 360°$, subtract 360°.

\begin{aligned}
MB &= RB + MH \\
MB &= 315° + 240° \\
MB &= 555° \\
MB &= 555° - 360° \\
MB &= 195°
\end{aligned}

**The magnetic bearing of the NDB is $MB = 195°$**. **[3 marks]**

**Step 2: Compute the True Bearing (TB) of the NDB**.

The true bearing is measured from true (geographic) north. The relationship between true and magnetic bearings is:
$$TB = MB + V_{\text{east}} \quad \text{or} \quad TB = MB - V_{\text{west}}$$

Since variation is **East**, we add:

\begin{aligned}
TB &= MB + V_{\text{east}} \\
TB &= 195° + 8° \\
TB &= 203°
\end{aligned}

**The true bearing of the NDB is $TB = 203°$**. **[2 marks]**

**Step 3 (Cross-check via True Heading)**.

\begin{aligned}
TH &= MH + V_{\text{east}} = 240° + 8° = 248° \\
TB &= RB + TH = 315° + 248° = 563° - 360° = 203° \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}

**[2 marks — for cross-verification]**

---

#### (b) **Errors in ADF Systems** [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

ADF systems are subject to several errors, the major ones being:

1. **Night Effect (Skywave Interference)** [1.5 marks]: At night, the ionosphere's D-layer dissipates, allowing LF/MF skywaves to reflect from higher layers and return to Earth. These skywaves interfere with the ground wave at the receiver, causing the bearing to fluctuate unpredictably — sometimes by 90° or more. Night effect is most severe at dawn/dusk and within 30–60 nm of the NDB.

2. **Mountain/Terrain Effect (Quadrantal Error)** [1.5 marks]: Mountainous terrain near the aircraft or NDB can re-radiate the signal due to induced currents in conductive rocks/soil. This re-radiation distorts the wavefront, causing bearing errors of up to ±90° in some quadrants. The error is most pronounced in the quadrants facing the mountain.

3. **Coastal Refraction** [1 mark]: When an NDB signal crosses a coastline, it refracts (bends) because the wave travels faster over seawater (conducting surface) than over land (less conducting). The bending causes the apparent bearing to shift. The error is most significant when the signal crosses the coast at an oblique angle.

4. **Bank Effect** [1 mark]: When the aircraft is banked, the loop antenna's plane tilts from horizontal, causing the figure-8 pattern's nulls to shift in azimuth. This introduces a bearing error proportional to the bank angle. Limiting bank angle to <10° minimizes this error.

5. **Station Interference** [1 mark]: When two NDBs operate on adjacent or harmonically related frequencies, their signals may overlap, causing erroneous bearings. Modern ADF receivers use narrow-band filters to mitigate this.

6. **Atmospheric Noise (Static)** [1 mark]: Lightning and electrical storms generate strong LF/MF noise, which can mask the NDB signal or create false bearings. Switching to INS/GPS is recommended in heavy storm activity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Sign of Variation**: Many students get confused about the East/West sign convention. **Mnemonic: "East is least (less)" for True vs Magnetic — TB < MB if East variation; conversely, TH < MH if East.** Drawing the north arrows on the page resolves all ambiguity.
> 2. **360° Wraparound**: If $RB + MH \geq 360°$, students forget to subtract 360°. Always reduce bearing to $[0°, 360°]$.
> 3. **Forgetting to Modulate $360°$ in TB**: When $TB \geq 360°$, again subtract 360°.
> 4. **Confusing Relative Bearing with Magnetic Bearing**: RB is from the nose; MB is from magnetic north. The RMI **directly shows MB**, not RB.
> 5. **Loop Antenna Diagram**: If asked to draw the figure-8 pattern, students often draw it as a perfect 8 without showing the **180° phase reversal** between the two lobes. Always show the + and − signs on the two maxima.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know for KTU ESE on ADF**:

- **ADF = Radio Compass** that points to a ground-based **NDB** instead of magnetic north.
- **NDB frequency band**: 190 kHz – 1750 kHz (LF/MF), channel spacing **500 Hz**, modulated by **1020 Hz** Morse ID tone.
- **Two antennas**: Loop (directional, figure-8) + Sense (omnidirectional, circular) → combined as **cardioid**.
- **Loop EMF equation**: $E_{\text{loop}} = k\cos(\theta)$ → **two maxima, two nulls, 180° ambiguity**.
- **Combined EMF equation**: $E_{\text{total}} = k[\cos(\theta) + 1]$ → cardioid, **single null at 180°, single max at 0°**.
- **Bearing conversions**:
  - $MB = RB + MH$
  - $TB = MB + V_{\text{east}}$ (or $TB = MB - V_{\text{west}}$)
  - $TB = RB + TH$
  - **Always reduce to $[0°, 360°]$**.
- **RMI**: Shows **Magnetic Bearing** of NDB on the needle head; **reciprocal MB** on the tail; aircraft's MH on the outer compass card.
- **Superheterodyne receiver**: RF amp → Mixer → IF (455 kHz) → Detector → AGC → Servo + Audio.
- **Errors**: Night effect, Terrain/Quadrantal, Coastal refraction, Bank effect, Station interference, Atmospheric noise.
- **Cardioid Direction**: The **null** of the cardioid points **away from the NDB** (at $\theta = 180°$), and the **maximum points toward the NDB** (at $\theta = 0°$).
- **Mnemonic for Variation**: *"East is least, West is best"* — meaning when comparing MB vs TB, the one that is *least* (smaller) corresponds to *East* variation.
- **Use of ADF**: En-route navigation, non-precision approach, holding pattern entry, oceanic/remote area navigation, SAR (ELT homing).
- **Polarization**: NDB signals are **vertically polarized**; this is why a **vertical whip (sense antenna)** works for omnidirectional reception.
- **KTU Hot Topics**: 180° ambiguity resolution (most-asked), bearing arithmetic (every year), error types (high weightage).

> **End of Module 4 — Automatic Direction Finder Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PCAOT205 — Fundamentals of Aeronautics and Aircraft Instruments*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrument Landing System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Instrument Landing System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Instrument Landing System (ILS)** is a ground-based, precision radio navigation aid that provides highly accurate **lateral (azimuthal)**, **vertical (glide path)**, and **range (distance) guidance** to aircraft during the **final approach phase** to a runway, particularly under low-visibility meteorological conditions (Instrument Meteorological Conditions – IMC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (ICAO Annex 10 Compliant):** The ILS is a precision approach and landing navigation system which provides, by means of two distinct radio transmissions, accurate azimuth and elevation guidance to aircraft on approach. It operates in the **VHF band (108–111.95 MHz)** for the localizer and the **UHF band (329.15–335.00 MHz)** for the glide path, with marker beacons operating at **75 MHz**.

The system is classified by the **International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO)** into operational categories based on the **Decision Height (DH)** and **Runway Visual Range (RVR)** required for safe landing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ILS is still relevant in 2024:** Despite the advent of satellite-based augmentation systems (GBAS/SBAS), ILS remains the **primary precision landing aid** at the vast majority of international airports worldwide, including all major Kerala airports (COK, TRV, CCJ).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Highway on a Rainy Night"

Imagine driving a car toward a tunnel entrance on a foggy night. To keep you safe, the highway engineers have installed:

- **Two glowing white edge lines** (these guide you **left/right** → the **Localizer**)
- **A red horizontal line on the windshield** showing the correct vertical descent slope (this controls **up/down** → the **Glide Slope**)
- **Three rumble strips across the road** that beep as you cross them (these tell you **how far you are** from the tunnel mouth → the **Marker Beacons**)

The ILS works exactly like this invisible "tunnel" drawn in the sky. It produces two intersecting electromagnetic beams — one horizontal (localizer) and one inclined upward at a nominal **3° angle** (glide slope) — that meet at a precise point above the runway threshold. The aircraft's cockpit instruments (the **Course Deviation Indicator (CDI)** and **Horizontal Situation Indicator (HSI)**) keep the pilot centered within this invisible corridor, like a car following lane markings.

## 1.3 The Three Core Subsystems of ILS

The ILS architecture consists of three functionally independent but operationally integrated ground subsystems:

| Subsystem | Function | Frequency Band | Aircraft Indication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Localizer (LLZ)** | Lateral (azimuth) guidance | VHF 108.10 – 111.95 MHz | Horizontal needle of CDI/HSI |
| **Glide Slope (GS / GP)** | Vertical (elevation) guidance | UHF 329.15 – 335.00 MHz | Vertical needle of CDI/HSI |
| **Marker Beacons** | Range / distance to threshold | VHF 75 MHz | Light + audio tone in cockpit |

Additionally, the **Approach Lighting System (ALS)** is considered a complementary visual aid, although it is not strictly part of the radio ILS.

## 1.4 ICAO Operational Categories of ILS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** ILS categories define the lowest decision height a pilot may descend to before requiring visual reference to the runway.

| Category | Decision Height (DH) | Runway Visual Range (RVR) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CAT I** | $\geq$ **200 ft** (60 m) | $\geq$ **550 m** (1800 ft) | Standard precision approach |
| **CAT II** | **100 ft – 200 ft** (30–60 m) | $\geq$ **300 m** (1000 ft) | Low-visibility operations |
| **CAT IIIA** | $< 100$ ft (30 m) | $\geq$ **200 m** (700 ft) | Low-visibility operations |
| **CAT IIIB** | $< 50$ ft (15 m) | $\geq$ **75 m** (250 ft) | Near-zero visibility |
| **CAT IIIC** | No DH (zero) | No RVR limit | "Blind landing" (theoretical) |

## 1.5 Standard Performance Values (ICAO Annex 10)

These are the engineering constants that examiners love to test:

- **Localizer Course Width:** **700 ft** (210 m) at the runway threshold (defined as 4° wide angularly at the threshold for CAT I/II).
- **Glide Path Angle:** Nominal **3°** above the horizontal (configurable typically 2.5° to 3.5°).
- **Glide Path Beam Width:** Approximately **1.4°** (0.7° on either side of the centerline).
- **Modulation Frequencies:** **90 Hz** (left/lower) and **150 Hz** (right/upper) — used for DDM encoding.
- **Modulation Depth (each tone):** **20%** nominal (range 17.5% to 22.5%).
- **DDM at Course Line:** **0** (balanced tones).
- **Outer Marker Distance:** **3.5 NM to 6 NM** from threshold.
- **Middle Marker Distance:** **0.5 NM to 0.8 NM** from threshold.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Localizer (LLZ) — Lateral Guidance Subsystem

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

The localizer operates in the **VHF band (108.10 – 111.95 MHz)**, with channels separated by **50 kHz** (and sometimes 100 kHz). It radiates two highly directional antenna patterns that overlap to form a **course line** aligned with the extended runway centerline.

The two patterns are amplitude-modulated with two distinct audio tones:
- **90 Hz tone** → radiated to the **LEFT** of the runway centerline
- **150 Hz tone** → radiated to the **RIGHT** of the runway centerline

When the aircraft is exactly on the course line, both tones are received with **equal modulation depth (40% total, 20% each)**, and the airborne receiver's Course Deviation Indicator (CDI) shows the **horizontal needle centered**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Capture Effect" Phenomenon:** VHF signals exhibit the *capture effect*, meaning the stronger of the two overlapping patterns dominates. This produces a sharp, unambiguous course line with no false indications in the overlap region — a critical safety feature of ILS.

### 2.1.2 The Carrier-plus-Sideband (CSB) and Sideband-Only (SBO) Method

The localizer antenna array uses two simultaneous signals:
1. **CSB (Carrier + Sidebands):** A full carrier modulated by both 90 Hz and 150 Hz audio, radiated by a central antenna.
2. **SBO (Sidebands Only):** Suppressed-carrier signals containing only the 90 Hz or 150 Hz sidebands, radiated by pairs of antennas offset symmetrically from the runway centerline.

The **phasing** of the SBO pairs with respect to the CSB creates the directional patterns. This method is preferred because it gives a **steep, linear course structure** free from scalloping.

### 2.1.3 Course Sensitivity and Displacement Sensitivity

A critical operational parameter is the **Displacement Sensitivity (DS)**, which is the lateral deflection of the aircraft per unit of DDM. For a typical course width of 700 ft at threshold:

$$
DS = \frac{350 \text{ ft}}{0.155 \text{ DDM (full-scale)}} \approx 2258 \text{ ft per unit DDM}
$$

The **angular course width** of 4° corresponds to a full-scale DDM of **0.155**.

## 2.2 The Glide Slope (GS) — Vertical Guidance Subsystem

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

The glide slope operates in the **UHF band (329.15 – 335.00 MHz)**, with a paired channel relationship to the localizer (the localizer and GS frequencies of an ILS are **paired** in ICAO Annex 10). It is normally sited **750 ft to 1250 ft** from the runway threshold, offset **400 ft to 600 ft** from the runway centerline.

The GS antenna array is mounted on a **2-element M-array** (or, in modern systems, an end-fire waveguide or a **dual-frequency null-reference** system). The two patterns are modulated with:
- **150 Hz tone** → radiated to the **UPPER** (above the glide path) side
- **90 Hz tone** → radiated to the **LOWER** (below the glide path) side

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference from Localizer:** In the localizer, 90 Hz is LEFT; in the glide slope, 90 Hz is LOWER. This is a common KTU exam trap. The 90 Hz tone **always** points to the side of the runway (left/localizer and below/glide slope).

### 2.2.2 The Image Glide Slope Problem

The GS antenna radiates energy both **directly** and **via ground reflection**. This creates a second, unwanted "image" glide path at a steeper angle. The **null-reference** antenna system was developed specifically to suppress this image by placing a second antenna above the main one, whose pattern has a **null** at the false glide path angle.

## 2.3 The Marker Beacons — Range Guidance Subsystem

Marker beacons are simple 75 MHz transmitters placed at fixed distances from the runway threshold, which illuminate a cockpit indicator and emit an audio tone when the aircraft passes overhead.

| Beacon | Location (from threshold) | Audio Tone | Cockpit Light | Information Conveyed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Outer Marker (OM)** | **3.5 NM to 6 NM** | 400 Hz (low-pitched dash) | **Blue** | Final approach fix; FAF |
| **Middle Marker (MM)** | **0.5 NM to 0.8 NM** | 1300 Hz (alternating dots/dashes) | **Amber** | Approximately 200 ft AGL (CAT I DH) |
| **Inner Marker (IM)** | **0.15 NM** (CAT II/III only) | 3000 Hz (high-pitched dots) | **White** | Beginning of runway (for low-vis auto-coupler) |

The marker beacon fan-shaped radiation pattern is highly directive **upward** (vertical beam) and narrow **along the approach course** (elliptical footprint).

> [!WARNING]
> **Marker Beacons → DME Substitution:** Modern ILS installations are progressively **replacing marker beacons with DME** (Distance Measuring Equipment) for range information, because DME provides continuous distance readout rather than discrete fixes. KTU may ask why this is happening.

## 2.4 Difference in Depth of Modulation (DDM) — The Heart of ILS

The **DDM** is the *single most important* concept in ILS. It is the differential modulation between the 90 Hz and 150 Hz tones as measured by the airborne receiver:

$$
DDM = \frac{M_{90} - M_{150}}{M_{90} + M_{150}}
$$

Where:
- $M_{90}$ = Modulation depth of the 90 Hz tone
- $M_{150}$ = Modulation depth of the 150 Hz tone
- Both are expressed as decimal fractions (e.g., 0.20 for 20%).

### 2.4.1 Physical Meaning of DDM

- **DDM = 0** → Aircraft is **on course** (modulation depths are equal).
- **DDM > 0** → 90 Hz tone dominates → aircraft is to the **left of course / below glide path**.
- **DDM < 0** → 150 Hz tone dominates → aircraft is to the **right of course / above glide path**.

The aircraft's deviation indicator (CDI/HSI) needle deflection is **directly proportional to DDM**.

### 2.4.2 Sum of Depth of Modulation (SDM)

The **SDM** represents the total modulation energy and is used by the receiver to validate that the signal is strong enough for accurate course tracking:

$$
SDM = M_{90} + M_{150}
$$

A minimum SDM of **30%** (each tone 15%) is required by ICAO for the receiver to provide valid indications. If SDM falls below this, the receiver must flag the indication as **unreliable** (the famous "NAV FLAG").

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **essential cheat sheet** for any KTU numerical on ILS. Memorize the constants — they appear in nearly every problem.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Difference in Depth of Modulation | $DDM = \dfrac{M_{90}-M_{150}}{M_{90}+M_{150}}$ | DDM | dimensionless |
| 2 | Sum of Depth of Modulation | $SDM = M_{90}+M_{150}$ | SDM | dimensionless |
| 3 | Localizer Course Width at Threshold | **700 ft** (210 m) | — | ft / m |
| 4 | Localizer Angular Course Width | **4°** nominal | — | degrees |
| 5 | Full-Scale DDM (Localizer) | **0.155** | DDM$_{fs}$ | — |
| 6 | Glide Path Angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h_{thr}}{D_{ant}}\right)$ | $\theta$ | degrees |
| 7 | Standard Glide Path Angle | **3°** | — | degrees |
| 8 | Glide Path Beam Width | **1.4°** (0.7° half-width) | — | degrees |
| 9 | Height at OM (nominal) | $\approx 1500$ ft AGL | $h_{OM}$ | ft |
| 10 | Height at MM (nominal) | $\approx 200$ ft AGL | $h_{MM}$ | ft |
| 11 | Marker Beacon Frequency | **75 MHz** | $f$ | MHz |
| 12 | Localizer Frequency Band | **108.10 – 111.95 MHz** | $f$ | MHz |
| 13 | Glide Slope Frequency Band | **329.15 – 335.00 MHz** | $f$ | MHz |
| 14 | DDM-to-Displacement (LLZ) | $d = \dfrac{350 \text{ ft}}{0.155} \cdot \vert DDM \vert$ | $d$ | ft |
| 15 | Displacement Sensitivity (LLZ) | **350 ft / 0.155 DDM** | $DS$ | ft/DDM |
| 16 | Lateral Displacement from Course | $\Delta x = DS \cdot \vert DDM \vert$ | $\Delta x$ | ft |

> [!WARNING]
> **No Pipe Symbols in Tables:** In the rendered LaTeX above, vertical bar notation `$\vert x \vert$` is used in place of `$\mid x \mid$` or ASCII pipes to keep the markdown table parser intact. Students should write `$\lvert x \rvert$` in their KTU answer scripts.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of the ILS

The ILS concept is a masterclass in **fail-safe redundancy through geometric and frequency diversity**:

- **Geometric Diversity:** The localizer and glide path use *physically independent* ground antennas and the marker beacons use a *third independent* system — any single failure does not propagate.
- **Frequency Diversity:** Three separate frequency bands (VHF localizer, UHF glide path, VHF marker) are used so co-channel interference is impossible.
- **Modulation Diversity:** Two audio tones (90 Hz, 150 Hz) and a DDM/SDM encoding provide immunity to signal-level fluctuations.

This is why the ILS is the **international standard** for Category I, II, and III precision approaches, mandated by ICAO as a critical safety-of-life radio navigation service. The same DDM/SDM encoding is reused in the **VHF Omnidirectional Range (VOR)** system, making the concept a foundational pillar of aviation electronics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DDM from Modulation Depths

A KTU standard 3-mark or 7-mark question: *"Given the modulation depths of the 90 Hz and 150 Hz tones as received by an ILS airborne receiver, calculate the DDM and interpret the aircraft's lateral position."*

### Worked Example Problem

The airborne ILS receiver measures the following modulation depths while an aircraft is on a localizer approach:
- $M_{90} = 0.22$ (22%)
- $M_{150} = 0.18$ (18%)

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

From the KTU formula sheet:

$$
DDM = \frac{M_{90} - M_{150}}{M_{90} + M_{150}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
DDM = \frac{0.22 - 0.18}{0.22 + 0.18}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator and denominator.**

$$
DDM = \frac{0.04}{0.40}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide to obtain the dimensionless DDM.**

$$
DDM = 0.10
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to the standard percentage form used in cockpit instrumentation (often expressed in "dots" of CDI deflection).**

In percentage form:

$$
DDM_{\%} = 0.10 \times 100\% = 10\%
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

Since $DDM > 0$, the 90 Hz tone dominates. Per ICAO convention, this means the aircraft is to the **LEFT of the localizer course line**.

**Step 7 — Compute the lateral displacement from the course line (optional, KTU-favored).**

Using the displacement sensitivity from the formula sheet:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{350 \text{ ft}}{0.155} \cdot \vert DDM \vert
$$

$$
\Delta x = \frac{350}{0.155} \times 0.10
$$

$$
\Delta x = 2258.06 \times 0.10 = 225.8 \text{ ft}
$$

Therefore, the aircraft is approximately **225.8 ft to the left of the localizer course line**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Glide Path Angle from Threshold Geometry

A standard 7-mark derivation: *"Compute the glide path angle for an aircraft crossing the outer marker at a known altitude and distance from the localizer antenna."*

### Problem Statement

The ILS glide path antenna is located **1000 ft** from the runway threshold along the extended centerline. An aircraft crosses the outer marker at a horizontal distance of **5.0 NM** from the threshold at an altitude of **1500 ft** AGL. Calculate the actual glide path angle being flown and the deviation from the standard 3° nominal path.

**Step 1 — Convert all distances to consistent units (feet).**

1 NM = 6076.115 ft, so:

$$
D_{OM} = 5.0 \times 6076.115 = 30380.6 \text{ ft}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the distance from the glide path antenna to the outer marker (along the ground).**

Since the antenna is 1000 ft inside the threshold (or equivalently, 1000 ft short of the threshold from the OM perspective):

$$
D_{ant-to-OM} = 30380.6 - 1000 = 29380.6 \text{ ft}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the glide path angle formula.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h_{OM}}{D_{ant-to-OM}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1500}{29380.6}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the argument of the arctangent.**

$$
\frac{1500}{29380.6} = 0.05106
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the arctangent.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.05106)
$$

$$
\theta = 2.924^{\circ}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the deviation from the nominal 3° path.**

$$
\Delta\theta = 3.000^{\circ} - 2.924^{\circ} = +0.076^{\circ}
$$

The aircraft is **below** the nominal 3° path by approximately **0.076°**, or about **4.7 minutes of arc** — this is within normal CAT I tolerances (the ILS glide path is required to be accurate to within $\pm 0.07 \times \theta_{GP}$ angularly).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Coverage Volume of the Localizer

The ICAO-defined localizer coverage volume is a **sector** in azimuth and elevation where the signal is usable for guidance. It is **±10°** from the course line in azimuth (up to ±35° outside this sector the signal must exist but is not usable), and **±7°** in elevation (up to the horizon).

### Derivation of the Coverage Volume

The localizer coverage volume is defined as the region of space within which the aircraft receiver is guaranteed to provide a usable course indication. ICAO Annex 10 defines this as:

**Step 1 — Define the azimuthal coverage.**

$$
\Delta_{az} = \pm 10^{\circ} \text{ (full coverage)}
$$

$$
\Delta_{az, \text{extended}} = \text{up to } \pm 35^{\circ} \text{ (signal present, not usable)}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the vertical (elevation) coverage.**

$$
\Delta_{el} = +7^{\circ} \text{ above the horizontal up to the horizon}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the range coverage.**

The localizer must provide guidance up to a distance of at least:

$$
D_{LLZ} = 25 \text{ NM within } \pm 10^{\circ} \text{ of the front course}
$$

$$
D_{LLZ, \text{back}} = 17 \text{ NM for the back course (back-beam)}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Glide Slope Coverage and Receiver Performance

**Step 1 — Define the glide slope coverage volume.**

The glide slope provides usable guidance within:

$$
\Delta_{az, GS} = \pm 8^{\circ} \text{ azimuth (centered on the course line)}
$$

$$
\Delta_{el, GS} = 0.45 \times \theta_{GP} \text{ to } 1.75 \times \theta_{GP} \text{ in elevation}
$$

For a 3° glide path, this corresponds to approximately:

$$
\Delta_{el, GS} = 0.45 \times 3^{\circ} = 1.35^{\circ} \text{ to } 1.75 \times 3^{\circ} = 5.25^{\circ}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the corresponding height range at the OM distance.**

The lower limit of the glide slope coverage at the OM (5 NM = 30380 ft) is:

$$
h_{lower} = D_{OM} \times \tan(1.35^{\circ})
$$

$$
h_{lower} = 30380 \times 0.02355 = 715.6 \text{ ft AGL}
$$

The upper limit:

$$
h_{upper} = 30380 \times \tan(5.25^{\circ}) = 30380 \times 0.09186 = 2791.0 \text{ ft AGL}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

The glide slope signal is guaranteed usable between approximately **716 ft and 2791 ft AGL** at the OM distance — a vertical range of about **2075 ft** — ensuring the aircraft never loses GS guidance during the precision approach.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing ILS Subsystem Data

The following matrix is the **master decision table** for KTU sub-parts:

| Question Asks... | Use This Formula | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Lateral position from DDM | $\Delta x = \dfrac{350}{0.155} \cdot \vert DDM \vert$ | LLZ displacement sensitivity |
| Glide path angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{D}\right)$ | Geometry |
| Whether on course | Compare $M_{90}$ vs $M_{150}$ | DDM sign |
| Audio tone in cockpit | 90 Hz = left/below, 150 Hz = right/above | ICAO convention |
| Beacon at ~200 ft AGL | **Middle Marker** | Standard ILS layout |
| Beacon at FAF (3.5–6 NM) | **Outer Marker** | Standard ILS layout |
| Frequency of marker | **75 MHz** | ICAO Annex 10 |
| Frequency of localizer | **108.10 – 111.95 MHz** | ICAO Annex 10 |
| Frequency of glide slope | **329.15 – 335.00 MHz** | ICAO Annex 10 |
| Course line DDM | **DDM = 0** | Definition |
| Required receiver minimum SDM | **SDM $\geq$ 0.30** | ICAO Annex 10 |

---

## 3.6 Sample 14-Mark Style Comprehensive Problem

**Problem:** An aircraft is on a precision ILS approach to runway 27. The airborne receiver indicates the following:
- 90 Hz modulation depth = **19.5%**
- 150 Hz modulation depth = **20.5%**
- Indicated altitude at the outer marker = **1800 ft AGL**
- Distance from the localizer antenna to the threshold = **1000 ft**

**Determine:**
- (a) The DDM and lateral position relative to the course line. (7 marks)
- (b) The actual glide path angle and deviation from the 3° nominal. (7 marks)

### Solution

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Express modulation depths in decimal form:
$M_{90} = 0.195$, $M_{150} = 0.205$.

Step 2 — Apply the DDM formula:
$$
DDM = \frac{0.195 - 0.205}{0.195 + 0.205} = \frac{-0.010}{0.400} = -0.025
$$

Step 3 — Convert to percentage:
$DDM = -2.5\%$.

Step 4 — Interpret sign: Negative DDM means 150 Hz dominates → aircraft is to the **RIGHT of the localizer course line**.

Step 5 — Compute the lateral displacement:
$$
\Delta x = \frac{350}{0.155} \times \vert -0.025 \vert
$$

$$
\Delta x = 2258.06 \times 0.025 = 56.45 \text{ ft}
$$

**Result:** The aircraft is **approximately 56.5 ft to the right of the localizer course**.

---

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Distance from outer marker to threshold (assume OM at 5 NM):
$D_{OM-to-thr} = 5 \times 6076.115 = 30380.6$ ft.

Step 2 — Distance from localizer antenna to outer marker (antenna is 1000 ft past threshold):
$D_{ant-to-OM} = 30380.6 + 1000 = 31380.6$ ft.

Step 3 — Apply the glide path angle formula:
$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1800}{31380.6}\right)
$$

Step 4 — Compute the argument:
$$
\frac{1800}{31380.6} = 0.05736
$$

Step 5 — Evaluate the arctangent:
$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.05736) = 3.284^{\circ}
$$

Step 6 — Compute deviation from 3°:
$\Delta\theta = 3.284^{\circ} - 3.000^{\circ} = +0.284^{\circ}$.

**Result:** The aircraft is **above** the nominal 3° glide path by approximately **0.284°**, or roughly **17 minutes of arc**. The pilot must reduce the rate of descent to intercept the proper glide path.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ILS Ground Station Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The diagram below maps the **complete functional architecture** of an ILS ground installation, showing all subsystems, signal paths, and aircraft-side integrations. This is the **highest-yield diagram** for KTU board questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[ILS Control Room<br/>Monitor and Status Unit] --> B1[Localizer TX<br/>108.10 to 111.95 MHz]
    A1 --> B2[Glide Slope TX<br/>329.15 to 335.00 MHz]
    A1 --> B3[Marker Beacon TX<br/>75 MHz]
    A1 --> B4[DME Interrogator<br/>Paired Channel 1025 to 1150 MHz]
    A1 --> A2[Remote Maintenance<br/>Monitoring System RMS]

    B1 --> C1[Localizer Antenna Array<br/>VHF Log Periodic Dipole 8 to 14 elements]
    C1 --> D1[Radiated Beams<br/>90 Hz LEFT, 150 Hz RIGHT]

    B2 --> C2[Glide Slope Antenna Mast<br/>M Array or Null Reference]
    C2 --> D2[Radiated Beams<br/>90 Hz LOWER, 150 Hz UPPER]

    B3 --> C3[Outer Marker Antenna<br/>at 3.5 to 6 NM from THR]
    B3 --> C4[Middle Marker Antenna<br/>at 0.5 to 0.8 NM from THR]
    B3 --> C5[Inner Marker Antenna<br/>at 0.15 NM from THR, CAT II or III]
    C3 --> D3[Vertical Fan Beam 75 MHz<br/>400 Hz Audio Blue Lamp]
    C4 --> D4[Vertical Fan Beam 75 MHz<br/>1300 Hz Audio Amber Lamp]
    C5 --> D5[Vertical Fan Beam 75 MHz<br/>3000 Hz Audio White Lamp]

    D1 --> E1[Aircraft ILS Receiver<br/>Dual Channel VHF plus UHF]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    B4 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[CDI Course Deviation Indicator<br/>LLZ Horizontal Needle]
    E1 --> F2[CDI Vertical Needle<br/>GS Vertical Guidance]
    E1 --> F3[Marker Lamp Panel<br/>Blue Amber White]
    E1 --> F4[DME Digital Distance Readout]

    F1 --> G1[Autopilot or Flight Director<br/>Coupled Approach]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Final Approach and Landing]
```

## 4.2 ILS Approach Profile and Beam Geometry (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPACE[AIRSPACE VIEW]
        direction TB
        AC[Aircraft on Approach] -- Localizer 90 Hz / 150 Hz --> LLZ[Localizer Antenna<br/>Past Stop End of Runway]
        AC -- Glide Slope Beam --> GS[Glide Slope Mast<br/>Offset 400 to 600 ft from CL]
        AC -- Vertical Fan Beam --> OM[Outer Marker<br/>3.5 to 6 NM<br/>1500 ft AGL]
        AC -- Vertical Fan Beam --> MM[Middle Marker<br/>0.5 to 0.8 NM<br/>200 ft AGL]
    end

    GS -- Forms 3 deg path --> THR[Runway Threshold]
    LLZ -- Aligned with --> THR
    OM -- Approach Fix --> THR
    MM -- CAT I DH Reference --> THR
```

## 4.3 Frequency Pairing Logic (Localizer ↔ Glide Slope)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Localizer VHF Channel<br/>108.10 to 111.95 MHz] -- ICAO Annex 10 Pairing --> B[Glide Slope UHF Channel<br/>329.15 to 335.00 MHz]
    B -- One-to-one mapping --> C[Aircraft Receiver<br/>Auto-Tunes Both Bands]
    C --> D[Single ILS Channel Identified<br/>e.g. I-CAL Localizer 110.30 MHz<br/>GS 335.00 MHz]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Visual Question Tip:** When asked to draw the ILS approach geometry, always show the **localizer antenna beyond the stop end of the runway** (typically 1000 ft past the threshold end), the **glide slope antenna to the side of the runway** (400–600 ft offset), and the **markers as vertical fan-shaped lobes** slicing through the approach corridor.

## 4.4 DDM Encoding Logic (Data Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[90 Hz Tone Generator<br/>20 percent Mod Depth] --> C[Summing and Phasing Network]
    B[150 Hz Tone Generator<br/>20 percent Mod Depth] --> C
    C --> D[CSB Signal<br/>Carrier plus both sidebands]
    C --> E[SBO Signal 90 Hz<br/>Left side lobes]
    C --> F[SBO Signal 150 Hz<br/>Right side lobes]
    D --> G[Antenna Array Radiation]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Aircraft Receiver<br/>Detects M90 and M150]
    H --> I[DDM Computation]
    I --> J[CDI Deflection<br/>Sign of DDM determines direction]
    I --> K[NAV Flag<br/>Valid only if SDM greater than 30 percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — Definition) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Instrument Landing System (ILS). List its three main subsystems and state the frequency band used by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Instrument Landing System (ILS)** is a precision radio navigation aid used to guide aircraft during the final approach and landing phase, providing accurate lateral, vertical, and range guidance to the runway threshold under low-visibility conditions (ICAO Annex 10).

Its three main subsystems are:
1. **Localizer (LLZ):** Provides lateral (azimuth) guidance. Operates in the **VHF band (108.10 – 111.95 MHz)**.
2. **Glide Slope (GS):** Provides vertical (elevation) guidance. Operates in the **UHF band (329.15 – 335.00 MHz)**.
3. **Marker Beacons:** Provide range (distance) information at fixed points. Operate at **75 MHz** (VHF).

> **Marking Note (1 mark each):** Definition, Localizer + frequency, Glide Slope + frequency, Marker Beacons + frequency.

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual — Comparison) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between the localizer and the glide slope subsystems of an ILS in terms of (i) frequency band, (ii) modulation tone-side convention, and (iii) physical location of the ground antenna.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Localizer (LLZ) | Glide Slope (GS) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Frequency band** | VHF, **108.10 – 111.95 MHz** | UHF, **329.15 – 335.00 MHz** |
| (ii) **90 Hz tone** | **LEFT** of course line | **LOWER** (below glide path) |
| (iii) **150 Hz tone** | **RIGHT** of course line | **UPPER** (above glide path) |
| (iv) **Antenna location** | Beyond stop end of runway, on extended centerline | Beside runway, 400–600 ft offset, 750–1250 ft from threshold |
| (v) **Beam shape** | Horizontal fan, narrow in elevation | Inclined upward at 3° above horizontal |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **7 + 7** sub-parts. The two alternatives below offer genuine depth-of-content choice, not just rephrasing.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 MARKS) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the operating principle of the localizer subsystem of an ILS. Describe the **Carrier-plus-Sideband (CSB) and Sideband-Only (SBO)** method of course generation, with a clear block diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** An aircraft on a localizer approach measures 90 Hz modulation depth = 17.5% and 150 Hz modulation depth = 22.5%. Calculate (i) the DDM, (ii) the lateral displacement from the course line, and (iii) the side of the course line on which the aircraft is positioned. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Localizer Operating Principle (7 marks):**

1. **Operating Frequency:** The localizer operates in the VHF band, **108.10 – 111.95 MHz**, with channel spacing of 50 kHz. Each ILS installation is assigned a paired localizer/glide slope channel per ICAO Annex 10. **(1 mark)**

2. **Course Generation Principle:** Two directional radiation patterns are produced, one modulated by a **90 Hz audio tone** and the other by a **150 Hz audio tone**. These patterns are aligned such that one points to the left of the runway centerline and the other to the right. The **course line** is the locus in space where the modulation depths of both tones are equal. **(1 mark)**

3. **CSB and SBO Method:** 
   - The **CSB signal** is the full carrier amplitude-modulated simultaneously by both 90 Hz and 150 Hz audio. It is radiated from a **central antenna** element located on the runway centerline. **(1 mark)**
   - The **SBO signals** are suppressed-carrier emissions containing only the 90 Hz or 150 Hz sideband pairs. They are radiated from **pairs of antennas** symmetrically offset from the centerline (left and right). **(1 mark)**
   - By carefully adjusting the relative **amplitude and phase** of CSB and SBO, the radiated far-field pattern produces a sharp, **unambiguous course structure** free from the "scalloping" that plagued earlier two-tone systems. The capture effect of VHF propagation ensures the stronger pattern dominates. **(1 mark)**

4. **Course Structure and Beam Width:** The standard course width is **700 ft at the runway threshold** (corresponding to an angular width of approximately **4°**). Full-scale needle deflection corresponds to a DDM of **0.155**. The course is linear, with displacement sensitivity of **350 ft per 0.155 DDM = 2258 ft per unit DDM**. **(1 mark)**

5. **Coverage Volume:** The localizer provides usable guidance from ±10° azimuth and +7° elevation up to 25 NM range on the front course, with a back-course service of 17 NM. **(1 mark)**

6. **Block Diagram:** (Refer to the **DDM Encoding Logic** Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4 of these notes.) The diagram shows: 90 Hz and 150 Hz tone generators → summing and phasing network → CSB and SBO antenna arrays → far-field pattern → aircraft receiver → DDM computation → CDI needle. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $M_{90} = 0.175$, $M_{150} = 0.225$.

**(i) DDM Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
DDM = \frac{M_{90} - M_{150}}{M_{90} + M_{150}} = \frac{0.175 - 0.225}{0.175 + 0.225} = \frac{-0.050}{0.400} = -0.125
$$

In percentage: **DDM = −12.5%**.

**(ii) Lateral Displacement (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta x = \frac{350 \text{ ft}}{0.155} \cdot \vert -0.125 \vert
$$

$$
\Delta x = 2258.06 \times 0.125 = 282.3 \text{ ft}
$$

**Lateral displacement = 282.3 ft from the course line.**

**(iii) Side of Course (1 mark):**

Since DDM is **negative**, the **150 Hz tone dominates**, meaning the aircraft is to the **RIGHT of the localizer course line** (per ICAO convention).

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 MARKS) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Set]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the operating principle of the **Glide Slope (GS)** subsystem of an ILS. Explain the problem of the **"image glide slope"** and how it is overcome by the **null-reference antenna system**. (7 marks)

**(b)** An aircraft crosses the outer marker at a horizontal distance of **4.5 NM from the threshold** and at an altitude of **1450 ft AGL**. The glide path antenna is located **1000 ft past the threshold** along the extended centerline. Calculate (i) the actual glide path angle and (ii) the deviation from the standard 3° nominal glide path, indicating whether the aircraft is above or below the path. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Glide Slope Operating Principle (7 marks):**

1. **Purpose and Frequency:** The glide slope provides vertical guidance to the aircraft during the final approach, ensuring it follows a stable descent profile. It operates in the UHF band, **329.15 – 335.00 MHz**, paired with the localizer frequency per ICAO Annex 10. **(1 mark)**

2. **Antenna Location:** The GS antenna mast is located **750 ft to 1250 ft** from the runway threshold along the extended centerline, offset **400 ft to 600 ft** to one side of the centerline (typically the side away from the localizer for isolation). **(1 mark)**

3. **Modulation and Beam Shape:** Two overlapping radiation patterns are produced:
   - **150 Hz tone** → radiated to the **UPPER** side (above glide path)
   - **90 Hz tone** → radiated to the **LOWER** side (below glide path)
   The standard glide path is a **3°** inclined beam with a beam width of approximately **1.4°** (0.7° on either side). **(1 mark)**

4. **The Image Glide Slope Problem:** Because the GS antenna is mounted close to the ground, the radiated energy travels to the aircraft by both a **direct path** and a **ground-reflected path**. The reflection creates a second, spurious "image" glide path at a steeper angle (typically 6°–9° above horizontal). Aircraft flying below the desired 3° path could erroneously intercept this image path. **(2 marks)**

5. **Null-Reference Solution:** A second, smaller antenna is mounted a few meters **above** the main GS antenna, phased to produce a **deep radiation null** at the angle of the image glide path. By carefully adjusting the height and excitation, the image path is suppressed by 30 dB or more, eliminating the false indication. The two-tone DDM encoding (90 Hz lower / 150 Hz upper) is preserved. **(2 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given:
- Distance from threshold to OM: $D_{OM} = 4.5 \text{ NM}$
- Altitude at OM: $h = 1450 \text{ ft AGL}$
- GS antenna location: 1000 ft past threshold (along extended CL)

**Step 1 — Convert distance to feet (2 marks):**

$$
D_{OM} = 4.5 \times 6076.115 = 27342.5 \text{ ft}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute distance from GS antenna to OM (2 marks):**

$$
D_{ant-to-OM} = 27342.5 + 1000 = 28342.5 \text{ ft}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute glide path angle (2 marks):**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1450}{28342.5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.05116) = 2.929^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute deviation from 3° nominal (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta\theta = 2.929^{\circ} - 3.000^{\circ} = -0.071^{\circ}
$$

**Conclusion:** The aircraft is **below** the nominal 3° glide path by approximately **0.071°** (about **4.3 minutes of arc**), corresponding to a vertical deviation of approximately **35 ft below** the standard glide path at the OM location. The pilot should **increase the rate of descent** slightly to intercept the proper glide path. **(Bonus, not for marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on ILS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Confusion in DDM (most common, ~2 marks loss):** Students forget that **negative DDM = 150 Hz dominant = RIGHT of course / ABOVE glide path**. Always re-derive the sign from first principles: $DDM = M_{90} - M_{150}$. If positive, 90 Hz is dominant.
>
> 2. **90/150 Hz Side Convention Reversal (~2 marks loss):** In the localizer, **90 Hz = LEFT**; in the glide slope, **90 Hz = LOWER**. Mixing these up costs full sub-part marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert NM to Feet (~1.5 marks loss):** All glide path angle calculations require consistent units. **1 NM = 6076.115 ft.** Mixing NM and feet is the single most frequent numerical error in KTU papers.
>
> 4. **Missing the "Distance from GS antenna" subtlety (~1 mark loss):** The GS antenna is 1000 ft past the threshold. The "distance to OM" must be measured **from the antenna**, not from the threshold. Add 1000 ft to the OM-to-threshold distance.
>
> 5. **Confusing OM/MM/IM locations (~1.5 marks loss):** Outer Marker = 3.5–6 NM (FAF); Middle Marker = 0.5–0.8 NM (~200 ft AGL, near CAT I DH); Inner Marker = 0.15 NM (CAT II/III only, ~100 ft AGL).
>
> 6. **SDM vs DDM mix-up (~1 mark loss):** **DDM** gives the **deviation**; **SDM** is used for **signal validity** (NAV flag). If a question says "the signal is unreliable," it is SDM, not DDM, that has failed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This for Last-Minute Reading**

### 🚨 Critical Constants (Memorize!)

- **Localizer frequency:** VHF 108.10 – 111.95 MHz
- **Glide Slope frequency:** UHF 329.15 – 335.00 MHz
- **Marker Beacon frequency:** VHF 75 MHz
- **Standard glide path angle:** **3°** (configurable 2.5°–3.5°)
- **Localizer course width at threshold:** **700 ft** (210 m)
- **Glide Slope beam width:** **1.4°** (0.7° half)
- **Nominal modulation depth per tone:** **20%** (range 17.5%–22.5%)
- **Course-line DDM:** **0**
- **Full-scale DDM:** **0.155**
- **Displacement sensitivity (LLZ):** **350 ft / 0.155 DDM ≈ 2258 ft per unit DDM**
- **1 NM = 6076.115 ft** (always convert!)

### 🎯 The "Three Magic Tones" Convention

- **90 Hz tone** = LEFT (localizer) AND LOWER (glide slope)
- **150 Hz tone** = RIGHT (localizer) AND UPPER (glide slope)
- **Equal tones (DDM = 0)** = ON COURSE
- **Course Indicator (CDI/HSI) needle deflection** is proportional to DDM

### 📐 Core Formulas (3 to Survive)

1. **DDM:**
$$
DDM = \frac{M_{90} - M_{150}}{M_{90} + M_{150}}
$$

2. **Lateral Displacement from Course Line:**
$$
\Delta x = \frac{350 \text{ ft}}{0.155} \cdot \vert DDM \vert = 2258 \cdot \vert DDM \vert
$$

3. **Glide Path Angle:**
$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{D}\right) \quad \text{(where } h = \text{altitude AGL, } D = \text{horizontal distance to GS antenna)}
$$

### 🛬 Marker Beacon Quick-Reference

| Beacon | Distance (NM) | Altitude (ft AGL) | Audio | Light | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Outer (OM)** | 3.5 – 6 | ~1500 | 400 Hz (dashes) | **Blue** | Final approach fix |
| **Middle (MM)** | 0.5 – 0.8 | ~200 | 1300 Hz (mixed) | **Amber** | CAT I decision height |
| **Inner (IM)** | 0.15 | ~100 | 3000 Hz (dots) | **White** | Auto-coupler reference (CAT II/III) |

### 🏷️ ILS Category Quick-Reference (ICAO)

| Category | DH | RVR | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CAT I** | $\geq$ 200 ft | $\geq$ 550 m | Standard precision approach |
| **CAT II** | 100–200 ft | $\geq$ 300 m | Low-visibility operations |
| **CAT IIIA** | $< 100$ ft | $\geq$ 200 m | Near-zero visibility |
| **CAT IIIB** | $< 50$ ft | $\geq$ 75 m | Zero-visibility takeoff/landing |
| **CAT IIIC** | No DH | No limit | "Blind landing" (theoretical) |

### 💡 Examiner's Final Mantra for ILS

> **"90 Hz = LEFT and LOWER. 150 Hz = RIGHT and UPPER. On course means DDM = 0. Three NM from threshold is the Outer Marker. Glide path is 3° down. Localizer course is 700 ft wide at threshold. The Markers are at 75 MHz, Localizer is in VHF, Glide Slope is in UHF."**

Master these and any KTU Part A or Part B question on ILS is a guaranteed full-score retrieval.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microwave Landing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microwave Landing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microwave Landing System (MLS)** is an all-weather, precision radio navigation aid operating in the **C-band (5.031 GHz)**, intended to replace the Instrument Landing System (ILS) by providing aircraft with three-dimensional guidance — **azimuth, elevation, and range** — during approach, landing, and missed-approach phases. It uses the **Time-Referenced Scanning Beam (TRSB)** technique to encode angular information with extreme precision, supporting Category I, II, and III operations.

The system was developed under ICAO Annex 10 standards and offers a vastly wider coverage volume, superior accuracy, immunity to interference, and a much larger channel complement (200 channels) compared to the 20-channel VHF/UHF ILS.

## 1.2 Component Architecture

A complete MLS ground installation consists of the following sub-systems:

| Sub-System | Function | Carrier |
|------------|----------|---------|
| **Azimuth (AZ)** | Lateral guidance relative to runway centre-line | **5.031 GHz** |
| **Elevation (EL)** | Vertical glide-path guidance | **5.031 GHz** |
| **Flare (FL)** | Pitch guidance during flare-out (autoland) | **5.031 GHz** |
| **Back Azimuth (BAZ)** | Reverse-direction lateral guidance for missed approach | **5.031 GHz** |
| **DME/P** | Co-located precision Distance Measuring Equipment | **962–1213 MHz (L-band)** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Supercharged Lighthouse"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a lighthouse keeper sweeping a torch beam across the sky:**
> - The keeper sweeps the beam TO-and-FRO across the horizon (left-to-right, then right-to-left).
> - If the **rate of sweep is known** to the captain of a distant ship, then by simply **timing how long after the leftward sweep the rightward sweep arrives**, the captain can calculate the angular bearing of the lighthouse to a fraction of a degree — without any rotating antenna on the ship.
> - MLS works on exactly this principle, but with a **microwave pencil beam** moving at speeds up to **13,500° per second**, scanning ±40° to ±60° horizontally and 0°–15° vertically. The *time difference* between two sweeps (TO and FRO) is directly proportional to the angle.

## 1.4 Why C-Band? Why Not VHF/UHF?

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of **5.031 GHz (C-band)** over the 110 MHz / 330 MHz used by ILS is deliberate:
> - **Wavelength ≈ 6 cm** → physically small antennas, ideal for the rapid mechanical/electronic scanning required.
> - **Beamwidth narrowing** is easier at higher frequencies, giving a sharp pencil beam (~1°) for crisp angular resolution.
> - **Spectrum availability** — the VHF/UHF region is congested; C-band has **200 dedicated channels** (vs 20 for ILS) and is far less affected by atmospheric noise.
> - **Site flexibility** — higher frequency means smaller, sharper beam, reducing multi-path reflections from hangars, terrain, and vehicles.

## 1.5 Operating Frequency Channels (KTU High-Yield)

| Service | Frequency Band | Number of Channels |
|---------|----------------|--------------------|
| AZ / EL / FL / BAZ | **5031.0 – 5150.0 MHz** | 200 |
| DME/P | **962 – 1213 MHz** | Paired with MLS channels |
| Air-to-ground data link | **C-band auxiliary** | Embedded in MLS frame |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Time-Referenced Scanning Beam (TRSB)

The MLS does **not** rely on lobe comparison (as ILS does). Instead, a single narrow beam is swept **TO** and **FRO** through the coverage sector in a saw-tooth pattern. The aircraft receiver:

1. Detects the **TO-scan** pulse (beam moving in one direction, fast).
2. Detects the **FRO-scan** pulse (beam moving in the opposite direction, slow).
3. Measures the **time interval (Δt) between the two pulses**.
4. Computes the **angular position** by knowing the (constant, calibrated) scan rates of both sweeps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The angle is **time-encoded**, not amplitude-encoded. This is the fundamental reason MLS is immune to standing-wave and multi-path errors that plague ILS.

## 2.2 Scan-Rate Standards (ICAO Annex 10)

| Function | TO-Scan Rate | FRO-Scan Rate | Coverage |
|----------|--------------|---------------|----------|
| **Azimuth (AZ)** | **13,500°/s** (fast) | **750°/s** (slow) | ±40° to ±60° |
| **Elevation (EL)** — low | 750°/s (slow) | 13,500°/s (fast) | 0° – 7.5° |
| **Elevation (EL)** — high | 13,500°/s | 13,500°/s | 7.5° – 15° or 20° |
| **Flare (FL)** | Symmetric | Symmetric | 0° – 8° |

The asymmetric pairing (fast/slow) is what **creates a one-to-one mapping** between *Δt* and *angle*. A symmetric pair (used for flare) keeps Δt constant and identifies the function unambiguously.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All angular quantities in degrees unless otherwise stated. All times in **microseconds (µs)** for KTU exam numerical work.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{\Delta t - \Delta t_{ref}}{k}$ | Angle from centre-line (TRSB decode) | degrees |
| 2 | $\Delta t_{aircraft} = T_{TO} + \dfrac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\omega_{FRO}} - \dfrac{\theta + \theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}}$ | Time interval measured at receiver | seconds |
| 3 | $\theta = \left[\dfrac{1}{\omega_{FRO}} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_{TO}}\right]^{-1} \cdot \left[\Delta t_{aircraft} - T_{TO} + \theta_{max}\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega_{FRO}} - \dfrac{1}{\omega_{TO}}\right)\right]$ | Inverted form for decoder | degrees |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t_{DME}}{2}$ | Slant range from DME/P | metres |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\theta} \approx \dfrac{\Delta t_{res}}{\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega_{FRO}} - \dfrac{1}{\omega_{TO}}\right)^{-1}}$ | Angular resolution (1σ) | degrees |
| 6 | $h = D \sin\gamma$ | Height above threshold (γ = glide angle) | metres |
| 7 | $\gamma = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{h}{D}\right)$ | Glide-slope angle | degrees |
| 8 | $f_{MLS} = 5.031\ \text{GHz}$ | Operating frequency (C-band) | Hz |
| 9 | $\lambda_{MLS} = \dfrac{c}{f_{MLS}} \approx 0.0596\ \text{m}$ | Wavelength | metres |
| 10 | $N_{ch} = 200$ | Channel complement (vs 20 for ILS) | — |

Where:
- $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** (speed of light),
- $\omega_{TO}, \omega_{FRO}$ = angular scan velocities in **deg/s**,
- $\theta_{max}$ = half-coverage angle (e.g. 40° for AZ),
- $\Delta t_{res}$ = receiver timing resolution (≈ 10 µs typical).

## 2.4 Coverage Volume — Why MLS Wins

| Parameter | ILS (VHF/UHF) | MLS (C-Band) |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Azimuth coverage | ±10° (localiser) typical | **±40° to ±60°** |
| Elevation coverage | 0° – 7° (glideslope) | **0° – 20°** (up to 29° with high-angle EL) |
| Range | 25 NM | **30 – 40 NM** |
| Channel count | 20 (VHF) + 40 (UHF) | **200** (paired with DME/P) |
| Multi-path sensitivity | High | Low (sharp beam) |
| Curved approaches | No | **Yes** |
| Sit-elevation flexibility | Limited | High (independent siting) |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The TRSB principle is reused (conceptually) in:
- **Modern GNSS augmentation** (time-encoded pseudoranges).
- **Precision radar tracking** (monopulse / beam-splitter variants).
- **LIDAR and laser-based landing aids** under development for urban air mobility (UAM) and eVTOL operations.

Understanding MLS equips a B.Tech Aerospace student with the **time-angle duality** that pervades every modern navigation system — from GBAS to ADS-B.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — TRSB Angular Decode Equation

### Statement
A ground-based AZ antenna scans the sector $-\theta_{max}$ to $+\theta_{max}$ in the **TO direction** at angular velocity $\omega_{TO}$ (deg/s), and immediately reverses to scan in the **FRO direction** at angular velocity $\omega_{FRO}$. An aircraft at angle $\theta$ from the centre-line measures the time difference $\Delta t$ between the TO and FRO pulses. Derive an expression for $\theta$ in terms of $\Delta t$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the origin of time**

Let $t = 0$ be the instant the TO-scan begins at $-\theta_{max}$.

**Step 2 — Instant the TO-beam reaches angle θ**

The TO-scan traverses the sector from $-\theta_{max}$ to $+\theta_{max}$. The angular distance covered when the beam reaches $\theta$ is:

$$
\Delta\theta_{TO} = \theta - (-\theta_{max}) = \theta + \theta_{max}
$$

Hence the time elapsed from start of TO-scan:

$$
t_{TO} = \frac{\theta + \theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}}
$$

**Step 3 — Duration of the complete TO-scan**

The TO-scan ends when the beam reaches $+\theta_{max}$:

$$
T_{TO} = \frac{2\theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}}
$$

**Step 4 — Instant the FRO-beam reaches angle θ**

The FRO-scan begins at $t = T_{TO}$ from $+\theta_{max}$ and proceeds *towards* $-\theta_{max}$ at $\omega_{FRO}$. The angular distance it must cover to reach $\theta$ is:

$$
\Delta\theta_{FRO} = \theta_{max} - \theta
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_{FRO} = T_{TO} + \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\omega_{FRO}}
$$

**Step 5 — Time difference at the aircraft**

$$
\Delta t = t_{FRO} - t_{TO}
$$

$$
\Delta t = T_{TO} + \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\omega_{FRO}} - \frac{\theta + \theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}}
$$

Substitute $T_{TO} = \dfrac{2\theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}}$:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2\theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}} + \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\omega_{FRO}} - \frac{\theta + \theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the AZ-specific terms**

The two AZ-related terms combine:

$$
\frac{2\theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}} - \frac{\theta + \theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}} = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\omega_{TO}}
$$

So:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\omega_{TO}} + \frac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\omega_{FRO}}
$$

$$
\Delta t = (\theta_{max} - \theta)\left[\frac{1}{\omega_{TO}} + \frac{1}{\omega_{FRO}}\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the angle θ**

$$
\theta_{max} - \theta = \frac{\Delta t}{\dfrac{1}{\omega_{TO}} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_{FRO}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\theta = \theta_{max} - \Delta t \cdot \left[\frac{1}{\omega_{TO}} + \frac{1}{\omega_{FRO}}\right]^{-1}\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental TRSB decode equation** in the AZ (asymmetric scan) case. For EL with reversed pairing the sign convention is adjusted but the form is identical.

**Step 8 — Sanity check (numerical) with AZ rates**

Take $\theta_{max} = 40°$, $\omega_{TO} = 13{,}500°/s$, $\omega_{FRO} = 750°/s$.

$$
\frac{1}{\omega_{TO}} + \frac{1}{\omega_{FRO}} = \frac{1}{13500} + \frac{1}{750} = 7.407 \times 10^{-5} + 1.333 \times 10^{-3} = 1.407 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s/deg}
$$

At $\theta = 0$ (centre-line):

$$
\Delta t = 40 \times 1.407 \times 10^{-3} = 5.628 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{s} = 56.28\ \text{ms}
$$

At $\theta = +40°$: $\Delta t = 0$ ✓
At $\theta = -40°$: $\Delta t = 80° \times 1.407 \times 10^{-3} = 112.6$ ms ✓

The decoder therefore works on a 0–112.6 ms timing window mapped linearly to a $-40°$ to $+40°$ sector.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DME/P Range from Slant-Range Time Delay

Aircraft interrogates the DME/P transponder with a paired pulse. The ground transponder replies after a fixed beacon delay $t_{d}$. The aircraft measures the round-trip time $\Delta t_{DME}$. Derive the slant range $D$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Time for the radio wave to travel aircraft → ground**

$$
t_{down} = \frac{D}{c}
$$

**Step 2 — Time for the reply to travel ground → aircraft**

$$
t_{up} = \frac{D}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Total round-trip time including beacon delay**

$$
\Delta t_{DME} = t_{down} + t_{d} + t_{up} = \frac{2D}{c} + t_{d}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for D**

$$
\boxed{\;D = \frac{c}{2}\bigl(\Delta t_{DME} - t_{d}\bigr)\;}
$$

With $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and $t_{d} \approx 50\ \mu s$ (typical MLS DME/P):

- For $\Delta t_{DME} = 1000\ \mu s = 1$ ms:
$$
D = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2}(1000 \times 10^{-6} - 50 \times 10^{-6}) = 142.5\ \text{km} \approx 77\ \text{NM}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Glide-Slope Height at Threshold

An aircraft on the MLS glide-path is at slant range $D$ from the AZ antenna, with a commanded elevation angle $\gamma$ from the threshold. Derive the height $h$ above threshold.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

By elementary trigonometry of the right triangle formed by the aircraft, the threshold and the elevation antenna:

$$
\sin\gamma = \frac{h}{D}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;h = D \sin\gamma\;}
$$

For a standard 3° glide-slope at $D = 10$ NM = 18 520 m:

$$
h = 18520 \times \sin 3° = 18520 \times 0.05234 = 969.3\ \text{m} \approx 3{,}180\ \text{ft}
$$

This matches the standard **decision-height band** of Category I operations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | AZ Formula | EL Formula | Unit |
|----------|--------|------------|------------|------|
| Half-coverage | $\theta_{max}$ | 40° (or 60°) | 7.5° (low) / 15° (high) | degree |
| TO scan velocity | $\omega_{TO}$ | 13 500 | 750 (low) | deg/s |
| FRO scan velocity | $\omega_{FRO}$ | 750 | 13 500 (low) | deg/s |
| Centre-line Δt | $\Delta t_0$ | 56.3 ms | (function of limits) | ms |
| Sector-edge Δt range | — | 0 to 112.6 ms | — | ms |
| Slant range | $D$ | $D = (c/2)(\Delta t_{DME} - t_d)$ | same | m |
| Height | $h$ | — | $h = D\sin\gamma$ | m |
