# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q: Define the Microwave Landing System (MLS). List its sub-systems and state the operating frequency.**
> [3 Marks | CO4 / K3]

**Model Answer:**
The **Microwave Landing System (MLS)** is a precision radio navigation aid that operates at **5.031 GHz (C-band)** and provides three-dimensional guidance to aircraft during approach and landing using the **Time-Referenced Scanning Beam (TRSB)** principle.
Sub-systems:
1. **Azimuth (AZ)** – lateral guidance.
2. **Elevation (EL)** – vertical/glide-path guidance.
3. **Flare (FL)** – pitch guidance during flare-out.
4. **Back Azimuth (BAZ)** – reverse-direction guidance.
5. **DME/P** – precision distance measurement (962–1213 MHz).

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q: What is Time-Referenced Scanning Beam (TRSB)? How is angle encoded in this technique?**
> [3 Marks | CO4 / K2]

**Model Answer:**
TRSB is the angular measurement principle used in MLS. A narrow pencil beam (≈1° wide) sweeps **TO** and **FRO** through the coverage sector at two different known angular velocities (typically **13 500°/s** and **750°/s**). The aircraft receiver measures the **time interval (Δt) between the TO-pulse and the FRO-pulse**. Because the scan rates are constant and known, Δt is **linearly proportional to the angle** of the aircraft from the centre-line. The angle is therefore *time-encoded*, not amplitude-encoded — making the system immune to receiver gain variations and multi-path standing waves.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style | Module 4 / CO4]**

### ✦ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) ✦

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Time-Referenced Scanning Beam (TRSB)** principle used in the Microwave Landing System with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between the **measured time interval Δt** and the **angular position θ** of an aircraft from the runway centre-line, given the TO and FRO scan rates. **[7 Marks | K3/K4]**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**1. Block diagram** (draw the Aircraft Receiver block diagram of §4.2 above — full marks for labelled blocks: C-band antenna → LNA → BPF → down-converter → TO/FRO pulse detector → time-interval counter → microprocessor → angle output).

**2. Operating principle:**
- The ground AZ antenna radiates a narrow beam that sweeps the sector $-\theta_{max}$ to $+\theta_{max}$.
- The TO-scan is at $\omega_{TO} = 13{,}500°/s$ (fast).
- The FRO-scan is at $\omega_{FRO} = 750°/s$ (slow).
- The aircraft receiver detects both pulses and measures $\Delta t = t_{FRO} - t_{TO}$.

**3. Derivation** (write step-by-step, mirroring §3.1):
- $t_{TO} = \dfrac{\theta + \theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}}$
- $T_{TO} = \dfrac{2\theta_{max}}{\omega_{TO}}$
- $t_{FRO} = T_{TO} + \dfrac{\theta_{max} - \theta}{\omega_{FRO}}$
- $\Delta t = (\theta_{max} - \theta)\left[\dfrac{1}{\omega_{TO}} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_{FRO}}\right]$
- Solving:
$$
\theta = \theta_{max} - \Delta t \left[\dfrac{1}{\omega_{TO}} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_{FRO}}\right]^{-1}
$$

**4. Numerical check** (mention the centre-line $\Delta t = 56.3$ ms and edge values 0/112.6 ms).

**Q: (b)** With the help of a comparative table, discuss the **advantages of MLS over ILS**. Why is C-band preferred over VHF/UHF for landing aids? **[7 Marks | K4/K5]**

**Model Solution — (b):**

**1. Comparison Table (draw or write):**

| Parameter | ILS | MLS |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Operating frequency | 110 MHz / 330 MHz | 5.031 GHz |
| Angular technique | CSB + SBO (lobe comparison) | TRSB (time-encoded) |
| Channel count | 20 | 200 |
| Azimuth coverage | ±10° (typical) | ±40° to ±60° |
| Elevation coverage | 0°–7° | 0°–20° (or 29°) |
| Range | 25 NM | 30–40 NM |
| Multi-path sensitivity | High (broad beam) | Low (pencil beam) |
| Curved approach | No | Yes |
| Autoland support | Limited | Full (Cat II/III) |

**2. Why C-band?**
- **Smaller wavelength (~6 cm)** → compact antenna, fast mechanical/electronic scan feasible.
- **Narrow beamwidth** → sharp angular resolution.
- **Cleaner spectrum** → less congestion than VHF/UHF.
- **200 channels** vs only 20 for ILS — eliminates frequency congestion at busy airports.
- **Pencil beam** minimises reflections from terrain, buildings, and parked aircraft.
- **Higher data rate** for the auxiliary data word (e.g., runway heading, threshold distance, site identifier).

**3. Real-world impact:** MLS enables **curved and segmented approaches**, which reduce noise over residential areas and allow operations at airports surrounded by high terrain — critical for Kerala's topography (e.g., Calicut, Kannur).

---

### ✦ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice ✦

**Q: (a)** Describe the **sub-systems of MLS** in detail. Explain the role of **DME/P** and how it complements the angular guidance to give a complete three-dimensional position fix. **[7 Marks | K3]**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**1. Sub-systems** (write the table from §1.2 above with frequency and function).

**2. DME/P — Precision Distance Measuring Equipment:**
- Operating in the **L-band (962–1213 MHz)**.
- Co-located with the MLS ground station; **channel-paired** (200 channels).
- The aircraft interrogates with a paired pulse; the ground beacon replies after a fixed delay $t_d \approx 50\ \mu s$.
- The aircraft measures the round-trip time $\Delta t_{DME}$ and computes the slant range:

$$
D = \frac{c}{2}\bigl(\Delta t_{DME} - t_{d}\bigr)
$$

- **Two accuracy modes:**
  - **FA (Final Approach) mode:** accuracy ≈ 30 m (used within 5 NM for Cat III).
  - **IA (Initial Approach) mode:** accuracy ≈ 100 m (used 5–40 NM).

**3. 3-D Position fix:**
- AZ provides **θ_az** (lateral angle from centre-line).
- EL provides **θ_el** (vertical glide-path angle).
- DME/P provides **range D**.
- Aircraft computes:
  - Cross-track distance: $x = D \sin\theta_{az}$
  - Height above threshold: $h = D \sin\theta_{el}$
  - Along-track distance: $y = D \cos\theta_{az} \cos\theta_{el}$

This gives a **fully determined 3-D position** relative to the runway threshold.

**Q: (b)** A ground-based MLS Azimuth antenna scans the sector $-40°$ to $+40°$ with $\omega_{TO} = 13{,}500°/s$ and $\omega_{FRO} = 750°/s$. **(i)** Calculate the time interval measured by an aircraft at the **centre-line**. **(ii)** Find the angle θ of an aircraft that measures $\Delta t = 28$ ms. **(iii)** Determine the slant range if DME/P gives $\Delta t_{DME} = 245\ \mu s$ and beacon delay $t_d = 50\ \mu s$. **[7 Marks | K4 — Numerical]**

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Given:** $\theta_{max} = 40°$, $\omega_{TO} = 13{,}500°/s$, $\omega_{FRO} = 750°/s$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $t_d = 50\ \mu s$.

**(i) Centre-line time interval** ($\theta = 0$):

$$
\Delta t_0 = \theta_{max} \left[\frac{1}{\omega_{TO}} + \frac{1}{\omega_{FRO}}\right]
$$

$$
= 40 \times \left[\frac{1}{13500} + \frac{1}{750}\right]
$$

$$
= 40 \times (7.407 \times 10^{-5} + 1.333 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
= 40 \times 1.407 \times 10^{-3} = 5.628 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta t_0 = 56.28\ \text{ms}}
$$

**(ii) Angle for Δt = 28 ms:**

Using $\theta = \theta_{max} - \Delta t \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{\omega_{TO}} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_{FRO}}\right]^{-1}$:

$$
\theta = 40 - (28 \times 10^{-3}) \times \left[1.407 \times 10^{-3}\right]^{-1}
$$

$$
\theta = 40 - 19.90 = 20.10°
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 20.10° \text{ to the right of centre-line}}
$$

**(iii) Slant range from DME/P:**

$$
D = \frac{c}{2}\bigl(\Delta t_{DME} - t_d\bigr) = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2}(245 - 50) \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
D = 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 195 \times 10^{-6} = 29{,}250\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 29.25\ \text{km} \approx 15.79\ \text{NM}}
$$

**Verification using along-track geometry:** height = $D \sin\theta_{el}$ — would be applied for a 3° glide-slope to give $h = 1530$ m above threshold.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing pitfalls (KTU Examiner observations):**
> 1. **Confusing the TO and FRO scan rates** between Azimuth and Elevation. Remember: AZ uses fast-TO/slow-FRO; EL (low angle) uses slow-TO/fast-FRO. Reversing the rates gives a *negative-slope* decode, which is mathematically valid for the other function.
> 2. **Forgetting the beacon delay $t_d$** in DME/P range equations — students often write $D = c \Delta t/2$ and lose 1–2 marks. Always subtract $t_d$.
> 3. **Not drawing a labelled block diagram** in TRSB questions — the question explicitly says "with a neat block diagram" and the diagram carries 2–3 marks by itself.
> 4. **Using ILS terminology** in an MLS question (CSB, SBO, capture effect, etc.) — examiners immediately deduct marks. Use **TO-scan, FRO-scan, time interval, scan rate**.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers — write **ms** or **µs** explicitly.
> 6. **Writing "DME" without "/P"** — the precision variant DME/P is the MLS-paired system; standard DME has lower accuracy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **High-Density Revision Checklist (rapid-fire):**

- **MLS** = Microwave Landing System; operates at **5.031 GHz (C-band)**; **200 channels**.
- **TRSB** = Time-Referenced Scanning Beam — *angle is encoded in time*, not amplitude.
- **Two scans:** TO (fast) and FRO (slow) at different angular velocities.
- **AZ:** $\omega_{TO} = 13{,}500°/s$, $\omega_{FRO} = 750°/s$, coverage $\pm 40°$ to $\pm 60°$.
- **EL (low):** $\omega_{TO} = 750°/s$, $\omega_{FRO} = 13{,}500°/s$, coverage $0°$ to $7.5°$.
- **EL (high):** symmetric scans, coverage $7.5°$ to $20°$.
- **DME/P:** L-band (962–1213 MHz); $D = (c/2)(\Delta t_{DME} - t_d)$; 30 m (FA) / 100 m (IA) accuracy.
- **Wavelength at 5.031 GHz** ≈ **5.96 cm** → compact pencil-beam antenna.
- **Decode equation:** $\theta = \theta_{max} - \Delta t \left[\dfrac{1}{\omega_{TO}} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_{FRO}}\right]^{-1}$.
- **Coverage advantage over ILS:** wider azimuth (±60° vs ±10°), wider elevation (0°–20° vs 0°–7°), curved-approach capable.
- **Multi-path immunity:** narrow beam + time-encoding ⇒ no standing-wave errors.
- **3-D fix:** AZ + EL + DME/P → (x, y, z) relative to threshold.
- **Auxiliary data word** carries runway heading, threshold distance, station ID — embedded in the MLS frame.
- **ICAO Annex 10** is the standards reference — do **not** cite invented book titles.
- **Real-world use:** curved / segmented approaches, terrain-constrained airports (e.g., Calicut runway 10/28), Cat III autoland.
- **Future:** MLS and GLS (GNSS Landing System) co-exist; the time-angle duality principle carries forward into GBAS / SBAS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inertial Navigation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inertial Navigation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inertial Navigation System (INS)** is a fully self-contained, non-radiating, dead-reckoning navigation system that determines the position, velocity, and orientation of a vehicle by continuously measuring the specific force (acceleration) and angular rotation through onboard **inertial sensors** (accelerometers and gyroscopes), and integrating these measurements with respect to time within a known reference frame (typically an inertial frame such as the Earth-Centered Inertial — ECI, or Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed — ECEF).

The INS operates on **Newton's Laws of Motion** in an inertial reference frame. Because it requires **no external signal input** (no GPS, no radio, no ground station), it is the only navigation system that can operate in a fully autonomous manner — a property called **autonomy of navigation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Students are expected to clearly distinguish between **Gimbaled Inertial Navigation Systems (GINS)** and **Strapdown Inertial Navigation Systems (SDINS)**, explain the **Schuler tuning** concept, the **navigation loop equation**, the role of **gyros and accelerometers**, and the sources of **error growth (drift)** in INS.

The three core outputs of any INS are:

- **Position** $\rightarrow$ Latitude $(\phi)$, Longitude $(\lambda)$, Altitude $(h)$.
- **Velocity** $\rightarrow$ North $(V_N)$, East $(V_E)$, Down $(V_D)$ or equivalent components.
- **Attitude (Orientation)** $\rightarrow$ Roll $(\phi)$, Pitch $(\theta)$, Yaw $(\psi)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Sensor Pair:** Every INS contains exactly two classes of sensors:
> 1. **Gyroscopes (Gyros)** $\rightarrow$ measure **angular rate** $(\omega)$ — they preserve the reference frame orientation.
> 2. **Accelerometers** $\rightarrow$ measure **specific force** $(f)$ — they are integrated to obtain velocity and position.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Walking Blindfolded in a Known Room":**
> Imagine you are standing in the **centre of a perfectly known, square room** (your initial position and orientation are precisely known). You are then **blindfolded and deafened** — you can neither see the walls nor hear the outside world.
>
> The only things you can sense are:
> - The **push against your feet** when you step (this is what an **accelerometer** feels — the specific force).
> - The **twist of your shoulders** when you turn (this is what a **gyroscope** feels — angular rotation).
>
> If you keep a perfect internal diary — *“I stepped forward with force $f_1$ for time $t_1$, then turned $\psi$ degrees, then stepped with force $f_2$..."* — then by **mathematically integrating** (summing up small steps), you can reconstruct exactly where you are in the room at any instant.
>
> This diary-of-integration is precisely what an INS computer does! If your sensors are perfect, your reconstructed position is perfect. But **any tiny error in a step gets compounded** (integrated) over time, which is why INS errors grow with time — the famous **INS drift problem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The two integration chains** that make INS work are:
> $$\text{Angular rate } (\omega) \xrightarrow{\text{integrate once}} \text{ Attitude } (\psi,\theta,\phi)$$
> $$\text{Specific force } (f) \xrightarrow{\text{integrate once}} \text{ Velocity } (V) \xrightarrow{\text{integrate again}} \text{ Position } (x,y,z)$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The Two-Loop INS Architecture

An INS processes information in **two simultaneous closed loops**:

**Loop A — The Attitude (Stabilisation) Loop**
1. Gyroscopes sense the **angular rate** $\vec{\omega}$ of the vehicle body with respect to inertial space.
2. This signal is **integrated** by the navigation computer to obtain the **Euler angles** (roll, pitch, yaw) or a **quaternion** representing vehicle orientation.
3. The orientation is then used to **resolve the accelerometer outputs** from the body frame into the navigation frame.

**Loop B — The Navigation (Translation) Loop**
1. Accelerometers sense the **specific force** $\vec{f}$ (the non-gravitational force per unit mass acting on the vehicle).
2. The specific force is first **resolved into the navigation frame** using the attitude from Loop A.
3. **Gravity** $g$ is subtracted to obtain true acceleration in inertial space.
4. This is **integrated once** to obtain **velocity** $V(t)$.
5. Integrated **twice** (in cascade) to obtain **position** $r(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Force vs Acceleration — A Critical Distinction:**
> Accelerometers do **not** measure true acceleration $a$. They measure **specific force** $f = a - g$ (i.e., acceleration **minus** gravity). This is because an accelerometer at rest on Earth reads $1g$ upward, not zero. Failing to subtract gravity is one of the most common student errors in INS derivations.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the equations KTU examiners most frequently test in ESE Module 4 questions.**

| # | Equation / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning / Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Specific Force | $f = a - g$ | Non-gravitational force per unit mass; units of $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 2 | Specific Force Vector (Navigation Frame) | $f^n = \dot{V}^n + (2\omega_{ie}^n + \omega_{en}^n) \times V^n - g^n$ | Core navigation equation in $N$-frame |
| 3 | Gyro Measurement | $\omega_{ib}^b = \omega_{ie}^b + \omega_{en}^b + \omega_{nb}^b$ | Body rate = Earth rate + transport rate + body wrt nav frame |
| 4 | Velocity Update | $V(t) = V(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} [f^n(\tau) - g^n + \text{correction}] \, d\tau$ | First integration chain |
| 5 | Position Update | $r(t) = r(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} V(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Second integration chain |
| 6 | Attitude Update (Euler) | $\dot{\phi} = \omega_x + \tan\theta(\omega_y \sin\phi + \omega_z \cos\phi)$ | Roll rate equation |
| 7 | Attitude Update (Euler) | $\dot{\theta} = \omega_y \cos\phi - \omega_z \sin\phi$ | Pitch rate equation |
| 8 | Attitude Update (Euler) | $\dot{\psi} = (\omega_y \sin\phi + \omega_z \cos\phi)/\cos\theta$ | Yaw rate equation |
| 9 | Schuler Period | $T_{Sch} = 2\pi\sqrt{R/g}$ | $\approx 84.4 \text{ minutes}$ on Earth |
| 10 | Schuler Condition | $\omega_s = \sqrt{g/R}$ | Natural undamped oscillation frequency of a pendulum at Earth's centre |
| 11 | Earth Angular Rate | $\omega_{ie} = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/s}$ | Standard value for all KTU problems |
| 12 | Equatorial Earth Gravity | $g_e = 9.7803 \text{ m/s}^2$ | Used in Somigliana gravity model |
| 13 | Mean Earth Radius | $R_e = 6371 \text{ km}$ | Geometric mean radius |
| 14 | INS Error Growth (Position) | $\delta r(t) \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\delta a \, t^2$ | Accelerometer-bias driven position error |
| 15 | INS Error Growth (Velocity) | $\delta V(t) \approx \delta a \, t$ | Accelerometer-bias driven velocity error |
| 16 | Drift Rate (Gyro) | $\epsilon \, [^\circ/\text{hr}]$ | Angular random walk / bias stability metric |
| 17 | Coriolis Term | $2\omega_{ie}^n \times V^n$ | Apparent acceleration due to Earth's rotation |
| 18 | Centripetal Term | $\omega_{en}^n \times V^n$ | Transport rate effect on velocity update |
| 19 | Wander-Azimuth Frame | $N$ frame freely rotates in azimuth | Eliminates singularities near poles |
| 20 | Gimbal Angular Rate | $\omega_p = \omega_{ib} - \omega_{ie}$ | Platform rotation rate wrt inertial frame |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Symbol Caution:** In KTU answer scripts, write $\vert a \vert$ for magnitudes — never as a vertical bar inside a table. Use bold $\mathbf{a}$ or $\vert a \vert$ (LaTeX-rendered) to denote vector magnitude to preserve clean formatting.

---

### 2.3 Why INS Is Used in Aircraft

- **Fully Autonomous:** No external signals (works in GPS-denied environments, over oceans, polar regions, or in electronic warfare).
- **Jamming-Immune:** Cannot be jammed because it does not radiate.
- **High Bandwidth:** Provides **real-time**, high-rate (typically 50–200 Hz) position/velocity/attitude data.
- **Self-Contained:** Independent of weather, day/night, or ionospheric conditions.
- **Multi-Output:** Single system yields position, velocity, and full attitude.

> [!TIP]
> In modern aircraft, the INS is almost always **integrated with GPS** to form an **Integrated Navigation System (INS/GPS)**. The INS provides **smooth, high-rate** data between GPS updates, and the GPS provides **long-term stability** to bound the INS drift. This is the practical engineering solution to the drift problem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Fundamental Specific-Force Equation in the Navigation Frame

We begin with the **true inertial acceleration** of a vehicle whose position in the ECEF frame is $\vec{r}$. The total inertial acceleration is, by Newton's Second Law applied in an inertial frame:

$$\vec{a}_{i} = \frac{d^2 \vec{r}}{dt^2}$$

**Step 1** — Decompose acceleration into the *Earth-fixed (e)* frame and the *navigation (n)* frame. The acceleration in the rotating Earth frame is:

$$\vec{a}_{e} = \left(\frac{d\vec{V}_e}{dt}\right)_e$$

**Step 2** — Apply the **Coriolis theorem** to convert the time derivative from the rotating Earth frame to the inertial frame. For any vector $\vec{A}$:

$$\left(\frac{d\vec{A}}{dt}\right)_i = \left(\frac{d\vec{A}}{dt}\right)_e + \vec{\omega}_{ie} \times \vec{A}$$

Applied to $\vec{V}_e$:

$$\vec{a}_{i} = \dot{\vec{V}}_e + \vec{\omega}_{ie} \times \vec{V}_e$$

**Step 3** — Now express $\vec{V}_e$ in terms of the **navigation-frame velocity** $\vec{V}_n$ relative to the Earth. The rate of change of $\vec{V}_e$ as observed in the navigation frame is:

$$\dot{\vec{V}}_e = \dot{\vec{V}}_n + \vec{\omega}_{en} \times \vec{V}_n$$

where $\vec{\omega}_{en}$ is the **transport rate** (rate at which the navigation frame rotates relative to Earth).

**Step 4** — Substitute Step 3 into Step 2:

$$\vec{a}_{i} = \dot{\vec{V}}_n + \vec{\omega}_{en} \times \vec{V}_n + \vec{\omega}_{ie} \times \vec{V}_e$$

**Step 5** — Recognise that $\vec{V}_e \approx \vec{V}_n$ (vehicle velocity relative to Earth ≈ velocity in navigation frame, for our resolution), giving:

$$\vec{a}_{i} = \dot{\vec{V}}_n + (\vec{\omega}_{en} + \vec{\omega}_{ie}) \times \vec{V}_n$$

**Step 6** — Rearrange to express the time-derivative of velocity:

$$\dot{\vec{V}}_n = \vec{a}_{i} - (\vec{\omega}_{en} + \vec{\omega}_{ie}) \times \vec{V}_n$$

**Step 7** — Introduce the **gravitational acceleration** $\vec{g}$ (modelled to point downward in the navigation frame). Then $\vec{a}_{i} - \vec{g}$ is by definition the **specific force** $\vec{f}^n$ measured by the accelerometers:

$$\vec{f}^n \triangleq \vec{a}_{i} - \vec{g}^n$$

**Step 8** — Substitute and write the **master navigation equation**:

$$\boxed{\;\dot{\vec{V}}^n = \vec{f}^n - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \vec{V}^n + \vec{g}^n\;}$$

This is the **core INS navigation equation** in the local north-east-down navigation frame. It is the equation the navigation computer integrates to produce velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor of 2 in the Coriolis term?** The $2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n$ arises because Earth rotation contributes once in transforming the *frame* and once in transforming the *time derivative*, giving a doubled contribution. This is one of the most exam-tested pieces of theory in KTU Module 4.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Schuler Period

Consider an INS mounted on a vehicle. Suppose the navigation computer has a small tilt error $\delta\theta$ in its computed level. This tilt causes the accelerometer to mistakenly include a horizontal component of gravity $g\sin(\delta\theta) \approx g\,\delta\theta$. The resulting erroneous horizontal acceleration is:

$$\delta\ddot{x} = g\,\delta\theta$$

But the tilt itself is geometrically coupled to the position error. If the INS thinks it is at the Earth's centre, but is actually at radius $R$, then the apparent horizontal-tilt $\delta\theta$ is:

$$\delta\theta = \frac{\delta x}{R}$$

Differentiate twice and combine to get the **Schuler differential equation**:

$$\delta\ddot{x} + \frac{g}{R}\,\delta x = 0$$

This is a **simple harmonic oscillator** with natural (undamped) frequency:

$$\omega_{s} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$$

**Numerical evaluation** — using the canonical Earth values $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $R = 6.371 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$:

$$\omega_{s} = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{6.371 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{1.540 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.241 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/s}$$

The corresponding **Schuler period** is:

$$T_{Sch} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_s} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{R}{g}} \approx 5064 \text{ s} \approx 84.4 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Schuler Tuning** is the engineering act of configuring the INS feedback gains so that the natural oscillation period of any position error equals $T_{Sch}$. A Schuler-tuned INS has the beautiful property that **horizontal position errors oscillate boundedly** rather than growing without limit, which would otherwise happen for any constant tilt error. This is the single most important concept in classical INS design.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Accelerometer-Bias Driven Error Growth

Suppose a single accelerometer has a **constant bias** $\delta a$ (in $\text{m/s}^2$). The velocity error after time $t$ is the first integral:

$$\delta V(t) = \int_0^t \delta a \, d\tau = \delta a \cdot t$$

The position error after time $t$ is the second integral:

$$\delta r(t) = \int_0^t \delta V(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_0^t \delta a \cdot \tau \, d\tau = \frac{1}{2}\,\delta a \, t^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Position error grows **quadratically with time** for a constant accelerometer bias. This is the fundamental reason why long-duration missions (e.g., transoceanic flights) cannot rely on a free-running INS and require periodic aiding (GPS updates, Doppler radar, celestial updates, etc.).

For a **gyro bias** $\epsilon$ (in rad/s), the corresponding **attitude error** grows **linearly** as $\delta\theta = \epsilon t$, and the position error it induces grows **cubically**:

$$\delta r_{gyro}(t) \approx \tfrac{1}{6}\,g\,\epsilon\,t^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Many students mistakenly write $\delta r \sim t^2$ for *both* accelerometer and gyro errors. The correct order is: $\delta r_{accel} \sim t^2$ and $\delta r_{gyro} \sim t^3$ (because gyro errors must be doubly integrated via the Schuler loop).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of GINS vs SDINS

| Parameter | Gimbaled INS (GINS) | Strapdown INS (SDINS) |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Sensor Mounting | Mounted on a **stabilised platform** | Mounted **directly on vehicle body** |
| Platform Isolation | Sensors are isolated from vehicle rotation | Sensors experience full vehicle rotation |
| Gyro Mechanical Stress | Low (platform rotates slowly) | High (gyros must track large body rates) |
| Computer Load | Low (platform does the rotation) | **Very high** (computer must resolve body→nav at high rate) |
| Size & Weight | Bulky (gimbals, torque motors) | Compact and light |
| Reliability (Mean Time Between Failures) | Lower (many moving parts) | **Higher** (no moving parts) |
| Accuracy (Civilian) | Medium–High | High (modern ring-laser/FOG gyros) |
| Modern Aircraft Use | Mostly retired (1960s–80s) | **Universal** (since ~1990s) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower (with mass production) |
| Drift Sensitivity | Lower drift per axis | Higher drift (mitigated by better gyros) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Priority:** When asked to compare GINS and SDINS, always include the line *“Modern aircraft universally use SDINS with ring-laser gyros or fibre-optic gyros, while GINS is of historical importance.”*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic INS Signal Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a generic Inertial Navigation System, showing both the attitude loop and the navigation loop. Per the KTU safeguarding rules, all node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[VEHICLE BODY] --> B[GYROSCOPES]
    A --> C[ACCELEROMETERS]
    B --> D[INERTIAL MEASUREMENT UNIT IMU]
    C --> D
    D --> E[NAVIGATION COMPUTER]
    E --> F[ATTITUDE UPDATE EULER OR QUATERNION]
    E --> G[SPECIFIC FORCE RESOLUTION BODY TO NAV FRAME]
    G --> H[GRAVITY AND CORIOLIS COMPENSATION]
    H --> I[VELOCITY INTEGRATION]
    I --> J[POSITION INTEGRATION]
    F --> K[ATTITUDE OUTPUT ROLL PITCH YAW]
    I --> L[VELOCITY OUTPUT NORTH EAST DOWN]
    J --> M[POSITION OUTPUT LAT LON ALT]
    J --> N[FEEDBACK TO COMPENSATION BLOCK]
    N --> H
    F --> O[FEEDBACK TO RESOLUTION BLOCK]
    O --> G
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Gimbaled vs Strapdown Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GIMBALED[GIMBALED INS PLATFORM]
        GYRO1[PLATFORM GYRO] --> PLAT[STABILISED PLATFORM]
        ACC1[PLATFORM ACCELEROMETER] --> PLAT
        PLAT --> POUT[PLATFORM ALIGNED TO NAV FRAME]
    end
    subgraph STRAPDOWN[STRAPDOWN INS BODY MOUNTED]
        GYRO2[BODY GYRO] --> SCOMP[STRAPDOWN COMPUTER]
        ACC2[BODY ACCELEROMETER] --> SCOMP
        SCOMP --> SCOUT[COMPUTED NAV FRAME VIRTUAL]
    end
    POUT --> NAVOUT[NAVIGATION OUTPUT]
    SCOUT --> NAVOUT
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Schuler-Tuned INS Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TRUE POSITION] --> B[GEOMETRIC TILT DELTA THETA]
    B --> C[APPARENT ACCELERATION G TIMES DELTA THETA]
    C --> D[INTEGRATOR 1 VELOCITY ERROR]
    D --> E[INTEGRATOR 2 POSITION ERROR]
    E --> F[DIVIDE BY EARTH RADIUS R]
    F --> B
    F --> G[OSCILLATES AT SCHULER FREQUENCY]
```

---

### 4.4 Sensor-to-Output Mapping Table

| Sensor | Input to Sensor | Output of Sensor | Processed By | Final Output |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|--------------|--------------|
| Gyro (Ring Laser / FOG / MEMS) | Body angular rate $\omega_{ib}^b$ | Voltage / pulse rate proportional to $\omega$ | Navigation Computer | Euler angles or Quaternion |
| Accelerometer (Force-Balance / Quartz / MEMS) | Specific force $f^b$ | Voltage / current proportional to $f$ | Navigation Computer | Velocity and Position |
| Both Together | 6-DOF inertial motion | 6-axis inertial state vector | Integration Algorithms | Full 9-state navigation solution |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`
> **Define an Inertial Navigation System. List the two types of inertial sensors used in it.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Inertial Navigation System (INS)** is a self-contained, dead-reckoning navigation system that determines the position, velocity, and attitude of a vehicle by measuring the **specific force** and **angular rate** through onboard inertial sensors and integrating these measurements with respect to time, without requiring any external signal inputs.

The two types of inertial sensors are:
1. **Gyroscopes (Gyros)** — measure angular rate and preserve reference frame orientation.
2. **Accelerometers (Accels)** — measure specific force, which is integrated to yield velocity and position.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for naming the two sensors.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`
> **What is Schuler tuning? State the Schuler period for Earth.**

**Model Answer:**

**Schuler tuning** is a feedback design technique used in classical INS where the navigation loop gains are configured such that the natural oscillation period of any horizontal position error equals the **Schuler period** $T_{Sch} = 2\pi\sqrt{R/g}$. A Schuler-tuned INS exhibits **bounded oscillation** of position errors rather than unbounded drift.

The Schuler period for Earth is:

$$T_{Sch} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{6.371 \times 10^6}{9.81}} \approx 5064 \text{ s} \approx \mathbf{84.4 \text{ minutes}}$$

*(3 marks: 2 marks for the concept, 1 mark for the numerical period.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 4]`
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Gimbaled Inertial Navigation System (GINS)**. Compare it with the **Strapdown INS (SDINS)** in terms of sensor mounting, computational load, reliability, and modern applicability. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **fundamental specific-force equation** in the local north-east-down navigation frame. Clearly state the physical meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Working of GINS:**

In a GINS, the inertial sensors (gyroscopes and accelerometers) are mounted on a **physically stabilised platform** suspended within a set of **gimbals** (typically 3 or 4 gimbals for full 3-axis isolation).

**Working Principle:**
1. **Gimbals** are torque-motor-driven rings that can rotate freely about their axes. The innermost platform holds the sensors.
2. **Gyroscopes** on the platform sense any deviation of the platform from the desired orientation (e.g., level and north-aligned).
3. The sensed error signal is fed to **torque motors** on the gimbals, which rotate the platform in the opposite direction to **null the error**.
4. This continuous feedback **isolates the platform from vehicle rotation**, keeping it aligned with the navigation frame (e.g., local north-east-down).
5. The accelerometers, now on a level platform, measure only horizontal specific force (plus a $1g$ vertical component which the computer subtracts).
6. The computer integrates specific force to obtain velocity and then position in the navigation frame.

**Comparison Table (must be drawn in answer script):**

| Parameter | GINS | SDINS |
|-----------|------|-------|
| Sensor Mounting | On stabilised platform | Directly on body |
| Computational Load | Low | High |
| Reliability | Lower (moving parts) | Higher (no moving parts) |
| Modern Use | Historical | Universal today |

*(7 marks: 3 for block diagram + working, 4 for the comparison table.)*

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of the Specific-Force Equation:**

We begin with the **inertial acceleration** of the vehicle in an Earth-fixed frame, applying the **Coriolis theorem** step by step.

**Step 1:** Inertial acceleration in rotating Earth frame:

$$\vec{a}_{i} = \dot{\vec{V}}_{en} + \vec{\omega}_{ie} \times \vec{V}_{en}$$

where $\vec{V}_{en}$ is vehicle velocity relative to Earth and $\vec{\omega}_{ie}$ is the Earth rate.

**Step 2:** Resolve into the navigation frame using the transport rate $\vec{\omega}_{en}$:

$$\dot{\vec{V}}_{en} = \dot{\vec{V}}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en} \times \vec{V}^n$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:

$$\vec{a}_{i} = \dot{\vec{V}}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en} \times \vec{V}^n + \vec{\omega}_{ie} \times \vec{V}^n$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate $\dot{\vec{V}}^n$:

$$\dot{\vec{V}}^n = \vec{a}_{i} - (\vec{\omega}_{en} + \vec{\omega}_{ie}) \times \vec{V}^n$$

**Step 5:** Introduce **specific force** $f^n \triangleq a_i - g^n$ and combine Earth rate terms:

$$\boxed{\;\dot{\vec{V}}^n = \vec{f}^n - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \vec{V}^n + \vec{g}^n\;}$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| $\dot{\vec{V}}^n$ | Rate of change of velocity in the navigation frame (the desired output rate) |
| $\vec{f}^n$ | Specific force measured by accelerometers (resolved to nav frame) |
| $2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \vec{V}^n$ | **Coriolis acceleration** (apparent deflection due to Earth rotation) |
| $\vec{\omega}_{en}^n \times \vec{V}^n$ | **Transport-rate centripetal** term (apparent acceleration due to nav-frame motion over curved Earth) |
| $\vec{g}^n$ | Local gravitational acceleration vector |

*(7 marks: 5 for step-by-step derivation, 2 for the physical meaning table.)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 4]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the working of a **Strapdown Inertial Navigation System (SDINS)** with a block diagram. State **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of SDINS over GINS. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A tactical-grade INS has a residual accelerometer bias of $\delta a = 50 \,\mu g$ (where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$). Estimate the **velocity error** and **position error** after 10 minutes of free-running operation. Comment on the practical implications for a long-haul aircraft. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Working of SDINS:**

In an SDINS, the inertial sensors (gyroscopes and accelerometers) are **rigidly strapped (mounted) directly onto the vehicle body** — there is **no stabilised platform**.

**Working Principle:**
1. The **body-mounted gyroscopes** measure the full angular rate of the vehicle body, $\omega_{ib}^b$, relative to inertial space.
2. The **body-mounted accelerometers** measure the specific force in the body frame, $f^b$.
3. The **navigation computer** performs the orientation update (e.g., quaternion update or Euler integration) using gyro data, computing the **direction cosine matrix (DCM)** $C_b^n$ that transforms vectors from body to navigation frame.
4. The accelerometer output is then mathematically resolved: $f^n = C_b^n f^b$.
5. Gravity and Coriolis terms are subtracted, and the result is **integrated** to obtain velocity and position in the navigation frame.
6. Modern SDINS typically use **ring laser gyros (RLG)** or **fibre-optic gyros (FOG)** for the gyroscopes, and **quartz or MEMS accelerometers**.

**Two advantages of SDINS over GINS:**
1. **No moving parts** $\rightarrow$ higher reliability, lower maintenance, longer MTBF.
2. **Smaller size and lower weight** $\rightarrow$ easier to install in space-constrained airframes.

**Two disadvantages of SDINS over GINS:**
1. **Enormous computational load** — computer must update the DCM at high rate (often >1 kHz) to avoid numerical errors.
2. **Sensors see full vehicle dynamic range** — gyros must tolerate large body rates (e.g., 400°/s in a fighter), increasing sensor cost and complexity.

*(7 marks: 3 for block diagram + working, 2 for advantages, 2 for disadvantages.)*

---

**Part (b) — Velocity and Position Error Estimation:**

**Given:**
- Accelerometer bias: $\delta a = 50 \,\mu g = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Operating time: $t = 10 \text{ minutes} = 600 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Compute the bias in SI units:**

$$\delta a = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 = 4.905 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Velocity error after time $t$:**

$$\delta V = \delta a \cdot t = 4.905 \times 10^{-4} \times 600 = 0.2943 \text{ m/s}$$

Rounding: $\delta V \approx \mathbf{0.29 \text{ m/s}}$ (about 1.06 km/h).

**Step 3 — Position error after time $t$:**

$$\delta r = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\delta a \cdot t^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 4.905 \times 10^{-4} \times (600)^2$$

$$\delta r = 0.5 \times 4.905 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.6 \times 10^5$$

$$\delta r = 0.5 \times 176.58 = \mathbf{88.29 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — Practical implications for a long-haul aircraft:**

For a 10-hour transoceanic flight ($t = 36000$ s), scaling by the same $\delta a$:

$$\delta V = 4.905 \times 10^{-4} \times 36000 \approx 17.66 \text{ m/s} \approx 63.6 \text{ km/h}$$

$$\delta r = 0.5 \times 4.905 \times 10^{-4} \times (36000)^2 \approx 317,800 \text{ m} \approx 318 \text{ km}$$

This is **catastrophically large** for a long-haul flight — position error of 318 km is far beyond acceptable navigation accuracy (typically <1 km). Therefore, a **free-running INS is unusable for long-duration navigation** in civil aviation, and the system **must be periodically aided** by external sources such as **GPS**, **VOR/DME**, **Doppler radar**, or **celestial updates**.

*(7 marks: 3 for velocity error, 3 for position error, 1 for practical comment.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract gravity** from accelerometer output — many students write $a$ instead of $f = a - g$. This costs **2 marks minimum** in any derivation question.
> 2. **Missing the factor of 2** in the Coriolis term $2\omega_{ie} \times V$ — losing this gives the wrong specific-force equation, costing **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Conflating accelerometer and gyro error growth order** — remember: position error from accel-bias is $O(t^2)$, from gyro-bias is $O(t^3)$ (Schuler-loop dependent).
> 4. **Using wrong Earth values** — KTU typically expects $R = 6.371 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\omega_{ie} = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/s}$. Do not use rounded values like $\omega_{ie} = 7.3 \times 10^{-5}$ unless specified.
> 5. **Confusing "specific force" and "acceleration"** in word answers — these are not the same and KTU examiners will deduct marks for sloppy terminology.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Inertial Navigation Systems:**

- **INS** = Self-contained, dead-reckoning, no external signals required.
- **Two sensor types:** Gyros (angular rate) + Accelerometers (specific force).
- **Two integration chains:** $\omega \to \text{attitude}$; $f \to V \to \text{position}$.
- **GINS** uses a stabilised platform; **SDINS** mounts sensors on the body — modern aircraft use **SDINS**.
- **Master navigation equation:**
  $\dot{V}^n = f^n - (2\omega_{ie}^n + \omega_{en}^n) \times V^n + g^n$
- **Specific force** $f = a - g$ — must subtract gravity.
- **Coriolis term** has a factor of **2** because Earth rotation contributes twice (frame + derivative).
- **Schuler period** $T_{Sch} = 2\pi\sqrt{R/g} \approx \mathbf{84.4 \text{ minutes}}$ for Earth.
- **Schuler tuning** makes horizontal position error **bounded-oscillatory** rather than divergent.
- **Accelerometer bias** $\to$ position error grows as $\tfrac{1}{2}\delta a t^2$ (quadratic).
- **Gyro bias** $\to$ position error grows as $\tfrac{1}{6}g\epsilon t^3$ (cubic).
- **Schuler frequency** $\omega_s = \sqrt{g/R} \approx 1.241 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/s}$.
- **Earth rate** $\omega_{ie} = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/s}$ (memorise).
- **Mean Earth radius** $R_e = 6.371 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$ (memorise).
- **Modern aiding:** INS is almost always integrated with **GPS** in a Kalman-filter-based **INS/GPS** architecture.
- **Drift** is the central limitation — INS errors grow without bound if unaided.
- **Key historical milestones:** 1942 V-2 rocket (first operational INS) $\to$ 1950s inertial for bombers $\to$ 1970s laser gyros $\to$ 1990s MEMS $\to$ today: SDINS with FOG/RLG.
- **Examiner hot topics:** GINS vs SDINS comparison, derivation of navigation equation, Schuler period calculation, error-growth order, sensor roles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAOT205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF AERONAUTICS AND AIRCRAFT INSTRUMENTS (AERONAUTICAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Global Positioning System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a satellite-based radio-navigation system owned by the **United States Government** and operated by the **United States Space Force (USSF)**. It provides geo-spatial positioning, velocity, and time synchronization (PVT) information to a GPS receiver anywhere on or near the Earth where there is an unobstructed line of sight to four or more GPS satellites. In the context of **aviation instrumentation (PCAOT205)**, GPS is formally defined as a **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)** that forms the backbone of modern aircraft **Required Navigation Performance (RNP)** and **Area Navigation (RNAV)** operations. It replaces or supplements legacy ground-based navigation aids such as **VOR (VHF Omnidirectional Range)**, **DME (Distance Measuring Equipment)**, and **ILS (Instrument Landing System)**. > [!NOTE] > **ICAO Terminology Alert:** The international civil aviation body ICAO uses the generic term **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)** to encompass the US GPS, Russian GLONASS, European Galileo, and Chinese BeiDou. In KTU exam answers, always use GNSS when referring to the general system, and GPS only for the US-specific constellation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are lost in a vast field at night. You have a friend standing at three known landmarks, and each friend shouts their distance to you. If friend A says "I am 300 m from you", friend B says "I am 400 m from you", and friend C says "I am 500 m from you", by drawing three circles (with these radii) around your friends' positions, the **single point where all three circles intersect** is exactly where you are standing. This geometric technique is called **trilateration**. **Real-world mapping of the analogy:** - The three friends = Three GPS satellites in space (orbiting at ~20,200 km altitude). - The shouted distance = **Pseudorange**, calculated using the time a radio signal takes to travel from the satellite to your receiver. - The "field" = The entire Earth's surface and near-space (up to about 30,000 ft cruising altitude of commercial aircraft). - A 4th friend is required because both the satellite clock and your watch have tiny timing errors — this 4th satellite helps **solve for the time bias** and pin down the exact 3D position (latitude, longitude, and altitude). ## 1.3 Why GPS Matters in Aeronautics > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the **Next Generation Air Transportation System (NextGen)** and **Single European Sky ATM Research (SESAR)**, GPS/GNSS is the **primary means of navigation**. Modern airliners like the Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 are **paperless** in the cockpit precisely because GPS-based **Performance-Based Navigation (PBN)** has replaced conventional airway navigation. ### Key Functional Roles of GPS in Aircraft: - **En-route navigation** along RNAV routes - **Terminal area navigation** around airports - **Approach procedures** such as RNAV (GPS) approaches and LPV (Localizer Performance with Vertical guidance) approaches - **Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Broadcast (ADS-B)** position reporting - **Terrain Awareness and Warning System (TAWS)** predictive functions - **Synthetic Vision Systems (SVS)** and **Enhanced Vision Systems (EVS)** ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Value | | :--- | :--- | | Number of satellites in full constellation | **24 + 3 spares = 27 operational** | | Orbital altitude (MEO) | **20,200 km (10,900 nautical miles)** | | Orbital inclination | **55°** to the equatorial plane | | Orbital period | **11 hours 58 minutes (half a sidereal day)** | | Number of orbital planes | **6** | | Satellites per plane | **4** | | Carrier frequencies (L-band) | L1 = **1575.42 MHz**, L2 = **1227.60 MHz**, L5 = **1176.45 MHz** | | Speed of light (signal travel) | **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** | | Time to first fix (cold start) | **30–60 seconds** | | System accuracy (SPS) | **≤ 7.8 m (95% probability), 2D RMS** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Segments of GPS The operational GPS is divided into three distinct functional segments. **Memorize these for any 14-mark KTU question** — they are asked every cycle. ### 2.1.1 Space Segment - Comprises the **satellite constellation** (the 24+3 vehicles). - Each satellite broadcasts a **navigation message** containing: - Ephemeris data (its own precise orbital position) - Almanac data (coarse status of the whole constellation) - Satellite clock correction parameters - Ionospheric model coefficients - Atomic clocks onboard: **Rubidium** (older Block II/IIA) and **Cesium** (Block IIR) — accuracy of a few nanoseconds per day. - Solar-powered with battery backup. ### 2.1.2 Control Segment (OCS — Operational Control Segment) - **1 Master Control Station (MCS)** at Schriever Space Force Base, Colorado. - **6 Monitor Stations** distributed around the globe (Hawaii, Colorado, Cape Canaveral, Ascension Island, Diego Garcia, Kwajalein). - **4 Ground Antenna Stations** (upload stations) for S-band command uplink. - Functions: 1. Track satellites to compute orbital parameters. 2. Maintain satellite time synchronization with **USNO (United States Naval Observatory)** master clock. 3. Upload navigation data to satellites every **24 hours** (or more frequently as needed). 4. Detect and de-activate faulty satellites (SV — Space Vehicle — health flags). ### 2.1.3 User Segment - The **GPS receiver** onboard the aircraft. - Contains an antenna (typically mounted on the upper fuselage), RF front-end, correlator channels, and a navigation processor. - Must track a **minimum of 4 satellites** to compute a 3D fix. ## 2.2 The Ranging Concept — How Distance is Measured The fundamental equation that powers GPS is the **pseudorange equation**: $$ \rho = c \cdot \Delta t $$ where: - $\rho$ = **Pseudorange** (apparent range, in metres) - $c$ = Speed of light = $2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s - $\Delta t$ = Measured travel time of the signal > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "pseudo"?** A pseudorange is the range measured assuming the receiver clock is perfectly synchronized with GPS time. In reality, the receiver has a small clock bias $b$, so the measured distance is offset. The mathematical model that solves for the true range is what gives GPS its name — "ranging with pseudo-distances." ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | Engineering Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\rho$ | Pseudorange | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Range from satellite to receiver | | $b$ | Receiver clock bias (m) | Solved with 4 SVs | Removes time error from range | | $(x_s, y_s, z_s)$ | Satellite position in ECEF | From ephemeris | Known location of satellite | | $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ | User (receiver) position in ECEF | Output of PVT solver | The unknown we want | | $R_i$ | True geometric range to satellite $i$ | $R_i = \sqrt{(x_s - x_u)^2 + (y_s - y_u)^2 + (z_s - z_u)^2}$ | Distance in 3D Euclidean space | | $\sigma_{URE}$ | User Range Error | $\sigma_{URE} \approx 1.5$ m (post SA) | One-sigma ranging error budget | | $DOP$ | Dilution of Precision | $DOP = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^{-1})}$ | Geometry quality indicator | | $GDOP$ | Geometric DOP | Combines 3D + time | Overall positioning geometry | | $HDOP$ | Horizontal DOP | 2D horizontal | For lateral navigation accuracy | | $VDOP$ | Vertical DOP | Altitude component | Critical for approach procedures | | $PDOP$ | Position DOP | 3D position | Overall spatial DOP | | $TDOP$ | Time DOP | Clock component | Time-only geometry | | $UERE$ | User Equivalent Range Error | RSS of all error sources | Total error budget | | $\sigma_P$ | 2D position accuracy (95%) | $\sigma_P = HDOP \cdot UERE \cdot 2.08$ | Statistical position error | | $c$ | Speed of light | $2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s | Universal constant for RF | | $f_L$ | L1 frequency | $1575.42$ MHz | Civil primary carrier | | $\lambda$ | Wavelength at L1 | $\lambda_{L1} = c/f_{L1} \approx 19.05$ cm | Determines carrier-phase resolution | | $C/N_0$ | Carrier-to-Noise density | dB-Hz | Signal quality metric | > [!NOTE] > **Vertical vs Horizontal Accuracy:** GPS vertical accuracy is **roughly 1.6 to 2 times worse** than horizontal accuracy because VDOP is generally larger than HDOP. This is why LPV approaches (which rely on GPS vertical guidance) require stringent barometric aiding and RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) checks. ## 2.4 Ranging Codes — The Heart of the Signal GPS uses **Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)** to allow all satellites to broadcast on the same frequencies without interference. | Code | Frequency | Chip Rate | Wavelength (chip) | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **C/A (Coarse/Acquisition)** | L1 only | **1.023 Mbps** | 293 m | Civilian standard | | **P (Precision)** | L1 + L2 | **10.23 Mbps** | 29.3 m | Military, encrypted as P(Y) | | **M-code** | L1 + L2 | 5.115 Mbps | — | Modern military, separate spectrum | | **L1C, L5 (CNAV)** | L1, L5 | 1.023 / 10.23 Mbps | — | New civil signals (Block III) | **How ranging works with codes:** The receiver generates a **replica of the C/A code** for each satellite. By shifting its replica in time until correlation is maximized, it measures the **code-phase shift**, which is essentially the travel time. This technique is called **code-phase ranging**. ## 2.5 Error Budget — Sources of Inaccuracy The total error budget is built up by **Root Sum of Squares (RSS)** of independent error sources: | Error Source | Typical Magnitude (1σ) | Mitigation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Satellite clock error** | 1.0 m | Corrected via navigation message | | **Ephemeris (orbit) error** | 1.0 m | Updated every 24h by Control Segment | | **Ionospheric delay** | 2.0–10.0 m | **Dual-frequency (L1+L5) correction**; Klobuchar model | | **Tropospheric delay** | 0.5–2.0 m | Saastamoinen/Marini model | | **Multipath** | 0.5–1.0 m (aircraft) | Choke-ring antennas, narrow correlator | | **Receiver noise** | 0.5 m | Improved correlator design | **Selective Availability (SA):** Was a deliberate degradation of the civil signal — **switched off by President Clinton on 2 May 2000**. SA is OFF today, so this is now a historical note (still asked in KTU as a 3-mark question). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Position-Fix Equation (4-Satellite Trilateration) The mathematical heart of GPS. KTU expects you to be able to write this derivation in a 14-mark question. ### Step 1: Define the unknowns The receiver's true position in **ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed) Cartesian coordinates** is the unknown vector: $$ \vec{x}_u = (x_u,\; y_u,\; z_u) $$ But the receiver clock is not perfectly synchronized, so there is a fourth unknown — the **receiver clock bias** $b$ (in metres of equivalent range error). Total unknowns = **4**. ### Step 2: Write the measured pseudorange for satellite $i$ For the $i$-th satellite, whose known position (from ephemeris) is $\vec{x}_s^{(i)} = (x_s^{(i)}, y_s^{(i)}, z_s^{(i)})$: $$ \rho_i = R_i + b + \varepsilon_i $$ where: - $R_i$ = true geometric range - $b$ = receiver clock bias - $\varepsilon_i$ = combined measurement noise ### Step 3: Expand the true geometric range The true geometric range between the receiver and satellite $i$ is the Euclidean distance: $$ R_i = \sqrt{(x_s^{(i)} - x_u)^2 + (y_s^{(i)} - y_u)^2 + (z_s^{(i)} - z_u)^2} $$ ### Step 4: Write the full nonlinear equation $$ \rho_i = \sqrt{(x_s^{(i)} - x_u)^2 + (y_s^{(i)} - y_u)^2 + (z_s^{(i)} - z_u)^2} + b $$ This is **nonlinear** in $x_u, y_u, z_u$. We must linearize it. ### Step 5: Linearize via Taylor-series expansion Assume an initial guess $\vec{x}_0 = (x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$. Expand $R_i$ about this point, keeping only first-order terms (the standard GNSS linearization): $$ R_i \approx R_{i,0} + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x_u}\Big|_0 (x_u - x_0) + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial y_u}\Big|_0 (y_u - y_0) + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial z_u}\Big|_0 (z_u - z_0) $$ Compute the partial derivatives: $$ \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x_u} = \frac{-(x_s^{(i)} - x_u)}{R_i} $$ By the same logic: $$ \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial y_u} = \frac{-(y_s^{(i)} - y_u)}{R_i}, \quad \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial z_u} = \frac{-(z_s^{(i)} - z_u)}{R_i} $$ ### Step 6: Define the line-of-sight unit vector Let $\hat{e}_{x,i} = \dfrac{x_0 - x_s^{(i)}}{R_{i,0}}$. This is the $x$-component of the **line-of-sight (LOS) unit vector** from the satellite to the user (pointing inward). Similarly for $\hat{e}_{y,i}$ and $\hat{e}_{z,i}$. ### Step 7: Write the linearized measurement equation Define the **measurement residual**: $$ \Delta \rho_i = \rho_i - R_{i,0} - b_0 $$ Then the linearized equation becomes: $$ \Delta \rho_i = \hat{e}_{x,i} \cdot \Delta x + \hat{e}_{y,i} \cdot \Delta y + \hat{e}_{z,i} \cdot \Delta z + \Delta b $$ where $\Delta x = x_u - x_0$, $\Delta y = y_u - y_0$, $\Delta z = z_u - z_0$, $\Delta b = b - b_0$. ### Step 8: Stack 4 satellite equations into a matrix form $$ \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \Delta \rho_3 \\ \Delta \rho_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{e}_{x,1} & \hat{e}_{y,1} & \hat{e}_{z,1} & 1 \\ \hat{e}_{x,2} & \hat{e}_{y,2} & \hat{e}_{z,2} & 1 \\ \hat{e}_{x,3} & \hat{e}_{y,3} & \hat{e}_{z,3} & 1 \\ \hat{e}_{x,4} & \hat{e}_{y,4} & \hat{e}_{z,4} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ \Delta b \end{bmatrix} $$ In compact form: $$ \Delta \vec{\rho} = H \cdot \Delta \vec{x} $$ where $H$ is the $4 \times 4$ **geometry matrix**. ### Step 9: Solve using least squares (or direct matrix inversion for 4 SVs) $$ \Delta \vec{x} = H^{-1} \cdot \Delta \vec{\rho} $$ ### Step 10: Update the user position estimate $$ \vec{x}_u^{\text{new}} = \vec{x}_u^{\text{old}} + \Delta \vec{x} $$ The procedure is **iterated** until the update $\Delta \vec{x}$ becomes negligibly small (convergence in typically 5–10 iterations). ### Step 11: Compute DOP from the geometry matrix The covariance of the position estimate is proportional to: $$ \text{Cov}(\vec{x}_u) = \sigma_{URE}^2 (H^T H)^{-1} $$ The DOP matrix is: $$ DOP = \sqrt{\text{diag}\big((H^T H)^{-1}\big)} \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma_{URE}} $$ And the scalar DOPs: $$ GDOP = \sqrt{P_{11} + P_{22} + P_{33} + P_{44}}, \quad PDOP = \sqrt{P_{11} + P_{22} + P_{33}} $$ $$ HDOP = \sqrt{P_{11} + P_{22}}, \quad VDOP = \sqrt{P_{33}}, \quad TDOP = \sqrt{P_{44}} $$ where $P = (H^T H)^{-1}$ and subscripts denote diagonal entries corresponding to $x, y, z, b$. ## 3.2 Numerical Example: Worked-Out Pseudorange Calculation A GPS satellite is at position $(15,600, 12,000, 18,000)$ km in ECEF. A user is at the true position $(−1,500, 4,000, 5,000)$ km. The signal travel time measured by the receiver is $\Delta t = 56.7$ ms. Find the pseudorange. ### Step 1: Apply the speed-of-light formula $$ \rho = c \cdot \Delta t = (2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \times (56.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}) $$ ### Step 2: Compute the multiplication $$ \rho = 2.998 \times 10^8 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-2} $$ Group the powers of 10: $$ \rho = (2.998 \times 5.67) \times 10^{8-2} = (2.998 \times 5.67) \times 10^6 \text{ m} $$ ### Step 3: Multiply the coefficients $$ 2.998 \times 5.67 = 2.998 \times 5 + 2.998 \times 0.67 $$ $$ = 14.990 + 2.00866 = 16.99866 $$ So: $$ \rho \approx 1.69987 \times 10^7 \text{ m} = 16,998.7 \text{ km} $$ ### Step 4: Compare with the true geometric range $$ R = \sqrt{(15{,}600 - (-1{,}500))^2 + (12{,}000 - 4{,}000)^2 + (18{,}000 - 5{,}000)^2} $$ $$ R = \sqrt{(17{,}100)^2 + (8{,}000)^2 + (13{,}000)^2} $$ $$ = \sqrt{2.9241 \times 10^8 + 6.4000 \times 10^7 + 1.6900 \times 10^8} $$ $$ = \sqrt{(292.41 + 64.00 + 169.00) \times 10^6} = \sqrt{525.41 \times 10^6} \text{ km} $$ $$ R \approx 22{,}921 \text{ km} $$ ### Step 5: Extract the receiver clock bias $$ b = \rho - R = 16{,}998.7 - 22{,}921 = -5{,}922.3 \text{ km} $$ In time units: $b/c = -19.76$ ms, meaning the receiver clock is about 19.76 ms slow. > [!NOTE] > In a real receiver, all four pseudoranges (and four satellites) are solved simultaneously, so we get the position **and** $b$ in one shot. This single-satellite numerical check is purely for pedagogical understanding of what the "pseudorange" really contains. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — GPS Operational Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CSS["CONTROL SEGMENT - USA"] MCS["Master Control Station
Schriever AFB Colorado"] MS1["Monitor Station 1
Hawaii"] MS2["Monitor Station 2
Colorado Springs"] MS3["Monitor Station 3
Ascension Island"] MS4["Monitor Station 4
Diego Garcia"] MS5["Monitor Station 5
Kwajalein Atoll"] MS6["Monitor Station 6
Cape Canaveral"] UA1["Ground Antenna Upload 1"] UA2["Ground Antenna Upload 2"] end subgraph SSS["SPACE SEGMENT - MEO 20200 KM"] SAT1["Satellite SV1
Plane A Slot 1"] SAT2["Satellite SV2
Plane A Slot 2"] SAT3["Satellite SV3
Plane B Slot 3"] SAT4["Satellite SV4
Plane C Slot 4"] SAT5["Satellite SV5
Plane D Slot 5"] SAT6["Satellite SV6
Plane E Slot 6"] end subgraph USS["USER SEGMENT - AIRCRAFT"] ANT["GPS Antenna
Upper Fuselage"] RFE["RF Front End
L1 L2 L5"] COR["Correlator
12 to 24 channels"] NAV["Navigation Processor
PVT Solver"] DISP["Cockpit Display
ND and MFD"] FMS["Flight Management System
RNAV Database"] end MS1 -.Pseudorange tracking.-> MCS MS2 -.Pseudorange tracking.-> MCS MS3 -.Pseudorange tracking.-> MCS MS4 -.Pseudorange tracking.-> MCS MS5 -.Pseudorange tracking.-> MCS MS6 -.Pseudorange tracking.-> MCS MCS --> UA1 MCS --> UA2 UA1 -.S band upload.-> SAT1 UA1 -.S band upload.-> SAT2 UA1 -.S band upload.-> SAT3 UA2 -.S band upload.-> SAT4 UA2 -.S band upload.-> SAT5 UA2 -.S band upload.-> SAT6 SAT1 -.L band C/A and P code.-> ANT SAT2 -.L band C/A and P code.-> ANT SAT3 -.L band C/A and P code.-> ANT ANT --> RFE RFE --> COR COR --> NAV NAV --> DISP NAV --> FMS ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Trilateration Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR E["EARTH SURFACE
Receiver at user point U"] S1["Satellite SV1
Pseudorange rho1"] S2["Satellite SV2
Pseudorange rho2"] S3["Satellite SV3
Pseudorange rho3"] S4["Satellite SV4
Pseudorange rho4 - solves clock bias"] C1["Sphere of radius rho1
centre SV1"] C2["Sphere of radius rho2
centre SV2"] C3["Sphere of radius rho3
centre SV3"] C4["Sphere of radius rho4
centre SV4"] U["USER POSITION
Intersection point
Lat Long Alt"] S1 --> C1 S2 --> C2 S3 --> C3 S4 --> C4 C1 --> U C2 --> U C3 --> U C4 --> U ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — GPS Signal Structure ```mermaid flowchart TB CR["Carrier Wave
L1 1575.42 MHz
L2 1227.60 MHz
L5 1176.45 MHz"] CA["C/A Code
1.023 Mbps chip rate
Pseudo random noise
1 ms period"] PY["P(Y) Code
10.23 Mbps
Encrypted military"] NAV["Navigation Message
50 bps
Ephemeris Almanac Clock"] L1M["L1 Signal = CA + PY + NAV"] L2M["L2 Signal = PY + NAV"] L5M["L5 Signal = L5C + L5I + NAV"] CR --> L1M CR --> L2M CR --> L5M CA --> L1M PY --> L1M PY --> L2M NAV --> L1M NAV --> L2M NAV --> L5M ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Aviation GPS Integrity Chain (RAIM) ```mermaid flowchart TB SV["Satellite Broadcast
Position plus clock"] RX["Receiver Tracks
5 or more SVs"] RAIM["RAIM Algorithm
Fault Detection
and Exclusion"] CHK["Consistency Check
Compare all subsets of 4 SVs"] OK["Position Solution
PL within HAL and VAL"] FAIL["RAIM Warning
UNABLE APPROACH
flag to FMS"] SV --> RX RX --> RAIM RAIM --> CHK CHK --> OK CHK --> FAIL ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Engineering Insight:** The reason modern RNAV approaches require **5 satellites** (not just 4) is that the 5th satellite enables **Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM)**. If one satellite has a clock or ephemeris error, RAIM detects the inconsistency and either **excludes** the bad SV (FDE — Fault Detection and Exclusion) or warns the crew. Without RAIM, an undetected faulty satellite could cause a position error of hundreds of metres — **catastrophic on a final approach**. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]` **Define the Global Positioning System and list its three operational segments.** **Model Answer:** The Global Positioning System (GPS) is a satellite-based radio navigation system that provides three-dimensional position, velocity, and time (PVT) information to a properly equipped user anywhere on or near the Earth. The three operational segments of GPS are: 1. **Space Segment** — the constellation of 24+3 satellites in 6 orbital planes at 20,200 km altitude. 2. **Control Segment** — 1 Master Control Station, 6 Monitor Stations, and 4 Ground Antenna Upload Stations. 3. **User Segment** — the GPS receiver (in our case, the aircraft's avionics) that processes signals to compute position. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023, KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **What is Selective Availability? Why is it not a concern for civil aviation today?** **Model Answer:** **Selective Availability (SA)** was a deliberate degradation of the civil GPS signal's accuracy (dithering of the satellite clock and truncation of ephemeris data) imposed by the US Department of Defense to limit civil accuracy to ~100 m (2 dRMS). SA was **permanently switched off by an executive order of US President Bill Clinton on 2 May 2000** at 04:00 UTC. Since that date, civil aviation enjoys the full ~7.8 m (95%) accuracy of the Standard Positioning Service, making SA irrelevant to civil aviation today. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > The most common mistake in GPS 14-mark answers is to **write only the conceptual explanation and forget the math**. Always include: (a) the trilateration equation, (b) the role of the 4th satellite for clock bias, and (c) the DOP formula. Skipping any one of these is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of GPS. State any six specifications of the GPS satellite constellation. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** The working principle of GPS is based on **one-way time-of-arrival (TOA) ranging** from multiple satellites, combined with **3D trilateration**. **Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 above).** **Working Principle Step-by-Step:** 1. Each GPS satellite continuously broadcasts a **navigation message** modulated onto L-band carriers (L1, L2, L5). The message contains the satellite's precise orbital position (ephemeris) and a high-accuracy time stamp from its atomic clock. 2. The aircraft's GPS antenna receives these signals from **at least 4 satellites** in line of sight. 3. The receiver measures the **time of arrival** of each signal and subtracts the **time of transmission** (from the message). The difference is $\Delta t_i$. 4. The pseudorange to each satellite is computed as $\rho_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i$. 5. The receiver solves a system of 4 pseudorange equations (one per satellite) for 4 unknowns: $x_u, y_u, z_u$ (3D position) and $b$ (clock bias). 6. The resulting ECEF coordinates are converted to **geodetic latitude, longitude, and altitude** (WGS-84 ellipsoid) for cockpit display. **Six Specifications of the GPS Constellation:** 1. Total operational satellites: **24 + 3 active spares = 27** 2. Orbital altitude: **20,200 km (Medium Earth Orbit)** 3. Orbital inclination: **55°** to the equator 4. Number of orbital planes: **6** (4 satellites per plane, evenly spaced by 90° in argument of latitude) 5. Orbital period: **11 hours 58 minutes** (half a sidereal day) 6. Satellites are **reusable** (the same satellite reappears over the same point 4 minutes earlier each day) **(b) Explain the error sources in GPS. What is DOP and how does it affect position accuracy? [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** GPS errors fall into three broad categories: **A. Satellite-Originated Errors:** - **Satellite clock error:** Even atomic clocks drift. Corrected by clock parameters in the navigation message (residual error ~1.0 m, 1σ). - **Ephemeris (orbit) error:** The Control Segment updates orbits every 24 hours; between uploads the predicted orbit drifts (~1.0 m, 1σ). **B. Signal-Path Errors:** - **Ionospheric delay:** The ionosphere (60–1000 km altitude) is dispersive. L1 and L2 signals are delayed by different amounts. **Dual-frequency receivers** can correct this to a few cm. Single-frequency users apply the **Klobuchar model** with ~50% reduction. - **Tropospheric delay:** Non-dispersive (affects L1 and L2 equally). Mostly dry-air and wet-air components. Mitigated by the **Saastamoinen** or **Marini** model (~0.5–2.0 m error). - **Multipath:** Signal reflects off aircraft skin, ground, or terrain. Most dangerous near the ground. Mitigated by **choke-ring antennas** and **narrow correlator** receivers. **C. Receiver-Originated Errors:** - **Receiver noise and resolution:** ~0.5 m. - **Receiver clock bias:** Solved explicitly as the 4th unknown. **Dilution of Precision (DOP):** DOP is a **geometric multiplier** that captures how satellite geometry affects position error. It is the ratio of the user position error to the user range error (URE): $$ \sigma_P = DOP \times \sigma_{URE} $$ If satellites are well spread in the sky, errors in pseudoranges project into orthogonal directions and partially cancel, giving a **low DOP (good geometry)**. If satellites are clustered (e.g., all near the horizon), errors add up, giving a **high DOP (bad geometry)**. **Types of DOP:** - **GDOP** (Geometric) — total 4D (position + time) - **PDOP** (Position) — 3D spatial - **HDOP** (Horizontal) — 2D lateral - **VDOP** (Vertical) — altitude channel - **TDOP** (Time) — clock channel **Acceptance rule:** A PDOP ≤ 6 is generally required for en-route flight. For approach procedures, HDOP ≤ 4 and VDOP ≤ 6 are typical. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]` **(a) Derive the position-fix equations used in GPS, starting from pseudorange. Clearly state the role of the 4th satellite. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **The Pseudorange Equation:** The receiver measures the time taken for the signal to travel from satellite $i$ to itself. Multiplying by $c$ gives the **pseudorange**: $$ \rho_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i $$ In reality, the receiver clock has an unknown bias $b$ (in metres), and the satellite clock has a residual error after broadcast corrections $\delta t_s^{(i)}$. So the measured pseudorange is: $$ \rho_i = R_i + b - c\,\delta t_s^{(i)} + \varepsilon_i $$ For simplicity, absorb the satellite clock error into $\varepsilon_i$ (it is corrected via the navigation message): $$ \rho_i = R_i + b + \varepsilon_i $$ where the **true geometric range** is: $$ R_i = \sqrt{(x_s^{(i)} - x_u)^2 + (y_s^{(i)} - y_u)^2 + (z_s^{(i)} - z_u)^2} $$ **Why 4 satellites are needed (4 unknowns):** The unknowns we must solve for are: 1. $x_u$ — user x-coordinate 2. $y_u$ — user y-coordinate 3. $z_u$ — user z-coordinate 4. $b$ — receiver clock bias A single pseudorange equation is non-linear and contains all 4 unknowns. **Each additional satellite provides one more independent equation.** So with $N$ satellites, we have $N$ equations in 4 unknowns. The minimum number for a unique solution is **N = 4**. The first three satellites give the geometric position, and the **4th satellite resolves the time bias**. **Linearization and solution:** Expanding $R_i$ about an initial guess $\vec{x}_0$, we obtain the linear system (derived fully in Section 3.1): $$ \Delta \vec{\rho} = H \cdot \Delta \vec{x} $$ $$ \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \Delta \rho_3 \\ \Delta \rho_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{e}_{x,1} & \hat{e}_{y,1} & \hat{e}_{z,1} & 1 \\ \hat{e}_{x,2} & \hat{e}_{y,2} & \hat{e}_{z,2} & 1 \\ \hat{e}_{x,3} & \hat{e}_{y,3} & \hat{e}_{z,3} & 1 \\ \hat{e}_{x,4} & \hat{e}_{y,4} & \hat{e}_{z,4} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ \Delta b \end{bmatrix} $$ Solving (least-squares for $N>4$): $$ \Delta \vec{x} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \Delta \vec{\rho} $$ Iterate until convergence. The 4th column of $H$ (the column of ones) is the contribution of the clock bias $b$ — **this is what makes the 4th satellite essential.** **(b) Explain the concept of RAIM and its importance in civil aviation. [7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM)** is a technique used by GPS receivers in aircraft to **detect (and sometimes exclude) a faulty satellite** from the position solution, in real time, without external aids. **The Integrity Problem:** GPS was originally designed for military use. The US government guarantees a minimum signal-in-space accuracy but **does not guarantee a timely warning** if a satellite goes bad. A satellite clock failure or an erroneous ephemeris upload can cause a position error of hundreds of metres that may go unnoticed for **up to several hours** (until the next Control Segment upload). For an aircraft on a final approach, **this is unacceptable** — the **Vertical Alert Limit (VAL) is 50 m for LPV approaches**. **How RAIM Works:** 1. The receiver tracks **at least 5 satellites** in view (one more than the geometric minimum of 4). 2. It computes position solutions using **all possible combinations of 4 satellites** (10 combinations for 5 SVs, 15 for 6 SVs, etc.). 3. Each subset gives a slightly different position. The spread of these solutions is compared against a threshold. 4. If the spread is **small** (all subsets agree), the geometry is self-consistent and the position is **reliable**. 5. If the spread is **large**, one satellite is likely bad. The receiver identifies the bad SV (Fault Detection) and either **excludes** it (Fault Detection and Exclusion — FDE) or **inhibits approach guidance** ("UNABLE RAIM" annunciator on the FMS). **Key RAIM Parameters:** - **HAL (Horizontal Alert Limit):** Maximum tolerable horizontal position error. For en-route = 3.7 km; for approach = 40 m (LPV). - **VAL (Vertical Alert Limit):** Maximum tolerable vertical error. For LPV approach = **50 m**. - **Time to Alert (TTA):** ≤ 10 seconds for en-route, ≤ 6 seconds for approach. **Aviation Importance:** RAIM is the **single most important enabler of civil aviation GPS use**. Without it, civil aircraft could not use GPS as a **primary means of navigation** for IFR flight, RNAV routes, or GPS approaches. RAIM is mandated by **ICAO Annex 10** and **FAA TSO-C129a** (and the newer **TSO-C196** for SBAS-capable receivers, which uses **WAAS** to enhance RAIM into a continent-wide FDE system). > [!WARNING] > **Common student mistake:** Students often confuse RAIM with SBAS (WAAS, EGNOS). RAIM uses **only the satellites in view** to self-check. SBAS uses **geostationary satellites** (e.g., INMARSAT over the Indian Ocean for GAGAN) to provide additional integrity data. They are **complementary**, not the same. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall:** - **GPS is one of four GNSS systems** (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou). Use "GNSS" in formal ICAO contexts. - **24 + 3 = 27 satellites** in **6 planes**, **4 per plane**, at **20,200 km**, **55° inclination**, **~12-hour period** (actually 11h 58m). - **Three segments: Space, Control (OCS), User.** Master Control Station is at **Schriever AFB, Colorado**. - **Three carrier frequencies:** L1 = 1575.42 MHz (civil + military), L2 = 1227.60 MHz (military), L5 = 1176.45 MHz (new civil, safety-of-life). - **Two ranging codes:** C/A code (1.023 Mbps, civilian) and P(Y) code (10.23 Mbps, encrypted military). - **Pseudorange equation:** $\rho_i = R_i + b + \varepsilon_i$. **The 4th satellite is needed to solve for the clock bias $b$.** - **Dilution of Precision (DOP):** $GDOP \ge PDOP \ge \sqrt{HDOP^2 + VDOP^2}$. Lower DOP = better geometry. PDOP ≤ 6 is good. - **Errors in order of magnitude (1σ):** Ionospheric (~5 m) > Ephemeris (~1 m) > Clock (~1 m) > Tropospheric (~1 m) > Multipath (~0.5 m) > Receiver noise (~0.5 m). - **Selective Availability (SA) was permanently switched OFF on 2 May 2000.** No longer affects civil aviation. - **WGS-84** is the reference ellipsoid used by GPS. The official world geodetic datum. - **RAIM** requires **5 or more satellites** for fault detection; **6 or more** for fault exclusion. Modern receivers add **SBAS (WAAS, EGNOS, GAGAN, MSAS)** to provide integrity from geostationary augmentation satellites. - **Accuracy figures to memorize:** SPS = ~7.8 m (95%, 2D), PPS = ~2 m (military), WAAS-aided = ~1.5 m horizontal / ~3 m vertical. - **ICAO performance categories:** NPA (Non-Precision Approach) = 220 m HAL; APV-I = 40 m HAL / 50 m VAL (LP); APV-II = 40 m HAL / 20 m VAL (LPV); CAT-I equivalent = 16 m HAL / 4 m VAL (GLS only). - **GPS Modernization Block III:** Adds L1C, L5, higher power M-code, and search-and-rescue payload. - **Differential GPS (DGPS) and SBAS** both use range corrections broadcast from ground stations to improve accuracy to the **few-metre** level. - **Time is critical:** A **1 microsecond** clock error = **300 metres** of range error. This is why atomic clocks on satellites and the explicit solution for $b$ are non-negotiable. - **Typical exam key points (14-mark):** Always draw a labelled block diagram, write the pseudorange equation, list error sources with magnitudes, and conclude with aviation applications (RAIM, RNAV, SBAS). ---
